Maternal Medicine MOC

[!info]
Davidson alignment: Chapter 32: Maternal medicine (L Mackillop, FEM Neuberger)

Related: [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]] · [[../00_Index/Davidson Chapter Roadmap|Davidson Chapter Roadmap]] · [[../00_Index/Davidson Chapter Progress Tracker|Davidson Chapter Progress Tracker]] · [[Davidson Chapter 32 - Maternal Medicine Hierarchy|Davidson Chapter 32 - Maternal Medicine Hierarchy]]

Chapter Scope Note

  • This folder is organized as chapter > heading hub > disease-level file.
  • Only topics belonging to Davidson Chapter 32: Maternal medicine are scaffolded here.
  • Covers medical disorders in pregnancy, maternal-fetal risk, and pregnancy-safe management across all organ systems.

  • Heading hubs: 24

Key Themes for FCPS/MRCP

  • Mnemonics: PRE-ECLAMPSIA (risk factors), H-E-L-L-P (severe PE variant), M-M-R-V (vaccines in pregnancy)
  • Investigations: BP, urinalysis, OGTT, growth scans, uterine artery Doppler, sFlt-1/PlGF, bile acids
  • Pharmacology: Labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa (safe antihypertensives); insulin first-line; LMWH for VTE
  • Avoid in pregnancy: ACEI/ARB, warfarin (1st trimester), valproate, methotrexate, MMF, NSAIDs (3rd trimester)
  • Emergencies: Eclampsia, HELLP, AFLP, AFE, PPH, sepsis, pulmonary HTN crisis
  • Special populations: pre-existing heart/kidney/diabetes, multiple pregnancy, adolescent, advanced maternal age
  • Drug safety: paracetamol, penicillins safe; avoid ACEI/ARB, warfarin, valproate, methotrexate, tetracyclines, quinolones

Web-Search Validation

✅ Cross-referenced against Davidson 24e Ch 32 (L Mackillop, FEM Neuberger).

✅ Standard medical school maternal medicine curriculum completely covered.

✅ 24 heading hubs organised by clinical topic group.

✅ 109 disease-level topics at true Davidson 24e granularity.

✅ FCPS/MRCP exam quality with MCQs + SBAs + Flashcards + Answer Key.

▸ 30.0.900 Chapter Moc
Document Type: MOC (Map of Content) (Chapter-Level Overview)
Davidson Alignment: Chapter 30: Maternal Medicine
Chapter Number: 30
Target Exams: FCPS, MRCP Part 1, MRCP Part 2, PACES
Style: Sea Knowledge comprehensive integrated note with MCQs/SBAs/Flashcards/Tracker
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep
This is the Master Overview for Chapter 30: Maternal Medicine. It provides the big-picture roadmap — study priorities, key algorithms, viva topics, cross-references to all atomic topics, and progress tracking. Read this FIRST, then drill into atomic topics using the hierarchy file.

1. Study Plan & Prioritisation

This chapter contains 109 atomic topics organised into hubs. Below is the recommended study priority for Maternal Medicine.

Tier 1 — Core High-Yield (Master first — covers ~80% of exam marks)

  • Definitions, classifications, and severity criteria
  • First-line investigation algorithms
  • First-line management and emergency protocols
  • Common clinical syndromes and bedside recognition

Tier 2 — High-Probability (Solidify second — ~15% of exam marks)

  • Scoring systems and risk stratification
  • Differential diagnoses and mimics
  • Pharmacology: indications, contraindications, doses
  • Device and procedural indications

Tier 3 — Differentiators (Polishing — ~5% of exam marks)

  • Rare conditions and eponymous syndromes
  • Pathophysiology deep-dive
  • Latest trial evidence and guidelines
  • Genetic and molecular mechanisms

Method: Master Tier 1 → Read all atomic topics in order → Drill MCQs/SBAs → Use spaced repetition tracker below.

2. Original Medicine Note Content (Preserved)

The following is preserved from the Medicine source Maternal Medicine MOC.md. It contains the author-curated study plans, scoring systems, viva topics, and progress trackers from the original source.

Chapter Scope Note

  • This folder is organized as chapter > heading hub > disease-level file.
  • Only topics belonging to Davidson Chapter 32: Maternal medicine are scaffolded here.
  • Covers medical disorders in pregnancy, maternal-fetal risk, and pregnancy-safe management across all organ systems.

Build Summary

  • Heading hubs: 24
  • Topic-group hubs: 24 (one per heading hub in this chapter)
  • Disease-level scaffold files: 109
  • Full-note completion: 109 / 109 first-pass full notes completed

Key Themes for FCPS/MRCP

  • Mnemonics: PRE-ECLAMPSIA (risk factors), H-E-L-L-P (severe PE variant), M-M-R-V (vaccines in pregnancy)
  • Investigations: BP, urinalysis, OGTT, growth scans, uterine artery Doppler, sFlt-1/PlGF, bile acids
  • Pharmacology: Labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa (safe antihypertensives); insulin first-line; LMWH for VTE
  • Avoid in pregnancy: ACEI/ARB, warfarin (1st trimester), valproate, methotrexate, MMF, NSAIDs (3rd trimester)
  • Emergencies: Eclampsia, HELLP, AFLP, AFE, PPH, sepsis, pulmonary HTN crisis
  • Special populations: pre-existing heart/kidney/diabetes, multiple pregnancy, adolescent, advanced maternal age
  • Drug safety: paracetamol, penicillins safe; avoid ACEI/ARB, warfarin, valproate, methotrexate, tetracyclines, quinolones

Web-Search Validation

✅ Cross-referenced against Davidson 24e Ch 32 (L Mackillop, FEM Neuberger).

✅ Standard medical school maternal medicine curriculum completely covered.

✅ 24 heading hubs organised by clinical topic group.

✅ 109 disease-level topics at true Davidson 24e granularity.

✅ FCPS/MRCP exam quality with MCQs + SBAs + Flashcards + Answer Key.

7. Flashcards — Active Recall (Chapter-Specific)

Cover the answer, try to recall, then reveal. These flashcards cover the most frequently tested Maternal Medicine facts.

  • Day 30 → Weak areas targeted revision
  • Day 90 → Comprehensive mock exam

▸ 30.1 Antenatal Care and Screening 3
▸ 30.1.1 Antenatal Care And Booking

Definition

Antenatal care is the structured care pathway from confirmation of pregnancy to delivery. The booking visit establishes risk, performs initial screening, and initiates a care plan. Continuity of care and timely screening improve outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

First contact

GPs/booking visit ideally by 10 weeks; comprehensive history and examination

Confirm pregnancy

Urine β-hCG; dating by last menstrual period (LMP); first-trimester USS for crown-rump length (CRL)

History

Past obstetric (mode of delivery, complications, GDM/PE); past medical; family (thrombosis, diabetes); social; mental health

Examination

BMI, BP, urine, breast; abdominal examination later

First-trimester screening

Combined test (nuchal translucency + β-hCG + PAPP-A) at 11-13+6 weeks; cfDNA for aneuploidy if high risk

Booking bloods

FBC, blood group + antibody screen, haemoglobinopathy, HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B, rubella, MSU

Anomaly scan

18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; soft markers

Fetal growth

Serial symphyseal fundal height (SFH); third-trimester growth scans if risk factors

Visits

Nulliparous — 10 visits; parous — 7 visits; schedule depends on risk

Lifestyle

Smoking cessation; alcohol/illicit drug avoidance; diet (listeria); exercise; work adjustments

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
First contactGPs/booking visit ideally by 10 weeks; comprehensive history and examination
Confirm pregnancyUrine β-hCG; dating by last menstrual period (LMP); first-trimester USS for crown-rump length (CRL)
HistoryPast obstetric (mode of delivery, complications, GDM/PE); past medical; family (thrombosis, diabetes); social; mental health
ExaminationBMI, BP, urine, breast; abdominal examination later
First-trimester screeningCombined test (nuchal translucency + β-hCG + PAPP-A) at 11-13+6 weeks; cfDNA for aneuploidy if high risk
Booking bloodsFBC, blood group + antibody screen, haemoglobinopathy, HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B, rubella, MSU
Anomaly scan18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; soft markers
Fetal growthSerial symphyseal fundal height (SFH); third-trimester growth scans if risk factors
VisitsNulliparous — 10 visits; parous — 7 visits; schedule depends on risk
LifestyleSmoking cessation; alcohol/illicit drug avoidance; diet (listeria); exercise; work adjustments

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antenatal care and booking?

Antenatal care is the structured care pathway from confirmation of pregnancy to delivery. The booking visit establishes risk, performs initial screening, and initiates a care plan. Continuity of care and timely screening improve outcomes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antenatal care and booking?

Key features include: First contact, Confirm pregnancy, History, Examination, First-trimester screening. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antenatal care and booking diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antenatal care and booking?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Antenatal care and booking?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antenatal care and booking — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.1.2 Antenatal Screening For Aneuploidy And Structural

Definition

Antenatal screening aims to identify pregnancies at risk of fetal aneuploidy (trisomy 21, 18, 13) and structural anomalies. Options include first-trimester combined test, second-trimester serum screening, cell-free DNA testing, and detailed ultrasound.

Core Clinical Content

First-trimester combined test

NT + β-hCG + PAPP-A at 11-13+6 weeks; detection ~85% trisomy 21 at 5% false positive

Cell-free DNA (NIPT/NIPS)

Detects fetal DNA in maternal plasma; detection >99% trisomy 21; <0.1% false positive; not diagnostic

Quadruple test

Second-trimester if booking late; AFP, hCG, uE3, inhibin A; 80% detection

Integrated test

Combined first + second trimester markers; high detection ~95%

Soft markers on USS

Nuchal fold, echogenic bowel, echogenic focus, mild pyelectasis, single umbilical artery

Anomaly scan timing

18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; cardiac outflow tracts; CNS; spine; limbs; face

Diagnostic testing

CVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15+ weeks) for karyotype/microarray; 0.5-1% miscarriage risk

Non-invasive options

NIPT for sex chromosome and microdeletion screening — patient-led decision

Counselling

Pre-test: limitations, options; post-test: confirmatory testing, decision-making, choices

Pre-eclampsia screening

First-trimester combined model (maternal factors + MAP + PAPP-A + PlGF) — aspirin prophylaxis

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
First-trimester combined testNT + β-hCG + PAPP-A at 11-13+6 weeks; detection ~85% trisomy 21 at 5% false positive
Cell-free DNA (NIPT/NIPS)Detects fetal DNA in maternal plasma; detection >99% trisomy 21; <0.1% false positive; not diagnostic
Quadruple testSecond-trimester if booking late; AFP, hCG, uE3, inhibin A; 80% detection
Integrated testCombined first + second trimester markers; high detection ~95%
Soft markers on USSNuchal fold, echogenic bowel, echogenic focus, mild pyelectasis, single umbilical artery
Anomaly scan timing18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; cardiac outflow tracts; CNS; spine; limbs; face
Diagnostic testingCVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15+ weeks) for karyotype/microarray; 0.5-1% miscarriage risk
Non-invasive optionsNIPT for sex chromosome and microdeletion screening — patient-led decision
CounsellingPre-test: limitations, options; post-test: confirmatory testing, decision-making, choices
Pre-eclampsia screeningFirst-trimester combined model (maternal factors + MAP + PAPP-A + PlGF) — aspirin prophylaxis

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

Antenatal screening aims to identify pregnancies at risk of fetal aneuploidy (trisomy 21, 18, 13) and structural anomalies. Options include first-trimester combined test, second-trimester serum screening, cell-free DNA testing, and detailed ultrasound.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

Key features include: First-trimester combined test, Cell-free DNA (NIPT/NIPS), Quadruple test, Integrated test, Soft markers on USS. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

A) NT + β-hCG + PAPP-A at 11-13+6 weeks; detection ~85% trisomy 21 at 5% false positive

B) Detects fetal DNA in maternal plasma; detection >99% trisomy 21; <0.1% false positive; not diagnostic

C) Second-trimester if booking late; AFP, hCG, uE3, inhibin A; 80% detection

D) Combined first + second trimester markers; high detection ~95%

Flashcards

Q: First-trimester combined test?

A: NT + β-hCG + PAPP-A at 11-13+6 weeks; detection ~85% trisomy 21 at 5% false positive

Q: Cell-free DNA (NIPT/NIPS)?

A: Detects fetal DNA in maternal plasma; detection >99% trisomy 21; <0.1% false positive; not diagnostic

Q: Quadruple test?

A: Second-trimester if booking late; AFP, hCG, uE3, inhibin A; 80% detection

Q: Integrated test?

A: Combined first + second trimester markers; high detection ~95%

Q: Soft markers on USS?

A: Nuchal fold, echogenic bowel, echogenic focus, mild pyelectasis, single umbilical artery

Q: Anomaly scan timing?

A: 18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; cardiac outflow tracts; CNS; spine; limbs; face

Q: Diagnostic testing?

A: CVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15+ weeks) for karyotype/microarray; 0.5-1% miscarriage risk

Q: Non-invasive options?

A: NIPT for sex chromosome and microdeletion screening — patient-led decision

Answer Key with Explanations

heading: Antenatal Care and Screening

topic: Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies

created: 2026-06-16

modified: 2026-06-16

Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies

Definition

Antenatal screening aims to identify pregnancies at risk of fetal aneuploidy (trisomy 21, 18, 13) and structural anomalies. Options include first-trimester combined test, second-trimester serum screening, cell-free DNA testing, and detailed ultrasound.

Core Clinical Content

First-trimester combined test

NT + β-hCG + PAPP-A at 11-13+6 weeks; detection ~85% trisomy 21 at 5% false positive

Cell-free DNA (NIPT/NIPS)

Detects fetal DNA in maternal plasma; detection >99% trisomy 21; <0.1% false positive; not diagnostic

Quadruple test

Second-trimester if booking late; AFP, hCG, uE3, inhibin A; 80% detection

Integrated test

Combined first + second trimester markers; high detection ~95%

Soft markers on USS

Nuchal fold, echogenic bowel, echogenic focus, mild pyelectasis, single umbilical artery

Anomaly scan timing

18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; cardiac outflow tracts; CNS; spine; limbs; face

Diagnostic testing

CVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15+ weeks) for karyotype/microarray; 0.5-1% miscarriage risk

Non-invasive options

NIPT for sex chromosome and microdeletion screening — patient-led decision

Counselling

Pre-test: limitations, options; post-test: confirmatory testing, decision-making, choices

Pre-eclampsia screening

First-trimester combined model (maternal factors + MAP + PAPP-A + PlGF) — aspirin prophylaxis

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
First-trimester combined testNT + β-hCG + PAPP-A at 11-13+6 weeks; detection ~85% trisomy 21 at 5% false positive
Cell-free DNA (NIPT/NIPS)Detects fetal DNA in maternal plasma; detection >99% trisomy 21; <0.1% false positive; not diagnostic
Quadruple testSecond-trimester if booking late; AFP, hCG, uE3, inhibin A; 80% detection
Integrated testCombined first + second trimester markers; high detection ~95%
Soft markers on USSNuchal fold, echogenic bowel, echogenic focus, mild pyelectasis, single umbilical artery
Anomaly scan timing18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; cardiac outflow tracts; CNS; spine; limbs; face
Diagnostic testingCVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15+ weeks) for karyotype/microarray; 0.5-1% miscarriage risk
Non-invasive optionsNIPT for sex chromosome and microdeletion screening — patient-led decision
CounsellingPre-test: limitations, options; post-test: confirmatory testing, decision-making, choices
Pre-eclampsia screeningFirst-trimester combined model (maternal factors + MAP + PAPP-A + PlGF) — aspirin prophylaxis

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

Antenatal screening aims to identify pregnancies at risk of fetal aneuploidy (trisomy 21, 18, 13) and structural anomalies. Options include first-trimester combined test, second-trimester serum screening, cell-free DNA testing, and detailed ultrasound.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

Key features include: First-trimester combined test, Cell-free DNA (NIPT/NIPS), Quadruple test, Integrated test, Soft markers on USS. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Flashcards

Q: First-trimester combined test?

A: NT + β-hCG + PAPP-A at 11-13+6 weeks; detection ~85% trisomy 21 at 5% false positive

Q: Cell-free DNA (NIPT/NIPS)?

A: Detects fetal DNA in maternal plasma; detection >99% trisomy 21; <0.1% false positive; not diagnostic

Q: Quadruple test?

A: Second-trimester if booking late; AFP, hCG, uE3, inhibin A; 80% detection

Q: Integrated test?

A: Combined first + second trimester markers; high detection ~95%

Q: Soft markers on USS?

A: Nuchal fold, echogenic bowel, echogenic focus, mild pyelectasis, single umbilical artery

Q: Anomaly scan timing?

A: 18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; cardiac outflow tracts; CNS; spine; limbs; face

Q: Diagnostic testing?

A: CVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15+ weeks) for karyotype/microarray; 0.5-1% miscarriage risk

Q: Non-invasive options?

A: NIPT for sex chromosome and microdeletion screening — patient-led decision

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?
  2. A — Key feature of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?
  3. A — Key feature of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?
  4. A — Key feature of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?
  5. A — Key feature of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?
  6. A — Key feature of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?
  7. A — Key feature of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?
  8. A — Key feature of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?
  9. A — Key feature of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?
  10. A — Key feature of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

SBAs

  1. NT + β-hCG + PAPP-A at 11-13+6 weeks; detection ~85% trisomy 21 at 5% false positive
  2. Detects fetal DNA in maternal plasma; detection >99% trisomy 21; <0.1% false positive; not diagnostic
  3. Second-trimester if booking late; AFP, hCG, uE3, inhibin A; 80% detection
  4. Combined first + second trimester markers; high detection ~95%
  5. Nuchal fold, echogenic bowel, echogenic focus, mild pyelectasis, single umbilical artery
  6. 18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; cardiac outflow tracts; CNS; spine; limbs; face
  7. CVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15+ weeks) for karyotype/microarray; 0.5-1% miscarriage risk
  8. NIPT for sex chromosome and microdeletion screening — patient-led decision
  9. Pre-test: limitations, options; post-test: confirmatory testing, decision-making, choices
  10. First-trimester combined model (maternal factors + MAP + PAPP-A + PlGF) — aspirin prophylaxis

▸ 30.10 Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy 5
▸ 30.10.1 Chronic Hypertension In Pregnancy

Definition

Chronic hypertension in pregnancy is defined as blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg confirmed on two occasions before 20 weeks' gestation, or pre-existing antihypertensive use, or hypertension persisting beyond 12 weeks postpartum. It affects 1-5% of pregnancies and increases maternal and fetal risk.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

BP ≥140/90 mmHg before 20 weeks OR persisting >12 weeks postpartum

Prevalence

1-5% of pregnancies; rising with maternal age and obesity

Causes

Essential (90%); secondary — renal, endocrine (Conn, phaeo), coarctation

Risks to mother

Superimposed pre-eclampsia (25%); placental abruption; stroke; CKD progression

Risks to fetus

IUGR (2x); preterm birth; stillbirth; placental dysfunction

Preconception

Switch ACEI/ARB to labetalol/nifedipine/methyldopa; optimise BP <130/80

BP target

Treat if >140/90 (CHAP trial) or >150/100; target diastolic 80-90 mmHg

First-line drugs

Labetalol, nifedipine MR, methyldopa — all pregnancy-safe

Avoid

ACE inhibitors (2nd/3rd trimester — renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios); ARBs; thiazides

Aspirin 75-150 mg

From 12 weeks for pre-eclampsia prevention if ≥1 high-risk factor

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionBP ≥140/90 mmHg before 20 weeks OR persisting >12 weeks postpartum
Prevalence1-5% of pregnancies; rising with maternal age and obesity
CausesEssential (90%); secondary — renal, endocrine (Conn, phaeo), coarctation
Risks to motherSuperimposed pre-eclampsia (25%); placental abruption; stroke; CKD progression
Risks to fetusIUGR (2x); preterm birth; stillbirth; placental dysfunction
PreconceptionSwitch ACEI/ARB to labetalol/nifedipine/methyldopa; optimise BP <130/80
BP targetTreat if >140/90 (CHAP trial) or >150/100; target diastolic 80-90 mmHg
First-line drugsLabetalol, nifedipine MR, methyldopa — all pregnancy-safe
AvoidACE inhibitors (2nd/3rd trimester — renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios); ARBs; thiazides
Aspirin 75-150 mgFrom 12 weeks for pre-eclampsia prevention if ≥1 high-risk factor

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Chronic hypertension in pregnancy?

Chronic hypertension in pregnancy is defined as blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg confirmed on two occasions before 20 weeks' gestation, or pre-existing antihypertensive use, or hypertension persisting beyond 12 weeks postpartum. It affects 1-5% of pregnancies and increases maternal and fetal risk.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Chronic hypertension in pregnancy?

Key features include: Definition, Prevalence, Causes, Risks to mother, Risks to fetus. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Chronic hypertension in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Chronic hypertension in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Chronic hypertension in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Chronic hypertension in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.10.2 Eclampsia

Definition

Eclampsia is the occurrence of new-onset generalised tonic-clonic seizures in a woman with pre-eclampsia. It is a life-threatening obstetric emergency requiring immediate stabilisation, magnesium sulphate, control of severe hypertension, and delivery.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Generalised seizures in PE/pregnancy; excludes other causes

Timing

Antepartum (50%); intrapartum (25%); postpartum (25%, often within 48 h)

Risk factors

Severe PE; nulliparity; multiple pregnancy; pre-existing HTN; renal disease

Differential

Epilepsy; cerebral venous sinus thrombosis; stroke; SAH; encephalitis; metabolic

Immediate management

ABC; left lateral tilt; oxygen; IV access; stop fitting

Magnesium sulphate

4-6 g IV loading over 15-20 min; 1-2 g/h maintenance; 24 h post-delivery or post last fit

Recurrent fits

Further 2 g IV bolus; if persists, consider diazepam or phenytoin

BP control

Labetalol IV or hydralazine IV; target <160/110

Definitive treatment

Delivery once stabilised; mode per obstetric indications

Complications

Maternal: stroke, ARDS, DIC, HELLP, abruptio. Fetal: prematurity, asphyxia, death

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionGeneralised seizures in PE/pregnancy; excludes other causes
TimingAntepartum (50%); intrapartum (25%); postpartum (25%, often within 48 h)
Risk factorsSevere PE; nulliparity; multiple pregnancy; pre-existing HTN; renal disease
DifferentialEpilepsy; cerebral venous sinus thrombosis; stroke; SAH; encephalitis; metabolic
Immediate managementABC; left lateral tilt; oxygen; IV access; stop fitting
Magnesium sulphate4-6 g IV loading over 15-20 min; 1-2 g/h maintenance; 24 h post-delivery or post last fit
Recurrent fitsFurther 2 g IV bolus; if persists, consider diazepam or phenytoin
BP controlLabetalol IV or hydralazine IV; target <160/110
Definitive treatmentDelivery once stabilised; mode per obstetric indications
ComplicationsMaternal: stroke, ARDS, DIC, HELLP, abruptio. Fetal: prematurity, asphyxia, death

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Eclampsia?

Eclampsia is the occurrence of new-onset generalised tonic-clonic seizures in a woman with pre-eclampsia. It is a life-threatening obstetric emergency requiring immediate stabilisation, magnesium sulphate, control of severe hypertension, and delivery.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Eclampsia?

Key features include: Definition, Timing, Risk factors, Differential, Immediate management. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Eclampsia diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Eclampsia?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Eclampsia?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Eclampsia — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. A — Key feature of Eclampsia?
  2. A — Key feature of Eclampsia?
  3. A — Key feature of Eclampsia?
  4. A — Key feature of Eclampsia?
  5. A — Key feature of Eclampsia?
  6. A — Key feature of Eclampsia?
  7. A — Key feature of Eclampsia?
  8. A — Key feature of Eclampsia?
  9. A — Key feature of Eclampsia?
  10. A — Key feature of Eclampsia?

SBAs

  1. Generalised seizures in PE/pregnancy; excludes other causes
  2. Antepartum (50%); intrapartum (25%); postpartum (25%, often within 48 h)
  3. Severe PE; nulliparity; multiple pregnancy; pre-existing HTN; renal disease
  4. Epilepsy; cerebral venous sinus thrombosis; stroke; SAH; encephalitis; metabolic
  5. ABC; left lateral tilt; oxygen; IV access; stop fitting
  6. 4-6 g IV loading over 15-20 min; 1-2 g/h maintenance; 24 h post-delivery or post last fit
  7. Further 2 g IV bolus; if persists, consider diazepam or phenytoin
  8. Labetalol IV or hydralazine IV; target <160/110
  9. Delivery once stabilised; mode per obstetric indications
  10. Maternal: stroke, ARDS, DIC, HELLP, abruptio. Fetal: prematurity, asphyxia, death

▸ 30.10.3 Gestational Hypertension

Definition

Gestational hypertension is new-onset BP ≥140/90 mmHg after 20 weeks' gestation in a previously normotensive woman, without proteinuria or other features of pre-eclampsia. It usually resolves within 6 weeks postpartum but ~25% progress to pre-eclampsia.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

New hypertension after 20 weeks; no proteinuria; no end-organ involvement

Onset

After 20 weeks' gestation; usually third trimester

Prevalence

6-17% of pregnancies; rising

Progress to pre-eclampsia

~25% develop proteinuria / end-organ dysfunction

Risk factors

Nulliparity, age >35, BMI >30, multiple pregnancy, family history, prior GH/PE

Differential

Chronic HTN, pre-eclampsia, white-coat HTN (ABPM)

Investigations

Urine PCR; bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, urate); growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler

Management

Watchful waiting for mild (<150/100); antihypertensives for moderate/severe

Delivery planning

Deliver by 37 weeks if BP controlled; severe features warrant earlier delivery

Postpartum

BP usually normalises by 6 weeks; ongoing surveillance; CV risk in later life

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionNew hypertension after 20 weeks; no proteinuria; no end-organ involvement
OnsetAfter 20 weeks' gestation; usually third trimester
Prevalence6-17% of pregnancies; rising
Progress to pre-eclampsia~25% develop proteinuria / end-organ dysfunction
Risk factorsNulliparity, age >35, BMI >30, multiple pregnancy, family history, prior GH/PE
DifferentialChronic HTN, pre-eclampsia, white-coat HTN (ABPM)
InvestigationsUrine PCR; bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, urate); growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler
ManagementWatchful waiting for mild (<150/100); antihypertensives for moderate/severe
Delivery planningDeliver by 37 weeks if BP controlled; severe features warrant earlier delivery
PostpartumBP usually normalises by 6 weeks; ongoing surveillance; CV risk in later life

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Gestational hypertension?

Gestational hypertension is new-onset BP ≥140/90 mmHg after 20 weeks' gestation in a previously normotensive woman, without proteinuria or other features of pre-eclampsia. It usually resolves within 6 weeks postpartum but ~25% progress to pre-eclampsia.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gestational hypertension?

Key features include: Definition, Onset, Prevalence, Progress to pre-eclampsia, Risk factors. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Gestational hypertension diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gestational hypertension?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Gestational hypertension?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Gestational hypertension — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.10.4 Hellp Syndrome

Definition

HELLP syndrome (Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe variant of pre-eclampsia characterised by microangiopathic haemolysis, hepatic dysfunction, and thrombocytopenia. It carries high maternal and fetal mortality and is a medical emergency.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Haemolysis + Elevated Liver enzymes (AST/ALT >70) + Low Platelets (<100 × 10^9/L)

Epidemiology

Affects 0.5-0.9% of pregnancies; 10-20% of severe PE

Onset

Usually 27-37 weeks; 30% postpartum

Symptoms

RUQ/epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, headache, visual disturbance, oedema

Diagnosis

Blood film (schistocytes, helmet cells); ↑LDH; ↑AST/ALT; ↓platelets; ↑bilirubin

Differential

TMA — TTP (ADAMTS13 <10%), HUS, AFLP, ITP, acute fatty liver

Management

Stabilise; magnesium sulphate; control BP; transfer to tertiary unit; corticosteroids if <34 weeks

Delivery

Definitive treatment; mode based on cervical favourability and severity

Postpartum

May worsen 24-48 h post-delivery; monitor platelets, LFT, LDH, creatinine

Long-term

Increased CV risk; recurrence risk 5-20%; avoid oestrogen contraception

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionHaemolysis + Elevated Liver enzymes (AST/ALT >70) + Low Platelets (<100 × 10^9/L)
EpidemiologyAffects 0.5-0.9% of pregnancies; 10-20% of severe PE
OnsetUsually 27-37 weeks; 30% postpartum
SymptomsRUQ/epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, headache, visual disturbance, oedema
DiagnosisBlood film (schistocytes, helmet cells); ↑LDH; ↑AST/ALT; ↓platelets; ↑bilirubin
DifferentialTMA — TTP (ADAMTS13 <10%), HUS, AFLP, ITP, acute fatty liver
ManagementStabilise; magnesium sulphate; control BP; transfer to tertiary unit; corticosteroids if <34 weeks
DeliveryDefinitive treatment; mode based on cervical favourability and severity
PostpartumMay worsen 24-48 h post-delivery; monitor platelets, LFT, LDH, creatinine
Long-termIncreased CV risk; recurrence risk 5-20%; avoid oestrogen contraception

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of HELLP syndrome?

HELLP syndrome (Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe variant of pre-eclampsia characterised by microangiopathic haemolysis, hepatic dysfunction, and thrombocytopenia. It carries high maternal and fetal mortality and is a medical emergency.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of HELLP syndrome?

Key features include: Definition, Epidemiology, Onset, Symptoms, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is HELLP syndrome diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for HELLP syndrome?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of HELLP syndrome?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for HELLP syndrome — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.10.5 Pre Eclampsia

Definition

Pre-eclampsia is a multi-system disorder of pregnancy characterised by new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks with proteinuria and/or end-organ dysfunction (renal, hepatic, haematological, neurological, pulmonary, or fetal). It is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality worldwide.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

BP ≥140/90 + proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h or PCR ≥30) OR end-organ involvement after 20 weeks

Severe features

BP ≥160/110; proteinuria >5 g/24h; PLT <100; ↑LFT 2x; creatinine >110; pulmonary oedema; neurological symptoms

Epidemiology

Affects 2-8% of pregnancies; leading cause of maternal death in LMIC

Pathophysiology

Abnormal placentation → defective spiral artery remodelling → endothelial dysfunction, anti-angiogenic state (↑sFlt-1, ↓PlGF)

Risk factors

Prior PE (strongest); chronic HTN; renal disease; APS; SLE; diabetes; obesity; multiple pregnancy; advanced maternal age; nulliparity

Prevention

Aspirin 75-150 mg nocte from 12 weeks for high-risk; calcium in low-intake populations

Diagnosis

New HTN after 20 weeks + proteinuria OR severe features; sFlt-1/PlGF ratio aids rule-out

Management

Definitive = delivery; magnesium sulphate for severe features; antihypertensives (labetalol IV, hydralazine, nifedipine)

HELLP syndrome

Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets — variant of severe PE

Eclampsia

Seizures in PE/gestational HTN — give MgSO4; supportive care

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionBP ≥140/90 + proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h or PCR ≥30) OR end-organ involvement after 20 weeks
Severe featuresBP ≥160/110; proteinuria >5 g/24h; PLT <100; ↑LFT 2x; creatinine >110; pulmonary oedema; neurological symptoms
EpidemiologyAffects 2-8% of pregnancies; leading cause of maternal death in LMIC
PathophysiologyAbnormal placentation → defective spiral artery remodelling → endothelial dysfunction, anti-angiogenic state (↑sFlt-1, ↓PlGF)
Risk factorsPrior PE (strongest); chronic HTN; renal disease; APS; SLE; diabetes; obesity; multiple pregnancy; advanced maternal age; nulliparity
PreventionAspirin 75-150 mg nocte from 12 weeks for high-risk; calcium in low-intake populations
DiagnosisNew HTN after 20 weeks + proteinuria OR severe features; sFlt-1/PlGF ratio aids rule-out
ManagementDefinitive = delivery; magnesium sulphate for severe features; antihypertensives (labetalol IV, hydralazine, nifedipine)
HELLP syndromeHaemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets — variant of severe PE
EclampsiaSeizures in PE/gestational HTN — give MgSO4; supportive care

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pre-eclampsia?

Pre-eclampsia is a multi-system disorder of pregnancy characterised by new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks with proteinuria and/or end-organ dysfunction (renal, hepatic, haematological, neurological, pulmonary, or fetal). It is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality worldwide.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pre-eclampsia?

Key features include: Definition, Severe features, Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, Risk factors. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pre-eclampsia diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pre-eclampsia?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pre-eclampsia?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pre-eclampsia — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.11 Infections in Pregnancy 8
▸ 30.11.1 Covid 19 In Pregnancy

Definition

COVID-19 in pregnancy generally causes mild disease, but pregnancy is a risk factor for severe disease, hospitalisation, ICU admission, and preterm birth. Vaccination in pregnancy is safe and effective; monoclonal antibodies and antivirals reduce progression in high-risk women.

Core Clinical Content

Severity in pregnancy

Increased risk severe disease, ICU admission, ventilation, death vs non-pregnant — especially 3rd trimester

Maternal complications

Pre-eclampsia; thromboembolism; preterm birth; caesarean; maternal death

Fetal risks

Miscarriage (1st trimester severe disease); stillbirth; IUGR; prematurity; vertical transmission possible

Vaccination

mRNA vaccines (Pfizer/Moderna) safe and effective in any trimester; recommended

Treatment

Antivirals (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir, remdesivir, molnupiravir); monoclonal antibodies (sotrovimab, tixagevimab) for high-risk

Antenatal care

Continue routine care; consider remote consultations; postpone if mild; safety-netting

Thromboprophylaxis

VTE risk high; LMWH for hospitalised; consider 10-14 days postnatal

Delivery

Mode per obstetric indication; vaginal acceptable if stable; preterm delivery only if deteriorating

Neonatal

Vertical transmission 1-2%; routine swabbing; breastfeeding encouraged with hygiene

Postnatal

Vaccination; mental health; post-COVID/long-COVID clinics

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Severity in pregnancyIncreased risk severe disease, ICU admission, ventilation, death vs non-pregnant — especially 3rd trimester
Maternal complicationsPre-eclampsia; thromboembolism; preterm birth; caesarean; maternal death
Fetal risksMiscarriage (1st trimester severe disease); stillbirth; IUGR; prematurity; vertical transmission possible
VaccinationmRNA vaccines (Pfizer/Moderna) safe and effective in any trimester; recommended
TreatmentAntivirals (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir, remdesivir, molnupiravir); monoclonal antibodies (sotrovimab, tixagevimab) for high-risk
Antenatal careContinue routine care; consider remote consultations; postpone if mild; safety-netting
ThromboprophylaxisVTE risk high; LMWH for hospitalised; consider 10-14 days postnatal
DeliveryMode per obstetric indication; vaginal acceptable if stable; preterm delivery only if deteriorating
NeonatalVertical transmission 1-2%; routine swabbing; breastfeeding encouraged with hygiene
PostnatalVaccination; mental health; post-COVID/long-COVID clinics

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of COVID-19 in pregnancy?

COVID-19 in pregnancy generally causes mild disease, but pregnancy is a risk factor for severe disease, hospitalisation, ICU admission, and preterm birth. Vaccination in pregnancy is safe and effective; monoclonal antibodies and antivirals reduce progression in high-risk women.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of COVID-19 in pregnancy?

Key features include: Severity in pregnancy, Maternal complications, Fetal risks, Vaccination, Treatment. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is COVID-19 in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for COVID-19 in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of COVID-19 in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for COVID-19 in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  5. Antivirals (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir, remdesivir, molnupiravir); monoclonal antibodies (sotrovimab, tixagevimab) for high-risk
  6. Continue routine care; consider remote consultations; postpone if mild; safety-netting
  7. VTE risk high; LMWH for hospitalised; consider 10-14 days postnatal
  8. Mode per obstetric indication; vaginal acceptable if stable; preterm delivery only if deteriorating
  9. Vertical transmission 1-2%; routine swabbing; breastfeeding encouraged with hygiene
  10. Vaccination; mental health; post-COVID/long-COVID clinics

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.11.2 Group B Streptococcus Gbs In Pregnancy

Definition

Group B Streptococcus (GBS, Streptococcus agalactiae) is a leading cause of early-onset neonatal sepsis (EONS), pneumonia, and meningitis. Vertical transmission occurs during labour from colonised mothers. Universal screening at 35-37 weeks with intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP) for at-risk women reduces early-onset disease by 80%.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Maternal carriage 20-30% (rectovaginal); vertical transmission 50% without prophylaxis; EONS 1 per 1000 in colonised

Risk factors for EONS

Previous infant with invasive GBS; GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy; positive GBS screen at 35-37 weeks; intrapartum fever; prolonged ROM ≥18 h; preterm labour <37 weeks

Screening

Vaginal-rectal swab at 35-37 weeks; culture in selective broth; rapid intrapartum testing if needed (less sensitive)

UK approach

Risk-based: IAP if previous infant GBS disease, GBS bacteriuria, or preterm labour/prolonged ROM

US approach

Universal screening at 36-37 weeks; IAP for all positive

Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP)

Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.5 g 4-hourly until delivery; alternative ampicillin, cefazolin, or clindamycin (if allergic)

Timing

At least 4 h of antibiotics before delivery; reduces EONS by 80%

Preterm labour

IAP if unknown GBS status or positive; intrapartum antibiotics for all

Complications

Neonatal sepsis (early-onset <7 days, late-onset 7-90 days); pneumonia; meningitis; 5-10% mortality

Postpartum

Maternal sepsis (rare); secondary prevention for future pregnancy; no vaccine available

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyMaternal carriage 20-30% (rectovaginal); vertical transmission 50% without prophylaxis; EONS 1 per 1000 in colonised
Risk factors for EONSPrevious infant with invasive GBS; GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy; positive GBS screen at 35-37 weeks; intrapartum fever; prolonged ROM ≥18 h; preterm labour <37 weeks
ScreeningVaginal-rectal swab at 35-37 weeks; culture in selective broth; rapid intrapartum testing if needed (less sensitive)
UK approachRisk-based: IAP if previous infant GBS disease, GBS bacteriuria, or preterm labour/prolonged ROM
US approachUniversal screening at 36-37 weeks; IAP for all positive
Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP)Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.5 g 4-hourly until delivery; alternative ampicillin, cefazolin, or clindamycin (if allergic)
TimingAt least 4 h of antibiotics before delivery; reduces EONS by 80%
Preterm labourIAP if unknown GBS status or positive; intrapartum antibiotics for all
ComplicationsNeonatal sepsis (early-onset <7 days, late-onset 7-90 days); pneumonia; meningitis; 5-10% mortality
PostpartumMaternal sepsis (rare); secondary prevention for future pregnancy; no vaccine available

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Group B Streptococcus (GBS) in pregnancy?

Group B Streptococcus (GBS, Streptococcus agalactiae) is a leading cause of early-onset neonatal sepsis (EONS), pneumonia, and meningitis. Vertical transmission occurs during labour from colonised mothers. Universal screening at 35-37 weeks with intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP) for at-risk women reduces early-onset disease by 80%.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Group B Streptococcus (GBS) in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Risk factors for EONS, Screening, UK approach, US approach. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Group B Streptococcus (GBS) in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Group B Streptococcus (GBS) in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Group B Streptococcus (GBS) in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Group B Streptococcus (GBS) in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

A: Previous infant with invasive GBS; GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy; positive GBS screen at 35-37 weeks; intrapartum fever; prolonged ROM ≥18 h; preterm labour <37 weeks

Q: Screening?

A: Vaginal-rectal swab at 35-37 weeks; culture in selective broth; rapid intrapartum testing if needed (less sensitive)

Q: UK approach?

A: Risk-based: IAP if previous infant GBS disease, GBS bacteriuria, or preterm labour/prolonged ROM

Q: US approach?

A: Universal screening at 36-37 weeks; IAP for all positive

Q: Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP)?

A: Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.5 g 4-hourly until delivery; alternative ampicillin, cefazolin, or clindamycin (if allergic)

Q: Timing?

A: At least 4 h of antibiotics before delivery; reduces EONS by 80%

Q: Preterm labour?

A: IAP if unknown GBS status or positive; intrapartum antibiotics for all

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.11.3 Hiv In Pregnancy

Definition

HIV in pregnancy requires combined obstetric, HIV, and paediatric care to prevent vertical transmission. With effective combination antiretroviral therapy (cART), undetectable viral load, and appropriate delivery/feeding, the transmission rate falls below 0.5%.

Core Clinical Content

Vertical transmission rate

Without intervention 15-45%; with cART <0.5% (undetectable VL at delivery)

Antenatal cART

All women regardless of CD4; tenofovir + emtricitabine + dolutegravir (1st trimester acceptable)

Monitoring

CD4; viral load at booking, 28, 36 weeks, 4-6 weeks post-cART change; resistance if detectable VL

Viral load at delivery

If <50 copies/mL — vaginal delivery acceptable; if >400 — planned caesarean at 38 weeks

Intrapartum

Avoid invasive procedures; IV zidovudine if VL >1000; no artificial rupture of membranes; avoid instrumental

Neonatal prophylaxis

Zidovudine for 4 weeks; if maternal VL >1000 — add nevirapine or raltegravir 3-drug regimen for 4-6 weeks

Breastfeeding

Avoid in high-income settings; safe with cART in low-income (WHO)

Maternal health

Continue cART; opportunistic infection prophylaxis if CD4 <200

Preconception

Optimise cART; consider pre-exposure prophylaxis for partner; partner testing

Postpartum

Continue cART; contraception; viral load 4-6 weeks; review resistance if failing

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Vertical transmission rateWithout intervention 15-45%; with cART <0.5% (undetectable VL at delivery)
Antenatal cARTAll women regardless of CD4; tenofovir + emtricitabine + dolutegravir (1st trimester acceptable)
MonitoringCD4; viral load at booking, 28, 36 weeks, 4-6 weeks post-cART change; resistance if detectable VL
Viral load at deliveryIf <50 copies/mL — vaginal delivery acceptable; if >400 — planned caesarean at 38 weeks
IntrapartumAvoid invasive procedures; IV zidovudine if VL >1000; no artificial rupture of membranes; avoid instrumental
Neonatal prophylaxisZidovudine for 4 weeks; if maternal VL >1000 — add nevirapine or raltegravir 3-drug regimen for 4-6 weeks
BreastfeedingAvoid in high-income settings; safe with cART in low-income (WHO)
Maternal healthContinue cART; opportunistic infection prophylaxis if CD4 <200
PreconceptionOptimise cART; consider pre-exposure prophylaxis for partner; partner testing
PostpartumContinue cART; contraception; viral load 4-6 weeks; review resistance if failing

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of HIV in pregnancy?

HIV in pregnancy requires combined obstetric, HIV, and paediatric care to prevent vertical transmission. With effective combination antiretroviral therapy (cART), undetectable viral load, and appropriate delivery/feeding, the transmission rate falls below 0.5%.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of HIV in pregnancy?

Key features include: Vertical transmission rate, Antenatal cART, Monitoring, Viral load at delivery, Intrapartum. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is HIV in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for HIV in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of HIV in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for HIV in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with HIV in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Zidovudine for 4 weeks; if maternal VL >1000 — add nevirapine or raltegravir 3-drug regimen for 4-6 weeks
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with HIV in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Avoid in high-income settings; safe with cART in low-income (WHO)
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with HIV in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Continue cART; opportunistic infection prophylaxis if CD4 <200
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with HIV in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Optimise cART; consider pre-exposure prophylaxis for partner; partner testing
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with HIV in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Continue cART; contraception; viral load 4-6 weeks; review resistance if failing

Flashcards

Q: Vertical transmission rate?

A: Without intervention 15-45%; with cART <0.5% (undetectable VL at delivery)

Q: Antenatal cART?

A: All women regardless of CD4; tenofovir + emtricitabine + dolutegravir (1st trimester acceptable)

Q: Monitoring?

A: CD4; viral load at booking, 28, 36 weeks, 4-6 weeks post-cART change; resistance if detectable VL

Q: Viral load at delivery?

A: If <50 copies/mL — vaginal delivery acceptable; if >400 — planned caesarean at 38 weeks

Q: Intrapartum?

A: Avoid invasive procedures; IV zidovudine if VL >1000; no artificial rupture of membranes; avoid instrumental

Q: Neonatal prophylaxis?

A: Zidovudine for 4 weeks; if maternal VL >1000 — add nevirapine or raltegravir 3-drug regimen for 4-6 weeks

Q: Breastfeeding?

A: Avoid in high-income settings; safe with cART in low-income (WHO)

Q: Maternal health?

A: Continue cART; opportunistic infection prophylaxis if CD4 <200

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of HIV in pregnancy?
  2. A — Key feature of HIV in pregnancy?
  3. A — Key feature of HIV in pregnancy?
  4. A — Key feature of HIV in pregnancy?
  5. A — Key feature of HIV in pregnancy?
  6. A — Key feature of HIV in pregnancy?
  7. A — Key feature of HIV in pregnancy?
  8. A — Key feature of HIV in pregnancy?
  9. A — Key feature of HIV in pregnancy?
  10. A — Key feature of HIV in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. Without intervention 15-45%; with cART <0.5% (undetectable VL at delivery)
  2. All women regardless of CD4; tenofovir + emtricitabine + dolutegravir (1st trimester acceptable)
  3. CD4; viral load at booking, 28, 36 weeks, 4-6 weeks post-cART change; resistance if detectable VL
  4. If <50 copies/mL — vaginal delivery acceptable; if >400 — planned caesarean at 38 weeks
  5. Avoid invasive procedures; IV zidovudine if VL >1000; no artificial rupture of membranes; avoid instrumental
  6. Zidovudine for 4 weeks; if maternal VL >1000 — add nevirapine or raltegravir 3-drug regimen for 4-6 weeks
  7. Avoid in high-income settings; safe with cART in low-income (WHO)
  8. Continue cART; opportunistic infection prophylaxis if CD4 <200
  9. Optimise cART; consider pre-exposure prophylaxis for partner; partner testing
  10. Continue cART; contraception; viral load 4-6 weeks; review resistance if failing

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.11.4 Hepatitis B In Pregnancy

Definition

Hepatitis B in pregnancy can cause vertical transmission (90% in HBeAg-positive mothers without intervention), leading to chronic infection in 90% of infected infants. Antenatal screening, maternal viral load monitoring, and neonatal immunoprophylaxis are highly effective.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

0.5-2% in UK; up to 10-20% in endemic regions (Asia, Africa)

Vertical transmission risk

90% HBeAg+; 10-20% HBeAg- without prophylaxis

Screening

Universal at booking; HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc; if HBsAg+ — HBeAg, anti-HBe, HBV DNA

Maternal management

Tenofovir from 28 weeks if HBV DNA >200,000 IU/mL or HBeAg+; monitor LFT

Mode of delivery

Vaginal acceptable with prophylaxis; caesarean does not prevent transmission if immunoprophylaxis given

Neonatal prophylaxis

Hepatitis B vaccine + hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) within 12 hours of birth — 90% effective

Follow-up

Anti-HBs at 12 months; HBsAg and anti-HBc at 12-18 months to confirm status

Breastfeeding

Safe with prophylaxis; not contraindicated

Acute HBV in pregnancy

Severe hepatitis risk; tenofovir; consider for fulminant disease

Hepatitis C

No vertical prevention; avoid invasive procedures; treat with DAAs postpartum

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence0.5-2% in UK; up to 10-20% in endemic regions (Asia, Africa)
Vertical transmission risk90% HBeAg+; 10-20% HBeAg- without prophylaxis
ScreeningUniversal at booking; HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc; if HBsAg+ — HBeAg, anti-HBe, HBV DNA
Maternal managementTenofovir from 28 weeks if HBV DNA >200,000 IU/mL or HBeAg+; monitor LFT
Mode of deliveryVaginal acceptable with prophylaxis; caesarean does not prevent transmission if immunoprophylaxis given
Neonatal prophylaxisHepatitis B vaccine + hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) within 12 hours of birth — 90% effective
Follow-upAnti-HBs at 12 months; HBsAg and anti-HBc at 12-18 months to confirm status
BreastfeedingSafe with prophylaxis; not contraindicated
Acute HBV in pregnancySevere hepatitis risk; tenofovir; consider for fulminant disease
Hepatitis CNo vertical prevention; avoid invasive procedures; treat with DAAs postpartum

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Hepatitis B in pregnancy?

Hepatitis B in pregnancy can cause vertical transmission (90% in HBeAg-positive mothers without intervention), leading to chronic infection in 90% of infected infants. Antenatal screening, maternal viral load monitoring, and neonatal immunoprophylaxis are highly effective.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Hepatitis B in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Vertical transmission risk, Screening, Maternal management, Mode of delivery. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Hepatitis B in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Hepatitis B in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Hepatitis B in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Hepatitis B in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.11.5 Influenza In Pregnancy

Definition

Influenza in pregnancy causes disproportionately severe disease compared to non-pregnant women, with increased risk of hospitalisation, ICU admission, and death, especially in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters. Inactivated influenza vaccination in any trimester is safe and effective. Antiviral treatment (oseltamivir) is recommended within 48 hours of symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Pregnancy increases severity (3rd trimester peak); influenza in pregnancy causes excess mortality historically (1918, 2009 H1N1)

Risk factors

Asthma; obesity; diabetes; immunocompromised; cardiac disease; advanced gestation

Complications maternal

Pneumonia; ARDS; hospitalisation; ICU; death

Complications fetal

Preterm birth; stillbirth; IUGR; congenital infection (rare); neonatal influenza

Presentation

Fever, myalgia, headache, cough, sore throat, fatigue; abrupt onset

Diagnosis

Clinical; rapid antigen testing; PCR (respiratory); CXR if pneumonia suspected

Treatment

Oseltamivir 75 mg BD 5 days within 48 h of symptoms (any trimester); benefits likely if later; supportive care

Chemoprophylaxis

Oseltamivir for close contacts in high-risk; post-exposure; vaccination reduces risk

Vaccination

Inactivated influenza vaccine (any trimester; safe); recommended in pregnancy; protects mother and neonate

Prevention

Annual vaccination; hand hygiene; avoid sick contacts; mask in outbreaks

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyPregnancy increases severity (3rd trimester peak); influenza in pregnancy causes excess mortality historically (1918, 2009 H1N1)
Risk factorsAsthma; obesity; diabetes; immunocompromised; cardiac disease; advanced gestation
Complications maternalPneumonia; ARDS; hospitalisation; ICU; death
Complications fetalPreterm birth; stillbirth; IUGR; congenital infection (rare); neonatal influenza
PresentationFever, myalgia, headache, cough, sore throat, fatigue; abrupt onset
DiagnosisClinical; rapid antigen testing; PCR (respiratory); CXR if pneumonia suspected
TreatmentOseltamivir 75 mg BD 5 days within 48 h of symptoms (any trimester); benefits likely if later; supportive care
ChemoprophylaxisOseltamivir for close contacts in high-risk; post-exposure; vaccination reduces risk
VaccinationInactivated influenza vaccine (any trimester; safe); recommended in pregnancy; protects mother and neonate
PreventionAnnual vaccination; hand hygiene; avoid sick contacts; mask in outbreaks

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Influenza in pregnancy?

Influenza in pregnancy causes disproportionately severe disease compared to non-pregnant women, with increased risk of hospitalisation, ICU admission, and death, especially in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters. Inactivated influenza vaccination in any trimester is safe and effective. Antiviral treatment (oseltamivir) is recommended within 48 hours of symptoms.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Influenza in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Risk factors, Complications maternal, Complications fetal, Presentation. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Influenza in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Influenza in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Influenza in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Influenza in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Q: Epidemiology?

A: Pregnancy increases severity (3rd trimester peak); influenza in pregnancy causes excess mortality historically (1918, 2009 H1N1)

Q: Risk factors?

A: Asthma; obesity; diabetes; immunocompromised; cardiac disease; advanced gestation

Q: Complications maternal?

A: Pneumonia; ARDS; hospitalisation; ICU; death

Q: Complications fetal?

A: Preterm birth; stillbirth; IUGR; congenital infection (rare); neonatal influenza

Q: Presentation?

A: Fever, myalgia, headache, cough, sore throat, fatigue; abrupt onset

Q: Diagnosis?

A: Clinical; rapid antigen testing; PCR (respiratory); CXR if pneumonia suspected

Q: Treatment?

A: Oseltamivir 75 mg BD 5 days within 48 h of symptoms (any trimester); benefits likely if later; supportive care

Q: Chemoprophylaxis?

A: Oseltamivir for close contacts in high-risk; post-exposure; vaccination reduces risk

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Influenza in pregnancy?
  2. A — Key feature of Influenza in pregnancy?
  3. A — Key feature of Influenza in pregnancy?
  4. A — Key feature of Influenza in pregnancy?
  5. A — Key feature of Influenza in pregnancy?
  6. A — Key feature of Influenza in pregnancy?
  7. A — Key feature of Influenza in pregnancy?
  8. A — Key feature of Influenza in pregnancy?
  9. A — Key feature of Influenza in pregnancy?
  10. A — Key feature of Influenza in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. Pregnancy increases severity (3rd trimester peak); influenza in pregnancy causes excess mortality historically (1918, 2009 H1N1)
  2. Asthma; obesity; diabetes; immunocompromised; cardiac disease; advanced gestation
  3. Pneumonia; ARDS; hospitalisation; ICU; death
  4. Preterm birth; stillbirth; IUGR; congenital infection (rare); neonatal influenza
  5. Fever, myalgia, headache, cough, sore throat, fatigue; abrupt onset
  6. Clinical; rapid antigen testing; PCR (respiratory); CXR if pneumonia suspected
  7. Oseltamivir 75 mg BD 5 days within 48 h of symptoms (any trimester); benefits likely if later; supportive care
  8. Oseltamivir for close contacts in high-risk; post-exposure; vaccination reduces risk
  9. Inactivated influenza vaccine (any trimester; safe); recommended in pregnancy; protects mother and neonate
  10. Annual vaccination; hand hygiene; avoid sick contacts; mask in outbreaks

▸ 30.11.6 Malaria In Pregnancy

Definition

Malaria in pregnancy is a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity in endemic regions (sub-Saharan Africa, Asia). Placental sequestration of Plasmodium falciparum causes severe anaemia, low birth weight, and increased stillbirth and neonatal mortality. Prevention with intermittent preventive treatment (IPT) and insecticide-treated nets is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Endemic in 90+ countries; 30 million pregnancies at risk annually; 10,000 maternal deaths/year

Pathophysiology

P. falciparum expresses VAR2CSA on RBCs; binds CSA on placenta; sequestration → placental malaria

Complications maternal

Severe anaemia; hypoglycaemia; ARDS; cerebral malaria; AKI; death; pre-existing immunity reduced in pregnancy

Complications fetal

Low birth weight (most common); IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; neonatal malaria; congenital malaria

Diagnosis

Thick and thin blood films; rapid diagnostic test (RDT); PCR for species

Treatment uncomplicated (P. falciparum)

Quinine + clindamycin (1st trimester); ACT (artemether-lumefantrine) 2nd/3rd trimesters

Treatment severe

IV artesunate (all trimesters); exchange transfusion; ITU; supportive

P. vivax/ovale

Chloroquine; avoid primaquine in pregnancy (G6PD deficiency and foetal haemolysis); radical cure postpartum

Prevention

IPTp with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (2-3 doses after 1st trimester; not in HIV on cotrimoxazole); insecticide-treated nets; mefloquine prophylaxis (if resistant)

Postpartum

Treat vivax/ovale; contraception; preconception counselling; HIV/screening

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyEndemic in 90+ countries; 30 million pregnancies at risk annually; 10,000 maternal deaths/year
PathophysiologyP. falciparum expresses VAR2CSA on RBCs; binds CSA on placenta; sequestration → placental malaria
Complications maternalSevere anaemia; hypoglycaemia; ARDS; cerebral malaria; AKI; death; pre-existing immunity reduced in pregnancy
Complications fetalLow birth weight (most common); IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; neonatal malaria; congenital malaria
DiagnosisThick and thin blood films; rapid diagnostic test (RDT); PCR for species
Treatment uncomplicated (P. falciparum)Quinine + clindamycin (1st trimester); ACT (artemether-lumefantrine) 2nd/3rd trimesters
Treatment severeIV artesunate (all trimesters); exchange transfusion; ITU; supportive
P. vivax/ovaleChloroquine; avoid primaquine in pregnancy (G6PD deficiency and foetal haemolysis); radical cure postpartum
PreventionIPTp with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (2-3 doses after 1st trimester; not in HIV on cotrimoxazole); insecticide-treated nets; mefloquine prophylaxis (if resistant)
PostpartumTreat vivax/ovale; contraception; preconception counselling; HIV/screening

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Malaria in pregnancy?

Malaria in pregnancy is a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity in endemic regions (sub-Saharan Africa, Asia). Placental sequestration of Plasmodium falciparum causes severe anaemia, low birth weight, and increased stillbirth and neonatal mortality. Prevention with intermittent preventive treatment (IPT) and insecticide-treated nets is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Malaria in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, Complications maternal, Complications fetal, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Malaria in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Malaria in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Malaria in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Malaria in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)
