▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.11.7 Torch Infections In Pregnancy

Definition

TORCH infections (Toxoplasma, Other, Rubella, CMV, Herpes/HIV) and others (syphilis, parvovirus B19, varicella, listeria) can cause congenital infection with fetal loss, malformation, or long-term sequelae. Antenatal screening and prevention are key.

Core Clinical Content

Toxoplasmosis

Cat faeces, undercooked meat; hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications, chorioretinitis; spiramycin ± pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine after 1st trimester

Rubella

Vaccine-preventable; CRS — cataracts, sensorineural deafness, PDA; offer post-partum vaccination

CMV

Most common congenital infection; sensorineural hearing loss, microcephaly, periventricular calcifications; no proven treatment

Herpes simplex

Primary genital herpes near delivery → caesarean; recurrent — discussion; aciclovir safe

Parvovirus B19

Hydrops fetalis; aplastic crisis in mother; serology + MCA Doppler

Varicella

Pneumonitis in adult; fetal varicella syndrome (<20 weeks); shingles immunoglobulin to neonate if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after delivery

Syphilis

Routine screen; penicillin treatment; desensitize if allergic; Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction

Listeria

Unpasteurised cheese, deli meats; granulomatosis infantiseptica; ampicillin + gentamicin

Zika

Microcephaly; travel avoidance; semen transmission 6 months; no sexual partners from endemic areas

Screening

Rubella, syphilis, HIV, hepatitis B — all antenatal; varicella, CMV, toxoplasma if symptomatic

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ToxoplasmosisCat faeces, undercooked meat; hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications, chorioretinitis; spiramycin ± pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine after 1st trimester
RubellaVaccine-preventable; CRS — cataracts, sensorineural deafness, PDA; offer post-partum vaccination
CMVMost common congenital infection; sensorineural hearing loss, microcephaly, periventricular calcifications; no proven treatment
Herpes simplexPrimary genital herpes near delivery → caesarean; recurrent — discussion; aciclovir safe
Parvovirus B19Hydrops fetalis; aplastic crisis in mother; serology + MCA Doppler
VaricellaPneumonitis in adult; fetal varicella syndrome (<20 weeks); shingles immunoglobulin to neonate if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after delivery
SyphilisRoutine screen; penicillin treatment; desensitize if allergic; Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction
ListeriaUnpasteurised cheese, deli meats; granulomatosis infantiseptica; ampicillin + gentamicin
ZikaMicrocephaly; travel avoidance; semen transmission 6 months; no sexual partners from endemic areas
ScreeningRubella, syphilis, HIV, hepatitis B — all antenatal; varicella, CMV, toxoplasma if symptomatic

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of TORCH infections in pregnancy?

TORCH infections (Toxoplasma, Other, Rubella, CMV, Herpes/HIV) and others (syphilis, parvovirus B19, varicella, listeria) can cause congenital infection with fetal loss, malformation, or long-term sequelae. Antenatal screening and prevention are key.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of TORCH infections in pregnancy?

Key features include: Toxoplasmosis, Rubella, CMV, Herpes simplex, Parvovirus B19. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is TORCH infections in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for TORCH infections in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of TORCH infections in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for TORCH infections in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

B) Cat faeces, undercooked meat; hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications, chorioretinitis; spiramycin ± pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine after 1st trimester

C) Vaccine-preventable; CRS — cataracts, sensorineural deafness, PDA; offer post-partum vaccination

D) Most common congenital infection; sensorineural hearing loss, microcephaly, periventricular calcifications; no proven treatment

Answer: A

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with TORCH infections in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Cat faeces, undercooked meat; hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications, chorioretinitis; spiramycin ± pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine after 1st trimester
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with TORCH infections in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Vaccine-preventable; CRS — cataracts, sensorineural deafness, PDA; offer post-partum vaccination
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with TORCH infections in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Most common congenital infection; sensorineural hearing loss, microcephaly, periventricular calcifications; no proven treatment
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with TORCH infections in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Primary genital herpes near delivery → caesarean; recurrent — discussion; aciclovir safe
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with TORCH infections in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Hydrops fetalis; aplastic crisis in mother; serology + MCA Doppler
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with TORCH infections in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Pneumonitis in adult; fetal varicella syndrome (<20 weeks); shingles immunoglobulin to neonate if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after delivery
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with TORCH infections in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Routine screen; penicillin treatment; desensitize if allergic; Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with TORCH infections in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Unpasteurised cheese, deli meats; granulomatosis infantiseptica; ampicillin + gentamicin
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with TORCH infections in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Microcephaly; travel avoidance; semen transmission 6 months; no sexual partners from endemic areas
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with TORCH infections in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Rubella, syphilis, HIV, hepatitis B — all antenatal; varicella, CMV, toxoplasma if symptomatic

Flashcards

Q: Toxoplasmosis?

A: Cat faeces, undercooked meat; hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications, chorioretinitis; spiramycin ± pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine after 1st trimester

Q: Rubella?

A: Vaccine-preventable; CRS — cataracts, sensorineural deafness, PDA; offer post-partum vaccination

Q: CMV?

A: Most common congenital infection; sensorineural hearing loss, microcephaly, periventricular calcifications; no proven treatment

Q: Herpes simplex?

A: Primary genital herpes near delivery → caesarean; recurrent — discussion; aciclovir safe

Q: Parvovirus B19?

A: Hydrops fetalis; aplastic crisis in mother; serology + MCA Doppler

Q: Varicella?

A: Pneumonitis in adult; fetal varicella syndrome (<20 weeks); shingles immunoglobulin to neonate if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after delivery

Q: Syphilis?

A: Routine screen; penicillin treatment; desensitize if allergic; Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction

Q: Listeria?

A: Unpasteurised cheese, deli meats; granulomatosis infantiseptica; ampicillin + gentamicin

Answer Key with Explanations

  7. Routine screen; penicillin treatment; desensitize if allergic; Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.11.8 Varicella Zoster In Pregnancy

Definition

Varicella zoster virus (VZV) in pregnancy causes significant maternal and fetal morbidity. Maternal infection in the first 20 weeks can cause fetal varicella syndrome (limb hypoplasia, microcephaly, skin scarring, ocular abnormalities). Perinatal infection (5 days before to 2 days after delivery) risks severe neonatal varicella. VZ immunoglobulin (VZIG) and aciclovir are key interventions.

Core Clinical Content

Maternal varicella

Pneumonia (10-20%) — leading cause of death; hospitalisation; ARDS; treat with aciclovir

Fetal varicella syndrome (FVS)

<2% if <20 weeks; cutaneous scarring; limb hypoplasia; microcephaly; ocular (cataract, chorioretinitis); IUGR

Neonatal varicella

Severe if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after delivery (no time for IgG transfer); mortality 30% without treatment

VZIG

For susceptible (VZV IgG negative) exposed pregnant women within 96 h; reduces severity; does not prevent infection

Aciclovir

800 mg 5x daily 7 days if varicella in pregnancy (within 24 h of rash); IV if severe or pneumonitis

Diagnosis

Clinical; PCR vesicular fluid; serology (VZV IgG/IgM); CXR if pneumonitis

Exposure in pregnancy

Check VZV IgG status urgently (most adults immune); if non-immune and significant exposure, VZIG

Vaccination

Live vaccine (Varilrix, Varivax) — contraindicated in pregnancy; advise avoidance; 2 doses 4-8 weeks apart; avoid pregnancy 1 month after

Zoster (shingles)

Less risk to fetus (cell-mediated); aciclovir if severe

Postpartum

VZIG to neonate if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after; isolate mother and baby; aciclovir for baby

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Maternal varicellaPneumonia (10-20%) — leading cause of death; hospitalisation; ARDS; treat with aciclovir
Fetal varicella syndrome (FVS)<2% if <20 weeks; cutaneous scarring; limb hypoplasia; microcephaly; ocular (cataract, chorioretinitis); IUGR
Neonatal varicellaSevere if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after delivery (no time for IgG transfer); mortality 30% without treatment
VZIGFor susceptible (VZV IgG negative) exposed pregnant women within 96 h; reduces severity; does not prevent infection
Aciclovir800 mg 5x daily 7 days if varicella in pregnancy (within 24 h of rash); IV if severe or pneumonitis
DiagnosisClinical; PCR vesicular fluid; serology (VZV IgG/IgM); CXR if pneumonitis
Exposure in pregnancyCheck VZV IgG status urgently (most adults immune); if non-immune and significant exposure, VZIG
VaccinationLive vaccine (Varilrix, Varivax) — contraindicated in pregnancy; advise avoidance; 2 doses 4-8 weeks apart; avoid pregnancy 1 month after
Zoster (shingles)Less risk to fetus (cell-mediated); aciclovir if severe
PostpartumVZIG to neonate if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after; isolate mother and baby; aciclovir for baby

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Varicella zoster in pregnancy?

Varicella zoster virus (VZV) in pregnancy causes significant maternal and fetal morbidity. Maternal infection in the first 20 weeks can cause fetal varicella syndrome (limb hypoplasia, microcephaly, skin scarring, ocular abnormalities). Perinatal infection (5 days before to 2 days after delivery) risks severe neonatal varicella. VZ immunoglobulin (VZIG) and aciclovir are key interventions.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Varicella zoster in pregnancy?

Key features include: Maternal varicella, Fetal varicella syndrome (FVS), Neonatal varicella, VZIG, Aciclovir. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Varicella zoster in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Varicella zoster in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Varicella zoster in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Varicella zoster in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.12 Labour, Delivery and Postpartum 9
▸ 30.12.1 Amniotic Fluid Embolism Afe

Definition

Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency in which amniotic fluid, fetal cells, or debris enter the maternal circulation causing anaphylactoid reaction, cardiovascular collapse, respiratory distress, DIC, and often death. Despite resuscitation, mortality is 20-60%.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Anaphylactoid reaction to amniotic fluid/debris in maternal circulation

Epidemiology

1-2 in 40,000 deliveries; mortality 20-60%; leading cause of maternal death in some series

Risk factors

Older age; multiparity; caesarean; induction; multiple pregnancy; placenta praevia; abruption; uterine rupture

Classic triad

Sudden hypoxia and respiratory distress; shock/hypotension; DIC/bleeding — often with seizures

Pathophysiology

Amniotic fluid → pulmonary vasculature → anaphylactoid reaction, ARDS, cardiac failure, DIC

Differential

PE; eclampsia; aortic dissection; anaphylaxis; sepsis; haemorrhage; myocardial infarction

Management

ABC; high-quality CPR; oxygen 100%; immediate delivery; ITU; massive transfusion; supportive

Monitoring

Arterial line; CVP; urinary catheter; ABG; coagulation; echo

Complications

ARDS; AKI; DIC; HIE; death; neurological sequelae in survivors

Survivors

May need hysterectomy; psychological support; bereavement care

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionAnaphylactoid reaction to amniotic fluid/debris in maternal circulation
Epidemiology1-2 in 40,000 deliveries; mortality 20-60%; leading cause of maternal death in some series
Risk factorsOlder age; multiparity; caesarean; induction; multiple pregnancy; placenta praevia; abruption; uterine rupture
Classic triadSudden hypoxia and respiratory distress; shock/hypotension; DIC/bleeding — often with seizures
PathophysiologyAmniotic fluid → pulmonary vasculature → anaphylactoid reaction, ARDS, cardiac failure, DIC
DifferentialPE; eclampsia; aortic dissection; anaphylaxis; sepsis; haemorrhage; myocardial infarction
ManagementABC; high-quality CPR; oxygen 100%; immediate delivery; ITU; massive transfusion; supportive
MonitoringArterial line; CVP; urinary catheter; ABG; coagulation; echo
ComplicationsARDS; AKI; DIC; HIE; death; neurological sequelae in survivors
SurvivorsMay need hysterectomy; psychological support; bereavement care

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency in which amniotic fluid, fetal cells, or debris enter the maternal circulation causing anaphylactoid reaction, cardiovascular collapse, respiratory distress, DIC, and often death. Despite resuscitation, mortality is 20-60%.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

Key features include: Definition, Epidemiology, Risk factors, Classic triad, Pathophysiology. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. Which of the following is true about Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

A) Older age; multiparity; caesarean; induction; multiple pregnancy; placenta praevia; abruption; uterine rupture

B) Sudden hypoxia and respiratory distress; shock/hypotension; DIC/bleeding — often with seizures

C) Amniotic fluid → pulmonary vasculature → anaphylactoid reaction, ARDS, cardiac failure, DIC

D) PE; eclampsia; aortic dissection; anaphylaxis; sepsis; haemorrhage; myocardial infarction

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

A) Sudden hypoxia and respiratory distress; shock/hypotension; DIC/bleeding — often with seizures

B) Amniotic fluid → pulmonary vasculature → anaphylactoid reaction, ARDS, cardiac failure, DIC

C) PE; eclampsia; aortic dissection; anaphylaxis; sepsis; haemorrhage; myocardial infarction

D) ABC; high-quality CPR; oxygen 100%; immediate delivery; ITU; massive transfusion; supportive

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

A) Amniotic fluid → pulmonary vasculature → anaphylactoid reaction, ARDS, cardiac failure, DIC

B) PE; eclampsia; aortic dissection; anaphylaxis; sepsis; haemorrhage; myocardial infarction

C) ABC; high-quality CPR; oxygen 100%; immediate delivery; ITU; massive transfusion; supportive

D) Arterial line; CVP; urinary catheter; ABG; coagulation; echo

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

A) PE; eclampsia; aortic dissection; anaphylaxis; sepsis; haemorrhage; myocardial infarction

B) ABC; high-quality CPR; oxygen 100%; immediate delivery; ITU; massive transfusion; supportive

C) Arterial line; CVP; urinary catheter; ABG; coagulation; echo

D) ARDS; AKI; DIC; HIE; death; neurological sequelae in survivors

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

A) ABC; high-quality CPR; oxygen 100%; immediate delivery; ITU; massive transfusion; supportive

B) Arterial line; CVP; urinary catheter; ABG; coagulation; echo

C) ARDS; AKI; DIC; HIE; death; neurological sequelae in survivors

D) May need hysterectomy; psychological support; bereavement care

B) ARDS; AKI; DIC; HIE; death; neurological sequelae in survivors

C) May need hysterectomy; psychological support; bereavement care

Answer: A

A) ARDS; AKI; DIC; HIE; death; neurological sequelae in survivors

B) May need hysterectomy; psychological support; bereavement care

C) Anaphylactoid reaction to amniotic fluid/debris in maternal circulation

Flashcards

Q: Definition?

A: Anaphylactoid reaction to amniotic fluid/debris in maternal circulation

Q: Epidemiology?

A: 1-2 in 40,000 deliveries; mortality 20-60%; leading cause of maternal death in some series

Q: Risk factors?

A: Older age; multiparity; caesarean; induction; multiple pregnancy; placenta praevia; abruption; uterine rupture

Q: Classic triad?

A: Sudden hypoxia and respiratory distress; shock/hypotension; DIC/bleeding — often with seizures

Q: Pathophysiology?

A: Amniotic fluid → pulmonary vasculature → anaphylactoid reaction, ARDS, cardiac failure, DIC

Q: Differential?

A: PE; eclampsia; aortic dissection; anaphylaxis; sepsis; haemorrhage; myocardial infarction

Q: Management?

A: ABC; high-quality CPR; oxygen 100%; immediate delivery; ITU; massive transfusion; supportive

Q: Monitoring?

A: Arterial line; CVP; urinary catheter; ABG; coagulation; echo

▸ 30.12.2 Caesarean Section

Definition

Caesarean section is a surgical procedure for delivery through an abdominal and uterine incision. Rates are rising globally; Robson classification allows comparison. Indications include fetal distress, malpresentation, placenta praevia, previous classical section, and maternal request.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

20-30% in developed countries; up to 50%+ in some regions; WHO ideal 10-15%

Classification

Elective (planned) vs emergency (category 1-4); Robson classification (10 groups)

Indications

Fetal distress; malpresentation; previous classical section; placenta praevia; multiple pregnancy; failure to progress; maternal request

Pre-op

Anaesthetic review; consent; crossmatch; thromboprophylaxis; antibiotic prophylaxis; bladder catheter; WHO checklist

Anaesthesia

Spinal (most common for elective); epidural top-up for emergency; GA for emergencies or coagulopathy

Surgical technique

Pfannenstiel or low midline; lower segment transverse incision; peritoneal closure debated

Complications

Haemorrhage; infection (5-10%); wound haematoma; urinary tract injury; VTE; Hysterectomy rare

Post-op

Early mobilisation; VTE prophylaxis; regular analgesia; wound care; remove catheter early

Recovery

2-3 days hospital; 6 weeks postnatal review; driving after 4-6 weeks; contraception discussion

Subsequent pregnancy

Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) — 60-80% success; risk of uterine rupture 0.5%

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence20-30% in developed countries; up to 50%+ in some regions; WHO ideal 10-15%
ClassificationElective (planned) vs emergency (category 1-4); Robson classification (10 groups)
IndicationsFetal distress; malpresentation; previous classical section; placenta praevia; multiple pregnancy; failure to progress; maternal request
Pre-opAnaesthetic review; consent; crossmatch; thromboprophylaxis; antibiotic prophylaxis; bladder catheter; WHO checklist
AnaesthesiaSpinal (most common for elective); epidural top-up for emergency; GA for emergencies or coagulopathy
Surgical techniquePfannenstiel or low midline; lower segment transverse incision; peritoneal closure debated
ComplicationsHaemorrhage; infection (5-10%); wound haematoma; urinary tract injury; VTE; Hysterectomy rare
Post-opEarly mobilisation; VTE prophylaxis; regular analgesia; wound care; remove catheter early
Recovery2-3 days hospital; 6 weeks postnatal review; driving after 4-6 weeks; contraception discussion
Subsequent pregnancyVaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) — 60-80% success; risk of uterine rupture 0.5%

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Caesarean section?

Caesarean section is a surgical procedure for delivery through an abdominal and uterine incision. Rates are rising globally; Robson classification allows comparison. Indications include fetal distress, malpresentation, placenta praevia, previous classical section, and maternal request.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Caesarean section?

Key features include: Incidence, Classification, Indications, Pre-op, Anaesthesia. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Caesarean section diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Caesarean section?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Caesarean section?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Caesarean section — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.12.3 Fetal Monitoring In Labour

Definition

Fetal monitoring in labour detects fetal hypoxia/acidosis to allow timely intervention (operative delivery) and prevent perinatal death and long-term neurological injury. Intermittent auscultation (low risk) and continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) are used. CTG interpretation requires pattern recognition and adherence to guidelines (NICE).

Core Clinical Content

Methods

Intermittent auscultation (low-risk labours); continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) for high-risk; fetal scalp electrode if needed

Indications for continuous CTG

Previous C-section; oxytocin augmentation; epidural; meconium; abnormal admission CTG; maternal medical conditions; multiple pregnancy; IUGR; breech

Fetal heart rate features

Baseline (110-160 bpm); variability (5-25); accelerations; decelerations (early, late, variable, prolonged)

Reassuring

Baseline 110-160; variability >5; accelerations present; no decelerations

Non-reassuring

Reduced variability; tachycardia (>160); bradycardia (<110); variable decelerations; late decelerations; complicated FHR

Pathological

Bradycardia <100; absent variability with decelerations; prolonged deceleration >3 min; sinusoidal pattern

Management suspicious/pathological

Maternal reposition; oxygen; stop oxytocin; IV fluids; FBC; consider FBS (fetal blood sampling — pH <7.2 = deliver)

FBS

Fetal scalp blood pH; pH >7.25 continue; <7.2 deliver; 7.2-7.25 repeat in 30 min

When to deliver

Persistent pathological trace despite resuscitation; prolonged bradycardia; FBS pH <7.2

Limitations

High false positive; inter-observer variation; 50-60% of babies with non-reassuring CTG are not acidotic

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
MethodsIntermittent auscultation (low-risk labours); continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) for high-risk; fetal scalp electrode if needed
Indications for continuous CTGPrevious C-section; oxytocin augmentation; epidural; meconium; abnormal admission CTG; maternal medical conditions; multiple pregnancy; IUGR; breech
Fetal heart rate featuresBaseline (110-160 bpm); variability (5-25); accelerations; decelerations (early, late, variable, prolonged)
ReassuringBaseline 110-160; variability >5; accelerations present; no decelerations
Non-reassuringReduced variability; tachycardia (>160); bradycardia (<110); variable decelerations; late decelerations; complicated FHR
PathologicalBradycardia <100; absent variability with decelerations; prolonged deceleration >3 min; sinusoidal pattern
Management suspicious/pathologicalMaternal reposition; oxygen; stop oxytocin; IV fluids; FBC; consider FBS (fetal blood sampling — pH <7.2 = deliver)
FBSFetal scalp blood pH; pH >7.25 continue; <7.2 deliver; 7.2-7.25 repeat in 30 min
When to deliverPersistent pathological trace despite resuscitation; prolonged bradycardia; FBS pH <7.2
LimitationsHigh false positive; inter-observer variation; 50-60% of babies with non-reassuring CTG are not acidotic

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Fetal monitoring in labour?

Fetal monitoring in labour detects fetal hypoxia/acidosis to allow timely intervention (operative delivery) and prevent perinatal death and long-term neurological injury. Intermittent auscultation (low risk) and continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) are used. CTG interpretation requires pattern recognition and adherence to guidelines (NICE).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Fetal monitoring in labour?

Key features include: Methods, Indications for continuous CTG, Fetal heart rate features, Reassuring, Non-reassuring. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Fetal monitoring in labour diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Fetal monitoring in labour?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Fetal monitoring in labour?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Fetal monitoring in labour — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal monitoring in labour?

A) Intermittent auscultation (low-risk labours); continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) for high-risk; fetal scalp electrode if needed

B) Previous C-section; oxytocin augmentation; epidural; meconium; abnormal admission CTG; maternal medical conditions; multiple pregnancy; IUGR; breech

C) Baseline (110-160 bpm); variability (5-25); accelerations; decelerations (early, late, variable, prolonged)

D) Baseline 110-160; variability >5; accelerations present; no decelerations

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal monitoring in labour?

A) Previous C-section; oxytocin augmentation; epidural; meconium; abnormal admission CTG; maternal medical conditions; multiple pregnancy; IUGR; breech

B) Baseline (110-160 bpm); variability (5-25); accelerations; decelerations (early, late, variable, prolonged)

C) Baseline 110-160; variability >5; accelerations present; no decelerations

D) Reduced variability; tachycardia (>160); bradycardia (<110); variable decelerations; late decelerations; complicated FHR

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal monitoring in labour?

A) Baseline (110-160 bpm); variability (5-25); accelerations; decelerations (early, late, variable, prolonged)

B) Baseline 110-160; variability >5; accelerations present; no decelerations

C) Reduced variability; tachycardia (>160); bradycardia (<110); variable decelerations; late decelerations; complicated FHR

D) Bradycardia <100; absent variability with decelerations; prolonged deceleration >3 min; sinusoidal pattern

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal monitoring in labour?

A) Baseline 110-160; variability >5; accelerations present; no decelerations

B) Reduced variability; tachycardia (>160); bradycardia (<110); variable decelerations; late decelerations; complicated FHR

C) Bradycardia <100; absent variability with decelerations; prolonged deceleration >3 min; sinusoidal pattern

D) Maternal reposition; oxygen; stop oxytocin; IV fluids; FBC; consider FBS (fetal blood sampling — pH <7.2 = deliver)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal monitoring in labour?

A) Reduced variability; tachycardia (>160); bradycardia (<110); variable decelerations; late decelerations; complicated FHR

B) Bradycardia <100; absent variability with decelerations; prolonged deceleration >3 min; sinusoidal pattern

C) Maternal reposition; oxygen; stop oxytocin; IV fluids; FBC; consider FBS (fetal blood sampling — pH <7.2 = deliver)

D) Fetal scalp blood pH; pH >7.25 continue; <7.2 deliver; 7.2-7.25 repeat in 30 min

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal monitoring in labour?

A) Bradycardia <100; absent variability with decelerations; prolonged deceleration >3 min; sinusoidal pattern

B) Maternal reposition; oxygen; stop oxytocin; IV fluids; FBC; consider FBS (fetal blood sampling — pH <7.2 = deliver)

C) Fetal scalp blood pH; pH >7.25 continue; <7.2 deliver; 7.2-7.25 repeat in 30 min

D) Persistent pathological trace despite resuscitation; prolonged bradycardia; FBS pH <7.2

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal monitoring in labour?

A) Maternal reposition; oxygen; stop oxytocin; IV fluids; FBC; consider FBS (fetal blood sampling — pH <7.2 = deliver)

B) Fetal scalp blood pH; pH >7.25 continue; <7.2 deliver; 7.2-7.25 repeat in 30 min

C) Persistent pathological trace despite resuscitation; prolonged bradycardia; FBS pH <7.2

D) High false positive; inter-observer variation; 50-60% of babies with non-reassuring CTG are not acidotic

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal monitoring in labour?

A) Fetal scalp blood pH; pH >7.25 continue; <7.2 deliver; 7.2-7.25 repeat in 30 min

B) Persistent pathological trace despite resuscitation; prolonged bradycardia; FBS pH <7.2

C) High false positive; inter-observer variation; 50-60% of babies with non-reassuring CTG are not acidotic

D) Intermittent auscultation (low-risk labours); continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) for high-risk; fetal scalp electrode if needed

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal monitoring in labour?

A) Persistent pathological trace despite resuscitation; prolonged bradycardia; FBS pH <7.2

B) High false positive; inter-observer variation; 50-60% of babies with non-reassuring CTG are not acidotic

C) Intermittent auscultation (low-risk labours); continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) for high-risk; fetal scalp electrode if needed

D) Previous C-section; oxytocin augmentation; epidural; meconium; abnormal admission CTG; maternal medical conditions; multiple pregnancy; IUGR; breech

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal monitoring in labour?

A) High false positive; inter-observer variation; 50-60% of babies with non-reassuring CTG are not acidotic

B) Intermittent auscultation (low-risk labours); continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) for high-risk; fetal scalp electrode if needed

C) Previous C-section; oxytocin augmentation; epidural; meconium; abnormal admission CTG; maternal medical conditions; multiple pregnancy; IUGR; breech

D) Baseline (110-160 bpm); variability (5-25); accelerations; decelerations (early, late, variable, prolonged)

Answer: A

Flashcards

Q: Methods?

A: Intermittent auscultation (low-risk labours); continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) for high-risk; fetal scalp electrode if needed

Q: Indications for continuous CTG?

A: Previous C-section; oxytocin augmentation; epidural; meconium; abnormal admission CTG; maternal medical conditions; multiple pregnancy; IUGR; breech

Q: Fetal heart rate features?

A: Baseline (110-160 bpm); variability (5-25); accelerations; decelerations (early, late, variable, prolonged)

Q: Reassuring?

A: Baseline 110-160; variability >5; accelerations present; no decelerations

Q: Non-reassuring?

A: Reduced variability; tachycardia (>160); bradycardia (<110); variable decelerations; late decelerations; complicated FHR

Q: Pathological?

A: Bradycardia <100; absent variability with decelerations; prolonged deceleration >3 min; sinusoidal pattern

Q: Management suspicious/pathological?

A: Maternal reposition; oxygen; stop oxytocin; IV fluids; FBC; consider FBS (fetal blood sampling — pH <7.2 = deliver)

Q: FBS?

A: Fetal scalp blood pH; pH >7.25 continue; <7.2 deliver; 7.2-7.25 repeat in 30 min

Answer Key with Explanations

  4. Baseline 110-160; variability >5; accelerations present; no decelerations
  5. Reduced variability; tachycardia (>160); bradycardia (<110); variable decelerations; late decelerations; complicated FHR
  6. Bradycardia <100; absent variability with decelerations; prolonged deceleration >3 min; sinusoidal pattern
  7. Maternal reposition; oxygen; stop oxytocin; IV fluids; FBC; consider FBS (fetal blood sampling — pH <7.2 = deliver)
  8. Fetal scalp blood pH; pH >7.25 continue; <7.2 deliver; 7.2-7.25 repeat in 30 min

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.12.4 Induction Of Labour

Definition

Induction of labour (IOL) is the artificial initiation of labour before spontaneous onset. Indications include post-term pregnancy, pre-eclampsia, maternal medical conditions, fetal growth restriction, and prolonged rupture of membranes. Methods include mechanical (Foley catheter) and pharmacological (prostaglandins, oxytocin), with choice based on cervical favourability and indication.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

20-30% of pregnancies in developed countries; rising

Indications

Post-term (≥41 weeks); PE; maternal medical (diabetes, renal, lung); IUGR; cholestasis; PPROM; multiple pregnancy; Rhesus; previous stillbirth; maternal request (≥39 weeks)

Contraindications

Placenta praevia; vasa praevia; transverse lie; cord prolapse; prior classical C-section or T-incision; active genital herpes; invasive cervical cancer

Bishop score

Cervical favourability (0-13): dilation, effacement, station, position, consistency; <6 favours ripening

Cervical ripening

Prostaglandin E2 (dinoprostone) vaginal gel/pessary; misoprostol (off-label in UK); mechanical (Foley catheter); safe in most settings

Amniotomy

Artificial rupture of membranes (ARM); only if cervix favourable; combined with oxytocin for augmentation

Oxytocin

Syntocinon infusion for induction/augmentation; titrate to contractions (4-5 per 10 min); monitor continuous CTG; risk of hyperstimulation

Monitoring

Continuous CTG; partogram; maternal observations; pain control; review progress every 4 h (delay if <1 cm/2-4 h nullip, 1 cm/4 h multip)

Failed induction

Rest; repeat ripening; consider C-section if failed after adequate trial

Complications

Hyperstimulation (treat with tocolysis, terbutaline); uterine rupture (rare); failed induction; PPH; cord prolapse with ARM; uterine atony

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence20-30% of pregnancies in developed countries; rising
IndicationsPost-term (≥41 weeks); PE; maternal medical (diabetes, renal, lung); IUGR; cholestasis; PPROM; multiple pregnancy; Rhesus; previous stillbirth; maternal request (≥39 weeks)
ContraindicationsPlacenta praevia; vasa praevia; transverse lie; cord prolapse; prior classical C-section or T-incision; active genital herpes; invasive cervical cancer
Bishop scoreCervical favourability (0-13): dilation, effacement, station, position, consistency; <6 favours ripening
Cervical ripeningProstaglandin E2 (dinoprostone) vaginal gel/pessary; misoprostol (off-label in UK); mechanical (Foley catheter); safe in most settings
AmniotomyArtificial rupture of membranes (ARM); only if cervix favourable; combined with oxytocin for augmentation
OxytocinSyntocinon infusion for induction/augmentation; titrate to contractions (4-5 per 10 min); monitor continuous CTG; risk of hyperstimulation
MonitoringContinuous CTG; partogram; maternal observations; pain control; review progress every 4 h (delay if <1 cm/2-4 h nullip, 1 cm/4 h multip)
Failed inductionRest; repeat ripening; consider C-section if failed after adequate trial
ComplicationsHyperstimulation (treat with tocolysis, terbutaline); uterine rupture (rare); failed induction; PPH; cord prolapse with ARM; uterine atony

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Induction of labour?

Induction of labour (IOL) is the artificial initiation of labour before spontaneous onset. Indications include post-term pregnancy, pre-eclampsia, maternal medical conditions, fetal growth restriction, and prolonged rupture of membranes. Methods include mechanical (Foley catheter) and pharmacological (prostaglandins, oxytocin), with choice based on cervical favourability and indication.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Induction of labour?

Key features include: Incidence, Indications, Contraindications, Bishop score, Cervical ripening. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Induction of labour diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Induction of labour?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Induction of labour?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Induction of labour — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.12.5 Mastitis And Breast Abscess

Definition

Mastitis (inflammation of breast tissue, often with infection) affects 1 in 5 breastfeeding women, usually in the first 3 months. Staphylococcus aureus is the most common pathogen. Most cases respond to antibiotics and continued breastfeeding; abscesses require drainage.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

Lactational mastitis 10-20%; breast abscess 0.1-3%

Risk factors

Cracked nipples; poor attachment; engorgement; blocked ducts; infrequent feeding; rapid weaning; immunocompromise

Pathogens

S. aureus (most); coagulase-negative staph; Strep; rarely MRSA; community-acquired

Presentation

Unilateral breast pain, swelling, erythema, warmth; systemic (fever, malaise, myalgia); wedge-shaped area

Diagnosis

Clinical; milk culture if recurrent/severe; USS if abscess suspected; aspiration if fluid

Differential

Engorgement; blocked duct; breast abscess; inflammatory breast cancer; Paget's; mastitis from other organisms

Management

Continue breastfeeding (drain affected side); analgesia (paracetamol; ibuprofen); cold compresses; ensure attachment; frequent feeding

Antibiotics

Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS 10-14 days; erythromycin or clarithromycin if allergic; co-amoxiclav if severe

Breast abscess

Needle aspiration (US-guided) or incision and drainage; antibiotics; continue feeding

Prevention

Correct attachment; frequent feeding; treat cracked nipples; avoid sudden weaning

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
IncidenceLactational mastitis 10-20%; breast abscess 0.1-3%
Risk factorsCracked nipples; poor attachment; engorgement; blocked ducts; infrequent feeding; rapid weaning; immunocompromise
PathogensS. aureus (most); coagulase-negative staph; Strep; rarely MRSA; community-acquired
PresentationUnilateral breast pain, swelling, erythema, warmth; systemic (fever, malaise, myalgia); wedge-shaped area
DiagnosisClinical; milk culture if recurrent/severe; USS if abscess suspected; aspiration if fluid
DifferentialEngorgement; blocked duct; breast abscess; inflammatory breast cancer; Paget's; mastitis from other organisms
ManagementContinue breastfeeding (drain affected side); analgesia (paracetamol; ibuprofen); cold compresses; ensure attachment; frequent feeding
AntibioticsFlucloxacillin 500 mg QDS 10-14 days; erythromycin or clarithromycin if allergic; co-amoxiclav if severe
Breast abscessNeedle aspiration (US-guided) or incision and drainage; antibiotics; continue feeding
PreventionCorrect attachment; frequent feeding; treat cracked nipples; avoid sudden weaning

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Mastitis and breast abscess?

Mastitis (inflammation of breast tissue, often with infection) affects 1 in 5 breastfeeding women, usually in the first 3 months. Staphylococcus aureus is the most common pathogen. Most cases respond to antibiotics and continued breastfeeding; abscesses require drainage.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Mastitis and breast abscess?

Key features include: Incidence, Risk factors, Pathogens, Presentation, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Mastitis and breast abscess diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Mastitis and breast abscess?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Mastitis and breast abscess?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Mastitis and breast abscess — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Q: Incidence?

A: Lactational mastitis 10-20%; breast abscess 0.1-3%

Q: Risk factors?

A: Cracked nipples; poor attachment; engorgement; blocked ducts; infrequent feeding; rapid weaning; immunocompromise

Q: Pathogens?

A: S. aureus (most); coagulase-negative staph; Strep; rarely MRSA; community-acquired

Q: Presentation?

A: Unilateral breast pain, swelling, erythema, warmth; systemic (fever, malaise, myalgia); wedge-shaped area

Q: Diagnosis?

A: Clinical; milk culture if recurrent/severe; USS if abscess suspected; aspiration if fluid

Q: Differential?

A: Engorgement; blocked duct; breast abscess; inflammatory breast cancer; Paget's; mastitis from other organisms

Q: Management?

A: Continue breastfeeding (drain affected side); analgesia (paracetamol; ibuprofen); cold compresses; ensure attachment; frequent feeding

Q: Antibiotics?

A: Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS 10-14 days; erythromycin or clarithromycin if allergic; co-amoxiclav if severe

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.12.6 Operative Vaginal Delivery

Definition

Operative vaginal delivery (OVD) — ventouse (vacuum) or forceps — is used to expedite delivery in the second stage of labour for fetal or maternal indications. Choice of instrument depends on clinical circumstances, operator experience, and station. Successful OVD avoids caesarean section and its associated morbidity.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

10-15% of vaginal deliveries; varies by hospital and population

Indications

Fetal distress (CTG abnormalities); maternal exhaustion; prolonged second stage; medical conditions (cardiac, neurological — avoid Valsalva); after epidural

Prerequisites

Fully dilated; ruptured membranes; engaged head (station +2 or below); cephalic presentation; known fetal position; adequate pelvis; empty bladder; experienced operator; maternal consent

Outlet OVD

Head at perineum; scalp visible; easy delivery; low risk

Low OVD

Leading point at +2 or below; rotation up to 45° acceptable

Mid-cavity OVD

Head engaged but above +2; higher risk; rarely used; senior operator

Ventouse

Vacuum cup; silastic or metal Kiwi; less maternal trauma; higher neonatal cephalhaematoma; less effective for rotation; avoid <34 weeks (cephalic) and face/breech

Forceps

Wrigley's (outlet), Neville-Barnes (low), Kielland (rotation); more maternal trauma but faster delivery; safer for preterm and face presentations

Complications maternal

Perineal trauma (3rd/4th degree); PPH; vaginal/cervical lacerations; urinary retention; fistula (rare); pelvic floor dysfunction

Complications neonatal

Cephalhaematoma; caput; retinal haemorrhage; facial nerve palsy (forceps); shoulder dystocia; subgaleal haemorrhage (ventouse) — emergency

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence10-15% of vaginal deliveries; varies by hospital and population
IndicationsFetal distress (CTG abnormalities); maternal exhaustion; prolonged second stage; medical conditions (cardiac, neurological — avoid Valsalva); after epidural
PrerequisitesFully dilated; ruptured membranes; engaged head (station +2 or below); cephalic presentation; known fetal position; adequate pelvis; empty bladder; experienced operator; maternal consent
Outlet OVDHead at perineum; scalp visible; easy delivery; low risk
Low OVDLeading point at +2 or below; rotation up to 45° acceptable
Mid-cavity OVDHead engaged but above +2; higher risk; rarely used; senior operator
VentouseVacuum cup; silastic or metal Kiwi; less maternal trauma; higher neonatal cephalhaematoma; less effective for rotation; avoid <34 weeks (cephalic) and face/breech
ForcepsWrigley's (outlet), Neville-Barnes (low), Kielland (rotation); more maternal trauma but faster delivery; safer for preterm and face presentations
Complications maternalPerineal trauma (3rd/4th degree); PPH; vaginal/cervical lacerations; urinary retention; fistula (rare); pelvic floor dysfunction
Complications neonatalCephalhaematoma; caput; retinal haemorrhage; facial nerve palsy (forceps); shoulder dystocia; subgaleal haemorrhage (ventouse) — emergency

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Operative vaginal delivery?

Operative vaginal delivery (OVD) — ventouse (vacuum) or forceps — is used to expedite delivery in the second stage of labour for fetal or maternal indications. Choice of instrument depends on clinical circumstances, operator experience, and station. Successful OVD avoids caesarean section and its associated morbidity.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Operative vaginal delivery?

Key features include: Incidence, Indications, Prerequisites, Outlet OVD, Low OVD. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Operative vaginal delivery diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Operative vaginal delivery?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Operative vaginal delivery?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Operative vaginal delivery — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  9. A — Key feature of Operative vaginal delivery?
  10. A — Key feature of Operative vaginal delivery?

SBAs

  1. 10-15% of vaginal deliveries; varies by hospital and population
  2. Fetal distress (CTG abnormalities); maternal exhaustion; prolonged second stage; medical conditions (cardiac, neurological — avoid Valsalva); after epidural
  3. Fully dilated; ruptured membranes; engaged head (station +2 or below); cephalic presentation; known fetal position; adequate pelvis; empty bladder; experienced operator; maternal consent
  4. Head at perineum; scalp visible; easy delivery; low risk
  5. Leading point at +2 or below; rotation up to 45° acceptable
  6. Head engaged but above +2; higher risk; rarely used; senior operator
  7. Vacuum cup; silastic or metal Kiwi; less maternal trauma; higher neonatal cephalhaematoma; less effective for rotation; avoid <34 weeks (cephalic) and face/breech
  8. Wrigley's (outlet), Neville-Barnes (low), Kielland (rotation); more maternal trauma but faster delivery; safer for preterm and face presentations
  9. Perineal trauma (3rd/4th degree); PPH; vaginal/cervical lacerations; urinary retention; fistula (rare); pelvic floor dysfunction
  10. Cephalhaematoma; caput; retinal haemorrhage; facial nerve palsy (forceps); shoulder dystocia; subgaleal haemorrhage (ventouse) — emergency

▸ 30.12.7 Postpartum Haemorrhage Pph

Definition

Postpartum haemorrhage is blood loss ≥500 mL within 24 hours of delivery (or any loss causing haemodynamic compromise). It is the leading cause of maternal mortality worldwide. The 4 Ts (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin) guide cause and management.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Primary PPH: ≥500 mL within 24 h; major PPH ≥1000 mL; secondary PPH: 24 h-12 weeks

Incidence

5-10% of deliveries; major PPH 1-3%

Causes — 4 Ts

Tone (atonic uterus, 70%); Trauma (15%); Tissue (retained placenta, 10%); Thrombin (coagulopathy, 5%)

Risk factors

Previous PPH; multiple pregnancy; macrosomia; prolonged labour; augmentation; placenta praevia; abruption; coagulopathy

Prevention (active management 3rd stage)

Oxytocin 10 IU IM/IV; controlled cord traction; uterine massage

Initial management

ABC; call for help; 2 large-bore IVs; fluids; bloods incl. FBC, coagulation, crossmatch

Medical treatment

Oxytocin infusion; ergometrine (avoid in HTN); carboprost (avoid in asthma); misoprostol; tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 h

Surgical treatment

Bimanual compression; intrauterine balloon (Bakri); B-Lynch suture; arterial ligation; hysterectomy

Resuscitation

Activate massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 ratio PRBC:FFP:platelets); cell salvage; warming

Secondary PPH

Endometritis; retained products; treat with antibiotics ± evacuation

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPrimary PPH: ≥500 mL within 24 h; major PPH ≥1000 mL; secondary PPH: 24 h-12 weeks
Incidence5-10% of deliveries; major PPH 1-3%
Causes — 4 TsTone (atonic uterus, 70%); Trauma (15%); Tissue (retained placenta, 10%); Thrombin (coagulopathy, 5%)
Risk factorsPrevious PPH; multiple pregnancy; macrosomia; prolonged labour; augmentation; placenta praevia; abruption; coagulopathy
Prevention (active management 3rd stage)Oxytocin 10 IU IM/IV; controlled cord traction; uterine massage
Initial managementABC; call for help; 2 large-bore IVs; fluids; bloods incl. FBC, coagulation, crossmatch
Medical treatmentOxytocin infusion; ergometrine (avoid in HTN); carboprost (avoid in asthma); misoprostol; tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 h
Surgical treatmentBimanual compression; intrauterine balloon (Bakri); B-Lynch suture; arterial ligation; hysterectomy
ResuscitationActivate massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 ratio PRBC:FFP:platelets); cell salvage; warming
Secondary PPHEndometritis; retained products; treat with antibiotics ± evacuation

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)?

Postpartum haemorrhage is blood loss ≥500 mL within 24 hours of delivery (or any loss causing haemodynamic compromise). It is the leading cause of maternal mortality worldwide. The 4 Ts (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin) guide cause and management.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Causes — 4 Ts, Risk factors, Prevention (active management 3rd stage). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Q: Definition?

Q: Incidence?

Q: Causes — 4 Ts?

A: Tone (atonic uterus, 70%); Trauma (15%); Tissue (retained placenta, 10%); Thrombin (coagulopathy, 5%)

Q: Risk factors?

A: Previous PPH; multiple pregnancy; macrosomia; prolonged labour; augmentation; placenta praevia; abruption; coagulopathy

Q: Prevention (active management 3rd stage)?

Q: Initial management?

A: ABC; call for help; 2 large-bore IVs; fluids; bloods incl. FBC, coagulation, crossmatch

Q: Medical treatment?

A: Oxytocin infusion; ergometrine (avoid in HTN); carboprost (avoid in asthma); misoprostol; tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 h

Q: Surgical treatment?

▸ 30.12.8 Sepsis In Pregnancy And The Puerperium

Definition

Sepsis is a leading direct cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy-specific sources include chorioamnionitis, endometritis, urinary tract infection, wound infection, and mastitis. Recognition (modified SIRS/qSOFA), early antibiotics, and source control are critical.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Leading direct cause of maternal death in UK MBRRACE; 5-10% of severe maternal morbidity

Sources

Genital tract (chorioamnionitis, endometritis); UTI; wound infection; mastitis; pneumonia; line infection

Screening tools

SIRS modified for pregnancy (WCC >16, HR >110, T >38 or <36, RR >22); qSOFA — altered mental state, RR >22, BP <100

Red flags

Fever, hypothermia, tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypotension, altered mental state, abdominal pain, foul lochia

Microbiology

Group A Strep (GAS); E. coli; S. aureus; anaerobes; Clostridium

Initial management

Sepsis 6 — oxygen; blood cultures; IV antibiotics within 1 h; IV fluids; lactate; urine output

Empirical antibiotics

Piperacillin-tazobactam + gentamicin; consider metronidazole; add clindamycin if GAS suspected

Source control

Evacuation of retained products; surgical intervention; line removal

Escalation

ITU; inotropes; mechanical ventilation; multidisciplinary team

Prevention

Hand hygiene; GBS prophylaxis; aseptic technique; reduce induction duration

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyLeading direct cause of maternal death in UK MBRRACE; 5-10% of severe maternal morbidity
SourcesGenital tract (chorioamnionitis, endometritis); UTI; wound infection; mastitis; pneumonia; line infection
Screening toolsSIRS modified for pregnancy (WCC >16, HR >110, T >38 or <36, RR >22); qSOFA — altered mental state, RR >22, BP <100
Red flagsFever, hypothermia, tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypotension, altered mental state, abdominal pain, foul lochia
MicrobiologyGroup A Strep (GAS); E. coli; S. aureus; anaerobes; Clostridium
Initial managementSepsis 6 — oxygen; blood cultures; IV antibiotics within 1 h; IV fluids; lactate; urine output
Empirical antibioticsPiperacillin-tazobactam + gentamicin; consider metronidazole; add clindamycin if GAS suspected
Source controlEvacuation of retained products; surgical intervention; line removal
EscalationITU; inotropes; mechanical ventilation; multidisciplinary team
PreventionHand hygiene; GBS prophylaxis; aseptic technique; reduce induction duration

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Sepsis is a leading direct cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy-specific sources include chorioamnionitis, endometritis, urinary tract infection, wound infection, and mastitis. Recognition (modified SIRS/qSOFA), early antibiotics, and source control are critical.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Sources, Screening tools, Red flags, Microbiology. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium. Most appropriate next step in management? | Leading direct cause of maternal death in UK MBRRACE; 5-10% of severe maternal morbidity

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium. Most appropriate next step in management? | SIRS modified for pregnancy (WCC >16, HR >110, T >38 or <36, RR >22); qSOFA — altered mental state, RR >22, BP <100
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium. Most appropriate next step in management? | Fever, hypothermia, tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypotension, altered mental state, abdominal pain, foul lochia
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium. Most appropriate next step in management? | Group A Strep (GAS); E. coli; S. aureus; anaerobes; Clostridium
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium. Most appropriate next step in management? | Sepsis 6 — oxygen; blood cultures; IV antibiotics within 1 h; IV fluids; lactate; urine output
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium. Most appropriate next step in management? | Piperacillin-tazobactam + gentamicin; consider metronidazole; add clindamycin if GAS suspected
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium. Most appropriate next step in management? | Evacuation of retained products; surgical intervention; line removal
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium. Most appropriate next step in management? | ITU; inotropes; mechanical ventilation; multidisciplinary team
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium. Most appropriate next step in management? | Hand hygiene; GBS prophylaxis; aseptic technique; reduce induction duration

Flashcards

Q: Epidemiology?

A: Leading direct cause of maternal death in UK MBRRACE; 5-10% of severe maternal morbidity

Q: Sources?

A: Genital tract (chorioamnionitis, endometritis); UTI; wound infection; mastitis; pneumonia; line infection

Q: Screening tools?

A: SIRS modified for pregnancy (WCC >16, HR >110, T >38 or <36, RR >22); qSOFA — altered mental state, RR >22, BP <100

Q: Red flags?

A: Fever, hypothermia, tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypotension, altered mental state, abdominal pain, foul lochia

Q: Microbiology?

A: Group A Strep (GAS); E. coli; S. aureus; anaerobes; Clostridium

Q: Initial management?

A: Sepsis 6 — oxygen; blood cultures; IV antibiotics within 1 h; IV fluids; lactate; urine output

Q: Empirical antibiotics?

A: Piperacillin-tazobactam + gentamicin; consider metronidazole; add clindamycin if GAS suspected

Q: Source control?

A: Evacuation of retained products; surgical intervention; line removal

Answer Key with Explanations

  2. Genital tract (chorioamnionitis, endometritis); UTI; wound infection; mastitis; pneumonia; line infection
  3. SIRS modified for pregnancy (WCC >16, HR >110, T >38 or <36, RR >22); qSOFA — altered mental state, RR >22, BP <100

  5. Group A Strep (GAS); E. coli; S. aureus; anaerobes; Clostridium
  6. Sepsis 6 — oxygen; blood cultures; IV antibiotics within 1 h; IV fluids; lactate; urine output
  7. Piperacillin-tazobactam + gentamicin; consider metronidazole; add clindamycin if GAS suspected

▸ 30.12.9 Vaginal Birth After Caesarean Vbac

Definition

Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) is a successful vaginal delivery in a woman with a previous caesarean section. Trial of labour after caesarean (TOLAC) is appropriate in selected women with one prior low-transverse C-section. Success rates are 60-80% with appropriate selection; uterine rupture risk is ~0.5%.

Core Clinical Content

Eligibility

One prior low-transverse C-section; no other uterine scar; adequate pelvis; no recurrent indication; obstetrician + anaesthesia available

Success rate

60-80% overall; 70-90% if prior vaginal delivery; 60% if no prior vaginal

Uterine rupture risk

0.5% for TOLAC vs 0.03% for elective repeat C-section; 1% with prostaglandin induction

Contraindications

Prior classical/vertical/T incision; prior uterine rupture; prior extensive surgery; placenta praevia/accreta; multiple pregnancy (relative); patient refusal

Benefits of success

Avoid repeat C-section; lower maternal morbidity; lower VTE/infection; faster recovery; future pregnancies less complex

Risks of TOLAC

Uterine rupture (emergency hysterectomy, fetal death, maternal haemorrhage); failed TOLAC → C-section (higher morbidity)

Place of birth

Tertiary unit with on-site obstetrician, anaesthetist, theatre, blood bank; continuous CTG; IV access; 1:1 midwifery care

Monitoring

Continuous CTG; review 2nd stage progress; avoid prolonged 2nd stage; consider early ARM; avoid prostaglandin if possible

Analgesia

Epidural not contraindicated; allows faster intervention if needed

Future pregnancies

Each repeat C-section increases placenta praevia/accreta risk; generally 2-3 VBACs accepted; shared decision-making

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EligibilityOne prior low-transverse C-section; no other uterine scar; adequate pelvis; no recurrent indication; obstetrician + anaesthesia available
Success rate60-80% overall; 70-90% if prior vaginal delivery; 60% if no prior vaginal
Uterine rupture risk0.5% for TOLAC vs 0.03% for elective repeat C-section; 1% with prostaglandin induction
ContraindicationsPrior classical/vertical/T incision; prior uterine rupture; prior extensive surgery; placenta praevia/accreta; multiple pregnancy (relative); patient refusal
Benefits of successAvoid repeat C-section; lower maternal morbidity; lower VTE/infection; faster recovery; future pregnancies less complex
Risks of TOLACUterine rupture (emergency hysterectomy, fetal death, maternal haemorrhage); failed TOLAC → C-section (higher morbidity)
Place of birthTertiary unit with on-site obstetrician, anaesthetist, theatre, blood bank; continuous CTG; IV access; 1:1 midwifery care
MonitoringContinuous CTG; review 2nd stage progress; avoid prolonged 2nd stage; consider early ARM; avoid prostaglandin if possible
AnalgesiaEpidural not contraindicated; allows faster intervention if needed
Future pregnanciesEach repeat C-section increases placenta praevia/accreta risk; generally 2-3 VBACs accepted; shared decision-making

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC)?

Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) is a successful vaginal delivery in a woman with a previous caesarean section. Trial of labour after caesarean (TOLAC) is appropriate in selected women with one prior low-transverse C-section. Success rates are 60-80% with appropriate selection; uterine rupture risk is ~0.5%.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC)?

Key features include: Eligibility, Success rate, Uterine rupture risk, Contraindications, Benefits of success. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.13 Liver and Gastrointestinal Disease in Pregnancy 8
▸ 30.13.1 Acute Fatty Liver Of Pregnancy Aflp

Definition

Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening mitochondrial disorder presenting in the 3rd trimester with nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, jaundice, hepatic failure, encephalopathy, and DIC. Delivery is curative but supportive care is critical.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Microvesicular fatty infiltration of liver; 3rd trimester; liver failure, encephalopathy, DIC

Epidemiology

1 in 7,000-20,000; associated with LCHAD fetal enzyme deficiency

Symptoms

Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, abdominal pain, jaundice, thirst, polyuria, encephalopathy

Investigations

LFT (AST/ALT ↑); bilirubin ↑; coagulopathy (INR ↑); hypoglycaemia; hyperammonaemia; leucocytosis; renal failure; elevated creatinine

Swansea criteria

≥6 of: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, bilirubin >14, hypoglycaemia <4, uric acid >340, leucocytosis >11, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy, coagulopathy

Differential

HELLP, viral hepatitis, drug-induced, TTP, autoimmune hepatitis

Management

Stabilise (glucose, FFP, platelets); ITU; delivery once stabilised; supportive care

Delivery

Vaginal preferred if stable; may need LSCS

Postpartum

Recovery over days to weeks; LFT and coagulopathy; monitor for pancreatitis, AKI

Future pregnancy

Recurrence low; screen fetus for LCHAD; avoid in subsequent

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionMicrovesicular fatty infiltration of liver; 3rd trimester; liver failure, encephalopathy, DIC
Epidemiology1 in 7,000-20,000; associated with LCHAD fetal enzyme deficiency
SymptomsNausea, vomiting, anorexia, abdominal pain, jaundice, thirst, polyuria, encephalopathy
InvestigationsLFT (AST/ALT ↑); bilirubin ↑; coagulopathy (INR ↑); hypoglycaemia; hyperammonaemia; leucocytosis; renal failure; elevated creatinine
Swansea criteria≥6 of: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, bilirubin >14, hypoglycaemia <4, uric acid >340, leucocytosis >11, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy, coagulopathy
DifferentialHELLP, viral hepatitis, drug-induced, TTP, autoimmune hepatitis
ManagementStabilise (glucose, FFP, platelets); ITU; delivery once stabilised; supportive care
DeliveryVaginal preferred if stable; may need LSCS
PostpartumRecovery over days to weeks; LFT and coagulopathy; monitor for pancreatitis, AKI
Future pregnancyRecurrence low; screen fetus for LCHAD; avoid in subsequent

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)?

Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening mitochondrial disorder presenting in the 3rd trimester with nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, jaundice, hepatic failure, encephalopathy, and DIC. Delivery is curative but supportive care is critical.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)?

Key features include: Definition, Epidemiology, Symptoms, Investigations, Swansea criteria. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. Which of the following is true about Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)?

A) 1 in 7,000-20,000; associated with LCHAD fetal enzyme deficiency

B) Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, abdominal pain, jaundice, thirst, polyuria, encephalopathy

C) LFT (AST/ALT ↑); bilirubin ↑; coagulopathy (INR ↑); hypoglycaemia; hyperammonaemia; leucocytosis; renal failure; elevated creatinine

D) ≥6 of: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, bilirubin >14, hypoglycaemia <4, uric acid >340, leucocytosis >11, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy, coagulopathy

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)?

A) Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, abdominal pain, jaundice, thirst, polyuria, encephalopathy

B) LFT (AST/ALT ↑); bilirubin ↑; coagulopathy (INR ↑); hypoglycaemia; hyperammonaemia; leucocytosis; renal failure; elevated creatinine

C) ≥6 of: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, bilirubin >14, hypoglycaemia <4, uric acid >340, leucocytosis >11, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy, coagulopathy

D) HELLP, viral hepatitis, drug-induced, TTP, autoimmune hepatitis

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)?

A) LFT (AST/ALT ↑); bilirubin ↑; coagulopathy (INR ↑); hypoglycaemia; hyperammonaemia; leucocytosis; renal failure; elevated creatinine

B) ≥6 of: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, bilirubin >14, hypoglycaemia <4, uric acid >340, leucocytosis >11, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy, coagulopathy

C) HELLP, viral hepatitis, drug-induced, TTP, autoimmune hepatitis

D) Stabilise (glucose, FFP, platelets); ITU; delivery once stabilised; supportive care

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)?

A) ≥6 of: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, bilirubin >14, hypoglycaemia <4, uric acid >340, leucocytosis >11, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy, coagulopathy

B) HELLP, viral hepatitis, drug-induced, TTP, autoimmune hepatitis

C) Stabilise (glucose, FFP, platelets); ITU; delivery once stabilised; supportive care

D) Vaginal preferred if stable; may need LSCS

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)?

B) Stabilise (glucose, FFP, platelets); ITU; delivery once stabilised; supportive care

C) Vaginal preferred if stable; may need LSCS

D) Recovery over days to weeks; LFT and coagulopathy; monitor for pancreatitis, AKI

Answer: A

A) Stabilise (glucose, FFP, platelets); ITU; delivery once stabilised; supportive care

B) Vaginal preferred if stable; may need LSCS

C) Recovery over days to weeks; LFT and coagulopathy; monitor for pancreatitis, AKI

D) Recurrence low; screen fetus for LCHAD; avoid in subsequent

B) Recovery over days to weeks; LFT and coagulopathy; monitor for pancreatitis, AKI

C) Recurrence low; screen fetus for LCHAD; avoid in subsequent
