Q: Definition?

A: Microvesicular fatty infiltration of liver; 3rd trimester; liver failure, encephalopathy, DIC

Q: Epidemiology?

Q: Symptoms?

A: Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, abdominal pain, jaundice, thirst, polyuria, encephalopathy

Q: Investigations?

Q: Swansea criteria?

A: ≥6 of: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, bilirubin >14, hypoglycaemia <4, uric acid >340, leucocytosis >11, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy, coagulopathy

Q: Differential?

Q: Management?

Q: Delivery?

A: Vaginal preferred if stable; may need LSCS

▸ 30.13.2 Acute Pancreatitis In Pregnancy

Definition

Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy is uncommon (1 in 1000-3000 pregnancies) but carries significant maternal and fetal morbidity. Most cases are due to gallstones; hypertriglyceridaemia, alcohol, and idiopathic causes account for the remainder. Management is supportive with delivery considered in severe cases.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

1 in 1000-3000 pregnancies; rare but serious

Causes

Gallstones (60-70% — commonest); hypertriglyceridaemia (familial or secondary); alcohol; idiopathic; drugs; hyperparathyroidism; post-ERCP; trauma

Presentation

Severe epigastric pain radiating to back; nausea/vomiting; peritonism; fever; shock; jaundice

Diagnosis

Serum amylase >3x ULN; lipase more specific; USS to detect gallstones; MRCP if needed; CT rarely; differential: PUD, biliary colic, abruption, PE

Severity assessment

Atlanta criteria; BISAP score; CRP >150; organ failure (Atlanta severe)

Complications

Pancreatic necrosis; pseudocyst; ARDS; AKI; DIC; sepsis; death; preterm labour; fetal loss

Hypertriglyceridaemia-induced

Triglycerides >1000 mg/dL (>11 mmol/L); risk at >2000; usually with familial hypertriglyceridaemia

Management

NPO + IV fluids; analgesia (pethidine or morphine); NG tube if vomiting; antibiotics if infected necrosis; supportive care

ERCP/surgical

ERCP with sphincterotomy if biliary obstruction with cholangitis; cholecystectomy in 2nd trimester if stones; avoid in 1st and 3rd

Delivery

Vaginal preferred if stable; consider early delivery in severe disease; mode per obstetric; close fetal monitoring

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence1 in 1000-3000 pregnancies; rare but serious
CausesGallstones (60-70% — commonest); hypertriglyceridaemia (familial or secondary); alcohol; idiopathic; drugs; hyperparathyroidism; post-ERCP; trauma
PresentationSevere epigastric pain radiating to back; nausea/vomiting; peritonism; fever; shock; jaundice
DiagnosisSerum amylase >3x ULN; lipase more specific; USS to detect gallstones; MRCP if needed; CT rarely; differential: PUD, biliary colic, abruption, PE
Severity assessmentAtlanta criteria; BISAP score; CRP >150; organ failure (Atlanta severe)
ComplicationsPancreatic necrosis; pseudocyst; ARDS; AKI; DIC; sepsis; death; preterm labour; fetal loss
Hypertriglyceridaemia-inducedTriglycerides >1000 mg/dL (>11 mmol/L); risk at >2000; usually with familial hypertriglyceridaemia
ManagementNPO + IV fluids; analgesia (pethidine or morphine); NG tube if vomiting; antibiotics if infected necrosis; supportive care
ERCP/surgicalERCP with sphincterotomy if biliary obstruction with cholangitis; cholecystectomy in 2nd trimester if stones; avoid in 1st and 3rd
DeliveryVaginal preferred if stable; consider early delivery in severe disease; mode per obstetric; close fetal monitoring

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy?

Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy is uncommon (1 in 1000-3000 pregnancies) but carries significant maternal and fetal morbidity. Most cases are due to gallstones; hypertriglyceridaemia, alcohol, and idiopathic causes account for the remainder. Management is supportive with delivery considered in severe cases.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Causes, Presentation, Diagnosis, Severity assessment. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.13.3 Cirrhosis And Portal Hypertension In Pregnancy

Definition

Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy are uncommon as most cirrhotic women are beyond reproductive age or have amenorrhoea. However, with improved care, more women with compensated cirrhosis are becoming pregnant. Pregnancy carries significant risks of variceal bleeding, hepatic decompensation, and maternal mortality.

Core Clinical Content

Preconception

Optimise; screen for varices; beta-blocker; eradication of varices; counsel on risks; consider MELD score

Maternal risks

Variceal bleeding (5-30%); hepatic decompensation (ascites, encephalopathy, jaundice); postpartum haemorrhage; mortality 1-2% (compensated) up to 50% (decompensated)

Fetal risks

Miscarriage; preterm; low birth weight; stillbirth; congenital anomalies if on teratogens

Preconception screening

Upper GI endoscopy (varices); ultrasound (spleen, ascites, liver nodularity); MELD score; viral load if viral cause

Antenatal care

MDT (hepatologist, obstetrician, anaesthetist); endoscopic surveillance; LMWH if portal vein thrombosis or thrombocytopenia; avoid NSAIDs

Variceal bleeding

Most common in 2nd trimester; band ligation preferred; terlipressin (use cautiously); transfusion; rescue TIPSS if refractory

Beta-blockers

Propranolol or carvedilol for variceal prophylaxis; safe in pregnancy

Drugs to avoid

Statins (teratogenic); ACEI/ARB; methotrexate; some herbal products; high-dose vitamins A and E

Delivery

Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage (avoid Valsalva); avoid ergometrine; C-section for obstetric indication (haemorrhage risk)

Postpartum

Monitor for decompensation; VTE prophylaxis; breastfeeding compatible with most liver medications

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PreconceptionOptimise; screen for varices; beta-blocker; eradication of varices; counsel on risks; consider MELD score
Maternal risksVariceal bleeding (5-30%); hepatic decompensation (ascites, encephalopathy, jaundice); postpartum haemorrhage; mortality 1-2% (compensated) up to 50% (decompensated)
Fetal risksMiscarriage; preterm; low birth weight; stillbirth; congenital anomalies if on teratogens
Preconception screeningUpper GI endoscopy (varices); ultrasound (spleen, ascites, liver nodularity); MELD score; viral load if viral cause
Antenatal careMDT (hepatologist, obstetrician, anaesthetist); endoscopic surveillance; LMWH if portal vein thrombosis or thrombocytopenia; avoid NSAIDs
Variceal bleedingMost common in 2nd trimester; band ligation preferred; terlipressin (use cautiously); transfusion; rescue TIPSS if refractory
Beta-blockersPropranolol or carvedilol for variceal prophylaxis; safe in pregnancy
Drugs to avoidStatins (teratogenic); ACEI/ARB; methotrexate; some herbal products; high-dose vitamins A and E
DeliveryVaginal preferred; assisted second stage (avoid Valsalva); avoid ergometrine; C-section for obstetric indication (haemorrhage risk)
PostpartumMonitor for decompensation; VTE prophylaxis; breastfeeding compatible with most liver medications

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy?

Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy are uncommon as most cirrhotic women are beyond reproductive age or have amenorrhoea. However, with improved care, more women with compensated cirrhosis are becoming pregnant. Pregnancy carries significant risks of variceal bleeding, hepatic decompensation, and maternal mortality.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy?

Key features include: Preconception, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, Preconception screening, Antenatal care. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy?

A) Optimise; screen for varices; beta-blocker; eradication of varices; counsel on risks; consider MELD score

B) Variceal bleeding (5-30%); hepatic decompensation (ascites, encephalopathy, jaundice); postpartum haemorrhage; mortality 1-2% (compensated) up to 50% (decompensated)

C) Miscarriage; preterm; low birth weight; stillbirth; congenital anomalies if on teratogens

D) Upper GI endoscopy (varices); ultrasound (spleen, ascites, liver nodularity); MELD score; viral load if viral cause

Answer: A

Flashcards

Q: Preconception?

A: Optimise; screen for varices; beta-blocker; eradication of varices; counsel on risks; consider MELD score

Q: Maternal risks?

A: Variceal bleeding (5-30%); hepatic decompensation (ascites, encephalopathy, jaundice); postpartum haemorrhage; mortality 1-2% (compensated) up to 50% (decompensated)

Q: Fetal risks?

A: Miscarriage; preterm; low birth weight; stillbirth; congenital anomalies if on teratogens

Q: Preconception screening?

A: Upper GI endoscopy (varices); ultrasound (spleen, ascites, liver nodularity); MELD score; viral load if viral cause

Q: Antenatal care?

A: MDT (hepatologist, obstetrician, anaesthetist); endoscopic surveillance; LMWH if portal vein thrombosis or thrombocytopenia; avoid NSAIDs

Q: Variceal bleeding?

A: Most common in 2nd trimester; band ligation preferred; terlipressin (use cautiously); transfusion; rescue TIPSS if refractory

Q: Beta-blockers?

A: Propranolol or carvedilol for variceal prophylaxis; safe in pregnancy

Q: Drugs to avoid?

A: Statins (teratogenic); ACEI/ARB; methotrexate; some herbal products; high-dose vitamins A and E

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.13.4 Gallstones And Biliary Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Gallstone disease is the most common non-obstetric cause of abdominal pain in pregnancy due to elevated oestrogen (increased cholesterol secretion) and progesterone (biliary stasis). Most cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease includes biliary colic, acute cholecystitis, choledocholithiasis, and gallstone pancreatitis.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

Asymptomatic gallstones in 3-4% pregnancies; symptomatic 0.1%

Risk factors

Pre-existing gallstones; obesity; high-fat diet; rapid weight loss; multiparity; family history; oral oestrogens

Biliary sludge

Common in pregnancy; usually resolves postpartum

Biliary colic

Episodic RUQ/epigastric pain; resolves within hours; no fever; no Murphy's sign; no LFT derangement

Acute cholecystitis

RUQ pain; fever; Murphy's sign; leucocytosis; LFT may be mildly deranged; USS shows gallstones + wall thickening + pericholecystic fluid

Choledocholithiasis

Biliary colic + jaundice; cholangitis if Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain)

Investigations

USS (first-line; safe); LFT; FBC; amylase; MRCP if needed; ERCP if cholangitis

Management asymptomatic

Reassure; no surgery

Management symptomatic

Conservative (NPO, IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics); ERCP if cholangitis (radiation-minimising technique); cholecystectomy in 2nd trimester if recurrent/refractory

Postpartum

Symptoms often resolve; consider interval cholecystectomy if recurrent; contraception (avoid oestrogen)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
IncidenceAsymptomatic gallstones in 3-4% pregnancies; symptomatic 0.1%
Risk factorsPre-existing gallstones; obesity; high-fat diet; rapid weight loss; multiparity; family history; oral oestrogens
Biliary sludgeCommon in pregnancy; usually resolves postpartum
Biliary colicEpisodic RUQ/epigastric pain; resolves within hours; no fever; no Murphy's sign; no LFT derangement
Acute cholecystitisRUQ pain; fever; Murphy's sign; leucocytosis; LFT may be mildly deranged; USS shows gallstones + wall thickening + pericholecystic fluid
CholedocholithiasisBiliary colic + jaundice; cholangitis if Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain)
InvestigationsUSS (first-line; safe); LFT; FBC; amylase; MRCP if needed; ERCP if cholangitis
Management asymptomaticReassure; no surgery
Management symptomaticConservative (NPO, IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics); ERCP if cholangitis (radiation-minimising technique); cholecystectomy in 2nd trimester if recurrent/refractory
PostpartumSymptoms often resolve; consider interval cholecystectomy if recurrent; contraception (avoid oestrogen)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?

Gallstone disease is the most common non-obstetric cause of abdominal pain in pregnancy due to elevated oestrogen (increased cholesterol secretion) and progesterone (biliary stasis). Most cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease includes biliary colic, acute cholecystitis, choledocholithiasis, and gallstone pancreatitis.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Risk factors, Biliary sludge, Biliary colic, Acute cholecystitis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  7. A — Key feature of Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?
  8. A — Key feature of Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?
  9. A — Key feature of Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?
  10. A — Key feature of Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. Asymptomatic gallstones in 3-4% pregnancies; symptomatic 0.1%
  2. Pre-existing gallstones; obesity; high-fat diet; rapid weight loss; multiparity; family history; oral oestrogens
  3. Common in pregnancy; usually resolves postpartum
  4. Episodic RUQ/epigastric pain; resolves within hours; no fever; no Murphy's sign; no LFT derangement
  5. RUQ pain; fever; Murphy's sign; leucocytosis; LFT may be mildly deranged; USS shows gallstones + wall thickening + pericholecystic fluid
  6. Biliary colic + jaundice; cholangitis if Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain)
  7. USS (first-line; safe); LFT; FBC; amylase; MRCP if needed; ERCP if cholangitis
  8. Reassure; no surgery
  9. Conservative (NPO, IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics); ERCP if cholangitis (radiation-minimising technique); cholecystectomy in 2nd trimester if recurrent/refractory
  10. Symptoms often resolve; consider interval cholecystectomy if recurrent; contraception (avoid oestrogen)

▸ 30.13.5 Hyperemesis Gravidarum

Definition

Hyperemesis gravidarum is severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy causing dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, and weight loss >5% of pre-pregnancy weight, requiring hospital admission. It typically begins before 12 weeks and resolves by 16-20 weeks.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Severe nausea/vomiting before 20 weeks; dehydration; ketones; >5% weight loss; needs admission

Epidemiology

0.3-2% of pregnancies; commoner in younger, primigravid, non-smokers, multiple pregnancy

Aetiology

hCG, oestrogen, GDF15, thyroid, H. pylori, psychosomatic

Differential

Molar pregnancy; UTI; thyrotoxicosis; Addison's; pancreatitis; hepatitis; GI obstruction

Complications

Wernicke's (thiamine); Mallory-Weiss; oesophageal tear; hypokalaemia; hyponatraemia; renal impairment

Investigations

U&E, LFT, TFT (transient hyperthyroidism in 50%), USS (molar/twins)

First-line treatment

Reassurance; lifestyle; ginger; P6 acupressure; antihistamines (cyclizine, promethazine)

Second-line

Metoclopramide; ondansetron (data reassuring but theoretical cardiac risk); prochlorperazine

Third-line

Corticosteroids (prednisolone/prednisone — avoid 1st trimester if possible); enteral/parenteral nutrition

Prevention

Pre-emptive multivitamin + ginger at conception in high-risk women

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionSevere nausea/vomiting before 20 weeks; dehydration; ketones; >5% weight loss; needs admission
Epidemiology0.3-2% of pregnancies; commoner in younger, primigravid, non-smokers, multiple pregnancy
AetiologyhCG, oestrogen, GDF15, thyroid, H. pylori, psychosomatic
DifferentialMolar pregnancy; UTI; thyrotoxicosis; Addison's; pancreatitis; hepatitis; GI obstruction
ComplicationsWernicke's (thiamine); Mallory-Weiss; oesophageal tear; hypokalaemia; hyponatraemia; renal impairment
InvestigationsU&E, LFT, TFT (transient hyperthyroidism in 50%), USS (molar/twins)
First-line treatmentReassurance; lifestyle; ginger; P6 acupressure; antihistamines (cyclizine, promethazine)
Second-lineMetoclopramide; ondansetron (data reassuring but theoretical cardiac risk); prochlorperazine
Third-lineCorticosteroids (prednisolone/prednisone — avoid 1st trimester if possible); enteral/parenteral nutrition
PreventionPre-emptive multivitamin + ginger at conception in high-risk women

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Hyperemesis gravidarum?

Hyperemesis gravidarum is severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy causing dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, and weight loss >5% of pre-pregnancy weight, requiring hospital admission. It typically begins before 12 weeks and resolves by 16-20 weeks.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Hyperemesis gravidarum?

Key features include: Definition, Epidemiology, Aetiology, Differential, Complications. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Hyperemesis gravidarum diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Hyperemesis gravidarum?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Hyperemesis gravidarum?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Hyperemesis gravidarum — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.13.6 Inflammatory Bowel Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) — Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis — commonly affects women in childbearing years. Active disease at conception is the strongest predictor of flare in pregnancy and adverse outcomes. Most medications (5-ASA, corticosteroids, anti-TNF) are safe; methotrexate is teratogenic and contraindicated.

Core Clinical Content

Fertility

Generally normal in UC and quiescent Crohn; decreased in active disease; pelvic surgery affects fertility

Preconception

Achieve remission ≥3-6 months before conception; continue maintenance therapy; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months)

Disease activity

Active disease at conception → 70% active during pregnancy; remission → 70% stay in remission

Maternal risks

Flare; malnutrition; anaemia; thromboembolism; surgical complications; need for surgery

Fetal risks

Active disease → preterm; LBW; stillbirth; miscarriage; congenital abnormalities if on teratogens

Safe medications

Mesalazine (5-ASA); sulfasalazine (with folate); corticosteroids (prednisolone, budesonide); azathioprine/6-MP; infliximab/adalimumab (limited placental transfer 1st trimester, then crosses — check levels)

Avoid

Methotrexate (teratogenic); mycophenolate (teratogenic — switch pre-conception); metronidazole in 1st trimester (some concerns); ciclosporin (limited data but used in severe UC)

Investigations during pregnancy

Faecal calprotectin; CRP; USS; MRI without gadolinium; flexible sigmoidoscopy safe; colonoscopy rarely if essential

Management of flare

Similar to non-pregnant: 5-ASA; steroids; biologic; consider colectomy if life-threatening (UC)

Delivery

Vaginal usual; consider C-section if active perianal Crohn; mode per obstetric indication; continue medications intrapartum

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
FertilityGenerally normal in UC and quiescent Crohn; decreased in active disease; pelvic surgery affects fertility
PreconceptionAchieve remission ≥3-6 months before conception; continue maintenance therapy; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months)
Disease activityActive disease at conception → 70% active during pregnancy; remission → 70% stay in remission
Maternal risksFlare; malnutrition; anaemia; thromboembolism; surgical complications; need for surgery
Fetal risksActive disease → preterm; LBW; stillbirth; miscarriage; congenital abnormalities if on teratogens
Safe medicationsMesalazine (5-ASA); sulfasalazine (with folate); corticosteroids (prednisolone, budesonide); azathioprine/6-MP; infliximab/adalimumab (limited placental transfer 1st trimester, then crosses — check levels)
AvoidMethotrexate (teratogenic); mycophenolate (teratogenic — switch pre-conception); metronidazole in 1st trimester (some concerns); ciclosporin (limited data but used in severe UC)
Investigations during pregnancyFaecal calprotectin; CRP; USS; MRI without gadolinium; flexible sigmoidoscopy safe; colonoscopy rarely if essential
Management of flareSimilar to non-pregnant: 5-ASA; steroids; biologic; consider colectomy if life-threatening (UC)
DeliveryVaginal usual; consider C-section if active perianal Crohn; mode per obstetric indication; continue medications intrapartum

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy?

Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) — Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis — commonly affects women in childbearing years. Active disease at conception is the strongest predictor of flare in pregnancy and adverse outcomes. Most medications (5-ASA, corticosteroids, anti-TNF) are safe; methotrexate is teratogenic and contraindicated.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Fertility, Preconception, Disease activity, Maternal risks, Fetal risks. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy?

A) Vaginal usual; consider C-section if active perianal Crohn; mode per obstetric indication; continue medications intrapartum

B) Generally normal in UC and quiescent Crohn; decreased in active disease; pelvic surgery affects fertility

C) Achieve remission ≥3-6 months before conception; continue maintenance therapy; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months)

D) Active disease at conception → 70% active during pregnancy; remission → 70% stay in remission

Answer: A

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Generally normal in UC and quiescent Crohn; decreased in active disease; pelvic surgery affects fertility
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Achieve remission ≥3-6 months before conception; continue maintenance therapy; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months)
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Active disease at conception → 70% active during pregnancy; remission → 70% stay in remission
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Flare; malnutrition; anaemia; thromboembolism; surgical complications; need for surgery
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Active disease → preterm; LBW; stillbirth; miscarriage; congenital abnormalities if on teratogens
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Mesalazine (5-ASA); sulfasalazine (with folate); corticosteroids (prednisolone, budesonide); azathioprine/6-MP; infliximab/adalimumab (limited placental transfer 1st trimester, then crosses — check levels)
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Methotrexate (teratogenic); mycophenolate (teratogenic — switch pre-conception); metronidazole in 1st trimester (some concerns); ciclosporin (limited data but used in severe UC)
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Faecal calprotectin; CRP; USS; MRI without gadolinium; flexible sigmoidoscopy safe; colonoscopy rarely if essential
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Similar to non-pregnant: 5-ASA; steroids; biologic; consider colectomy if life-threatening (UC)
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Vaginal usual; consider C-section if active perianal Crohn; mode per obstetric indication; continue medications intrapartum

Flashcards

Q: Fertility?

A: Generally normal in UC and quiescent Crohn; decreased in active disease; pelvic surgery affects fertility

Q: Preconception?

A: Achieve remission ≥3-6 months before conception; continue maintenance therapy; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months)

Q: Disease activity?

A: Active disease at conception → 70% active during pregnancy; remission → 70% stay in remission

Q: Maternal risks?

A: Flare; malnutrition; anaemia; thromboembolism; surgical complications; need for surgery

Q: Fetal risks?

A: Active disease → preterm; LBW; stillbirth; miscarriage; congenital abnormalities if on teratogens

Q: Safe medications?

A: Mesalazine (5-ASA); sulfasalazine (with folate); corticosteroids (prednisolone, budesonide); azathioprine/6-MP; infliximab/adalimumab (limited placental transfer 1st trimester, then crosses — check levels)

Q: Avoid?

A: Methotrexate (teratogenic); mycophenolate (teratogenic — switch pre-conception); metronidazole in 1st trimester (some concerns); ciclosporin (limited data but used in severe UC)

Q: Investigations during pregnancy?

A: Faecal calprotectin; CRP; USS; MRI without gadolinium; flexible sigmoidoscopy safe; colonoscopy rarely if essential

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  2. A — Key feature of Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy?

  10. A — Key feature of Inflammatory bowel disease in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. Generally normal in UC and quiescent Crohn; decreased in active disease; pelvic surgery affects fertility
  2. Achieve remission ≥3-6 months before conception; continue maintenance therapy; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months)
  3. Active disease at conception → 70% active during pregnancy; remission → 70% stay in remission
  4. Flare; malnutrition; anaemia; thromboembolism; surgical complications; need for surgery
  5. Active disease → preterm; LBW; stillbirth; miscarriage; congenital abnormalities if on teratogens
  6. Mesalazine (5-ASA); sulfasalazine (with folate); corticosteroids (prednisolone, budesonide); azathioprine/6-MP; infliximab/adalimumab (limited placental transfer 1st trimester, then crosses — check levels)
  7. Methotrexate (teratogenic); mycophenolate (teratogenic — switch pre-conception); metronidazole in 1st trimester (some concerns); ciclosporin (limited data but used in severe UC)
  8. Faecal calprotectin; CRP; USS; MRI without gadolinium; flexible sigmoidoscopy safe; colonoscopy rarely if essential
  9. Similar to non-pregnant: 5-ASA; steroids; biologic; consider colectomy if life-threatening (UC)
  10. Vaginal usual; consider C-section if active perianal Crohn; mode per obstetric indication; continue medications intrapartum

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.13.7 Intrahepatic Cholestasis Of Pregnancy Icp

Definition

Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy is a pruritic liver disorder of pregnancy, typically presenting in the 2nd or 3rd trimester with intense itching (especially palms and soles), elevated bile acids, and abnormal LFTs. It is associated with increased risk of stillbirth, preterm birth, and fetal distress.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Pruritus in pregnancy + abnormal LFT and/or bile acids >10 μmol/L

Prevalence

0.5-1.5% in Europe; up to 5-15% in South American (Araucanian) and South Asian populations

Pathophysiology

Impaired hepatic bile acid excretion; genetic (ABCB4/MDR3) and hormonal factors

Symptoms

Intense pruritus (palms, soles) without rash; worse at night; steatorrhoea; dark urine

Diagnosis

Bile acids >10 μmol/L (gold standard); raised ALT/AST; ALP poorly specific; exclude viral hepatitis, PBC

Fetal risks

Stillbirth (esp. if bile acids >40); preterm birth; meconium staining; fetal distress; postpartum haemorrhage

Maternal management

Ursodeoxycholic acid 10-15 mg/kg/day — first-line; reduces pruritus and may reduce fetal risk

Monitoring

Weekly bile acids and LFT; weekly CTG from 34 weeks; growth scans; patient education on stillbirth

Delivery

Induce at 36-37 weeks (or earlier if severe; bile acids >40 deliver at 36)

Postpartum

Resolves; LFT normalises within 6-12 weeks; recurrence 60-70%; long-term risk of gallstones, biliary cirrhosis

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPruritus in pregnancy + abnormal LFT and/or bile acids >10 μmol/L
Prevalence0.5-1.5% in Europe; up to 5-15% in South American (Araucanian) and South Asian populations
PathophysiologyImpaired hepatic bile acid excretion; genetic (ABCB4/MDR3) and hormonal factors
SymptomsIntense pruritus (palms, soles) without rash; worse at night; steatorrhoea; dark urine
DiagnosisBile acids >10 μmol/L (gold standard); raised ALT/AST; ALP poorly specific; exclude viral hepatitis, PBC
Fetal risksStillbirth (esp. if bile acids >40); preterm birth; meconium staining; fetal distress; postpartum haemorrhage
Maternal managementUrsodeoxycholic acid 10-15 mg/kg/day — first-line; reduces pruritus and may reduce fetal risk
MonitoringWeekly bile acids and LFT; weekly CTG from 34 weeks; growth scans; patient education on stillbirth
DeliveryInduce at 36-37 weeks (or earlier if severe; bile acids >40 deliver at 36)
PostpartumResolves; LFT normalises within 6-12 weeks; recurrence 60-70%; long-term risk of gallstones, biliary cirrhosis

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP)?

Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy is a pruritic liver disorder of pregnancy, typically presenting in the 2nd or 3rd trimester with intense itching (especially palms and soles), elevated bile acids, and abnormal LFTs. It is associated with increased risk of stillbirth, preterm birth, and fetal distress.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP)?

Key features include: Definition, Prevalence, Pathophysiology, Symptoms, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.13.8 Viral Hepatitis C And E In Pregnancy

Definition

Viral hepatitis C (HCV) and E (HEV) in pregnancy are less common than HBV but carry specific risks. HCV has a low vertical transmission rate (5%) but is increased with co-infection (HIV, high viral load). HEV in pregnancy is associated with severe hepatitis and fulminant liver failure, particularly in the 3rd trimester.

Core Clinical Content

Hepatitis C (HCV)

Most common blood-borne virus; affects 1-2% pregnancies; vertical transmission 5% (higher with HIV co-infection or high VL)

Hepatitis E (HEV)

Faecal-oral; endemic (Asia, Africa, Central America); 3rd-trimester mortality 20-30% (vs <1% in non-pregnant)

HCV diagnosis

Anti-HCV; HCV RNA PCR; LFT; genotype

HEV diagnosis

Anti-HEV IgM/IgG; HEV RNA; LFT; consider in unexplained hepatitis

HCV management

Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) deferred until postpartum; monitor LFT; avoid invasive procedures in labour (scalp electrodes); avoid prolonged ROM; avoid instrumental if possible

HCV delivery

Mode does not affect transmission; avoid internal fetal monitoring; avoid prolonged ROM >6 h

HCV breastfeeding

Safe (if no cracked nipples)

HCV neonate

Test at 12-18 months (anti-HCV); PCR if early

HEV management

Supportive; ribavirin not used (teratogenic); consider delivery if severe; fulminant failure may need transplant

HEV prevention

Avoid undercooked meat (zoonotic); safe water; HEV vaccine (Hecolin) in endemic areas

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Hepatitis C (HCV)Most common blood-borne virus; affects 1-2% pregnancies; vertical transmission 5% (higher with HIV co-infection or high VL)
Hepatitis E (HEV)Faecal-oral; endemic (Asia, Africa, Central America); 3rd-trimester mortality 20-30% (vs <1% in non-pregnant)
HCV diagnosisAnti-HCV; HCV RNA PCR; LFT; genotype
HEV diagnosisAnti-HEV IgM/IgG; HEV RNA; LFT; consider in unexplained hepatitis
HCV managementDirect-acting antivirals (DAAs) deferred until postpartum; monitor LFT; avoid invasive procedures in labour (scalp electrodes); avoid prolonged ROM; avoid instrumental if possible
HCV deliveryMode does not affect transmission; avoid internal fetal monitoring; avoid prolonged ROM >6 h
HCV breastfeedingSafe (if no cracked nipples)
HCV neonateTest at 12-18 months (anti-HCV); PCR if early
HEV managementSupportive; ribavirin not used (teratogenic); consider delivery if severe; fulminant failure may need transplant
HEV preventionAvoid undercooked meat (zoonotic); safe water; HEV vaccine (Hecolin) in endemic areas

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

Viral hepatitis C (HCV) and E (HEV) in pregnancy are less common than HBV but carry specific risks. HCV has a low vertical transmission rate (5%) but is increased with co-infection (HIV, high viral load). HEV in pregnancy is associated with severe hepatitis and fulminant liver failure, particularly in the 3rd trimester.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

Key features include: Hepatitis C (HCV), Hepatitis E (HEV), HCV diagnosis, HEV diagnosis, HCV management. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

A) Most common blood-borne virus; affects 1-2% pregnancies; vertical transmission 5% (higher with HIV co-infection or high VL)

B) Faecal-oral; endemic (Asia, Africa, Central America); 3rd-trimester mortality 20-30% (vs <1% in non-pregnant)

C) Anti-HCV; HCV RNA PCR; LFT; genotype

D) Anti-HEV IgM/IgG; HEV RNA; LFT; consider in unexplained hepatitis

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

A) Faecal-oral; endemic (Asia, Africa, Central America); 3rd-trimester mortality 20-30% (vs <1% in non-pregnant)

B) Anti-HCV; HCV RNA PCR; LFT; genotype

C) Anti-HEV IgM/IgG; HEV RNA; LFT; consider in unexplained hepatitis

D) Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) deferred until postpartum; monitor LFT; avoid invasive procedures in labour (scalp electrodes); avoid prolonged ROM; avoid instrumental if possible

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

A) Anti-HCV; HCV RNA PCR; LFT; genotype

B) Anti-HEV IgM/IgG; HEV RNA; LFT; consider in unexplained hepatitis

C) Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) deferred until postpartum; monitor LFT; avoid invasive procedures in labour (scalp electrodes); avoid prolonged ROM; avoid instrumental if possible

D) Mode does not affect transmission; avoid internal fetal monitoring; avoid prolonged ROM >6 h

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

A) Anti-HEV IgM/IgG; HEV RNA; LFT; consider in unexplained hepatitis

B) Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) deferred until postpartum; monitor LFT; avoid invasive procedures in labour (scalp electrodes); avoid prolonged ROM; avoid instrumental if possible

C) Mode does not affect transmission; avoid internal fetal monitoring; avoid prolonged ROM >6 h

D) Safe (if no cracked nipples)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

A) Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) deferred until postpartum; monitor LFT; avoid invasive procedures in labour (scalp electrodes); avoid prolonged ROM; avoid instrumental if possible

B) Mode does not affect transmission; avoid internal fetal monitoring; avoid prolonged ROM >6 h

C) Safe (if no cracked nipples)

D) Test at 12-18 months (anti-HCV); PCR if early

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

A) Mode does not affect transmission; avoid internal fetal monitoring; avoid prolonged ROM >6 h

B) Safe (if no cracked nipples)

C) Test at 12-18 months (anti-HCV); PCR if early

D) Supportive; ribavirin not used (teratogenic); consider delivery if severe; fulminant failure may need transplant

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

A) Safe (if no cracked nipples)

B) Test at 12-18 months (anti-HCV); PCR if early

C) Supportive; ribavirin not used (teratogenic); consider delivery if severe; fulminant failure may need transplant

D) Avoid undercooked meat (zoonotic); safe water; HEV vaccine (Hecolin) in endemic areas

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

A) Test at 12-18 months (anti-HCV); PCR if early

B) Supportive; ribavirin not used (teratogenic); consider delivery if severe; fulminant failure may need transplant

C) Avoid undercooked meat (zoonotic); safe water; HEV vaccine (Hecolin) in endemic areas

D) Most common blood-borne virus; affects 1-2% pregnancies; vertical transmission 5% (higher with HIV co-infection or high VL)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

A) Supportive; ribavirin not used (teratogenic); consider delivery if severe; fulminant failure may need transplant

B) Avoid undercooked meat (zoonotic); safe water; HEV vaccine (Hecolin) in endemic areas

C) Most common blood-borne virus; affects 1-2% pregnancies; vertical transmission 5% (higher with HIV co-infection or high VL)

D) Faecal-oral; endemic (Asia, Africa, Central America); 3rd-trimester mortality 20-30% (vs <1% in non-pregnant)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

A) Avoid undercooked meat (zoonotic); safe water; HEV vaccine (Hecolin) in endemic areas

B) Most common blood-borne virus; affects 1-2% pregnancies; vertical transmission 5% (higher with HIV co-infection or high VL)

C) Faecal-oral; endemic (Asia, Africa, Central America); 3rd-trimester mortality 20-30% (vs <1% in non-pregnant)

D) Anti-HCV; HCV RNA PCR; LFT; genotype

Answer: A

Flashcards

Q: Hepatitis C (HCV)?

A: Most common blood-borne virus; affects 1-2% pregnancies; vertical transmission 5% (higher with HIV co-infection or high VL)

Q: Hepatitis E (HEV)?

A: Faecal-oral; endemic (Asia, Africa, Central America); 3rd-trimester mortality 20-30% (vs <1% in non-pregnant)

Q: HCV diagnosis?

A: Anti-HCV; HCV RNA PCR; LFT; genotype

Q: HEV diagnosis?

A: Anti-HEV IgM/IgG; HEV RNA; LFT; consider in unexplained hepatitis

Q: HCV management?

A: Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) deferred until postpartum; monitor LFT; avoid invasive procedures in labour (scalp electrodes); avoid prolonged ROM; avoid instrumental if possible

Q: HCV delivery?

A: Mode does not affect transmission; avoid internal fetal monitoring; avoid prolonged ROM >6 h

Q: HCV breastfeeding?

A: Safe (if no cracked nipples)

Q: HCV neonate?

A: Test at 12-18 months (anti-HCV); PCR if early

Answer Key with Explanations

  2. Faecal-oral; endemic (Asia, Africa, Central America); 3rd-trimester mortality 20-30% (vs <1% in non-pregnant)
  3. Anti-HCV; HCV RNA PCR; LFT; genotype
  4. Anti-HEV IgM/IgG; HEV RNA; LFT; consider in unexplained hepatitis
  5. Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) deferred until postpartum; monitor LFT; avoid invasive procedures in labour (scalp electrodes); avoid prolonged ROM; avoid instrumental if possible
  6. Mode does not affect transmission; avoid internal fetal monitoring; avoid prolonged ROM >6 h

  8. Test at 12-18 months (anti-HCV); PCR if early
  9. Supportive; ribavirin not used (teratogenic); consider delivery if severe; fulminant failure may need transplant
  10. Avoid undercooked meat (zoonotic); safe water; HEV vaccine (Hecolin) in endemic areas

▸ 30.14 Malignancy in Pregnancy 3
▸ 30.14.1 Breast Cancer In Pregnancy

Definition

Breast cancer in pregnancy is the most common malignancy in pregnancy (1 in 3,000-10,000 pregnancies), often presenting at advanced stage due to pregnancy-related breast changes. Treatment is individualised according to gestational age, stage, and tumour biology, balancing maternal prognosis with fetal safety.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

1 in 3,000-10,000; most common cancer in pregnancy; median age 33

Risk factors

BRCA1/2; family history; older maternal age; not associated with pregnancy itself

Presentation

Lump (most common); nipple discharge; skin changes; often delayed diagnosis (mimics physiological changes)

Diagnosis

Triple assessment: clinical exam; imaging (USS first-line; mammogram with shielding); core biopsy

Staging

MRI without gadolinium; CXR; liver USS; bone scan deferred; sentinel node biopsy with technetium (limited); blue dye avoid (anaphylaxis)

Treatment surgery

Mastectomy or wide local excision; safe all trimesters; SLNB with technetium

Chemotherapy

Avoid 1st trimester (teratogenic); AC or FAC regimens from 2nd trimester; stop 3 weeks before delivery; not after 34-35 weeks

Avoid in pregnancy

Radiotherapy; trastuzumab (HER2+ — oligohydramnios); endocrine therapy (tamoxifen — teratogenic); methotrexate

Delivery

Timing based on treatment; if possible delay until fetal maturity; vaginal if obstetric; avoid iatrogenic prematurity

Prognosis

Often advanced at diagnosis; stage-matched similar to non-pregnant; prognosis improving with modern treatment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence1 in 3,000-10,000; most common cancer in pregnancy; median age 33
Risk factorsBRCA1/2; family history; older maternal age; not associated with pregnancy itself
PresentationLump (most common); nipple discharge; skin changes; often delayed diagnosis (mimics physiological changes)
DiagnosisTriple assessment: clinical exam; imaging (USS first-line; mammogram with shielding); core biopsy
StagingMRI without gadolinium; CXR; liver USS; bone scan deferred; sentinel node biopsy with technetium (limited); blue dye avoid (anaphylaxis)
Treatment surgeryMastectomy or wide local excision; safe all trimesters; SLNB with technetium
ChemotherapyAvoid 1st trimester (teratogenic); AC or FAC regimens from 2nd trimester; stop 3 weeks before delivery; not after 34-35 weeks
Avoid in pregnancyRadiotherapy; trastuzumab (HER2+ — oligohydramnios); endocrine therapy (tamoxifen — teratogenic); methotrexate
DeliveryTiming based on treatment; if possible delay until fetal maturity; vaginal if obstetric; avoid iatrogenic prematurity
PrognosisOften advanced at diagnosis; stage-matched similar to non-pregnant; prognosis improving with modern treatment

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Breast cancer in pregnancy?

Breast cancer in pregnancy is the most common malignancy in pregnancy (1 in 3,000-10,000 pregnancies), often presenting at advanced stage due to pregnancy-related breast changes. Treatment is individualised according to gestational age, stage, and tumour biology, balancing maternal prognosis with fetal safety.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Breast cancer in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Risk factors, Presentation, Diagnosis, Staging. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Breast cancer in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Breast cancer in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Breast cancer in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Breast cancer in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.14.2 Cancer In Pregnancy

Definition

Cancer complicates 1 in 1,000-2,000 pregnancies. The most common malignancies are breast, cervical, melanoma, thyroid, lymphoma, and leukaemia. Management requires MDT balancing maternal prognosis, fetal viability, and gestational age.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

1 in 1,000-2,000 pregnancies; commoner as maternal age rises

Common cancers

Breast (most common), cervical, melanoma, thyroid, lymphoma, leukaemia, colorectal

Diagnosis

Often delayed because symptoms overlap with pregnancy; imaging without ionising radiation; tissue diagnosis essential

Staging

MRI without gadolinium; ultrasound; CXR with shielding; avoid CT/PET where possible

Surgery

Safe all trimesters; optimal 2nd trimester

Chemotherapy

Avoid 1st trimester (teratogenicity); safe in 2nd/3rd trimesters; avoid chemo 3 weeks before delivery (myelosuppression)

Radiotherapy

Generally avoided in pregnancy; scatter risks; only if fetus can be shielded and life-saving

Targeted/immunotherapy

Limited data; trastuzumab causes oligohydramnios; checkpoint inhibitors can cause miscarriage

End-of-pregnancy care

Delivery by 35-37 weeks to allow postpartum treatment; consider corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity

Termination

Considered if aggressive malignancy; patient's choice; MDT input

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence1 in 1,000-2,000 pregnancies; commoner as maternal age rises
Common cancersBreast (most common), cervical, melanoma, thyroid, lymphoma, leukaemia, colorectal
DiagnosisOften delayed because symptoms overlap with pregnancy; imaging without ionising radiation; tissue diagnosis essential
StagingMRI without gadolinium; ultrasound; CXR with shielding; avoid CT/PET where possible
SurgerySafe all trimesters; optimal 2nd trimester
ChemotherapyAvoid 1st trimester (teratogenicity); safe in 2nd/3rd trimesters; avoid chemo 3 weeks before delivery (myelosuppression)
RadiotherapyGenerally avoided in pregnancy; scatter risks; only if fetus can be shielded and life-saving
Targeted/immunotherapyLimited data; trastuzumab causes oligohydramnios; checkpoint inhibitors can cause miscarriage
End-of-pregnancy careDelivery by 35-37 weeks to allow postpartum treatment; consider corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity
TerminationConsidered if aggressive malignancy; patient's choice; MDT input

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Cancer in pregnancy?

Cancer complicates 1 in 1,000-2,000 pregnancies. The most common malignancies are breast, cervical, melanoma, thyroid, lymphoma, and leukaemia. Management requires MDT balancing maternal prognosis, fetal viability, and gestational age.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cancer in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Common cancers, Diagnosis, Staging, Surgery. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Cancer in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cancer in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Cancer in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Cancer in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Cancer in pregnancy?

A) 1 in 1,000-2,000 pregnancies; commoner as maternal age rises

B) Breast (most common), cervical, melanoma, thyroid, lymphoma, leukaemia, colorectal

C) Often delayed because symptoms overlap with pregnancy; imaging without ionising radiation; tissue diagnosis essential

D) MRI without gadolinium; ultrasound; CXR with shielding; avoid CT/PET where possible

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cancer in pregnancy?

A) Breast (most common), cervical, melanoma, thyroid, lymphoma, leukaemia, colorectal

B) Often delayed because symptoms overlap with pregnancy; imaging without ionising radiation; tissue diagnosis essential

C) MRI without gadolinium; ultrasound; CXR with shielding; avoid CT/PET where possible

D) Safe all trimesters; optimal 2nd trimester

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cancer in pregnancy?

A) Often delayed because symptoms overlap with pregnancy; imaging without ionising radiation; tissue diagnosis essential

B) MRI without gadolinium; ultrasound; CXR with shielding; avoid CT/PET where possible

C) Safe all trimesters; optimal 2nd trimester

D) Avoid 1st trimester (teratogenicity); safe in 2nd/3rd trimesters; avoid chemo 3 weeks before delivery (myelosuppression)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cancer in pregnancy?

A) MRI without gadolinium; ultrasound; CXR with shielding; avoid CT/PET where possible

B) Safe all trimesters; optimal 2nd trimester

C) Avoid 1st trimester (teratogenicity); safe in 2nd/3rd trimesters; avoid chemo 3 weeks before delivery (myelosuppression)

D) Generally avoided in pregnancy; scatter risks; only if fetus can be shielded and life-saving

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cancer in pregnancy?

A) Safe all trimesters; optimal 2nd trimester

B) Avoid 1st trimester (teratogenicity); safe in 2nd/3rd trimesters; avoid chemo 3 weeks before delivery (myelosuppression)

C) Generally avoided in pregnancy; scatter risks; only if fetus can be shielded and life-saving

D) Limited data; trastuzumab causes oligohydramnios; checkpoint inhibitors can cause miscarriage

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cancer in pregnancy?

A) Avoid 1st trimester (teratogenicity); safe in 2nd/3rd trimesters; avoid chemo 3 weeks before delivery (myelosuppression)

B) Generally avoided in pregnancy; scatter risks; only if fetus can be shielded and life-saving

C) Limited data; trastuzumab causes oligohydramnios; checkpoint inhibitors can cause miscarriage

D) Delivery by 35-37 weeks to allow postpartum treatment; consider corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity

Answer: A

▸ 30.14.3 Gestational Trophoblastic Disease Gtd

Definition

Gestational trophoblastic disease encompasses hydatidiform mole (complete and partial), invasive mole, choriocarcinoma, and placental site trophoblastic tumour. hCG is the key biomarker. Most are cured with appropriate management and surveillance.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Spectrum from molar pregnancy to metastatic GTN

Complete mole

46,XX (or XY) androgenetic; empty egg + sperm duplication; no fetus; hCG very high; 2-3% choriocarcinoma

Partial mole

69,XXX/XXY/XYY; triploid; fetus present but abnormal; hCG mildly raised; 0.5% choriocarcinoma

Presentation

Vaginal bleeding; hyperemesis; hyperthyroidism (hCG); uterine size > dates; theca lutein cysts; pre-eclampsia <20 weeks

Diagnosis

USS — 'snowstorm' (complete); cystic spaces with fetus (partial); hCG >100,000

Management molar

Suction curettage (evacuation); Rh prophylaxis if Rh-; send for histology

Surveillance

Serial hCG weekly until normal, then monthly for 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial); reliable contraception

GTN

Rising or plateau hCG; choriocarcinoma; metastasis to lung (80%), vagina, liver, brain

Treatment GTN

Low-risk: methotrexate or actinomycin-D; high-risk: EMA-CO (etoposide, methotrexate, actinomycin, cyclophosphamide, vincristine)

Future pregnancy

Normal fertility; 1% recurrence; hCG 6-8 weeks postpartum; send histology

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionSpectrum from molar pregnancy to metastatic GTN
Complete mole46,XX (or XY) androgenetic; empty egg + sperm duplication; no fetus; hCG very high; 2-3% choriocarcinoma
Partial mole69,XXX/XXY/XYY; triploid; fetus present but abnormal; hCG mildly raised; 0.5% choriocarcinoma
PresentationVaginal bleeding; hyperemesis; hyperthyroidism (hCG); uterine size > dates; theca lutein cysts; pre-eclampsia <20 weeks
DiagnosisUSS — 'snowstorm' (complete); cystic spaces with fetus (partial); hCG >100,000
Management molarSuction curettage (evacuation); Rh prophylaxis if Rh-; send for histology
SurveillanceSerial hCG weekly until normal, then monthly for 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial); reliable contraception
GTNRising or plateau hCG; choriocarcinoma; metastasis to lung (80%), vagina, liver, brain
Treatment GTNLow-risk: methotrexate or actinomycin-D; high-risk: EMA-CO (etoposide, methotrexate, actinomycin, cyclophosphamide, vincristine)
Future pregnancyNormal fertility; 1% recurrence; hCG 6-8 weeks postpartum; send histology

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD)?

Gestational trophoblastic disease encompasses hydatidiform mole (complete and partial), invasive mole, choriocarcinoma, and placental site trophoblastic tumour. hCG is the key biomarker. Most are cured with appropriate management and surveillance.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD)?

Key features include: Definition, Complete mole, Partial mole, Presentation, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.15 Mental Health in Pregnancy 4
▸ 30.15.1 Antenatal And Postnatal Depression

Definition

Antenatal (prenatal) and postnatal (postpartum) depression are common and treatable mood disorders affecting 10-15% of women in pregnancy and the year after birth. Distinguishing from normal postpartum adjustment ('baby blues'), anxiety, and postpartum psychosis is critical. Untreated illness has significant impact on mother, baby, and family.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

Antenatal depression 10-15%; postnatal depression 10-15%; underdiagnosed

Risk factors

Prior depression (strongest); family history; lack of support; domestic violence; recent stress; teenage; substance use; social isolation

Screening

Whooley + GAD-2 at booking and 6 weeks; EPDS ≥13 or positive Q10 (suicidal ideation) prompts further assessment

Symptoms

Persistent low mood; anhedonia; sleep disturbance (despite baby sleeping); appetite change; fatigue; guilt; poor concentration; suicidal thoughts; poor bonding

Differential

Baby blues (3-5 days, self-limiting); anxiety; OCD; PTSD; thyroid dysfunction; anaemia; postnatal psychosis (psychotic features)

Treatment

Stepped care: self-help, peer support; psychological therapies (CBT, IPT — first-line); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation)

Sertraline

First-line SSRI in pregnancy/lactation; lowest transfer to breast milk; data reassuring

Avoid

Paroxetine (1st-trimester cardiac risk); benzodiazepines (neonatal withdrawal, floppy baby); lithium (cardiac — monitor); valproate (developmental delay)

ECT

For severe, psychotic, or treatment-resistant depression; safe in pregnancy

Multidisciplinary care

GP; HV; obstetrician; psychiatrist; perinatal mental health team; safeguarding; crisis plan

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PrevalenceAntenatal depression 10-15%; postnatal depression 10-15%; underdiagnosed
Risk factorsPrior depression (strongest); family history; lack of support; domestic violence; recent stress; teenage; substance use; social isolation
ScreeningWhooley + GAD-2 at booking and 6 weeks; EPDS ≥13 or positive Q10 (suicidal ideation) prompts further assessment
SymptomsPersistent low mood; anhedonia; sleep disturbance (despite baby sleeping); appetite change; fatigue; guilt; poor concentration; suicidal thoughts; poor bonding
DifferentialBaby blues (3-5 days, self-limiting); anxiety; OCD; PTSD; thyroid dysfunction; anaemia; postnatal psychosis (psychotic features)
TreatmentStepped care: self-help, peer support; psychological therapies (CBT, IPT — first-line); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation)
SertralineFirst-line SSRI in pregnancy/lactation; lowest transfer to breast milk; data reassuring
AvoidParoxetine (1st-trimester cardiac risk); benzodiazepines (neonatal withdrawal, floppy baby); lithium (cardiac — monitor); valproate (developmental delay)
ECTFor severe, psychotic, or treatment-resistant depression; safe in pregnancy
Multidisciplinary careGP; HV; obstetrician; psychiatrist; perinatal mental health team; safeguarding; crisis plan

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antenatal and postnatal depression?

Antenatal (prenatal) and postnatal (postpartum) depression are common and treatable mood disorders affecting 10-15% of women in pregnancy and the year after birth. Distinguishing from normal postpartum adjustment ('baby blues'), anxiety, and postpartum psychosis is critical. Untreated illness has significant impact on mother, baby, and family.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antenatal and postnatal depression?

Key features include: Prevalence, Risk factors, Screening, Symptoms, Differential. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antenatal and postnatal depression diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antenatal and postnatal depression?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Antenatal and postnatal depression?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antenatal and postnatal depression — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Antenatal and postnatal depression?

A) Antenatal depression 10-15%; postnatal depression 10-15%; underdiagnosed

B) Prior depression (strongest); family history; lack of support; domestic violence; recent stress; teenage; substance use; social isolation

C) Whooley + GAD-2 at booking and 6 weeks; EPDS ≥13 or positive Q10 (suicidal ideation) prompts further assessment

D) Persistent low mood; anhedonia; sleep disturbance (despite baby sleeping); appetite change; fatigue; guilt; poor concentration; suicidal thoughts; poor bonding

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antenatal and postnatal depression?

A) Prior depression (strongest); family history; lack of support; domestic violence; recent stress; teenage; substance use; social isolation

B) Whooley + GAD-2 at booking and 6 weeks; EPDS ≥13 or positive Q10 (suicidal ideation) prompts further assessment

C) Persistent low mood; anhedonia; sleep disturbance (despite baby sleeping); appetite change; fatigue; guilt; poor concentration; suicidal thoughts; poor bonding

D) Baby blues (3-5 days, self-limiting); anxiety; OCD; PTSD; thyroid dysfunction; anaemia; postnatal psychosis (psychotic features)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antenatal and postnatal depression?

A) Whooley + GAD-2 at booking and 6 weeks; EPDS ≥13 or positive Q10 (suicidal ideation) prompts further assessment

B) Persistent low mood; anhedonia; sleep disturbance (despite baby sleeping); appetite change; fatigue; guilt; poor concentration; suicidal thoughts; poor bonding

C) Baby blues (3-5 days, self-limiting); anxiety; OCD; PTSD; thyroid dysfunction; anaemia; postnatal psychosis (psychotic features)

D) Stepped care: self-help, peer support; psychological therapies (CBT, IPT — first-line); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antenatal and postnatal depression?

A) Persistent low mood; anhedonia; sleep disturbance (despite baby sleeping); appetite change; fatigue; guilt; poor concentration; suicidal thoughts; poor bonding

B) Baby blues (3-5 days, self-limiting); anxiety; OCD; PTSD; thyroid dysfunction; anaemia; postnatal psychosis (psychotic features)

C) Stepped care: self-help, peer support; psychological therapies (CBT, IPT — first-line); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation)

D) First-line SSRI in pregnancy/lactation; lowest transfer to breast milk; data reassuring

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antenatal and postnatal depression?

A) Baby blues (3-5 days, self-limiting); anxiety; OCD; PTSD; thyroid dysfunction; anaemia; postnatal psychosis (psychotic features)

B) Stepped care: self-help, peer support; psychological therapies (CBT, IPT — first-line); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation)

C) First-line SSRI in pregnancy/lactation; lowest transfer to breast milk; data reassuring

D) Paroxetine (1st-trimester cardiac risk); benzodiazepines (neonatal withdrawal, floppy baby); lithium (cardiac — monitor); valproate (developmental delay)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antenatal and postnatal depression?

A) Stepped care: self-help, peer support; psychological therapies (CBT, IPT — first-line); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation)

C) Paroxetine (1st-trimester cardiac risk); benzodiazepines (neonatal withdrawal, floppy baby); lithium (cardiac — monitor); valproate (developmental delay)

Flashcards

Q: Prevalence?

A: Antenatal depression 10-15%; postnatal depression 10-15%; underdiagnosed

Q: Risk factors?

A: Prior depression (strongest); family history; lack of support; domestic violence; recent stress; teenage; substance use; social isolation

Q: Screening?

A: Whooley + GAD-2 at booking and 6 weeks; EPDS ≥13 or positive Q10 (suicidal ideation) prompts further assessment

Q: Symptoms?

A: Persistent low mood; anhedonia; sleep disturbance (despite baby sleeping); appetite change; fatigue; guilt; poor concentration; suicidal thoughts; poor bonding

Q: Differential?

A: Baby blues (3-5 days, self-limiting); anxiety; OCD; PTSD; thyroid dysfunction; anaemia; postnatal psychosis (psychotic features)

Q: Treatment?

A: Stepped care: self-help, peer support; psychological therapies (CBT, IPT — first-line); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation)

Q: Sertraline?

A: First-line SSRI in pregnancy/lactation; lowest transfer to breast milk; data reassuring

Q: Avoid?

A: Paroxetine (1st-trimester cardiac risk); benzodiazepines (neonatal withdrawal, floppy baby); lithium (cardiac — monitor); valproate (developmental delay)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.15.2 Perinatal Mental Health Disorders

Definition

Perinatal mental health disorders — including antenatal and postnatal depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD, postpartum psychosis, and tokophobia — are common and treatable but under-recognised. Untreated illness has significant impact on mother, baby, and family.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

Antenatal depression 10-15%; postnatal depression 10-15%; anxiety 15%; postpartum psychosis 0.1-0.2%

Risk factors

Prior mental illness (strongest); recent stressful events; lack of support; domestic abuse; teenage; substance use

Screening

Whooley questions + GAD-2 at booking and 6 weeks postnatal; Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS)

Postpartum depression

Onset within 4-6 weeks; persistent low mood, anhedonia, anxiety, guilt; suicidal ideation; impact on bonding

Postpartum psychosis

Onset within 2-4 weeks; mania, delusions, hallucinations, disorganised behaviour — psychiatric emergency

Anxiety disorders

Common; generalised anxiety, OCD (intrusive thoughts of harm to baby — ego-dystonic), PTSD (especially after traumatic birth)

Tokophobia

Severe fear of childbirth; consider delivery by caesarean if persistent

Treatment

Psychological therapies first-line (CBT, IPT); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation); ECT for severe/psychotic

Drug safety

Paroxetine (cardiac risk — 1st trimester); benzodiazepines (neonatal withdrawal); lithium (cardiac — monitor levels); valproate contraindicated

Multidisciplinary care

Perinatal mental health team; GP; HV; obstetrician; psychiatrist; crisis plan; safeguarding

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PrevalenceAntenatal depression 10-15%; postnatal depression 10-15%; anxiety 15%; postpartum psychosis 0.1-0.2%
Risk factorsPrior mental illness (strongest); recent stressful events; lack of support; domestic abuse; teenage; substance use
ScreeningWhooley questions + GAD-2 at booking and 6 weeks postnatal; Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS)
Postpartum depressionOnset within 4-6 weeks; persistent low mood, anhedonia, anxiety, guilt; suicidal ideation; impact on bonding
Postpartum psychosisOnset within 2-4 weeks; mania, delusions, hallucinations, disorganised behaviour — psychiatric emergency
Anxiety disordersCommon; generalised anxiety, OCD (intrusive thoughts of harm to baby — ego-dystonic), PTSD (especially after traumatic birth)
TokophobiaSevere fear of childbirth; consider delivery by caesarean if persistent
TreatmentPsychological therapies first-line (CBT, IPT); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation); ECT for severe/psychotic
Drug safetyParoxetine (cardiac risk — 1st trimester); benzodiazepines (neonatal withdrawal); lithium (cardiac — monitor levels); valproate contraindicated
Multidisciplinary carePerinatal mental health team; GP; HV; obstetrician; psychiatrist; crisis plan; safeguarding

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Perinatal mental health disorders?

Perinatal mental health disorders — including antenatal and postnatal depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD, postpartum psychosis, and tokophobia — are common and treatable but under-recognised. Untreated illness has significant impact on mother, baby, and family.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Perinatal mental health disorders?

Key features include: Prevalence, Risk factors, Screening, Postpartum depression, Postpartum psychosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Perinatal mental health disorders diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Perinatal mental health disorders?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Perinatal mental health disorders?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Perinatal mental health disorders — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)
