▸ 30.15.3 Postpartum Psychosis

Definition

Postpartum (puerperal) psychosis is a rare psychiatric emergency occurring within 2-4 weeks of delivery, affecting 0.1-0.2% of women. It presents with mania, delusions, hallucinations, and disorganised behaviour, and carries a high risk of suicide and infanticide. Rapid recognition, hospitalisation, and treatment are essential.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

1-2 per 1000 deliveries; psychiatric emergency

Onset

Within 2-4 weeks postpartum (most within 2 weeks); rapid onset over days

Risk factors

Prior postpartum psychosis (25-50% recurrence); bipolar disorder; prior psychosis; family history of bipolar/psychosis; first pregnancy; sleep deprivation

Presentation

Mania (elated, irritable, agitated); delusions (often related to baby — intruder, contamination, harm); hallucinations; disorganised behaviour; insomnia; poor insight

Differential

Severe postnatal depression with psychotic features (later onset, no elation); delirium (eclampsia, sepsis, drug-induced); bipolar relapse

Dangers

Suicide (5%); infanticide (4%); neglect of baby

Treatment

Urgent hospitalisation (often Mother and Baby Unit if available); antipsychotics (olanzapine, haloperidol); lithium (monitor levels); benzodiazepines; ECT if severe

Lithium

Effective but teratogenic (cardiac — Ebstein anomaly) and toxic in neonate; monitor levels; breastfeeding caution; restart in 2nd trimester if needed

Prognosis

Majority recover with treatment; 50% relapse in subsequent postpartum period; risk of bipolar diagnosis lifelong

Prevention (recurrence)

Early identification; lithium prophylaxis from delivery; supervised treatment; preconception planning

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence1-2 per 1000 deliveries; psychiatric emergency
OnsetWithin 2-4 weeks postpartum (most within 2 weeks); rapid onset over days
Risk factorsPrior postpartum psychosis (25-50% recurrence); bipolar disorder; prior psychosis; family history of bipolar/psychosis; first pregnancy; sleep deprivation
PresentationMania (elated, irritable, agitated); delusions (often related to baby — intruder, contamination, harm); hallucinations; disorganised behaviour; insomnia; poor insight
DifferentialSevere postnatal depression with psychotic features (later onset, no elation); delirium (eclampsia, sepsis, drug-induced); bipolar relapse
DangersSuicide (5%); infanticide (4%); neglect of baby
TreatmentUrgent hospitalisation (often Mother and Baby Unit if available); antipsychotics (olanzapine, haloperidol); lithium (monitor levels); benzodiazepines; ECT if severe
LithiumEffective but teratogenic (cardiac — Ebstein anomaly) and toxic in neonate; monitor levels; breastfeeding caution; restart in 2nd trimester if needed
PrognosisMajority recover with treatment; 50% relapse in subsequent postpartum period; risk of bipolar diagnosis lifelong
Prevention (recurrence)Early identification; lithium prophylaxis from delivery; supervised treatment; preconception planning

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Postpartum psychosis?

Postpartum (puerperal) psychosis is a rare psychiatric emergency occurring within 2-4 weeks of delivery, affecting 0.1-0.2% of women. It presents with mania, delusions, hallucinations, and disorganised behaviour, and carries a high risk of suicide and infanticide. Rapid recognition, hospitalisation, and treatment are essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Postpartum psychosis?

Key features include: Incidence, Onset, Risk factors, Presentation, Differential. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Postpartum psychosis diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Postpartum psychosis?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Postpartum psychosis?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Postpartum psychosis — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.15.4 Substance Misuse In Pregnancy

Definition

Substance misuse in pregnancy — alcohol, tobacco, opioids, stimulants, cannabis, and others — is associated with significant maternal, fetal, and neonatal harm. Identification (universal screening), brief intervention, harm reduction, and specialist multidisciplinary support improve outcomes for mother and baby.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

Alcohol 10-15%; smoking 10-15% (declining); cannabis 3-5%; opioids 0.5-1%; stimulants variable

Universal screening

Brief validated tools (T-ACE, AUDIT-C for alcohol; brief tobacco intervention); non-judgmental

Alcohol effects

Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD); IUGR; preterm; facial dysmorphism; CNS abnormalities; no safe threshold

Smoking effects

Placental abruption (2x); placenta praevia; IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; SIDS (2-3x); childhood respiratory disease; OME

Opioid effects

Neonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS); IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; maternal overdose; HIV/HCV transmission (IV use)

Stimulants (cocaine, amphetamines)

Placental abruption; preterm; IUGR; congenital anomalies; neonatal withdrawal; SIDS

Cannabis

IUGR; preterm; childhood cognitive/behavioural issues; emerging data on neurodevelopment

Management

Brief intervention; motivational interviewing; referral to addiction services; harm reduction; supervised OST (methadone or buprenorphine) for opioids; nicotine replacement safe

Labour and delivery

Avoid pethidine in opioid users (precipitates withdrawal); pain control often higher; monitor for withdrawal; NAS monitoring of neonate

Postpartum

Safeguarding; social services; contraception; LARC; relapse prevention; peer support

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PrevalenceAlcohol 10-15%; smoking 10-15% (declining); cannabis 3-5%; opioids 0.5-1%; stimulants variable
Universal screeningBrief validated tools (T-ACE, AUDIT-C for alcohol; brief tobacco intervention); non-judgmental
Alcohol effectsFetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD); IUGR; preterm; facial dysmorphism; CNS abnormalities; no safe threshold
Smoking effectsPlacental abruption (2x); placenta praevia; IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; SIDS (2-3x); childhood respiratory disease; OME
Opioid effectsNeonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS); IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; maternal overdose; HIV/HCV transmission (IV use)
Stimulants (cocaine, amphetamines)Placental abruption; preterm; IUGR; congenital anomalies; neonatal withdrawal; SIDS
CannabisIUGR; preterm; childhood cognitive/behavioural issues; emerging data on neurodevelopment
ManagementBrief intervention; motivational interviewing; referral to addiction services; harm reduction; supervised OST (methadone or buprenorphine) for opioids; nicotine replacement safe
Labour and deliveryAvoid pethidine in opioid users (precipitates withdrawal); pain control often higher; monitor for withdrawal; NAS monitoring of neonate
PostpartumSafeguarding; social services; contraception; LARC; relapse prevention; peer support

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Substance misuse in pregnancy?

Substance misuse in pregnancy — alcohol, tobacco, opioids, stimulants, cannabis, and others — is associated with significant maternal, fetal, and neonatal harm. Identification (universal screening), brief intervention, harm reduction, and specialist multidisciplinary support improve outcomes for mother and baby.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Substance misuse in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Universal screening, Alcohol effects, Smoking effects, Opioid effects. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Substance misuse in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Substance misuse in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Substance misuse in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Substance misuse in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Flashcards

Q: Prevalence?

A: Alcohol 10-15%; smoking 10-15% (declining); cannabis 3-5%; opioids 0.5-1%; stimulants variable

Q: Universal screening?

A: Brief validated tools (T-ACE, AUDIT-C for alcohol; brief tobacco intervention); non-judgmental

Q: Alcohol effects?

A: Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD); IUGR; preterm; facial dysmorphism; CNS abnormalities; no safe threshold

Q: Smoking effects?

A: Placental abruption (2x); placenta praevia; IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; SIDS (2-3x); childhood respiratory disease; OME

Q: Opioid effects?

A: Neonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS); IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; maternal overdose; HIV/HCV transmission (IV use)

Q: Stimulants (cocaine, amphetamines)?

A: Placental abruption; preterm; IUGR; congenital anomalies; neonatal withdrawal; SIDS

Q: Cannabis?

A: IUGR; preterm; childhood cognitive/behavioural issues; emerging data on neurodevelopment

Q: Management?

A: Brief intervention; motivational interviewing; referral to addiction services; harm reduction; supervised OST (methadone or buprenorphine) for opioids; nicotine replacement safe

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Substance misuse in pregnancy?
  2. A — Key feature of Substance misuse in pregnancy?
  3. A — Key feature of Substance misuse in pregnancy?
  4. A — Key feature of Substance misuse in pregnancy?
  5. A — Key feature of Substance misuse in pregnancy?
  6. A — Key feature of Substance misuse in pregnancy?
  7. A — Key feature of Substance misuse in pregnancy?
  8. A — Key feature of Substance misuse in pregnancy?
  9. A — Key feature of Substance misuse in pregnancy?
  10. A — Key feature of Substance misuse in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. Alcohol 10-15%; smoking 10-15% (declining); cannabis 3-5%; opioids 0.5-1%; stimulants variable
  2. Brief validated tools (T-ACE, AUDIT-C for alcohol; brief tobacco intervention); non-judgmental
  3. Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD); IUGR; preterm; facial dysmorphism; CNS abnormalities; no safe threshold
  4. Placental abruption (2x); placenta praevia; IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; SIDS (2-3x); childhood respiratory disease; OME
  5. Neonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS); IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; maternal overdose; HIV/HCV transmission (IV use)
  6. Placental abruption; preterm; IUGR; congenital anomalies; neonatal withdrawal; SIDS
  7. IUGR; preterm; childhood cognitive/behavioural issues; emerging data on neurodevelopment
  8. Brief intervention; motivational interviewing; referral to addiction services; harm reduction; supervised OST (methadone or buprenorphine) for opioids; nicotine replacement safe
  9. Avoid pethidine in opioid users (precipitates withdrawal); pain control often higher; monitor for withdrawal; NAS monitoring of neonate
  10. Safeguarding; social services; contraception; LARC; relapse prevention; peer support

▸ 30.16 Multiple Pregnancy and Obstetric Disorders 2
▸ 30.16.1 Multiple Pregnancy Twins And Higher Order

Definition

Multiple pregnancy — most commonly dichorionic diamniotic (DCDA) twins but also monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA), monochorionic monoamniotic (MCMA), and higher-order — carries significantly higher maternal and fetal risks. Specialist care is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

1 in 80 pregnancies; rising with assisted conception and maternal age

Zygosity

DCDA — 2 placentas, 2 sacs; MCDA — 1 placenta, 2 sacs; MCMA — 1 placenta, 1 sac (rare, high risk)

Diagnosis

First-trimester USS — chorionicity determined by lambda/twin-peak sign (DCDA) vs T-sign (MCMA/MCDA)

Maternal risks

Hyperemesis; anaemia; pre-eclampsia (2-3x); GDM; postpartum haemorrhage; VTE

Fetal risks (DCDA)

Preterm 50%; IUGR; discordant growth; twin-twin transfusion; stillbirth

Fetal risks (MCMA/MCDA)

Twin-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) 10-15%; cord entanglement; selective growth restriction; TRAP sequence

Antenatal care

DCDA — every 4 weeks to 36; MCDA — every 2 weeks from 16; USS growth and Doppler; screen for TTTS

TTTS management

Laser ablation of placental anastomoses; amnioreduction; selective feticide in severe cases

Timing of delivery

DCDA 37 weeks; MCDA 36-37; MCMA 32-34 (inpatient monitoring from 26-28 weeks)

Mode of delivery

DCDA — vaginal if cephalic/cephalic; MCMA — caesarean; MCDA depends on presentation

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence1 in 80 pregnancies; rising with assisted conception and maternal age
ZygosityDCDA — 2 placentas, 2 sacs; MCDA — 1 placenta, 2 sacs; MCMA — 1 placenta, 1 sac (rare, high risk)
DiagnosisFirst-trimester USS — chorionicity determined by lambda/twin-peak sign (DCDA) vs T-sign (MCMA/MCDA)
Maternal risksHyperemesis; anaemia; pre-eclampsia (2-3x); GDM; postpartum haemorrhage; VTE
Fetal risks (DCDA)Preterm 50%; IUGR; discordant growth; twin-twin transfusion; stillbirth
Fetal risks (MCMA/MCDA)Twin-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) 10-15%; cord entanglement; selective growth restriction; TRAP sequence
Antenatal careDCDA — every 4 weeks to 36; MCDA — every 2 weeks from 16; USS growth and Doppler; screen for TTTS
TTTS managementLaser ablation of placental anastomoses; amnioreduction; selective feticide in severe cases
Timing of deliveryDCDA 37 weeks; MCDA 36-37; MCMA 32-34 (inpatient monitoring from 26-28 weeks)
Mode of deliveryDCDA — vaginal if cephalic/cephalic; MCMA — caesarean; MCDA depends on presentation

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order)?

Multiple pregnancy — most commonly dichorionic diamniotic (DCDA) twins but also monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA), monochorionic monoamniotic (MCMA), and higher-order — carries significantly higher maternal and fetal risks. Specialist care is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order)?

Key features include: Prevalence, Zygosity, Diagnosis, Maternal risks, Fetal risks (DCDA). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Mind Map

Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order)

Q: Prevalence?

A: 1 in 80 pregnancies; rising with assisted conception and maternal age

Q: Zygosity?

A: DCDA — 2 placentas, 2 sacs; MCDA — 1 placenta, 2 sacs; MCMA — 1 placenta, 1 sac (rare, high risk)

Q: Diagnosis?

A: First-trimester USS — chorionicity determined by lambda/twin-peak sign (DCDA) vs T-sign (MCMA/MCDA)

Q: Maternal risks?

A: Hyperemesis; anaemia; pre-eclampsia (2-3x); GDM; postpartum haemorrhage; VTE

Q: Fetal risks (DCDA)?

A: Preterm 50%; IUGR; discordant growth; twin-twin transfusion; stillbirth

Q: Fetal risks (MCMA/MCDA)?

A: Twin-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) 10-15%; cord entanglement; selective growth restriction; TRAP sequence

Q: Antenatal care?

A: DCDA — every 4 weeks to 36; MCDA — every 2 weeks from 16; USS growth and Doppler; screen for TTTS

Q: TTTS management?

A: Laser ablation of placental anastomoses; amnioreduction; selective feticide in severe cases

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.16.2 Preterm Labour And Birth

Definition

Preterm labour is onset of regular uterine contractions with cervical change before 37 weeks' gestation. Preterm birth is the leading cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity. Prevention (progesterone, cerclage) and management (tocolysis, corticosteroids, magnesium) are key.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Delivery before 37 weeks; very preterm <32; extremely preterm <28

Prevalence

7-10% of births; rising; leading cause of neonatal mortality

Risk factors

Prior PTB (strongest); multiple pregnancy; infection (UTI, chorioamnionitis); cervical insufficiency; smoking; substance use; short cervix

Prediction

Cervical length USS 16-24 weeks; short cervix <25 mm at 24 weeks — intervention

Prevention

Progesterone (vaginal 200 mg nocte) for short cervix or prior PTB; cerclage for prior PTB + short cervix; treat infection

Corticosteroids

Betamethasone 12 mg IM 24 h apart or dexamethasone 6 mg IM 12 h × 4 — between 24-34 weeks; reduces RDS, IVH, NEC, death

Tocolysis

Nifedipine (first-line); atosiban; indomethacin (<32 weeks only); beta-agonists; allow 48 h for steroids

Magnesium sulphate

For neuroprotection if delivery <30 weeks; 4 g IV loading

Antibiotics

For group B Strep prophylaxis if previous GBS, GBS in current pregnancy, or unknown status in preterm labour

Mode of delivery

Vaginal if cephalic and reassuring; caesarean for breech, fetal distress, or obstetric indication

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionDelivery before 37 weeks; very preterm <32; extremely preterm <28
Prevalence7-10% of births; rising; leading cause of neonatal mortality
Risk factorsPrior PTB (strongest); multiple pregnancy; infection (UTI, chorioamnionitis); cervical insufficiency; smoking; substance use; short cervix
PredictionCervical length USS 16-24 weeks; short cervix <25 mm at 24 weeks — intervention
PreventionProgesterone (vaginal 200 mg nocte) for short cervix or prior PTB; cerclage for prior PTB + short cervix; treat infection
CorticosteroidsBetamethasone 12 mg IM 24 h apart or dexamethasone 6 mg IM 12 h × 4 — between 24-34 weeks; reduces RDS, IVH, NEC, death
TocolysisNifedipine (first-line); atosiban; indomethacin (<32 weeks only); beta-agonists; allow 48 h for steroids
Magnesium sulphateFor neuroprotection if delivery <30 weeks; 4 g IV loading
AntibioticsFor group B Strep prophylaxis if previous GBS, GBS in current pregnancy, or unknown status in preterm labour
Mode of deliveryVaginal if cephalic and reassuring; caesarean for breech, fetal distress, or obstetric indication

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Preterm labour and birth?

Preterm labour is onset of regular uterine contractions with cervical change before 37 weeks' gestation. Preterm birth is the leading cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity. Prevention (progesterone, cerclage) and management (tocolysis, corticosteroids, magnesium) are key.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Preterm labour and birth?

Key features include: Definition, Prevalence, Risk factors, Prediction, Prevention. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Preterm labour and birth diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Preterm labour and birth?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Preterm labour and birth?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Preterm labour and birth — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. Which of the following is true about Preterm labour and birth?

A) For neuroprotection if delivery <30 weeks; 4 g IV loading

B) For group B Strep prophylaxis if previous GBS, GBS in current pregnancy, or unknown status in preterm labour

C) Vaginal if cephalic and reassuring; caesarean for breech, fetal distress, or obstetric indication

D) Delivery before 37 weeks; very preterm <32; extremely preterm <28

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Preterm labour and birth?

A) For group B Strep prophylaxis if previous GBS, GBS in current pregnancy, or unknown status in preterm labour

B) Vaginal if cephalic and reassuring; caesarean for breech, fetal distress, or obstetric indication

C) Delivery before 37 weeks; very preterm <32; extremely preterm <28

D) 7-10% of births; rising; leading cause of neonatal mortality

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Preterm labour and birth?

A) Vaginal if cephalic and reassuring; caesarean for breech, fetal distress, or obstetric indication

B) Delivery before 37 weeks; very preterm <32; extremely preterm <28

C) 7-10% of births; rising; leading cause of neonatal mortality

D) Prior PTB (strongest); multiple pregnancy; infection (UTI, chorioamnionitis); cervical insufficiency; smoking; substance use; short cervix

Answer: A

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Preterm labour and birth. Most appropriate next step in management? | Delivery before 37 weeks; very preterm <32; extremely preterm <28
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Preterm labour and birth. Most appropriate next step in management? | 7-10% of births; rising; leading cause of neonatal mortality
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Preterm labour and birth. Most appropriate next step in management? | Prior PTB (strongest); multiple pregnancy; infection (UTI, chorioamnionitis); cervical insufficiency; smoking; substance use; short cervix
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Preterm labour and birth. Most appropriate next step in management? | Cervical length USS 16-24 weeks; short cervix <25 mm at 24 weeks — intervention
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Preterm labour and birth. Most appropriate next step in management? | Progesterone (vaginal 200 mg nocte) for short cervix or prior PTB; cerclage for prior PTB + short cervix; treat infection
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Preterm labour and birth. Most appropriate next step in management? | Betamethasone 12 mg IM 24 h apart or dexamethasone 6 mg IM 12 h × 4 — between 24-34 weeks; reduces RDS, IVH, NEC, death
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Preterm labour and birth. Most appropriate next step in management? | Nifedipine (first-line); atosiban; indomethacin (<32 weeks only); beta-agonists; allow 48 h for steroids
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Preterm labour and birth. Most appropriate next step in management? | For neuroprotection if delivery <30 weeks; 4 g IV loading
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Preterm labour and birth. Most appropriate next step in management? | For group B Strep prophylaxis if previous GBS, GBS in current pregnancy, or unknown status in preterm labour
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Preterm labour and birth. Most appropriate next step in management? | Vaginal if cephalic and reassuring; caesarean for breech, fetal distress, or obstetric indication

Flashcards

Q: Definition?

A: Delivery before 37 weeks; very preterm <32; extremely preterm <28

Q: Prevalence?

A: 7-10% of births; rising; leading cause of neonatal mortality

Q: Risk factors?

A: Prior PTB (strongest); multiple pregnancy; infection (UTI, chorioamnionitis); cervical insufficiency; smoking; substance use; short cervix

Q: Prediction?

A: Cervical length USS 16-24 weeks; short cervix <25 mm at 24 weeks — intervention

Q: Prevention?

A: Progesterone (vaginal 200 mg nocte) for short cervix or prior PTB; cerclage for prior PTB + short cervix; treat infection

Q: Corticosteroids?

A: Betamethasone 12 mg IM 24 h apart or dexamethasone 6 mg IM 12 h × 4 — between 24-34 weeks; reduces RDS, IVH, NEC, death

Q: Tocolysis?

A: Nifedipine (first-line); atosiban; indomethacin (<32 weeks only); beta-agonists; allow 48 h for steroids

Q: Magnesium sulphate?

A: For neuroprotection if delivery <30 weeks; 4 g IV loading

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  10. A — Key feature of Preterm labour and birth?

SBAs

  1. Delivery before 37 weeks; very preterm <32; extremely preterm <28
  2. 7-10% of births; rising; leading cause of neonatal mortality
  3. Prior PTB (strongest); multiple pregnancy; infection (UTI, chorioamnionitis); cervical insufficiency; smoking; substance use; short cervix
  4. Cervical length USS 16-24 weeks; short cervix <25 mm at 24 weeks — intervention
  5. Progesterone (vaginal 200 mg nocte) for short cervix or prior PTB; cerclage for prior PTB + short cervix; treat infection
  6. Betamethasone 12 mg IM 24 h apart or dexamethasone 6 mg IM 12 h × 4 — between 24-34 weeks; reduces RDS, IVH, NEC, death
  7. Nifedipine (first-line); atosiban; indomethacin (<32 weeks only); beta-agonists; allow 48 h for steroids
  8. For neuroprotection if delivery <30 weeks; 4 g IV loading
  9. For group B Strep prophylaxis if previous GBS, GBS in current pregnancy, or unknown status in preterm labour
  10. Vaginal if cephalic and reassuring; caesarean for breech, fetal distress, or obstetric indication

▸ 30.17 Neurological Disease in Pregnancy 7
▸ 30.17.1 Bell S Palsy And Cranial Neuropathies In Pregnancy

Definition

Bell's palsy (idiopathic facial nerve palsy) is more common in pregnancy (3x) and the puerperium. It presents with sudden unilateral lower motor neuron facial weakness. Most cases resolve spontaneously; treatment with corticosteroids improves outcomes and is safe in pregnancy.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

1 in 2000-6000 pregnancies; 3x non-pregnant; peak in 3rd trimester and 1st postpartum week

Aetiology

Oedema of facial nerve in fallopian canal; viral (HSV, HZV) association; hypertension; pre-eclampsia risk factor

Presentation

Sudden unilateral facial weakness (forehead involved); hyperacusis; loss of taste anterior 2/3 tongue; decreased lacrimation; post-auricular pain

Differential

Stroke (forehead spared); Ramsay Hunt (VZV); Lyme; otitis media; parotid tumour; Guillain-Barré

Investigations

Clinical diagnosis; consider neuroimaging if atypical (forehead spared, progressive, recurrent); audiometry if indicated

Treatment

Prednisolone 50 mg × 10 days within 72 h of onset; eye protection (artificial tears, tape at night); antivirals (valaciclovir) if severe

Prognosis

85% complete recovery within 3 months; 15% have residual weakness; synkinesis; recurrence 5-10% subsequent pregnancies

Eye care

Critical to prevent exposure keratopathy; lubricate; taping at night; ophthalmology review if corneal exposure

Other cranial neuropathies

Trigeminal neuralgia (carbamazepine caution); optic neuritis (MS-related); vestibular schwannoma (monitor)

Postpartum

Monitor infant if on prednisolone; breastfeeding compatible; review at 3 months

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Epidemiology1 in 2000-6000 pregnancies; 3x non-pregnant; peak in 3rd trimester and 1st postpartum week
AetiologyOedema of facial nerve in fallopian canal; viral (HSV, HZV) association; hypertension; pre-eclampsia risk factor
PresentationSudden unilateral facial weakness (forehead involved); hyperacusis; loss of taste anterior 2/3 tongue; decreased lacrimation; post-auricular pain
DifferentialStroke (forehead spared); Ramsay Hunt (VZV); Lyme; otitis media; parotid tumour; Guillain-Barré
InvestigationsClinical diagnosis; consider neuroimaging if atypical (forehead spared, progressive, recurrent); audiometry if indicated
TreatmentPrednisolone 50 mg × 10 days within 72 h of onset; eye protection (artificial tears, tape at night); antivirals (valaciclovir) if severe
Prognosis85% complete recovery within 3 months; 15% have residual weakness; synkinesis; recurrence 5-10% subsequent pregnancies
Eye careCritical to prevent exposure keratopathy; lubricate; taping at night; ophthalmology review if corneal exposure
Other cranial neuropathiesTrigeminal neuralgia (carbamazepine caution); optic neuritis (MS-related); vestibular schwannoma (monitor)
PostpartumMonitor infant if on prednisolone; breastfeeding compatible; review at 3 months

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy?

Bell's palsy (idiopathic facial nerve palsy) is more common in pregnancy (3x) and the puerperium. It presents with sudden unilateral lower motor neuron facial weakness. Most cases resolve spontaneously; treatment with corticosteroids improves outcomes and is safe in pregnancy.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Aetiology, Presentation, Differential, Investigations. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.17.2 Epilepsy In Pregnancy

Definition

Epilepsy affects 0.5-1% of pregnant women. Most anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs) are teratogenic to varying degrees. Preconception counselling, monotherapy at the lowest effective dose with high-dose folic acid, and specialist antenatal care optimise outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

0.5-1% of pregnancies; AEDs in 0.3-0.5%

Preconception

Switch to lowest-risk AED (lamotrigine/levetiracetam); monotherapy; lowest effective dose; folic acid 5 mg

Teratogenicity

Sodium valproate highest (10% major malformations, neurodevelopmental delay); carbimazole; phenytoin; phenobarbital

Safer AEDs

Lamotrigine and levetiracetam — lowest risk; monitor levels in 2nd/3rd trimesters (lamotrigine clearance rises)

Fetal risks

Major malformations (4-10% with valproate); NTDs (valproate, carbamazepine); fetal AED syndrome

Maternal risks

Increased seizure frequency in 30% (hormones, sleep deprivation, drug levels); SUDEP

Antenatal care

AED levels each trimester; anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; alpha-fetoprotein; targeted USS

Labour and delivery

Continue AEDs; IM vitamin K for neonate; avoid pethidine (metabolite lowers threshold); avoid dehydration/sleep deprivation

Postpartum

AED levels may rise — reduce dose if toxicity; breastfeeding usually safe; contraception (avoid COCP with enzyme inducers)

Counselling

Avoid valproate in women of childbearing age if possible; use LARC; emergency contraception unaffected

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence0.5-1% of pregnancies; AEDs in 0.3-0.5%
PreconceptionSwitch to lowest-risk AED (lamotrigine/levetiracetam); monotherapy; lowest effective dose; folic acid 5 mg
TeratogenicitySodium valproate highest (10% major malformations, neurodevelopmental delay); carbimazole; phenytoin; phenobarbital
Safer AEDsLamotrigine and levetiracetam — lowest risk; monitor levels in 2nd/3rd trimesters (lamotrigine clearance rises)
Fetal risksMajor malformations (4-10% with valproate); NTDs (valproate, carbamazepine); fetal AED syndrome
Maternal risksIncreased seizure frequency in 30% (hormones, sleep deprivation, drug levels); SUDEP
Antenatal careAED levels each trimester; anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; alpha-fetoprotein; targeted USS
Labour and deliveryContinue AEDs; IM vitamin K for neonate; avoid pethidine (metabolite lowers threshold); avoid dehydration/sleep deprivation
PostpartumAED levels may rise — reduce dose if toxicity; breastfeeding usually safe; contraception (avoid COCP with enzyme inducers)
CounsellingAvoid valproate in women of childbearing age if possible; use LARC; emergency contraception unaffected

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Epilepsy in pregnancy?

Epilepsy affects 0.5-1% of pregnant women. Most anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs) are teratogenic to varying degrees. Preconception counselling, monotherapy at the lowest effective dose with high-dose folic acid, and specialist antenatal care optimise outcomes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Epilepsy in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Preconception, Teratogenicity, Safer AEDs, Fetal risks. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Epilepsy in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Epilepsy in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Epilepsy in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Epilepsy in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Epilepsy in pregnancy?

A) 0.5-1% of pregnancies; AEDs in 0.3-0.5%

B) Switch to lowest-risk AED (lamotrigine/levetiracetam); monotherapy; lowest effective dose; folic acid 5 mg

C) Sodium valproate highest (10% major malformations, neurodevelopmental delay); carbimazole; phenytoin; phenobarbital

D) Lamotrigine and levetiracetam — lowest risk; monitor levels in 2nd/3rd trimesters (lamotrigine clearance rises)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Epilepsy in pregnancy?

A) Switch to lowest-risk AED (lamotrigine/levetiracetam); monotherapy; lowest effective dose; folic acid 5 mg

B) Sodium valproate highest (10% major malformations, neurodevelopmental delay); carbimazole; phenytoin; phenobarbital

C) Lamotrigine and levetiracetam — lowest risk; monitor levels in 2nd/3rd trimesters (lamotrigine clearance rises)

D) Major malformations (4-10% with valproate); NTDs (valproate, carbamazepine); fetal AED syndrome

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Epilepsy in pregnancy?

A) Sodium valproate highest (10% major malformations, neurodevelopmental delay); carbimazole; phenytoin; phenobarbital

B) Lamotrigine and levetiracetam — lowest risk; monitor levels in 2nd/3rd trimesters (lamotrigine clearance rises)

C) Major malformations (4-10% with valproate); NTDs (valproate, carbamazepine); fetal AED syndrome

D) Increased seizure frequency in 30% (hormones, sleep deprivation, drug levels); SUDEP

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Epilepsy in pregnancy?

A) Lamotrigine and levetiracetam — lowest risk; monitor levels in 2nd/3rd trimesters (lamotrigine clearance rises)

B) Major malformations (4-10% with valproate); NTDs (valproate, carbamazepine); fetal AED syndrome

C) Increased seizure frequency in 30% (hormones, sleep deprivation, drug levels); SUDEP

D) AED levels each trimester; anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; alpha-fetoprotein; targeted USS

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Epilepsy in pregnancy?

A) Major malformations (4-10% with valproate); NTDs (valproate, carbamazepine); fetal AED syndrome

B) Increased seizure frequency in 30% (hormones, sleep deprivation, drug levels); SUDEP

C) AED levels each trimester; anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; alpha-fetoprotein; targeted USS

D) Continue AEDs; IM vitamin K for neonate; avoid pethidine (metabolite lowers threshold); avoid dehydration/sleep deprivation

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Epilepsy in pregnancy?

A) Increased seizure frequency in 30% (hormones, sleep deprivation, drug levels); SUDEP

B) AED levels each trimester; anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; alpha-fetoprotein; targeted USS

C) Continue AEDs; IM vitamin K for neonate; avoid pethidine (metabolite lowers threshold); avoid dehydration/sleep deprivation

D) AED levels may rise — reduce dose if toxicity; breastfeeding usually safe; contraception (avoid COCP with enzyme inducers)

Answer: A

C) AED levels may rise — reduce dose if toxicity; breastfeeding usually safe; contraception (avoid COCP with enzyme inducers)

▸ 30.17.3 Headache In Pregnancy

Definition

Headache is the most common neurological symptom in pregnancy. Most are benign (tension, migraine), but pregnancy-specific and serious causes include pre-eclampsia, subarachnoid haemorrhage, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, and pituitary apoplexy. New severe headache needs urgent evaluation.

Core Clinical Content

Common causes

Tension (most common); migraine (improves in 60%); sinusitis; neck pain

Red flags

Thunderclap onset; worst-ever; new after 20 weeks; fever; focal deficits; seizures; post-partum onset; papilloedema

Migraine

Often improves in 2nd/3rd trimesters (rising oestrogen); aura unchanged; severe — exclude CVST

Pre-eclampsia headache

Bifrontal, throbbing, worse in morning; new after 20 weeks; check BP, urate, urine PCR

Subarachnoid haemorrhage

Thunderclap; vomiting; photophobia; neck stiffness; CT then LP if negative

CVST

Headache progressive over days; seizures; focal deficits; postpartum; risk factors — dehydration, infection, prothrombotic

Pituitary apoplexy

Sudden severe headache; visual loss; ophthalmoplegia; cortisol deficiency — emergency

Imaging

MRI without contrast preferred; CT if MRI unavailable (low fetal dose)

Treatment migraine

Paracetamol 1st; ibuprofen 2nd trimester; triptans limited data; metoclopramide; avoid ergot

Prevention

Avoid triggers; propranolol safe; topiramate teratogenic

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Common causesTension (most common); migraine (improves in 60%); sinusitis; neck pain
Red flagsThunderclap onset; worst-ever; new after 20 weeks; fever; focal deficits; seizures; post-partum onset; papilloedema
MigraineOften improves in 2nd/3rd trimesters (rising oestrogen); aura unchanged; severe — exclude CVST
Pre-eclampsia headacheBifrontal, throbbing, worse in morning; new after 20 weeks; check BP, urate, urine PCR
Subarachnoid haemorrhageThunderclap; vomiting; photophobia; neck stiffness; CT then LP if negative
CVSTHeadache progressive over days; seizures; focal deficits; postpartum; risk factors — dehydration, infection, prothrombotic
Pituitary apoplexySudden severe headache; visual loss; ophthalmoplegia; cortisol deficiency — emergency
ImagingMRI without contrast preferred; CT if MRI unavailable (low fetal dose)
Treatment migraineParacetamol 1st; ibuprofen 2nd trimester; triptans limited data; metoclopramide; avoid ergot
PreventionAvoid triggers; propranolol safe; topiramate teratogenic

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Headache in pregnancy?

Headache is the most common neurological symptom in pregnancy. Most are benign (tension, migraine), but pregnancy-specific and serious causes include pre-eclampsia, subarachnoid haemorrhage, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, and pituitary apoplexy. New severe headache needs urgent evaluation.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Headache in pregnancy?

Key features include: Common causes, Red flags, Migraine, Pre-eclampsia headache, Subarachnoid haemorrhage. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Headache in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Headache in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Headache in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Headache in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.17.4 Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension In Pregnancy

Definition

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH, pseudotumour cerebri) in pregnancy typically presents with headache, visual disturbances, and papilloedema. It is more common in obese women of childbearing age. Management is conservative in mild cases; acetazolamide and surgical intervention may be needed for progressive visual loss.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Commoner in obese women of childbearing years; incidence 0.9-2.2 per 100,000; rising with obesity

Pathophysiology

Impaired CSF absorption; raised intracranial pressure without mass lesion or hydrocephalus

Presentation

Headache (worse lying down/Valsalva); pulsatile tinnitus; transient visual obscurations; diplopia (CN VI palsy); visual loss; papilloedema

Investigations

Fundoscopy; visual fields; CT/MRI (rule out mass/venous sinus thrombosis); LP with opening pressure >25 cmH2O; CSF normal composition

Differential

Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (pregnancy risk); pre-eclampsia; hypertensive encephalopathy; intracranial mass

Treatment

Weight management; acetazolamide (250-500 mg BD; limited data in pregnancy — case-by-case); topiramate (avoid — teratogenic); FBP monitoring

Surgical

Optic nerve sheath fenestration (vision loss); VP shunt; LP shunt; venous sinus stenting (selected)

Repeated LP

Therapeutic LPs to reduce pressure; not definitive

Pregnancy considerations

Avoid dehydration; consider delivery route (assisted second stage to avoid Valsalva); mode of delivery per obstetric indication

Postpartum

Symptoms often improve; continue monitoring; preconception weight loss; consider bariatric surgery; recurrence common

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyCommoner in obese women of childbearing years; incidence 0.9-2.2 per 100,000; rising with obesity
PathophysiologyImpaired CSF absorption; raised intracranial pressure without mass lesion or hydrocephalus
PresentationHeadache (worse lying down/Valsalva); pulsatile tinnitus; transient visual obscurations; diplopia (CN VI palsy); visual loss; papilloedema
InvestigationsFundoscopy; visual fields; CT/MRI (rule out mass/venous sinus thrombosis); LP with opening pressure >25 cmH2O; CSF normal composition
DifferentialCerebral venous sinus thrombosis (pregnancy risk); pre-eclampsia; hypertensive encephalopathy; intracranial mass
TreatmentWeight management; acetazolamide (250-500 mg BD; limited data in pregnancy — case-by-case); topiramate (avoid — teratogenic); FBP monitoring
SurgicalOptic nerve sheath fenestration (vision loss); VP shunt; LP shunt; venous sinus stenting (selected)
Repeated LPTherapeutic LPs to reduce pressure; not definitive
Pregnancy considerationsAvoid dehydration; consider delivery route (assisted second stage to avoid Valsalva); mode of delivery per obstetric indication
PostpartumSymptoms often improve; continue monitoring; preconception weight loss; consider bariatric surgery; recurrence common

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH, pseudotumour cerebri) in pregnancy typically presents with headache, visual disturbances, and papilloedema. It is more common in obese women of childbearing age. Management is conservative in mild cases; acetazolamide and surgical intervention may be needed for progressive visual loss.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, Presentation, Investigations, Differential. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

A) Commoner in obese women of childbearing years; incidence 0.9-2.2 per 100,000; rising with obesity

B) Impaired CSF absorption; raised intracranial pressure without mass lesion or hydrocephalus

C) Headache (worse lying down/Valsalva); pulsatile tinnitus; transient visual obscurations; diplopia (CN VI palsy); visual loss; papilloedema

D) Fundoscopy; visual fields; CT/MRI (rule out mass/venous sinus thrombosis); LP with opening pressure >25 cmH2O; CSF normal composition

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

A) Impaired CSF absorption; raised intracranial pressure without mass lesion or hydrocephalus

B) Headache (worse lying down/Valsalva); pulsatile tinnitus; transient visual obscurations; diplopia (CN VI palsy); visual loss; papilloedema

C) Fundoscopy; visual fields; CT/MRI (rule out mass/venous sinus thrombosis); LP with opening pressure >25 cmH2O; CSF normal composition

D) Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (pregnancy risk); pre-eclampsia; hypertensive encephalopathy; intracranial mass

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

A) Headache (worse lying down/Valsalva); pulsatile tinnitus; transient visual obscurations; diplopia (CN VI palsy); visual loss; papilloedema

B) Fundoscopy; visual fields; CT/MRI (rule out mass/venous sinus thrombosis); LP with opening pressure >25 cmH2O; CSF normal composition

C) Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (pregnancy risk); pre-eclampsia; hypertensive encephalopathy; intracranial mass

D) Weight management; acetazolamide (250-500 mg BD; limited data in pregnancy — case-by-case); topiramate (avoid — teratogenic); FBP monitoring

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

A) Fundoscopy; visual fields; CT/MRI (rule out mass/venous sinus thrombosis); LP with opening pressure >25 cmH2O; CSF normal composition

B) Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (pregnancy risk); pre-eclampsia; hypertensive encephalopathy; intracranial mass

C) Weight management; acetazolamide (250-500 mg BD; limited data in pregnancy — case-by-case); topiramate (avoid — teratogenic); FBP monitoring

D) Optic nerve sheath fenestration (vision loss); VP shunt; LP shunt; venous sinus stenting (selected)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

A) Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (pregnancy risk); pre-eclampsia; hypertensive encephalopathy; intracranial mass

B) Weight management; acetazolamide (250-500 mg BD; limited data in pregnancy — case-by-case); topiramate (avoid — teratogenic); FBP monitoring

C) Optic nerve sheath fenestration (vision loss); VP shunt; LP shunt; venous sinus stenting (selected)

D) Therapeutic LPs to reduce pressure; not definitive

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

A) Weight management; acetazolamide (250-500 mg BD; limited data in pregnancy — case-by-case); topiramate (avoid — teratogenic); FBP monitoring

B) Optic nerve sheath fenestration (vision loss); VP shunt; LP shunt; venous sinus stenting (selected)

C) Therapeutic LPs to reduce pressure; not definitive

D) Avoid dehydration; consider delivery route (assisted second stage to avoid Valsalva); mode of delivery per obstetric indication

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in pregnancy?

A) Optic nerve sheath fenestration (vision loss); VP shunt; LP shunt; venous sinus stenting (selected)

B) Therapeutic LPs to reduce pressure; not definitive

C) Avoid dehydration; consider delivery route (assisted second stage to avoid Valsalva); mode of delivery per obstetric indication

D) Symptoms often improve; continue monitoring; preconception weight loss; consider bariatric surgery; recurrence common

Answer: A

Flashcards

Q: Epidemiology?

A: Commoner in obese women of childbearing years; incidence 0.9-2.2 per 100,000; rising with obesity

Q: Pathophysiology?

A: Impaired CSF absorption; raised intracranial pressure without mass lesion or hydrocephalus

Q: Presentation?

A: Headache (worse lying down/Valsalva); pulsatile tinnitus; transient visual obscurations; diplopia (CN VI palsy); visual loss; papilloedema

Q: Investigations?

A: Fundoscopy; visual fields; CT/MRI (rule out mass/venous sinus thrombosis); LP with opening pressure >25 cmH2O; CSF normal composition

Q: Differential?

A: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (pregnancy risk); pre-eclampsia; hypertensive encephalopathy; intracranial mass

Q: Treatment?

A: Weight management; acetazolamide (250-500 mg BD; limited data in pregnancy — case-by-case); topiramate (avoid — teratogenic); FBP monitoring

Q: Surgical?

A: Optic nerve sheath fenestration (vision loss); VP shunt; LP shunt; venous sinus stenting (selected)

Q: Repeated LP?

A: Therapeutic LPs to reduce pressure; not definitive

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 30.17.5 Multiple Sclerosis In Pregnancy

Definition

Multiple sclerosis affects women 2-3x more than men, often in childbearing years. Pregnancy is associated with reduced relapse rate (especially 3rd trimester) and increased relapse postpartum. Disease-modifying drugs require careful planning around conception.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

1 in 1,000 women; often diagnosed in 20s-30s

Effect of pregnancy

Relapse rate falls (esp. 3rd trimester); rises 3-6 months postpartum (transient)

Effect of MS on pregnancy

No increase in fetal malformations; possible ↑caesarean; small ↑low birth weight

Disease-modifying drugs

Stop before conception: fingolimod (2 months), natalizumab (3 months); interferon-β and glatiramer safe until pregnancy confirmed

Teratogenic

Cladribine; teriflunomide; contraindicated; washout with cholestyramine for teriflunomide

Relapse treatment

Methylprednisolone IV 3-5 days; consider IVIG if severe; MRI without gadolinium if needed

Labour and delivery

Vaginal preferred; epidural does not worsen MS; forceps/ventouse per obstetric indication

Postpartum

Restart DMD if breastfeeding not chosen; exclusive breastfeeding may reduce relapses

Fatigue

Common in pregnancy; manage with rest, support, vitamin D

Long-term

No long-term effect of pregnancy on MS progression

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence1 in 1,000 women; often diagnosed in 20s-30s
Effect of pregnancyRelapse rate falls (esp. 3rd trimester); rises 3-6 months postpartum (transient)
Effect of MS on pregnancyNo increase in fetal malformations; possible ↑caesarean; small ↑low birth weight
Disease-modifying drugsStop before conception: fingolimod (2 months), natalizumab (3 months); interferon-β and glatiramer safe until pregnancy confirmed
TeratogenicCladribine; teriflunomide; contraindicated; washout with cholestyramine for teriflunomide
Relapse treatmentMethylprednisolone IV 3-5 days; consider IVIG if severe; MRI without gadolinium if needed
Labour and deliveryVaginal preferred; epidural does not worsen MS; forceps/ventouse per obstetric indication
PostpartumRestart DMD if breastfeeding not chosen; exclusive breastfeeding may reduce relapses
FatigueCommon in pregnancy; manage with rest, support, vitamin D
Long-termNo long-term effect of pregnancy on MS progression

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy?

Multiple sclerosis affects women 2-3x more than men, often in childbearing years. Pregnancy is associated with reduced relapse rate (especially 3rd trimester) and increased relapse postpartum. Disease-modifying drugs require careful planning around conception.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Effect of pregnancy, Effect of MS on pregnancy, Disease-modifying drugs, Teratogenic. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.17.6 Myasthenia Gravis In Pregnancy

Definition

Myasthenia gravis (MG) in pregnancy has a variable course — exacerbations are unpredictable, with risk highest in the first trimester and postpartum. Treatment with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and corticosteroids is safe; immunosuppression and plasma exchange may be needed for myasthenic crisis.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Bimodal: early (<40, female predominance) and late (male predominance); 1 in 10,000-50,000

Effect of pregnancy

Unpredictable; 30-40% worsen (1st trimester, postpartum); 30% improve; 30% stable

Maternal risks

Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure); exacerbation; aspiration; fatigue

Fetal risks

Neonatal myasthenia (10-20% — transient); preterm; arthrogryposis (maternal anti-AChR antibodies)

Diagnosis

Anti-AChR antibodies (85%); anti-MuSK; EMG (decremental response); CT chest (thymoma)

Treatment

Pyridostigmine (first-line; safe); corticosteroids (safe); azathioprine (safe); IVIG and plasma exchange (safe; for crisis)

Drugs to avoid

Magnesium sulphate (worsens MG — use with caution in PE); aminoglycosides; fluoroquinolones; beta-blockers; some anaesthetic agents

Myasthenic crisis

Respiratory failure; bulbar weakness; treat with IVIG or plasma exchange + steroids; consider ITU/ventilation

Delivery

Vaginal preferred; epidural (avoid opioid respiratory depression); forceps/ventouse if fatigue in 2nd stage; avoid suxamethonium

Postpartum

Neonatal monitoring (respiratory, feeding) for 48-72 h; breastfeeding compatible with most drugs; high-dose steroids may need coverage

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyBimodal: early (<40, female predominance) and late (male predominance); 1 in 10,000-50,000
Effect of pregnancyUnpredictable; 30-40% worsen (1st trimester, postpartum); 30% improve; 30% stable
Maternal risksMyasthenic crisis (respiratory failure); exacerbation; aspiration; fatigue
Fetal risksNeonatal myasthenia (10-20% — transient); preterm; arthrogryposis (maternal anti-AChR antibodies)
DiagnosisAnti-AChR antibodies (85%); anti-MuSK; EMG (decremental response); CT chest (thymoma)
TreatmentPyridostigmine (first-line; safe); corticosteroids (safe); azathioprine (safe); IVIG and plasma exchange (safe; for crisis)
Drugs to avoidMagnesium sulphate (worsens MG — use with caution in PE); aminoglycosides; fluoroquinolones; beta-blockers; some anaesthetic agents
Myasthenic crisisRespiratory failure; bulbar weakness; treat with IVIG or plasma exchange + steroids; consider ITU/ventilation
DeliveryVaginal preferred; epidural (avoid opioid respiratory depression); forceps/ventouse if fatigue in 2nd stage; avoid suxamethonium
PostpartumNeonatal monitoring (respiratory, feeding) for 48-72 h; breastfeeding compatible with most drugs; high-dose steroids may need coverage

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Myasthenia gravis in pregnancy?

Myasthenia gravis (MG) in pregnancy has a variable course — exacerbations are unpredictable, with risk highest in the first trimester and postpartum. Treatment with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and corticosteroids is safe; immunosuppression and plasma exchange may be needed for myasthenic crisis.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Myasthenia gravis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Effect of pregnancy, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Myasthenia gravis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Myasthenia gravis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Myasthenia gravis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Myasthenia gravis in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Flashcards

Q: Epidemiology?

A: Bimodal: early (<40, female predominance) and late (male predominance); 1 in 10,000-50,000

Q: Effect of pregnancy?

A: Unpredictable; 30-40% worsen (1st trimester, postpartum); 30% improve; 30% stable

Q: Maternal risks?

A: Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure); exacerbation; aspiration; fatigue

Q: Fetal risks?

A: Neonatal myasthenia (10-20% — transient); preterm; arthrogryposis (maternal anti-AChR antibodies)

Q: Diagnosis?

A: Anti-AChR antibodies (85%); anti-MuSK; EMG (decremental response); CT chest (thymoma)

Q: Treatment?

A: Pyridostigmine (first-line; safe); corticosteroids (safe); azathioprine (safe); IVIG and plasma exchange (safe; for crisis)

Q: Drugs to avoid?

A: Magnesium sulphate (worsens MG — use with caution in PE); aminoglycosides; fluoroquinolones; beta-blockers; some anaesthetic agents

Q: Myasthenic crisis?

A: Respiratory failure; bulbar weakness; treat with IVIG or plasma exchange + steroids; consider ITU/ventilation

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.17.7 Stroke In Pregnancy And The Puerperium

Definition

Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium — ischaemic and haemorrhagic — is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Risk is highest in the postpartum period. Causes include pre-eclampsia, hypertension, paradoxical emboli, arterial dissection, and SAH from aneurysm rupture.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

30 per 100,000 pregnancies; 3x non-pregnant

Timing

Highest postpartum; 50% postpartum; 30% antenatal; 20% intrapartum

Ischaemic stroke risk factors

Pre-eclampsia/HELLP; hypertension; migraine; cardiac disease; APS; prothrombotic states; paradoxical emboli

Haemorrhagic stroke

Subarachnoid (aneurysm/AVM rupture); intracerebral (hypertensive, eclampsia); cortical vein thrombosis

Presentation

Sudden focal deficit; seizures; severe headache; altered consciousness; postpartum thunderclap headache

Investigation

Urgent CT/MRI; CT/MR venogram for CVST; CT/MR angiography; thrombophilia screen postpartum

Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis

Headache, seizures, focal deficits; treat with LMWH even if haemorrhagic conversion

Management ischaemic

Thrombolysis considered (limited evidence in pregnancy); MDT decision; aspirin 75-300 mg after haemorrhage excluded

SAH

Urgent neurosurgical input; securing aneurysm (coiling preferred)

Prevention

BP control in PE; MDT; preconception counselling; avoid dehydration; consider LMWH in APS

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence30 per 100,000 pregnancies; 3x non-pregnant
TimingHighest postpartum; 50% postpartum; 30% antenatal; 20% intrapartum
Ischaemic stroke risk factorsPre-eclampsia/HELLP; hypertension; migraine; cardiac disease; APS; prothrombotic states; paradoxical emboli
Haemorrhagic strokeSubarachnoid (aneurysm/AVM rupture); intracerebral (hypertensive, eclampsia); cortical vein thrombosis
PresentationSudden focal deficit; seizures; severe headache; altered consciousness; postpartum thunderclap headache
InvestigationUrgent CT/MRI; CT/MR venogram for CVST; CT/MR angiography; thrombophilia screen postpartum
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosisHeadache, seizures, focal deficits; treat with LMWH even if haemorrhagic conversion
Management ischaemicThrombolysis considered (limited evidence in pregnancy); MDT decision; aspirin 75-300 mg after haemorrhage excluded
SAHUrgent neurosurgical input; securing aneurysm (coiling preferred)
PreventionBP control in PE; MDT; preconception counselling; avoid dehydration; consider LMWH in APS

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium — ischaemic and haemorrhagic — is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Risk is highest in the postpartum period. Causes include pre-eclampsia, hypertension, paradoxical emboli, arterial dissection, and SAH from aneurysm rupture.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Key features include: Incidence, Timing, Ischaemic stroke risk factors, Haemorrhagic stroke, Presentation. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. 30 per 100,000 pregnancies; 3x non-pregnant
  2. Highest postpartum; 50% postpartum; 30% antenatal; 20% intrapartum
  3. Pre-eclampsia/HELLP; hypertension; migraine; cardiac disease; APS; prothrombotic states; paradoxical emboli
  4. Subarachnoid (aneurysm/AVM rupture); intracerebral (hypertensive, eclampsia); cortical vein thrombosis
  5. Sudden focal deficit; seizures; severe headache; altered consciousness; postpartum thunderclap headache
  6. Urgent CT/MRI; CT/MR venogram for CVST; CT/MR angiography; thrombophilia screen postpartum
  7. Headache, seizures, focal deficits; treat with LMWH even if haemorrhagic conversion
  8. Thrombolysis considered (limited evidence in pregnancy); MDT decision; aspirin 75-300 mg after haemorrhage excluded
  9. Urgent neurosurgical input; securing aneurysm (coiling preferred)
  10. BP control in PE; MDT; preconception counselling; avoid dehydration; consider LMWH in APS

▸ 30.18 Obesity and Nutrition in Pregnancy 2
▸ 30.18.1 Nutrition And Vitamins In Pregnancy

Definition

Adequate nutrition and supplementation in pregnancy support maternal health and fetal development. Routine supplementation with folic acid and vitamin D is recommended; iron is added for anaemia. Avoid excess vitamin A (teratogenic) and foodborne pathogens.

Core Clinical Content

Energy needs

+200-300 kcal/day in 2nd/3rd trimesters; obese women do not need additional calories

Folic acid

400 μg/day preconception to 12 weeks (reduce NTDs); 5 mg if high risk (prior NTD, antiepileptics, diabetes, obesity)

Vitamin D

10 μg/day (400 IU) for all; monitor 25-OH-D if at risk; deficiency → neonatal rickets

Iron

Routine from 16 weeks; 200 mg ferrous sulphate; IV if intolerance or severe

Vitamin A

Avoid excess (>700 μg retinol); teratogenic; avoid liver, high-dose supplements; beta-carotene safe

Calcium

1 g/day; supplementation in low-intake populations reduces pre-eclampsia

Iodine

140 μg/day; iodised salt; avoid kelp supplements

Foods to avoid

Soft cheeses, pâté, undercooked meat (listeria, toxoplasma); raw fish, high-mercury fish; alcohol

Caffeine

<200 mg/day (2 cups coffee) — high intake may ↑miscarriage, IUGR

Vegetarian/vegan

B12 supplementation; monitor iron, zinc, protein, omega-3

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Energy needs+200-300 kcal/day in 2nd/3rd trimesters; obese women do not need additional calories
Folic acid400 μg/day preconception to 12 weeks (reduce NTDs); 5 mg if high risk (prior NTD, antiepileptics, diabetes, obesity)
Vitamin D10 μg/day (400 IU) for all; monitor 25-OH-D if at risk; deficiency → neonatal rickets
IronRoutine from 16 weeks; 200 mg ferrous sulphate; IV if intolerance or severe
Vitamin AAvoid excess (>700 μg retinol); teratogenic; avoid liver, high-dose supplements; beta-carotene safe
Calcium1 g/day; supplementation in low-intake populations reduces pre-eclampsia
Iodine140 μg/day; iodised salt; avoid kelp supplements
Foods to avoidSoft cheeses, pâté, undercooked meat (listeria, toxoplasma); raw fish, high-mercury fish; alcohol
Caffeine<200 mg/day (2 cups coffee) — high intake may ↑miscarriage, IUGR
Vegetarian/veganB12 supplementation; monitor iron, zinc, protein, omega-3

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy?

Adequate nutrition and supplementation in pregnancy support maternal health and fetal development. Routine supplementation with folic acid and vitamin D is recommended; iron is added for anaemia. Avoid excess vitamin A (teratogenic) and foodborne pathogens.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy?

Key features include: Energy needs, Folic acid, Vitamin D, Iron, Vitamin A. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.18.2 Obesity In Pregnancy

Definition

Maternal obesity (BMI ≥30) is associated with increased risk of miscarriage, congenital anomalies, GDM, pre-eclampsia, macrosomia, caesarean section, VTE, and fetal death. Preconception weight loss and lifestyle modification improve outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

20-30% of pregnant women in developed countries; rising

Risks to mother

Miscarriage; GDM (3-4x); pre-eclampsia (2-3x); caesarean; PPH; VTE; anaesthetic difficulty; wound infection

Risks to fetus

NTD; cardiac defects; stillbirth (2x); macrosomia; shoulder dystocia; childhood obesity

Preconception

Weight loss (5-10%); folic acid 5 mg; screen for diabetes; counsel on risks

Antenatal care

Anomaly scan quality may be limited; consider repeat or alternative imaging; serial growth scans

Thromboprophylaxis

Risk assess; LMWH if BMI >40 or additional risk factors; consider for 10 days postpartum

GDM screening

Earlier OGTT at 24-28 weeks (or earlier if risk factors)

Weight gain

Institute of Medicine guidelines — underweight 12.5-18 kg; normal 11.5-16; overweight 7-11.5; obese 5-9

Bariatric surgery

Conceive 12-18 months post-surgery; monitor iron, B12, folate, calcium, vitamin D; risk of internal hernia

Labour

Higher caesarean; anaesthetic review; consider early epidural; PPH risk

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence20-30% of pregnant women in developed countries; rising
Risks to motherMiscarriage; GDM (3-4x); pre-eclampsia (2-3x); caesarean; PPH; VTE; anaesthetic difficulty; wound infection
Risks to fetusNTD; cardiac defects; stillbirth (2x); macrosomia; shoulder dystocia; childhood obesity
PreconceptionWeight loss (5-10%); folic acid 5 mg; screen for diabetes; counsel on risks
Antenatal careAnomaly scan quality may be limited; consider repeat or alternative imaging; serial growth scans
ThromboprophylaxisRisk assess; LMWH if BMI >40 or additional risk factors; consider for 10 days postpartum
GDM screeningEarlier OGTT at 24-28 weeks (or earlier if risk factors)
Weight gainInstitute of Medicine guidelines — underweight 12.5-18 kg; normal 11.5-16; overweight 7-11.5; obese 5-9
Bariatric surgeryConceive 12-18 months post-surgery; monitor iron, B12, folate, calcium, vitamin D; risk of internal hernia
LabourHigher caesarean; anaesthetic review; consider early epidural; PPH risk

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Obesity in pregnancy?

Maternal obesity (BMI ≥30) is associated with increased risk of miscarriage, congenital anomalies, GDM, pre-eclampsia, macrosomia, caesarean section, VTE, and fetal death. Preconception weight loss and lifestyle modification improve outcomes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Obesity in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Risks to mother, Risks to fetus, Preconception, Antenatal care. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Obesity in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Obesity in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Obesity in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Obesity in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)
