Flashcards

Q: Prevalence?

A: 20-30% of pregnant women in developed countries; rising

Q: Risks to mother?

A: Miscarriage; GDM (3-4x); pre-eclampsia (2-3x); caesarean; PPH; VTE; anaesthetic difficulty; wound infection

Q: Risks to fetus?

A: NTD; cardiac defects; stillbirth (2x); macrosomia; shoulder dystocia; childhood obesity

Q: Preconception?

A: Weight loss (5-10%); folic acid 5 mg; screen for diabetes; counsel on risks

Q: Antenatal care?

A: Anomaly scan quality may be limited; consider repeat or alternative imaging; serial growth scans

Q: Thromboprophylaxis?

A: Risk assess; LMWH if BMI >40 or additional risk factors; consider for 10 days postpartum

Q: GDM screening?

A: Earlier OGTT at 24-28 weeks (or earlier if risk factors)

Q: Weight gain?

A: Institute of Medicine guidelines — underweight 12.5-18 kg; normal 11.5-16; overweight 7-11.5; obese 5-9

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Obesity in pregnancy?
  2. A — Key feature of Obesity in pregnancy?
  3. A — Key feature of Obesity in pregnancy?
  4. A — Key feature of Obesity in pregnancy?
  5. A — Key feature of Obesity in pregnancy?
  6. A — Key feature of Obesity in pregnancy?
  7. A — Key feature of Obesity in pregnancy?
  8. A — Key feature of Obesity in pregnancy?
  9. A — Key feature of Obesity in pregnancy?
  10. A — Key feature of Obesity in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. 20-30% of pregnant women in developed countries; rising
  2. Miscarriage; GDM (3-4x); pre-eclampsia (2-3x); caesarean; PPH; VTE; anaesthetic difficulty; wound infection
  3. NTD; cardiac defects; stillbirth (2x); macrosomia; shoulder dystocia; childhood obesity
  4. Weight loss (5-10%); folic acid 5 mg; screen for diabetes; counsel on risks
  5. Anomaly scan quality may be limited; consider repeat or alternative imaging; serial growth scans
  6. Risk assess; LMWH if BMI >40 or additional risk factors; consider for 10 days postpartum
  7. Earlier OGTT at 24-28 weeks (or earlier if risk factors)
  8. Institute of Medicine guidelines — underweight 12.5-18 kg; normal 11.5-16; overweight 7-11.5; obese 5-9
  9. Conceive 12-18 months post-surgery; monitor iron, B12, folate, calcium, vitamin D; risk of internal hernia
  10. Higher caesarean; anaesthetic review; consider early epidural; PPH risk

▸ 30.19 Obstetric Disorders 9
▸ 30.19.1 Breech Presentation And External Cephalic Version

Definition

Breech presentation is when the fetus presents with buttocks or feet first, occurring in 3-4% of term pregnancies. It is associated with increased perinatal morbidity. External cephalic version (ECV) can turn the breech to cephalic presentation antenatally, reducing the need for caesarean section.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Fetal buttocks or feet presenting at pelvic inlet; frank, complete, footling

Incidence

20% at 28 weeks; 3-4% at term

Risk factors

Prematurity; multiparity; multiple pregnancy; polyhydramnios; placenta praevia; uterine anomalies; fetal anomalies (anencephaly, hydrocephalus); contracted pelvis; previous breech

Diagnosis

Palpation (Leopold); USS confirms; 36-week check for all

External cephalic version (ECV)

Attempted at 36-37 weeks; success 50-60%; tocolysis (terbutaline); Rh status; USS-guided; complications: cord prolapse, abruption, fetal distress (0.5%)

Contraindications ECV

APH, placenta praevia, abnormal CTG, multiple pregnancy (except first twin), ruptured membranes, previous C-section (relative), uterine anomaly

Failed ECV

Offer trial of vaginal breech or planned C-section; mode based on clinician skill + patient preference

Vaginal breech delivery

Careful selection (Frank breech, flexed head, adequate pelvis, no footling); experienced clinician; continuous CTG; second stage assistance

Caesarean breech

Frank breech in primigravida often; footling/compete; failed ECV; maternal preference; previous C-section

Prognosis

Vaginal breech in selected cases similar outcomes to cephalic; morbidity higher in unselected

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionFetal buttocks or feet presenting at pelvic inlet; frank, complete, footling
Incidence20% at 28 weeks; 3-4% at term
Risk factorsPrematurity; multiparity; multiple pregnancy; polyhydramnios; placenta praevia; uterine anomalies; fetal anomalies (anencephaly, hydrocephalus); contracted pelvis; previous breech
DiagnosisPalpation (Leopold); USS confirms; 36-week check for all
External cephalic version (ECV)Attempted at 36-37 weeks; success 50-60%; tocolysis (terbutaline); Rh status; USS-guided; complications: cord prolapse, abruption, fetal distress (0.5%)
Contraindications ECVAPH, placenta praevia, abnormal CTG, multiple pregnancy (except first twin), ruptured membranes, previous C-section (relative), uterine anomaly
Failed ECVOffer trial of vaginal breech or planned C-section; mode based on clinician skill + patient preference
Vaginal breech deliveryCareful selection (Frank breech, flexed head, adequate pelvis, no footling); experienced clinician; continuous CTG; second stage assistance
Caesarean breechFrank breech in primigravida often; footling/compete; failed ECV; maternal preference; previous C-section
PrognosisVaginal breech in selected cases similar outcomes to cephalic; morbidity higher in unselected

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Breech presentation and external cephalic version?

Breech presentation is when the fetus presents with buttocks or feet first, occurring in 3-4% of term pregnancies. It is associated with increased perinatal morbidity. External cephalic version (ECV) can turn the breech to cephalic presentation antenatally, reducing the need for caesarean section.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Breech presentation and external cephalic version?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Risk factors, Diagnosis, External cephalic version (ECV). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Breech presentation and external cephalic version diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Breech presentation and external cephalic version?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Breech presentation and external cephalic version?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Breech presentation and external cephalic version — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.19.2 Fetal Growth Restriction Fgr Iugr

Definition

Fetal growth restriction (FGR), also called intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), describes a fetus that has not reached its genetically predetermined growth potential. It is associated with increased risk of stillbirth, neonatal morbidity, and long-term metabolic and cardiovascular disease. Antenatal detection with timely delivery reduces perinatal mortality.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Estimated fetal weight (EFW) <10th centile (population) or abdominal circumference (AC) <10th centile

Classification

Symmetric (20%): early onset, genetic/infectious; asymmetric (80%): late onset, uteroplacental insufficiency

Causes

Fetal: chromosomal, structural anomalies, infection (TORCH); Maternal: HTN, autoimmune, smoking, malnutrition; Placental: pre-eclampsia, abruption, mosaicism

Risk factors

Prior FGR (strongest); pre-eclampsia; chronic HTN; APS; smoking; multiple pregnancy; maternal disease

Screening

First-trimester combined screen (PAPP-A low); uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; serial fundal height

Diagnosis

USS: AC <10th centile; estimated fetal weight; umbilical artery Doppler; middle cerebral artery Doppler; cerebroplacental ratio

Monitoring

Serial growth scans (2-3 weeks); umbilical artery Doppler; MCA Doppler; cardiotocography; biophysical profile; liquor volume

Severe FGR

Abnormal ductus venosus; absent/reversed EDF in umbilical artery; delivery recommended based on gestation and findings

Timing of delivery

Depends on severity and Doppler findings; <30 weeks with abnormal DV may need delivery

Long-term

Neurodevelopmental delay; cerebral palsy; metabolic syndrome; HTN; cardiovascular disease (Barker hypothesis)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionEstimated fetal weight (EFW) <10th centile (population) or abdominal circumference (AC) <10th centile
ClassificationSymmetric (20%): early onset, genetic/infectious; asymmetric (80%): late onset, uteroplacental insufficiency
CausesFetal: chromosomal, structural anomalies, infection (TORCH); Maternal: HTN, autoimmune, smoking, malnutrition; Placental: pre-eclampsia, abruption, mosaicism
Risk factorsPrior FGR (strongest); pre-eclampsia; chronic HTN; APS; smoking; multiple pregnancy; maternal disease
ScreeningFirst-trimester combined screen (PAPP-A low); uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; serial fundal height
DiagnosisUSS: AC <10th centile; estimated fetal weight; umbilical artery Doppler; middle cerebral artery Doppler; cerebroplacental ratio
MonitoringSerial growth scans (2-3 weeks); umbilical artery Doppler; MCA Doppler; cardiotocography; biophysical profile; liquor volume
Severe FGRAbnormal ductus venosus; absent/reversed EDF in umbilical artery; delivery recommended based on gestation and findings
Timing of deliveryDepends on severity and Doppler findings; <30 weeks with abnormal DV may need delivery
Long-termNeurodevelopmental delay; cerebral palsy; metabolic syndrome; HTN; cardiovascular disease (Barker hypothesis)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR)?

Fetal growth restriction (FGR), also called intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), describes a fetus that has not reached its genetically predetermined growth potential. It is associated with increased risk of stillbirth, neonatal morbidity, and long-term metabolic and cardiovascular disease. Antenatal detection with timely delivery reduces perinatal mortality.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR)?

Key features include: Definition, Classification, Causes, Risk factors, Screening. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR)?

A) Abnormal ductus venosus; absent/reversed EDF in umbilical artery; delivery recommended based on gestation and findings

B) Depends on severity and Doppler findings; <30 weeks with abnormal DV may need delivery

C) Neurodevelopmental delay; cerebral palsy; metabolic syndrome; HTN; cardiovascular disease (Barker hypothesis)

D) Estimated fetal weight (EFW) <10th centile (population) or abdominal circumference (AC) <10th centile

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR)?

A) Depends on severity and Doppler findings; <30 weeks with abnormal DV may need delivery

B) Neurodevelopmental delay; cerebral palsy; metabolic syndrome; HTN; cardiovascular disease (Barker hypothesis)

C) Estimated fetal weight (EFW) <10th centile (population) or abdominal circumference (AC) <10th centile

D) Symmetric (20%): early onset, genetic/infectious; asymmetric (80%): late onset, uteroplacental insufficiency

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR)?

A) Neurodevelopmental delay; cerebral palsy; metabolic syndrome; HTN; cardiovascular disease (Barker hypothesis)

B) Estimated fetal weight (EFW) <10th centile (population) or abdominal circumference (AC) <10th centile

C) Symmetric (20%): early onset, genetic/infectious; asymmetric (80%): late onset, uteroplacental insufficiency

D) Fetal: chromosomal, structural anomalies, infection (TORCH); Maternal: HTN, autoimmune, smoking, malnutrition; Placental: pre-eclampsia, abruption, mosaicism

Answer: A

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR). Most appropriate next step in management? | Symmetric (20%): early onset, genetic/infectious; asymmetric (80%): late onset, uteroplacental insufficiency

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR). Most appropriate next step in management? | First-trimester combined screen (PAPP-A low); uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; serial fundal height
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR). Most appropriate next step in management? | USS: AC <10th centile; estimated fetal weight; umbilical artery Doppler; middle cerebral artery Doppler; cerebroplacental ratio
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR). Most appropriate next step in management? | Serial growth scans (2-3 weeks); umbilical artery Doppler; MCA Doppler; cardiotocography; biophysical profile; liquor volume
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR). Most appropriate next step in management? | Abnormal ductus venosus; absent/reversed EDF in umbilical artery; delivery recommended based on gestation and findings
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Fetal growth restriction (FGR-IUGR). Most appropriate next step in management? | Depends on severity and Doppler findings; <30 weeks with abnormal DV may need delivery

Flashcards

Q: Definition?

A: Estimated fetal weight (EFW) <10th centile (population) or abdominal circumference (AC) <10th centile

Q: Classification?

A: Symmetric (20%): early onset, genetic/infectious; asymmetric (80%): late onset, uteroplacental insufficiency

Q: Causes?

A: Fetal: chromosomal, structural anomalies, infection (TORCH); Maternal: HTN, autoimmune, smoking, malnutrition; Placental: pre-eclampsia, abruption, mosaicism

Q: Risk factors?

A: Prior FGR (strongest); pre-eclampsia; chronic HTN; APS; smoking; multiple pregnancy; maternal disease

Q: Screening?

A: First-trimester combined screen (PAPP-A low); uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; serial fundal height

Q: Diagnosis?

A: USS: AC <10th centile; estimated fetal weight; umbilical artery Doppler; middle cerebral artery Doppler; cerebroplacental ratio

Q: Monitoring?

A: Serial growth scans (2-3 weeks); umbilical artery Doppler; MCA Doppler; cardiotocography; biophysical profile; liquor volume

Q: Severe FGR?

A: Abnormal ductus venosus; absent/reversed EDF in umbilical artery; delivery recommended based on gestation and findings

Answer Key with Explanations

  1. Estimated fetal weight (EFW) <10th centile (population) or abdominal circumference (AC) <10th centile
  2. Symmetric (20%): early onset, genetic/infectious; asymmetric (80%): late onset, uteroplacental insufficiency
  3. Fetal: chromosomal, structural anomalies, infection (TORCH); Maternal: HTN, autoimmune, smoking, malnutrition; Placental: pre-eclampsia, abruption, mosaicism
  4. Prior FGR (strongest); pre-eclampsia; chronic HTN; APS; smoking; multiple pregnancy; maternal disease
  5. First-trimester combined screen (PAPP-A low); uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; serial fundal height
  6. USS: AC <10th centile; estimated fetal weight; umbilical artery Doppler; middle cerebral artery Doppler; cerebroplacental ratio
  7. Serial growth scans (2-3 weeks); umbilical artery Doppler; MCA Doppler; cardiotocography; biophysical profile; liquor volume
  8. Abnormal ductus venosus; absent/reversed EDF in umbilical artery; delivery recommended based on gestation and findings
  9. Depends on severity and Doppler findings; <30 weeks with abnormal DV may need delivery
  10. Neurodevelopmental delay; cerebral palsy; metabolic syndrome; HTN; cardiovascular disease (Barker hypothesis)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.19.3 Oligohydramnios

Definition

Oligohydramnios is reduced amniotic fluid volume (AFI <5 cm or DVP <2 cm). It is associated with fetal renal anomalies, IUGR, pre-labour rupture of membranes, and post-term pregnancy. Severe early-onset oligohydramnios causes pulmonary hypoplasia, skeletal deformities (Potter sequence), and high perinatal mortality.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

AFI <5 cm (or <5th centile) or DVP <2 cm

Incidence

0.5-5% at term; higher post-term (12%)

Causes

PPROM (most common at term); fetal renal anomalies (Potter, ARPKD, posterior urethral valves); IUGR/placental insufficiency; post-term; maternal drugs (NSAIDs, ACEI); twin-twin transfusion (donor); idiopathic

Presentation

Fundal height < dates; fetal parts easily palpable; reduced fetal movements

Investigations

Detailed anomaly scan (renal); UA Doppler (placental insufficiency); Kleihauer; review for PPROM; review drug history

Severe early (<24 weeks)

Pulmonary hypoplasia (high mortality); Potter facies; limb deformities; renal agenesis lethal

Monitoring

Serial USS; AFI twice weekly; CTG; consider admission if <34 weeks or severe

Management

Treat cause if possible; amnioinfusion in labour (equivocal); hydration (maternal oral/IV may increase AFI transiently)

Delivery

Induce at 36-37 weeks for isolated oligohydramnios; earlier if IUGR or non-reassuring; C-section for malpresentation; cord compression risk

Antenatal corticosteroids

If preterm delivery planned and <34 weeks

Prognosis

Depends on cause and gestation; isolated at term generally good

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionAFI <5 cm (or <5th centile) or DVP <2 cm
Incidence0.5-5% at term; higher post-term (12%)
CausesPPROM (most common at term); fetal renal anomalies (Potter, ARPKD, posterior urethral valves); IUGR/placental insufficiency; post-term; maternal drugs (NSAIDs, ACEI); twin-twin transfusion (donor); idiopathic
PresentationFundal height < dates; fetal parts easily palpable; reduced fetal movements
InvestigationsDetailed anomaly scan (renal); UA Doppler (placental insufficiency); Kleihauer; review for PPROM; review drug history
Severe early (<24 weeks)Pulmonary hypoplasia (high mortality); Potter facies; limb deformities; renal agenesis lethal
MonitoringSerial USS; AFI twice weekly; CTG; consider admission if <34 weeks or severe
ManagementTreat cause if possible; amnioinfusion in labour (equivocal); hydration (maternal oral/IV may increase AFI transiently)
DeliveryInduce at 36-37 weeks for isolated oligohydramnios; earlier if IUGR or non-reassuring; C-section for malpresentation; cord compression risk
Antenatal corticosteroidsIf preterm delivery planned and <34 weeks
PrognosisDepends on cause and gestation; isolated at term generally good

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Oligohydramnios?

Oligohydramnios is reduced amniotic fluid volume (AFI <5 cm or DVP <2 cm). It is associated with fetal renal anomalies, IUGR, pre-labour rupture of membranes, and post-term pregnancy. Severe early-onset oligohydramnios causes pulmonary hypoplasia, skeletal deformities (Potter sequence), and high perinatal mortality.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Oligohydramnios?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Causes, Presentation, Investigations. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Oligohydramnios diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Oligohydramnios?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Oligohydramnios?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Oligohydramnios — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.19.4 Placenta Accreta Spectrum

Definition

Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) covers placenta accreta, increta, and percreta — abnormal placental invasion into or through the myometrium. It is associated with significant peripartum haemorrhage, hysterectomy, and maternal mortality. Risk is greatest in women with prior caesarean section and placenta praevia.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Placenta abnormally adherent: accreta (myometrium); increta (through myometrium); percreta (to serosa/adjacent organs)

Incidence

0.2 per 1000; rising with C-section rates

Risk factors

Prior C-section (5% if 1; 25% if 2; 50%+ if ≥3 with praevia); placenta praevia; prior uterine surgery; Asherman; multiparity; advanced maternal age

Presentation

Often asymptomatic antenatally; diagnosed on USS; failed placental separation at delivery; PPH

USS features

Loss of clear retroplacental hypoechoic zone; placental lacunae ('Swiss cheese'); bladder line interruption; hypervascularity

MRI

Confirms depth; useful for posterior placenta and surgical planning

Management

MDT (tertiary centre) — obstetrics, gynae-oncology, vascular surgery, haematology, anaesthetics, neonatology, IR

Delivery

Planned C-section hysterectomy at 34-36 weeks; leave placenta in situ if extensive; avoid attempts at removal

Conservative

Leave placenta in situ + methotrexate (controversial) ± UAE; successful in 60-80% but risk of sepsis/hemorrhage

Future pregnancy

Fertility preserved if uterus retained; recurrence risk; need careful counselling

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPlacenta abnormally adherent: accreta (myometrium); increta (through myometrium); percreta (to serosa/adjacent organs)
Incidence0.2 per 1000; rising with C-section rates
Risk factorsPrior C-section (5% if 1; 25% if 2; 50%+ if ≥3 with praevia); placenta praevia; prior uterine surgery; Asherman; multiparity; advanced maternal age
PresentationOften asymptomatic antenatally; diagnosed on USS; failed placental separation at delivery; PPH
USS featuresLoss of clear retroplacental hypoechoic zone; placental lacunae ('Swiss cheese'); bladder line interruption; hypervascularity
MRIConfirms depth; useful for posterior placenta and surgical planning
ManagementMDT (tertiary centre) — obstetrics, gynae-oncology, vascular surgery, haematology, anaesthetics, neonatology, IR
DeliveryPlanned C-section hysterectomy at 34-36 weeks; leave placenta in situ if extensive; avoid attempts at removal
ConservativeLeave placenta in situ + methotrexate (controversial) ± UAE; successful in 60-80% but risk of sepsis/hemorrhage
Future pregnancyFertility preserved if uterus retained; recurrence risk; need careful counselling

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Placenta accreta spectrum?

Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) covers placenta accreta, increta, and percreta — abnormal placental invasion into or through the myometrium. It is associated with significant peripartum haemorrhage, hysterectomy, and maternal mortality. Risk is greatest in women with prior caesarean section and placenta praevia.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Placenta accreta spectrum?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Risk factors, Presentation, USS features. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Placenta accreta spectrum diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Placenta accreta spectrum?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Placenta accreta spectrum?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Placenta accreta spectrum — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

A) Placenta abnormally adherent: accreta (myometrium); increta (through myometrium); percreta (to serosa/adjacent organs)

B) 0.2 per 1000; rising with C-section rates

C) Prior C-section (5% if 1; 25% if 2; 50%+ if ≥3 with praevia); placenta praevia; prior uterine surgery; Asherman; multiparity; advanced maternal age

D) Often asymptomatic antenatally; diagnosed on USS; failed placental separation at delivery; PPH

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Placenta accreta spectrum?

A) 0.2 per 1000; rising with C-section rates

B) Prior C-section (5% if 1; 25% if 2; 50%+ if ≥3 with praevia); placenta praevia; prior uterine surgery; Asherman; multiparity; advanced maternal age

C) Often asymptomatic antenatally; diagnosed on USS; failed placental separation at delivery; PPH

D) Loss of clear retroplacental hypoechoic zone; placental lacunae ('Swiss cheese'); bladder line interruption; hypervascularity

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Placenta accreta spectrum?

A) Prior C-section (5% if 1; 25% if 2; 50%+ if ≥3 with praevia); placenta praevia; prior uterine surgery; Asherman; multiparity; advanced maternal age

B) Often asymptomatic antenatally; diagnosed on USS; failed placental separation at delivery; PPH

C) Loss of clear retroplacental hypoechoic zone; placental lacunae ('Swiss cheese'); bladder line interruption; hypervascularity

D) Confirms depth; useful for posterior placenta and surgical planning

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Placenta accreta spectrum?

A) Often asymptomatic antenatally; diagnosed on USS; failed placental separation at delivery; PPH

B) Loss of clear retroplacental hypoechoic zone; placental lacunae ('Swiss cheese'); bladder line interruption; hypervascularity

C) Confirms depth; useful for posterior placenta and surgical planning

D) MDT (tertiary centre) — obstetrics, gynae-oncology, vascular surgery, haematology, anaesthetics, neonatology, IR

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Placenta accreta spectrum?

A) Loss of clear retroplacental hypoechoic zone; placental lacunae ('Swiss cheese'); bladder line interruption; hypervascularity

B) Confirms depth; useful for posterior placenta and surgical planning

C) MDT (tertiary centre) — obstetrics, gynae-oncology, vascular surgery, haematology, anaesthetics, neonatology, IR

D) Planned C-section hysterectomy at 34-36 weeks; leave placenta in situ if extensive; avoid attempts at removal

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Placenta accreta spectrum?

A) Confirms depth; useful for posterior placenta and surgical planning

B) MDT (tertiary centre) — obstetrics, gynae-oncology, vascular surgery, haematology, anaesthetics, neonatology, IR

C) Planned C-section hysterectomy at 34-36 weeks; leave placenta in situ if extensive; avoid attempts at removal

D) Leave placenta in situ + methotrexate (controversial) ± UAE; successful in 60-80% but risk of sepsis/hemorrhage

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Placenta accreta spectrum?

A) MDT (tertiary centre) — obstetrics, gynae-oncology, vascular surgery, haematology, anaesthetics, neonatology, IR

B) Planned C-section hysterectomy at 34-36 weeks; leave placenta in situ if extensive; avoid attempts at removal

C) Leave placenta in situ + methotrexate (controversial) ± UAE; successful in 60-80% but risk of sepsis/hemorrhage

D) Fertility preserved if uterus retained; recurrence risk; need careful counselling

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Placenta accreta spectrum?

A) Planned C-section hysterectomy at 34-36 weeks; leave placenta in situ if extensive; avoid attempts at removal

B) Leave placenta in situ + methotrexate (controversial) ± UAE; successful in 60-80% but risk of sepsis/hemorrhage

C) Fertility preserved if uterus retained; recurrence risk; need careful counselling

D) Placenta abnormally adherent: accreta (myometrium); increta (through myometrium); percreta (to serosa/adjacent organs)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Placenta accreta spectrum?

A) Leave placenta in situ + methotrexate (controversial) ± UAE; successful in 60-80% but risk of sepsis/hemorrhage

B) Fertility preserved if uterus retained; recurrence risk; need careful counselling

▸ 30.19.5 Placenta Praevia

Definition

Placenta praevia is implantation of the placenta in the lower uterine segment, covering or near the internal cervical os. It presents with painless, recurrent, bright-red vaginal bleeding in the second half of pregnancy. Diagnosis is by transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound; delivery is by planned caesarean section.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Placenta covering or within 2 cm of internal os (major if covers, minor if low-lying)

Incidence

0.5-1% of pregnancies; rising with caesarean and advanced maternal age

Risk factors

Prior caesarean (major risk); prior placenta praevia; multiparity; multiple pregnancy; advanced maternal age; smoking; prior uterine surgery/curettage

Presentation

Painless bright-red bleeding (often recurrent); may be asymptomatic; malpresentation (breech/transverse)

Diagnosis

Transabdominal + transvaginal USS (definitive); MRI if accreta suspected; do NOT do digital vaginal examination

Management

Expectant if preterm (transfuse, steroids <34 weeks); iron supplementation; thromboprophylaxis; delivery by caesarean

Timing of delivery

Elective caesarean 36-37 weeks; emergency if heavy bleeding or fetal/maternal compromise

Placenta accreta

Placenta abnormally adherent (accreta/increta/percreta); risk with prior C-section + praevia; may need hysterectomy

Complications

Haemorrhage; shock; hysterectomy; preterm birth; fetal growth restriction; vasa praevia; recurrence 4-8%

Antenatal care

MDT (obstetrician, anaesthetist, haematologist); blood available; cell salvage; consider interventional radiology

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPlacenta covering or within 2 cm of internal os (major if covers, minor if low-lying)
Incidence0.5-1% of pregnancies; rising with caesarean and advanced maternal age
Risk factorsPrior caesarean (major risk); prior placenta praevia; multiparity; multiple pregnancy; advanced maternal age; smoking; prior uterine surgery/curettage
PresentationPainless bright-red bleeding (often recurrent); may be asymptomatic; malpresentation (breech/transverse)
DiagnosisTransabdominal + transvaginal USS (definitive); MRI if accreta suspected; do NOT do digital vaginal examination
ManagementExpectant if preterm (transfuse, steroids <34 weeks); iron supplementation; thromboprophylaxis; delivery by caesarean
Timing of deliveryElective caesarean 36-37 weeks; emergency if heavy bleeding or fetal/maternal compromise
Placenta accretaPlacenta abnormally adherent (accreta/increta/percreta); risk with prior C-section + praevia; may need hysterectomy
ComplicationsHaemorrhage; shock; hysterectomy; preterm birth; fetal growth restriction; vasa praevia; recurrence 4-8%
Antenatal careMDT (obstetrician, anaesthetist, haematologist); blood available; cell salvage; consider interventional radiology

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Placenta praevia?

Placenta praevia is implantation of the placenta in the lower uterine segment, covering or near the internal cervical os. It presents with painless, recurrent, bright-red vaginal bleeding in the second half of pregnancy. Diagnosis is by transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound; delivery is by planned caesarean section.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Placenta praevia?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Risk factors, Presentation, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Placenta praevia diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Placenta praevia?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Placenta praevia?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Placenta praevia — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.19.6 Placental Abruption

Definition

Placental abruption (abruptio placentae) is the premature separation of a normally implanted placenta from the uterine wall after 20 weeks' gestation and before delivery. It presents with painful vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness, and hypertonic ('woody') uterus, and is a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Premature separation of normally sited placenta after 20 weeks

Incidence

0.5-1% of pregnancies; 10-15% of perinatal mortality

Risk factors

Prior abruption (10x); hypertension (strongest); smoking; cocaine; trauma; PPROM; multiple pregnancy; polyhydramnios; thrombophilia

Presentation

Painful vaginal bleeding (80%); uterine tenderness/rigidity ('woody'); hypertonic uterus; absent/reduced fetal movements; shock; DIC; fetal distress/death

Concealed abruption

Retroplacental clot; no visible bleeding; 20%

Diagnosis

Clinical; USS (sensitivity <50%); CTG abnormal; exclude praevia

Investigations

FBC, coagulation (DIC screen), crossmatch, U&E, Kleihauer (if Rh-)

Management

Resuscitate (ABC, IV access, fluids); correct coagulopathy; delivery if unstable (vaginal if possible; C-section if fetal salvage required); steroids if preterm

Complications

Maternal: shock, DIC, AKI, PPH, Couvelaire uterus, Sheehan; Fetal: 15-20% mortality; preterm; asphyxia

Future pregnancy

Recurrence 5-15% (10% if one, 25% if two); aspirin prophylaxis; close surveillance

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPremature separation of normally sited placenta after 20 weeks
Incidence0.5-1% of pregnancies; 10-15% of perinatal mortality
Risk factorsPrior abruption (10x); hypertension (strongest); smoking; cocaine; trauma; PPROM; multiple pregnancy; polyhydramnios; thrombophilia
PresentationPainful vaginal bleeding (80%); uterine tenderness/rigidity ('woody'); hypertonic uterus; absent/reduced fetal movements; shock; DIC; fetal distress/death
Concealed abruptionRetroplacental clot; no visible bleeding; 20%
DiagnosisClinical; USS (sensitivity <50%); CTG abnormal; exclude praevia
InvestigationsFBC, coagulation (DIC screen), crossmatch, U&E, Kleihauer (if Rh-)
ManagementResuscitate (ABC, IV access, fluids); correct coagulopathy; delivery if unstable (vaginal if possible; C-section if fetal salvage required); steroids if preterm
ComplicationsMaternal: shock, DIC, AKI, PPH, Couvelaire uterus, Sheehan; Fetal: 15-20% mortality; preterm; asphyxia
Future pregnancyRecurrence 5-15% (10% if one, 25% if two); aspirin prophylaxis; close surveillance

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Placental abruption?

Placental abruption (abruptio placentae) is the premature separation of a normally implanted placenta from the uterine wall after 20 weeks' gestation and before delivery. It presents with painful vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness, and hypertonic ('woody') uterus, and is a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Placental abruption?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Risk factors, Presentation, Concealed abruption. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Placental abruption diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Placental abruption?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Placental abruption?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Placental abruption — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.19.7 Polyhydramnios

Definition

Polyhydramnios is excessive amniotic fluid volume (AFI >25 cm or deepest vertical pocket >8 cm). It affects 1-2% of pregnancies and is associated with fetal anomalies (especially GI obstruction and CNS), maternal diabetes, and multiple pregnancy. Severe cases present with abdominal discomfort, preterm labour, and cord prolapse.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Amniotic fluid index (AFI) >25 cm or deepest vertical pocket (DVP) >8 cm

Incidence

0.5-2% of pregnancies; mild common, severe rare

Causes

Idiopathic (60%); fetal anomalies (15-20% — GI obstruction, oesophageal atresia, duodenal atresia, anencephaly, NTDs); maternal diabetes (10-20%); multiple pregnancy (TTTS); fetal infection; anaemia (parvovirus, haemolysis)

Mild polyhydramnios

Often idiopathic; AFI 25-30; close surveillance

Severe polyhydramnios

AFI >35; preterm labour risk; cord prolapse; abruption; PPH; respiratory compromise

Presentation

Fundal height > dates; tense abdomen; fluid thrill; dyspnoea; preterm contractions

Investigations

Detailed anomaly scan; OGTT for diabetes; Kleihauer; maternal serology (parvovirus, CMV); fetal karyotype if indicated

Monitoring

Regular scans; growth; wellbeing; symphysis-fundal height

Management mild

Expectant; treat underlying cause if possible

Management severe

Amnioreduction (therapeutic amniocentesis); indomethacin (reduces fetal urine output — <32 weeks); close monitoring

Delivery

Risk of cord prolapse (ARM with controlled drain); PPH risk; breech presentation

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point

Incidence0.5-2% of pregnancies; mild common, severe rare
CausesIdiopathic (60%); fetal anomalies (15-20% — GI obstruction, oesophageal atresia, duodenal atresia, anencephaly, NTDs); maternal diabetes (10-20%); multiple pregnancy (TTTS); fetal infection; anaemia (parvovirus, haemolysis)
Mild polyhydramniosOften idiopathic; AFI 25-30; close surveillance
Severe polyhydramniosAFI >35; preterm labour risk; cord prolapse; abruption; PPH; respiratory compromise
PresentationFundal height > dates; tense abdomen; fluid thrill; dyspnoea; preterm contractions
InvestigationsDetailed anomaly scan; OGTT for diabetes; Kleihauer; maternal serology (parvovirus, CMV); fetal karyotype if indicated
MonitoringRegular scans; growth; wellbeing; symphysis-fundal height
Management mildExpectant; treat underlying cause if possible
Management severeAmnioreduction (therapeutic amniocentesis); indomethacin (reduces fetal urine output — <32 weeks); close monitoring
DeliveryRisk of cord prolapse (ARM with controlled drain); PPH risk; breech presentation

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Polyhydramnios?

Polyhydramnios is excessive amniotic fluid volume (AFI >25 cm or deepest vertical pocket >8 cm). It affects 1-2% of pregnancies and is associated with fetal anomalies (especially GI obstruction and CNS), maternal diabetes, and multiple pregnancy. Severe cases present with abdominal discomfort, preterm labour, and cord prolapse.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Polyhydramnios?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Causes, Mild polyhydramnios, Severe polyhydramnios. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Polyhydramnios diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Polyhydramnios?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Polyhydramnios?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Polyhydramnios — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.19.8 Pre Labour Rupture Of Membranes Prom Pprom

Definition

Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM) is rupture of membranes before onset of labour. When it occurs before 37 weeks it is termed preterm pre-labour rupture of membranes (PPROM) and is associated with infection, preterm delivery, and neonatal morbidity. Management balances prolonging pregnancy for fetal maturity against infection risk.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

PROM: ROM before labour at term; PPROM: ROM before labour <37 weeks

Diagnosis

History of gush; speculum showing pooling of fluid; nitrazine paper (amniotic fluid alkaline); ferning test; actim PROM (PAMG-1); USS oligohydramnios

PPROM incidence

2-4% of pregnancies; 30-40% of preterm births

Risk factors

Prior PPROM; infection (chorioamnionitis, GBS); multiple pregnancy; polyhydramnios; smoking; cervical insufficiency; low BMI

Complications

Chorioamnionitis (10-30%); preterm delivery; cord prolapse; placental abruption; neonatal sepsis; pulmonary hypoplasia (<24 weeks); deformity

Investigations

Maternal: FBC, CRP, temperature, pulse; swabs (GBS, chlamydia); high vaginal swab; USS (AFI, growth, presentation)

Management PPROM

Expectant if no infection: steroids (24-34 weeks); magnesium <32 weeks; latency antibiotics (erythromycin + amoxicillin) 7-10 days; GBS prophylaxis; LMWH; monitor for chorioamnionitis

Chorioamnionitis

Fever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness, foul discharge, fetal tachycardia — immediate delivery + IV antibiotics

PROM at term

Induce labour at 24 h (reduce infection; expectant is acceptable if wishes); GBS prophylaxis if prolonged

Prognosis

Latency inversely related to gestation; 50% deliver within 1 week PPROM at <34 weeks

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPROM: ROM before labour at term; PPROM: ROM before labour <37 weeks
DiagnosisHistory of gush; speculum showing pooling of fluid; nitrazine paper (amniotic fluid alkaline); ferning test; actim PROM (PAMG-1); USS oligohydramnios
PPROM incidence2-4% of pregnancies; 30-40% of preterm births
Risk factorsPrior PPROM; infection (chorioamnionitis, GBS); multiple pregnancy; polyhydramnios; smoking; cervical insufficiency; low BMI
ComplicationsChorioamnionitis (10-30%); preterm delivery; cord prolapse; placental abruption; neonatal sepsis; pulmonary hypoplasia (<24 weeks); deformity
InvestigationsMaternal: FBC, CRP, temperature, pulse; swabs (GBS, chlamydia); high vaginal swab; USS (AFI, growth, presentation)
Management PPROMExpectant if no infection: steroids (24-34 weeks); magnesium <32 weeks; latency antibiotics (erythromycin + amoxicillin) 7-10 days; GBS prophylaxis; LMWH; monitor for chorioamnionitis
ChorioamnionitisFever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness, foul discharge, fetal tachycardia — immediate delivery + IV antibiotics
PROM at termInduce labour at 24 h (reduce infection; expectant is acceptable if wishes); GBS prophylaxis if prolonged
PrognosisLatency inversely related to gestation; 50% deliver within 1 week PPROM at <34 weeks

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM-PPROM)?

Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM) is rupture of membranes before onset of labour. When it occurs before 37 weeks it is termed preterm pre-labour rupture of membranes (PPROM) and is associated with infection, preterm delivery, and neonatal morbidity. Management balances prolonging pregnancy for fetal maturity against infection risk.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM-PPROM)?

Key features include: Definition, Diagnosis, PPROM incidence, Risk factors, Complications. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM-PPROM) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM-PPROM)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM-PPROM)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM-PPROM) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.19.9 Rhesus Isoimmunisation And Anti D

Definition

Rhesus (Rh) isoimmunisation occurs when a Rh-negative mother is exposed to Rh-positive fetal red cells (often at delivery), producing anti-D IgG antibodies that can cross the placenta and cause haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN) in subsequent pregnancies. Anti-D prophylaxis at 28-30 weeks and post-delivery prevents sensitisation.

Core Clinical Content

Pathophysiology

Rh- mother sensitised to Rh+ fetal red cells → IgG anti-D → crosses placenta → HDFN in subsequent pregnancies

At-risk pregnancies

Any Rh- mother with Rh+ (or unknown) fetus; sensitising events: delivery, miscarriage, ectopic, CVS, amniocentesis, APH, trauma, ECV

Sensitising events

Anti-D 250-500 IU IM within 72 h of event; Kleihauer test to quantify fetomaternal haemorrhage and adjust dose

Routine prophylaxis

Anti-D 500 IU IM at 28 weeks (UK); 1500 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks (US)

Post-delivery

Anti-D 500 IU IM within 72 h of delivery of Rh+ baby; Kleihauer to confirm adequate dose

HDFN spectrum

Mild anaemia → hydrops fetalis (severe); kernicterus in neonate; intrauterine transfusion

Monitoring affected pregnancy

Maternal anti-D titres; middle cerebral artery Doppler (peak systolic velocity); serial USS; amniocentesis if needed

Treatment severe HDFN

Intrauterine transfusion; early delivery; exchange transfusion; IVIG neonatal

Already sensitised

No prophylaxis helps; monitor titres; consider plasmapheresis; MDT; consider IVIG; delivery timing critical

Future

Prophylaxis effective; rare failures due to silent sensitisation or inadequate dose

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PathophysiologyRh- mother sensitised to Rh+ fetal red cells → IgG anti-D → crosses placenta → HDFN in subsequent pregnancies
At-risk pregnanciesAny Rh- mother with Rh+ (or unknown) fetus; sensitising events: delivery, miscarriage, ectopic, CVS, amniocentesis, APH, trauma, ECV
Sensitising eventsAnti-D 250-500 IU IM within 72 h of event; Kleihauer test to quantify fetomaternal haemorrhage and adjust dose
Routine prophylaxisAnti-D 500 IU IM at 28 weeks (UK); 1500 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks (US)
Post-deliveryAnti-D 500 IU IM within 72 h of delivery of Rh+ baby; Kleihauer to confirm adequate dose
HDFN spectrumMild anaemia → hydrops fetalis (severe); kernicterus in neonate; intrauterine transfusion
Monitoring affected pregnancyMaternal anti-D titres; middle cerebral artery Doppler (peak systolic velocity); serial USS; amniocentesis if needed
Treatment severe HDFNIntrauterine transfusion; early delivery; exchange transfusion; IVIG neonatal
Already sensitisedNo prophylaxis helps; monitor titres; consider plasmapheresis; MDT; consider IVIG; delivery timing critical
FutureProphylaxis effective; rare failures due to silent sensitisation or inadequate dose

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?

Rhesus (Rh) isoimmunisation occurs when a Rh-negative mother is exposed to Rh-positive fetal red cells (often at delivery), producing anti-D IgG antibodies that can cross the placenta and cause haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN) in subsequent pregnancies. Anti-D prophylaxis at 28-30 weeks and post-delivery prevents sensitisation.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?

Key features include: Pathophysiology, At-risk pregnancies, Sensitising events, Routine prophylaxis, Post-delivery. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D. Most appropriate next step in management? | Anti-D 250-500 IU IM within 72 h of event; Kleihauer test to quantify fetomaternal haemorrhage and adjust dose
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D. Most appropriate next step in management? | Anti-D 500 IU IM at 28 weeks (UK); 1500 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks (US)
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D. Most appropriate next step in management? | Anti-D 500 IU IM within 72 h of delivery of Rh+ baby; Kleihauer to confirm adequate dose
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D. Most appropriate next step in management? | Mild anaemia → hydrops fetalis (severe); kernicterus in neonate; intrauterine transfusion
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D. Most appropriate next step in management? | Maternal anti-D titres; middle cerebral artery Doppler (peak systolic velocity); serial USS; amniocentesis if needed
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D. Most appropriate next step in management? | Intrauterine transfusion; early delivery; exchange transfusion; IVIG neonatal
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D. Most appropriate next step in management? | No prophylaxis helps; monitor titres; consider plasmapheresis; MDT; consider IVIG; delivery timing critical
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D. Most appropriate next step in management? | Prophylaxis effective; rare failures due to silent sensitisation or inadequate dose

Flashcards

Q: Pathophysiology?

A: Rh- mother sensitised to Rh+ fetal red cells → IgG anti-D → crosses placenta → HDFN in subsequent pregnancies

Q: At-risk pregnancies?

A: Any Rh- mother with Rh+ (or unknown) fetus; sensitising events: delivery, miscarriage, ectopic, CVS, amniocentesis, APH, trauma, ECV

Q: Sensitising events?

A: Anti-D 250-500 IU IM within 72 h of event; Kleihauer test to quantify fetomaternal haemorrhage and adjust dose

Q: Routine prophylaxis?

A: Anti-D 500 IU IM at 28 weeks (UK); 1500 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks (US)

Q: Post-delivery?

A: Anti-D 500 IU IM within 72 h of delivery of Rh+ baby; Kleihauer to confirm adequate dose

Q: HDFN spectrum?

A: Mild anaemia → hydrops fetalis (severe); kernicterus in neonate; intrauterine transfusion

Q: Monitoring affected pregnancy?

A: Maternal anti-D titres; middle cerebral artery Doppler (peak systolic velocity); serial USS; amniocentesis if needed

Q: Treatment severe HDFN?

A: Intrauterine transfusion; early delivery; exchange transfusion; IVIG neonatal

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?
  2. A — Key feature of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?
  3. A — Key feature of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?
  4. A — Key feature of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?
  5. A — Key feature of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?
  6. A — Key feature of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?
  7. A — Key feature of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?
  8. A — Key feature of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?
  9. A — Key feature of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?
  10. A — Key feature of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?

SBAs

  1. Rh- mother sensitised to Rh+ fetal red cells → IgG anti-D → crosses placenta → HDFN in subsequent pregnancies
  2. Any Rh- mother with Rh+ (or unknown) fetus; sensitising events: delivery, miscarriage, ectopic, CVS, amniocentesis, APH, trauma, ECV
  3. Anti-D 250-500 IU IM within 72 h of event; Kleihauer test to quantify fetomaternal haemorrhage and adjust dose
  4. Anti-D 500 IU IM at 28 weeks (UK); 1500 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks (US)
  5. Anti-D 500 IU IM within 72 h of delivery of Rh+ baby; Kleihauer to confirm adequate dose
  6. Mild anaemia → hydrops fetalis (severe); kernicterus in neonate; intrauterine transfusion
  7. Maternal anti-D titres; middle cerebral artery Doppler (peak systolic velocity); serial USS; amniocentesis if needed
  8. Intrauterine transfusion; early delivery; exchange transfusion; IVIG neonatal
  9. No prophylaxis helps; monitor titres; consider plasmapheresis; MDT; consider IVIG; delivery timing critical
  10. Prophylaxis effective; rare failures due to silent sensitisation or inadequate dose

▸ 30.2 Bone and Joint Disease in Pregnancy 1
▸ 30.2.1 Pelvic Girdle Pain And Low Back Pain In Pregnancy

Definition

Pelvic girdle pain (PGP) and low back pain (LBP) affect 50-70% of pregnant women. PGP arises from the sacroiliac joints and/or pubic symphysis; LBP from the lumbar spine. Most resolve postpartum; physiotherapy and analgesia are mainstays of treatment.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

PGP 20%; LBP 50-70%; 5-10% severe/disabling

Risk factors

Prior PGP/LBP; multiparity; BMI; high physical workload; stress; pelvic trauma

Symptoms PGP

Pain over SI joints or pubic symphysis; difficulty walking, climbing stairs, turning in bed; positive Patrick-Faber and active SLR tests

Symptoms LBP

Lumbar pain; worse with activity; tender over paraspinal muscles

Differential

Disc prolapse (radiculopathy); cauda equina (saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel — emergency); vertebral fracture (osteoporosis); infection; malignancy

Management

Paracetamol; physiotherapy; pelvic support belt; acupuncture; gentle exercise; avoid heavy lifting

Drugs

Paracetamol first-line; codeine in short courses; avoid NSAIDs (especially 3rd trimester)

Labour

Consider intra-partum physiotherapy; avoid lithotomy; consider water birth; trial of labour if pain manageable

Postpartum

Most resolve within 6 months; physiotherapy; manual therapy; persistent pain in 10%

Prevention

Preconception core stability; avoid excessive weight gain

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PrevalencePGP 20%; LBP 50-70%; 5-10% severe/disabling
Risk factorsPrior PGP/LBP; multiparity; BMI; high physical workload; stress; pelvic trauma
Symptoms PGPPain over SI joints or pubic symphysis; difficulty walking, climbing stairs, turning in bed; positive Patrick-Faber and active SLR tests
Symptoms LBPLumbar pain; worse with activity; tender over paraspinal muscles
DifferentialDisc prolapse (radiculopathy); cauda equina (saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel — emergency); vertebral fracture (osteoporosis); infection; malignancy
ManagementParacetamol; physiotherapy; pelvic support belt; acupuncture; gentle exercise; avoid heavy lifting
DrugsParacetamol first-line; codeine in short courses; avoid NSAIDs (especially 3rd trimester)
LabourConsider intra-partum physiotherapy; avoid lithotomy; consider water birth; trial of labour if pain manageable
PostpartumMost resolve within 6 months; physiotherapy; manual therapy; persistent pain in 10%
PreventionPreconception core stability; avoid excessive weight gain

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pelvic girdle pain and low back pain in pregnancy?

Pelvic girdle pain (PGP) and low back pain (LBP) affect 50-70% of pregnant women. PGP arises from the sacroiliac joints and/or pubic symphysis; LBP from the lumbar spine. Most resolve postpartum; physiotherapy and analgesia are mainstays of treatment.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pelvic girdle pain and low back pain in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Risk factors, Symptoms PGP, Symptoms LBP, Differential. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pelvic girdle pain and low back pain in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pelvic girdle pain and low back pain in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pelvic girdle pain and low back pain in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pelvic girdle pain and low back pain in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.20 Physiological Changes in Pregnancy 4
▸ 30.20.1 Cardiovascular Adaptations In Pregnancy

Definition

Cardiovascular adaptations to pregnancy include increased blood volume, cardiac output and heart rate, reduced systemic vascular resistance, and a hyperdynamic, vasodilated circulation. Recognising these changes is essential to avoid misinterpreting normal pregnancy findings as pathology.

Core Clinical Content

Cardiac output

Rises 30-50%; peaks 32-34 weeks; HR + stroke volume contributions

Stroke volume

Rises by ~30% in first two trimesters; falls in third trimester as IVC compression occurs

Auscultatory findings

Ejection systolic murmur (90%); S3; wide split S2 are normal

ECG changes

Sinus tachycardia; left axis shift; small Q in III/aVF; ST/T changes acceptable

Peripheral oedema

Common in 80%; due to venous stasis and reduced oncotic pressure

Supine hypotension

IVC compression from ~20 weeks — avoid supine position in late pregnancy

Labour haemodynamics

Cardiac output rises further (300-500 mL per contraction)

Postpartum autotransfusion

Placental return + IVC release; CO peaks immediately postpartum

Preload and afterload

Afterload reduced (low SVR); preload increased (plasma volume)

Clinical implications

Chest pain/dyspnoea at rest, syncope, AF, new MR — all abnormal and need workup

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Cardiac outputRises 30-50%; peaks 32-34 weeks; HR + stroke volume contributions
Stroke volumeRises by ~30% in first two trimesters; falls in third trimester as IVC compression occurs
Auscultatory findingsEjection systolic murmur (90%); S3; wide split S2 are normal
ECG changesSinus tachycardia; left axis shift; small Q in III/aVF; ST/T changes acceptable
Peripheral oedemaCommon in 80%; due to venous stasis and reduced oncotic pressure
Supine hypotensionIVC compression from ~20 weeks — avoid supine position in late pregnancy
Labour haemodynamicsCardiac output rises further (300-500 mL per contraction)
Postpartum autotransfusionPlacental return + IVC release; CO peaks immediately postpartum
Preload and afterloadAfterload reduced (low SVR); preload increased (plasma volume)
Clinical implicationsChest pain/dyspnoea at rest, syncope, AF, new MR — all abnormal and need workup

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy?

Cardiovascular adaptations to pregnancy include increased blood volume, cardiac output and heart rate, reduced systemic vascular resistance, and a hyperdynamic, vasodilated circulation. Recognising these changes is essential to avoid misinterpreting normal pregnancy findings as pathology.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy?

Key features include: Cardiac output, Stroke volume, Auscultatory findings, ECG changes, Peripheral oedema. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

A) Placental return + IVC release; CO peaks immediately postpartum

B) Afterload reduced (low SVR); preload increased (plasma volume)

C) Chest pain/dyspnoea at rest, syncope, AF, new MR — all abnormal and need workup

D) Rises 30-50%; peaks 32-34 weeks; HR + stroke volume contributions

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy?

A) Afterload reduced (low SVR); preload increased (plasma volume)

B) Chest pain/dyspnoea at rest, syncope, AF, new MR — all abnormal and need workup

C) Rises 30-50%; peaks 32-34 weeks; HR + stroke volume contributions

D) Rises by ~30% in first two trimesters; falls in third trimester as IVC compression occurs

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy?

A) Chest pain/dyspnoea at rest, syncope, AF, new MR — all abnormal and need workup

B) Rises 30-50%; peaks 32-34 weeks; HR + stroke volume contributions

C) Rises by ~30% in first two trimesters; falls in third trimester as IVC compression occurs

D) Ejection systolic murmur (90%); S3; wide split S2 are normal

Answer: A

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Rises 30-50%; peaks 32-34 weeks; HR + stroke volume contributions
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Rises by ~30% in first two trimesters; falls in third trimester as IVC compression occurs
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Ejection systolic murmur (90%); S3; wide split S2 are normal
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Sinus tachycardia; left axis shift; small Q in III/aVF; ST/T changes acceptable
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Common in 80%; due to venous stasis and reduced oncotic pressure
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | IVC compression from ~20 weeks — avoid supine position in late pregnancy
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Cardiac output rises further (300-500 mL per contraction)
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Placental return + IVC release; CO peaks immediately postpartum
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Afterload reduced (low SVR); preload increased (plasma volume)
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Cardiovascular adaptations in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Chest pain/dyspnoea at rest, syncope, AF, new MR — all abnormal and need workup

Flashcards

Q: Cardiac output?

A: Rises 30-50%; peaks 32-34 weeks; HR + stroke volume contributions

Q: Stroke volume?

A: Rises by ~30% in first two trimesters; falls in third trimester as IVC compression occurs

Q: Auscultatory findings?

A: Ejection systolic murmur (90%); S3; wide split S2 are normal

Q: ECG changes?

A: Sinus tachycardia; left axis shift; small Q in III/aVF; ST/T changes acceptable

Q: Peripheral oedema?

A: Common in 80%; due to venous stasis and reduced oncotic pressure

Q: Supine hypotension?

A: IVC compression from ~20 weeks — avoid supine position in late pregnancy

Q: Labour haemodynamics?

A: Cardiac output rises further (300-500 mL per contraction)

Q: Postpartum autotransfusion?

A: Placental return + IVC release; CO peaks immediately postpartum

Answer Key with Explanations

SBAs

  1. Rises 30-50%; peaks 32-34 weeks; HR + stroke volume contributions
  2. Rises by ~30% in first two trimesters; falls in third trimester as IVC compression occurs
  3. Ejection systolic murmur (90%); S3; wide split S2 are normal
  4. Sinus tachycardia; left axis shift; small Q in III/aVF; ST/T changes acceptable
  5. Common in 80%; due to venous stasis and reduced oncotic pressure
  6. IVC compression from ~20 weeks — avoid supine position in late pregnancy
  7. Cardiac output rises further (300-500 mL per contraction)
  8. Placental return + IVC release; CO peaks immediately postpartum
  9. Afterload reduced (low SVR); preload increased (plasma volume)
  10. Chest pain/dyspnoea at rest, syncope, AF, new MR — all abnormal and need workup

▸ 30.20.2 Haematological Adaptations In Pregnancy

Definition

Haematological changes in pregnancy include plasma volume expansion outpacing red cell mass (dilutional anaemia), leucocytosis, thrombocytopenia (gestational), and a prothrombotic shift in coagulation factors. These influence normal reference ranges and threshold for investigation.

Core Clinical Content

Plasma volume

+40-50%; peaks 32 weeks

Red cell mass

+20-30% (greater with iron supplementation)

Physiological anaemia

Hb ~110 g/L (trimester 1) and 105 g/L (trimester 3) is normal

MCV

Rises; mean ~85-95 fL

White cell count

Rises to 9-15 × 10^9/L; neutrophils predominate

Platelets

Mild fall (gestational thrombocytopenia); <100 × 10^9/L needs workup

Coagulation factors

I, II, VII, VIII, IX, X, XII, XIII rise; protein S falls; APC resistance rises

Fibrinolysis

Reduced (PAI-1, PAI-2 rise) — net prothrombotic state

ESR

Markedly elevated; not useful in pregnancy

Clinical implications

VTE risk 4-5x; consider LMWH prophylaxis at delivery

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Plasma volume+40-50%; peaks 32 weeks
Red cell mass+20-30% (greater with iron supplementation)
Physiological anaemiaHb ~110 g/L (trimester 1) and 105 g/L (trimester 3) is normal
MCVRises; mean ~85-95 fL
White cell countRises to 9-15 × 10^9/L; neutrophils predominate
PlateletsMild fall (gestational thrombocytopenia); <100 × 10^9/L needs workup
Coagulation factorsI, II, VII, VIII, IX, X, XII, XIII rise; protein S falls; APC resistance rises
FibrinolysisReduced (PAI-1, PAI-2 rise) — net prothrombotic state
ESRMarkedly elevated; not useful in pregnancy
Clinical implicationsVTE risk 4-5x; consider LMWH prophylaxis at delivery

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Haematological adaptations in pregnancy?

Haematological changes in pregnancy include plasma volume expansion outpacing red cell mass (dilutional anaemia), leucocytosis, thrombocytopenia (gestational), and a prothrombotic shift in coagulation factors. These influence normal reference ranges and threshold for investigation.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Haematological adaptations in pregnancy?

Key features include: Plasma volume, Red cell mass, Physiological anaemia, MCV, White cell count. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Haematological adaptations in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Haematological adaptations in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Haematological adaptations in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Haematological adaptations in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Mind Map

Haematological adaptations in pregnancy
├── Definition & Core Concept
├── Clinical Features
│   ├── Presentation

├── Investigation
├── Management
│   ├── Acute (pregnancy-safe)
│   └── Long-term

    ├── Maternal
    └── Fetal
