▸ 30.20.3 Physiological Changes In Pregnancy Overview

Definition

Comprehensive overview of the multisystem physiological adaptations to pregnancy, including cardiovascular, respiratory, renal, haematological, metabolic, and endocrine changes that distinguish the pregnant state and influence disease presentation, investigation, and management.

Core Clinical Content

Cardiac output

+30-50%; peaks at 32-34 weeks; falls to baseline by 2 weeks postpartum

Plasma volume

+40-50% by term; mediates dilutional 'physiological anaemia'

Heart rate

+10-20 bpm by third trimester

Blood pressure

Falls in 1st/2nd trimesters; nadir at ~20 weeks; returns to pre-pregnancy by term

Systemic vascular resistance

Falls ~30% due to progesterone-mediated vasodilation

Renal blood flow and GFR

+50-60%; creatinine falls (normal pregnancy creatinine <77 μmol/L)

Tidal volume and minute ventilation

+30-50%; respiratory alkalosis with PCO2 ~30 mmHg

Coagulation

Hypercoagulable state; factors I, VII, VIII, IX, X rise; protein S falls

Thyroid-binding globulin

Rises (oestrogen effect); total T4/T3 rise; free T4 unchanged

Drug pharmacokinetics

Increased Vd, GFR, hepatic flow; altered protein binding; clinical dose adjustment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Cardiac output+30-50%; peaks at 32-34 weeks; falls to baseline by 2 weeks postpartum
Plasma volume+40-50% by term; mediates dilutional 'physiological anaemia'
Heart rate+10-20 bpm by third trimester
Blood pressureFalls in 1st/2nd trimesters; nadir at ~20 weeks; returns to pre-pregnancy by term
Systemic vascular resistanceFalls ~30% due to progesterone-mediated vasodilation
Renal blood flow and GFR+50-60%; creatinine falls (normal pregnancy creatinine <77 μmol/L)
Tidal volume and minute ventilation+30-50%; respiratory alkalosis with PCO2 ~30 mmHg
CoagulationHypercoagulable state; factors I, VII, VIII, IX, X rise; protein S falls
Thyroid-binding globulinRises (oestrogen effect); total T4/T3 rise; free T4 unchanged
Drug pharmacokineticsIncreased Vd, GFR, hepatic flow; altered protein binding; clinical dose adjustment

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?

Comprehensive overview of the multisystem physiological adaptations to pregnancy, including cardiovascular, respiratory, renal, haematological, metabolic, and endocrine changes that distinguish the pregnant state and influence disease presentation, investigation, and management.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?

Key features include: Cardiac output, Plasma volume, Heart rate, Blood pressure, Systemic vascular resistance. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Physiological changes in pregnancy overview diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Physiological changes in pregnancy overview — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Physiological changes in pregnancy overview. Most appropriate next step in management? | Increased Vd, GFR, hepatic flow; altered protein binding; clinical dose adjustment

Flashcards

Q: Cardiac output?

A: +30-50%; peaks at 32-34 weeks; falls to baseline by 2 weeks postpartum

Q: Plasma volume?

A: +40-50% by term; mediates dilutional 'physiological anaemia'

Q: Heart rate?

A: +10-20 bpm by third trimester

Q: Blood pressure?

A: Falls in 1st/2nd trimesters; nadir at ~20 weeks; returns to pre-pregnancy by term

Q: Systemic vascular resistance?

A: Falls ~30% due to progesterone-mediated vasodilation

Q: Renal blood flow and GFR?

A: +50-60%; creatinine falls (normal pregnancy creatinine <77 μmol/L)

Q: Tidal volume and minute ventilation?

A: +30-50%; respiratory alkalosis with PCO2 ~30 mmHg

Q: Coagulation?

A: Hypercoagulable state; factors I, VII, VIII, IX, X rise; protein S falls

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?
  2. A — Key feature of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?
  3. A — Key feature of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?
  4. A — Key feature of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?
  5. A — Key feature of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?
  6. A — Key feature of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?
  7. A — Key feature of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?
  8. A — Key feature of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?
  9. A — Key feature of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?
  10. A — Key feature of Physiological changes in pregnancy overview?

SBAs

  1. +30-50%; peaks at 32-34 weeks; falls to baseline by 2 weeks postpartum
  2. +40-50% by term; mediates dilutional 'physiological anaemia'
  3. +10-20 bpm by third trimester
  4. Falls in 1st/2nd trimesters; nadir at ~20 weeks; returns to pre-pregnancy by term
  5. Falls ~30% due to progesterone-mediated vasodilation
  6. +50-60%; creatinine falls (normal pregnancy creatinine <77 μmol/L)
  7. +30-50%; respiratory alkalosis with PCO2 ~30 mmHg
  8. Hypercoagulable state; factors I, VII, VIII, IX, X rise; protein S falls
  9. Rises (oestrogen effect); total T4/T3 rise; free T4 unchanged
  10. Increased Vd, GFR, hepatic flow; altered protein binding; clinical dose adjustment

▸ 30.20.4 Renal And Urinary Tract Adaptations In Pregnancy

Definition

Renal adaptations in pregnancy include marked increases in renal plasma flow and GFR, leading to lower serum creatinine and urea, glycosuria, and proteinuria. Hydronephrosis (especially right-sided) and ureteric dilatation are common.

Core Clinical Content

Renal plasma flow

+50-80% by mid-pregnancy

GFR

+50%; peaks mid-pregnancy; falls towards term

Serum creatinine

Falls (~35-70 μmol/L); values >77 μmol/L abnormal

Urea

Falls (~1-4 mmol/L); >4.5 mmol/L suggests renal impairment

Uric acid

Falls in 1st/2nd trimesters; rises in pre-eclampsia

Glycosuria

Common (50%) — reduced tubular reabsorption, not necessarily diabetes

Proteinuria

Up to 300 mg/24h normal; >300 mg/24h = significant

Hydronephrosis

Right > left; mechanical obstruction; risk of pyelonephritis

UTI risk

Increased urinary stasis + glycosuria; asymptomatic bacteriuria needs treatment

Drug clearance

Renally cleared drugs (e.g. lithium, digoxin, β-lactams) may need dose adjustment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Renal plasma flow+50-80% by mid-pregnancy
GFR+50%; peaks mid-pregnancy; falls towards term
Serum creatinineFalls (~35-70 μmol/L); values >77 μmol/L abnormal
UreaFalls (~1-4 mmol/L); >4.5 mmol/L suggests renal impairment
Uric acidFalls in 1st/2nd trimesters; rises in pre-eclampsia
GlycosuriaCommon (50%) — reduced tubular reabsorption, not necessarily diabetes
ProteinuriaUp to 300 mg/24h normal; >300 mg/24h = significant
HydronephrosisRight > left; mechanical obstruction; risk of pyelonephritis
UTI riskIncreased urinary stasis + glycosuria; asymptomatic bacteriuria needs treatment
Drug clearanceRenally cleared drugs (e.g. lithium, digoxin, β-lactams) may need dose adjustment

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy?

Renal adaptations in pregnancy include marked increases in renal plasma flow and GFR, leading to lower serum creatinine and urea, glycosuria, and proteinuria. Hydronephrosis (especially right-sided) and ureteric dilatation are common.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy?

Key features include: Renal plasma flow, GFR, Serum creatinine, Urea, Uric acid. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.21 Renal Disease in Pregnancy 5
▸ 30.21.1 Chronic Kidney Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy poses risks of accelerated maternal renal decline, hypertension, pre-eclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and preterm birth. Preconception counselling and tight BP control are key. Pregnancy is generally avoided if creatinine >180 μmol/L (CKD 3b-5).

Core Clinical Content

Definitions

CKD stages 1-5 by GFR; pregnancy generally avoided if Cr >180 μmol/L or GFR <30

Preconception

Optimise BP; switch ACEI/ARB; review immunosuppression; counsel on outcomes

Maternal risks

Worsening renal function (especially Cr >125 μmol/L); pre-eclampsia (40-80%); hypertension

Fetal risks

IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; miscarriage; neonatal morbidity

CKD stage prognosis

Stage 1-2: 90-95% live birth; Stage 3: 70-90%; Stage 4-5: 30-50%

BP control

Labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa; target <130/80; avoid ACEI/ARB

Monitoring

U&E 4-6 weekly; urate; urine PCR; growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler; anaemia management (EPO)

Immunosuppression

Azathioprine, prednisolone, calcineurin inhibitors safe; MMF teratogenic — switch to azathioprine pre-conception

Delivery

Vaginal preferred; consider induction at 37 weeks; ITU availability

Postpartum

Monitor renal recovery; some never return to baseline; future pregnancy counselling

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionsCKD stages 1-5 by GFR; pregnancy generally avoided if Cr >180 μmol/L or GFR <30
PreconceptionOptimise BP; switch ACEI/ARB; review immunosuppression; counsel on outcomes
Maternal risksWorsening renal function (especially Cr >125 μmol/L); pre-eclampsia (40-80%); hypertension
Fetal risksIUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; miscarriage; neonatal morbidity
CKD stage prognosisStage 1-2: 90-95% live birth; Stage 3: 70-90%; Stage 4-5: 30-50%
BP controlLabetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa; target <130/80; avoid ACEI/ARB
MonitoringU&E 4-6 weekly; urate; urine PCR; growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler; anaemia management (EPO)
ImmunosuppressionAzathioprine, prednisolone, calcineurin inhibitors safe; MMF teratogenic — switch to azathioprine pre-conception
DeliveryVaginal preferred; consider induction at 37 weeks; ITU availability
PostpartumMonitor renal recovery; some never return to baseline; future pregnancy counselling

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?

Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy poses risks of accelerated maternal renal decline, hypertension, pre-eclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and preterm birth. Preconception counselling and tight BP control are key. Pregnancy is generally avoided if creatinine >180 μmol/L (CKD 3b-5).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Definitions, Preconception, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, CKD stage prognosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Flashcards

Q: Definitions?

A: CKD stages 1-5 by GFR; pregnancy generally avoided if Cr >180 μmol/L or GFR <30

Q: Preconception?

A: Optimise BP; switch ACEI/ARB; review immunosuppression; counsel on outcomes

Q: Maternal risks?

A: Worsening renal function (especially Cr >125 μmol/L); pre-eclampsia (40-80%); hypertension

Q: Fetal risks?

A: IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; miscarriage; neonatal morbidity

Q: CKD stage prognosis?

A: Stage 1-2: 90-95% live birth; Stage 3: 70-90%; Stage 4-5: 30-50%

Q: BP control?

A: Labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa; target <130/80; avoid ACEI/ARB

Q: Monitoring?

A: U&E 4-6 weekly; urate; urine PCR; growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler; anaemia management (EPO)

Q: Immunosuppression?

A: Azathioprine, prednisolone, calcineurin inhibitors safe; MMF teratogenic — switch to azathioprine pre-conception

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?
  2. A — Key feature of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?
  3. A — Key feature of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?
  4. A — Key feature of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?
  5. A — Key feature of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?
  6. A — Key feature of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?
  7. A — Key feature of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?
  8. A — Key feature of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?
  9. A — Key feature of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?
  10. A — Key feature of Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. CKD stages 1-5 by GFR; pregnancy generally avoided if Cr >180 μmol/L or GFR <30
  2. Optimise BP; switch ACEI/ARB; review immunosuppression; counsel on outcomes
  3. Worsening renal function (especially Cr >125 μmol/L); pre-eclampsia (40-80%); hypertension
  4. IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; miscarriage; neonatal morbidity
  5. Stage 1-2: 90-95% live birth; Stage 3: 70-90%; Stage 4-5: 30-50%
  6. Labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa; target <130/80; avoid ACEI/ARB
  7. U&E 4-6 weekly; urate; urine PCR; growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler; anaemia management (EPO)
  8. Azathioprine, prednisolone, calcineurin inhibitors safe; MMF teratogenic — switch to azathioprine pre-conception
  9. Vaginal preferred; consider induction at 37 weeks; ITU availability
  10. Monitor renal recovery; some never return to baseline; future pregnancy counselling

▸ 30.21.2 Glomerulonephritis In Pregnancy

Definition

Glomerulonephritis (GN) in pregnancy — including IgA nephropathy, membranous nephropathy, focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), and lupus nephritis — requires careful management. Pregnancy is generally safe with preserved renal function but carries risks of disease progression, hypertension, and adverse fetal outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Preconception

Stable renal function >1-2 years; creatinine <125 μmol/L; proteinuria <1 g/24h; BP controlled; switch teratogens (MMF → azathioprine)

IgA nephropathy

Most common primary GN; often well tolerated if Cr <125; pre-eclampsia risk increased

Membranous nephropathy

Nephrotic syndrome; risk of VTE; ACEI/ARB pre-conception; monitor

FSGS

Risk of disease progression; nephrotic-range proteinuria worsens outcomes

Lupus nephritis (class III/IV/V)

Active disease at conception → 50% flare; quiescent for 6 months → 5-10% flare; careful MDT

Maternal risks

Worsening renal function; hypertension; pre-eclampsia; nephrotic syndrome; VTE; infection; CKD progression

Fetal risks

Preterm; IUGR; miscarriage; stillbirth; neonatal complications of prematurity

Investigations

Monthly U&E; urinalysis; urine PCR; 24-h proteinuria; anti-dsDNA, C3/C4 (lupus); anti-PLA2R (membranous); complement

Management

Treat hypertension (labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa); avoid ACEI/ARB; steroids; azathioprine; calcineurin inhibitors; LMWH if nephrotic

Postpartum

Resume ACEI/ARB; review immunosuppression; breastfeeding; contraception (avoid oestrogen if high VTE risk)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PreconceptionStable renal function >1-2 years; creatinine <125 μmol/L; proteinuria <1 g/24h; BP controlled; switch teratogens (MMF → azathioprine)
IgA nephropathyMost common primary GN; often well tolerated if Cr <125; pre-eclampsia risk increased
Membranous nephropathyNephrotic syndrome; risk of VTE; ACEI/ARB pre-conception; monitor
FSGSRisk of disease progression; nephrotic-range proteinuria worsens outcomes
Lupus nephritis (class III/IV/V)Active disease at conception → 50% flare; quiescent for 6 months → 5-10% flare; careful MDT
Maternal risksWorsening renal function; hypertension; pre-eclampsia; nephrotic syndrome; VTE; infection; CKD progression
Fetal risksPreterm; IUGR; miscarriage; stillbirth; neonatal complications of prematurity
InvestigationsMonthly U&E; urinalysis; urine PCR; 24-h proteinuria; anti-dsDNA, C3/C4 (lupus); anti-PLA2R (membranous); complement
ManagementTreat hypertension (labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa); avoid ACEI/ARB; steroids; azathioprine; calcineurin inhibitors; LMWH if nephrotic
PostpartumResume ACEI/ARB; review immunosuppression; breastfeeding; contraception (avoid oestrogen if high VTE risk)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy?

Glomerulonephritis (GN) in pregnancy — including IgA nephropathy, membranous nephropathy, focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), and lupus nephritis — requires careful management. Pregnancy is generally safe with preserved renal function but carries risks of disease progression, hypertension, and adverse fetal outcomes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Preconception, IgA nephropathy, Membranous nephropathy, FSGS, Lupus nephritis (class III/IV/V). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.21.3 Renal Stones In Pregnancy

Definition

Renal stone disease (nephrolithiasis) in pregnancy affects 1 in 1,500 pregnancies. Physiological hydronephrosis and stasis of pregnancy increase risk. Stones may present with flank pain, haematuria, or infection. Most pass spontaneously; management is supportive with intervention reserved for obstruction, infection, or intractable pain.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

1 in 1,500 pregnancies; similar to non-pregnant; right > left

Composition

Calcium oxalate (75%); calcium phosphate; uric acid; struvite; cystine

Risk factors

Personal/family history; dehydration; high oxalate diet; hyperparathyroidism; gout; UTI; IBD; bariatric surgery

Presentation

Severe flank pain radiating to groin; haematuria (90%); nausea/vomiting; dysuria; may mimic abruption, appendicitis, pyelonephritis

Investigations

Urine dipstick + culture; USS (first-line; safe; hydronephrosis common); MRI urogram if needed; low-dose CT rarely

Differential

Pyelonephritis; appendicitis; placental abruption; ovarian torsion; ectopic; AAA (older)

Management conservative

Hydration; analgesia (paracetamol; codeine; morphine; avoid NSAIDs especially in 3rd trimester); tamsulosin (medical expulsive)

Indications for intervention

Obstruction with infection; bilateral obstruction; solitary kidney; intractable pain/vomiting; declining renal function

Interventions

Ureteric stent (PCN if stenting fails); ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy (in experienced hands); ESWL contraindicated

Delivery

Not affected unless obstructed in labour; vaginal preferred; monitor renal function

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence1 in 1,500 pregnancies; similar to non-pregnant; right > left
CompositionCalcium oxalate (75%); calcium phosphate; uric acid; struvite; cystine
Risk factorsPersonal/family history; dehydration; high oxalate diet; hyperparathyroidism; gout; UTI; IBD; bariatric surgery
PresentationSevere flank pain radiating to groin; haematuria (90%); nausea/vomiting; dysuria; may mimic abruption, appendicitis, pyelonephritis
InvestigationsUrine dipstick + culture; USS (first-line; safe; hydronephrosis common); MRI urogram if needed; low-dose CT rarely
DifferentialPyelonephritis; appendicitis; placental abruption; ovarian torsion; ectopic; AAA (older)
Management conservativeHydration; analgesia (paracetamol; codeine; morphine; avoid NSAIDs especially in 3rd trimester); tamsulosin (medical expulsive)
Indications for interventionObstruction with infection; bilateral obstruction; solitary kidney; intractable pain/vomiting; declining renal function
InterventionsUreteric stent (PCN if stenting fails); ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy (in experienced hands); ESWL contraindicated
DeliveryNot affected unless obstructed in labour; vaginal preferred; monitor renal function

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Renal stones in pregnancy?

Renal stone disease (nephrolithiasis) in pregnancy affects 1 in 1,500 pregnancies. Physiological hydronephrosis and stasis of pregnancy increase risk. Stones may present with flank pain, haematuria, or infection. Most pass spontaneously; management is supportive with intervention reserved for obstruction, infection, or intractable pain.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Renal stones in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Composition, Risk factors, Presentation, Investigations. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Renal stones in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Renal stones in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Renal stones in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Renal stones in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

B) Hydration; analgesia (paracetamol; codeine; morphine; avoid NSAIDs especially in 3rd trimester); tamsulosin (medical expulsive)

C) Obstruction with infection; bilateral obstruction; solitary kidney; intractable pain/vomiting; declining renal function

D) Ureteric stent (PCN if stenting fails); ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy (in experienced hands); ESWL contraindicated

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Renal stones in pregnancy?

A) Hydration; analgesia (paracetamol; codeine; morphine; avoid NSAIDs especially in 3rd trimester); tamsulosin (medical expulsive)

B) Obstruction with infection; bilateral obstruction; solitary kidney; intractable pain/vomiting; declining renal function

C) Ureteric stent (PCN if stenting fails); ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy (in experienced hands); ESWL contraindicated

D) Not affected unless obstructed in labour; vaginal preferred; monitor renal function

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Renal stones in pregnancy?

A) Obstruction with infection; bilateral obstruction; solitary kidney; intractable pain/vomiting; declining renal function

B) Ureteric stent (PCN if stenting fails); ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy (in experienced hands); ESWL contraindicated

C) Not affected unless obstructed in labour; vaginal preferred; monitor renal function

D) 1 in 1,500 pregnancies; similar to non-pregnant; right > left

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Renal stones in pregnancy?

A) Ureteric stent (PCN if stenting fails); ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy (in experienced hands); ESWL contraindicated

B) Not affected unless obstructed in labour; vaginal preferred; monitor renal function

C) 1 in 1,500 pregnancies; similar to non-pregnant; right > left

D) Calcium oxalate (75%); calcium phosphate; uric acid; struvite; cystine

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Renal stones in pregnancy?

A) Not affected unless obstructed in labour; vaginal preferred; monitor renal function

B) 1 in 1,500 pregnancies; similar to non-pregnant; right > left

C) Calcium oxalate (75%); calcium phosphate; uric acid; struvite; cystine

D) Personal/family history; dehydration; high oxalate diet; hyperparathyroidism; gout; UTI; IBD; bariatric surgery

Answer: A

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Renal stones in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Calcium oxalate (75%); calcium phosphate; uric acid; struvite; cystine

Flashcards

Q: Incidence?

A: 1 in 1,500 pregnancies; similar to non-pregnant; right > left

Q: Composition?

A: Calcium oxalate (75%); calcium phosphate; uric acid; struvite; cystine

Q: Risk factors?

A: Personal/family history; dehydration; high oxalate diet; hyperparathyroidism; gout; UTI; IBD; bariatric surgery

Q: Presentation?

A: Severe flank pain radiating to groin; haematuria (90%); nausea/vomiting; dysuria; may mimic abruption, appendicitis, pyelonephritis

Q: Investigations?

A: Urine dipstick + culture; USS (first-line; safe; hydronephrosis common); MRI urogram if needed; low-dose CT rarely

Q: Differential?

A: Pyelonephritis; appendicitis; placental abruption; ovarian torsion; ectopic; AAA (older)

Q: Management conservative?

A: Hydration; analgesia (paracetamol; codeine; morphine; avoid NSAIDs especially in 3rd trimester); tamsulosin (medical expulsive)

Q: Indications for intervention?

A: Obstruction with infection; bilateral obstruction; solitary kidney; intractable pain/vomiting; declining renal function

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 30.21.4 Renal Transplant And Pregnancy

Definition

Pregnancy in renal transplant recipients is achievable with stable graft function but requires multidisciplinary care. Optimal outcomes require stable graft function for >1-2 years, no recent rejection, baseline creatinine <125 μmol/L, and stable immunosuppression.

Core Clinical Content

Eligibility

Stable graft >1-2 years; Cr <125 μmol/L; proteinuria <0.5 g/24h; no recent rejection

Maternal risks

Pre-eclampsia (30-50%); infection; graft rejection (10%); deterioration of renal function

Fetal risks

Preterm birth; IUGR; low birth weight; neonatal immunosuppression

Safe immunosuppression

Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, ciclosporin); azathioprine; prednisolone — all pregnancy-compatible

Avoid in pregnancy

Mycophenolate (teratogenic — switch pre-conception); mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus)

Monitoring

Monthly U&E; trough tacrolimus levels; proteinuria; CMV/ BK viral load; growth scans

BP control

Labetalol; nifedipine; target <130/80

Delivery

Vaginal if obstetrically suitable; caesarean if pelvic kidney obstructing birth canal

Postpartum

Continue immunosuppression; breastfeeding generally safe; re-introduce MMF after weaning

Contraception

Avoid oestrogen (VTE/immunosuppression); progesterone-only or IUD preferred

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EligibilityStable graft >1-2 years; Cr <125 μmol/L; proteinuria <0.5 g/24h; no recent rejection
Maternal risksPre-eclampsia (30-50%); infection; graft rejection (10%); deterioration of renal function
Fetal risksPreterm birth; IUGR; low birth weight; neonatal immunosuppression
Safe immunosuppressionCalcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, ciclosporin); azathioprine; prednisolone — all pregnancy-compatible
Avoid in pregnancyMycophenolate (teratogenic — switch pre-conception); mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus)
MonitoringMonthly U&E; trough tacrolimus levels; proteinuria; CMV/ BK viral load; growth scans
BP controlLabetalol; nifedipine; target <130/80
DeliveryVaginal if obstetrically suitable; caesarean if pelvic kidney obstructing birth canal
PostpartumContinue immunosuppression; breastfeeding generally safe; re-introduce MMF after weaning
ContraceptionAvoid oestrogen (VTE/immunosuppression); progesterone-only or IUD preferred

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Renal transplant and pregnancy?

Pregnancy in renal transplant recipients is achievable with stable graft function but requires multidisciplinary care. Optimal outcomes require stable graft function for >1-2 years, no recent rejection, baseline creatinine <125 μmol/L, and stable immunosuppression.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Renal transplant and pregnancy?

Key features include: Eligibility, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, Safe immunosuppression, Avoid in pregnancy. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Renal transplant and pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Renal transplant and pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Renal transplant and pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Renal transplant and pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.21.5 Urinary Tract Infection In Pregnancy

Definition

Urinary tract infection in pregnancy is common due to urinary stasis, glycosuria, and altered immunity. Asymptomatic bacteriuria (2-10%) must be treated because it frequently progresses to pyelonephritis (20-40%), with risk of preterm labour and low birth weight.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

Asymptomatic bacteriuria 2-10%; cystitis 1-4%; pyelonephritis 1-2%

Pathogens

E. coli (75-90%); Klebsiella, Proteus, group B Strep, Enterococcus

Asymptomatic bacteriuria

10^5 CFU/mL on MSU — must be treated even without symptoms

Cystitis

Dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain; no fever or systemic features

Pyelonephritis

Fever, flank pain, nausea, vomiting; can cause sepsis, preterm labour, ARDS

Investigations

MSU culture; treat empirically in pyelonephritis; blood cultures if febrile

Treatment ASB/cystitis

Nitrofurantoin (avoid near term), cephalexin, amoxicillin — 7 days

Pyelonephritis

IV ceftriaxone or co-amoxiclav; admit; IV fluids; switch to oral when afebrile 24-48 h

Drug safety

Avoid: trimethoprim (1st trimester — neural tube defects), quinolones, tetracyclines

Recurrence

Monthly MSU until delivery; prophylactic nitrofurantoin or cephalexin if recurrent

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PrevalenceAsymptomatic bacteriuria 2-10%; cystitis 1-4%; pyelonephritis 1-2%
PathogensE. coli (75-90%); Klebsiella, Proteus, group B Strep, Enterococcus
Asymptomatic bacteriuria>10^5 CFU/mL on MSU — must be treated even without symptoms
CystitisDysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain; no fever or systemic features
PyelonephritisFever, flank pain, nausea, vomiting; can cause sepsis, preterm labour, ARDS
InvestigationsMSU culture; treat empirically in pyelonephritis; blood cultures if febrile
Treatment ASB/cystitisNitrofurantoin (avoid near term), cephalexin, amoxicillin — 7 days
PyelonephritisIV ceftriaxone or co-amoxiclav; admit; IV fluids; switch to oral when afebrile 24-48 h
Drug safetyAvoid: trimethoprim (1st trimester — neural tube defects), quinolones, tetracyclines
RecurrenceMonthly MSU until delivery; prophylactic nitrofurantoin or cephalexin if recurrent

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

Urinary tract infection in pregnancy is common due to urinary stasis, glycosuria, and altered immunity. Asymptomatic bacteriuria (2-10%) must be treated because it frequently progresses to pyelonephritis (20-40%), with risk of preterm labour and low birth weight.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Pathogens, Asymptomatic bacteriuria, Cystitis, Pyelonephritis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Urinary tract infection in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Urinary tract infection in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. Which of the following is true about Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

A) Dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain; no fever or systemic features

B) Fever, flank pain, nausea, vomiting; can cause sepsis, preterm labour, ARDS

C) MSU culture; treat empirically in pyelonephritis; blood cultures if febrile

D) Nitrofurantoin (avoid near term), cephalexin, amoxicillin — 7 days

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

A) Fever, flank pain, nausea, vomiting; can cause sepsis, preterm labour, ARDS

B) MSU culture; treat empirically in pyelonephritis; blood cultures if febrile

C) Nitrofurantoin (avoid near term), cephalexin, amoxicillin — 7 days

D) IV ceftriaxone or co-amoxiclav; admit; IV fluids; switch to oral when afebrile 24-48 h

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

A) MSU culture; treat empirically in pyelonephritis; blood cultures if febrile

B) Nitrofurantoin (avoid near term), cephalexin, amoxicillin — 7 days

C) IV ceftriaxone or co-amoxiclav; admit; IV fluids; switch to oral when afebrile 24-48 h

D) Avoid: trimethoprim (1st trimester — neural tube defects), quinolones, tetracyclines

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

A) Nitrofurantoin (avoid near term), cephalexin, amoxicillin — 7 days

B) IV ceftriaxone or co-amoxiclav; admit; IV fluids; switch to oral when afebrile 24-48 h

C) Avoid: trimethoprim (1st trimester — neural tube defects), quinolones, tetracyclines

D) Monthly MSU until delivery; prophylactic nitrofurantoin or cephalexin if recurrent

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

A) IV ceftriaxone or co-amoxiclav; admit; IV fluids; switch to oral when afebrile 24-48 h

B) Avoid: trimethoprim (1st trimester — neural tube defects), quinolones, tetracyclines

C) Monthly MSU until delivery; prophylactic nitrofurantoin or cephalexin if recurrent

D) Asymptomatic bacteriuria 2-10%; cystitis 1-4%; pyelonephritis 1-2%

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

A) Avoid: trimethoprim (1st trimester — neural tube defects), quinolones, tetracyclines

B) Monthly MSU until delivery; prophylactic nitrofurantoin or cephalexin if recurrent

C) Asymptomatic bacteriuria 2-10%; cystitis 1-4%; pyelonephritis 1-2%

D) E. coli (75-90%); Klebsiella, Proteus, group B Strep, Enterococcus

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Urinary tract infection in pregnancy?

A) Monthly MSU until delivery; prophylactic nitrofurantoin or cephalexin if recurrent

B) Asymptomatic bacteriuria 2-10%; cystitis 1-4%; pyelonephritis 1-2%

C) E. coli (75-90%); Klebsiella, Proteus, group B Strep, Enterococcus

D) >10^5 CFU/mL on MSU — must be treated even without symptoms

Answer: A

Flashcards

Q: Prevalence?

A: Asymptomatic bacteriuria 2-10%; cystitis 1-4%; pyelonephritis 1-2%

Q: Pathogens?

A: E. coli (75-90%); Klebsiella, Proteus, group B Strep, Enterococcus

Q: Asymptomatic bacteriuria?

A: >10^5 CFU/mL on MSU — must be treated even without symptoms

Q: Cystitis?

A: Dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain; no fever or systemic features

Q: Pyelonephritis?

A: Fever, flank pain, nausea, vomiting; can cause sepsis, preterm labour, ARDS

Q: Investigations?

A: MSU culture; treat empirically in pyelonephritis; blood cultures if febrile

Q: Treatment ASB/cystitis?

A: Nitrofurantoin (avoid near term), cephalexin, amoxicillin — 7 days

Q: Pyelonephritis?

A: IV ceftriaxone or co-amoxiclav; admit; IV fluids; switch to oral when afebrile 24-48 h

▸ 30.22 Respiratory Disease in Pregnancy 5
▸ 30.22.1 Asthma In Pregnancy

Definition

Asthma is the most common chronic respiratory disease in pregnancy, affecting 4-8% of women. Control may improve, worsen, or stay the same; poorly controlled asthma poses greater risk to mother and fetus than asthma medications.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

4-8% of pregnancies; most common chronic respiratory condition

Course

~30% improve; ~30% worsen; ~40% unchanged — variable, monitor closely

Maternal risks (uncontrolled)

Pre-eclampsia; GDM; preterm birth; caesarean; pneumonia

Fetal risks (uncontrolled)

IUGR; low birth weight; stillbirth; neonatal hypoxia

Safe medications

Inhaled SABA (salbutamol); ICS (budesonide safe); LABA; theophylline; oral steroids if needed

Avoid

Uncontrolled symptoms > medications; leukotriene antagonists data limited but not contraindicated

Acute severe asthma

Same management as non-pregnant — oxygen, SABA, systemic steroids, magnesium

Monitoring

Peak expiratory flow; symptom diary; consider FeNO; review every 4 weeks

Labour and delivery

Continue inhaled therapy; SABA if needed; epidural analgesia preferred

Postpartum

Continue medications; breastfeeding compatible; monitor infant for steroid exposure if high-dose oral

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence4-8% of pregnancies; most common chronic respiratory condition
Course~30% improve; ~30% worsen; ~40% unchanged — variable, monitor closely
Maternal risks (uncontrolled)Pre-eclampsia; GDM; preterm birth; caesarean; pneumonia
Fetal risks (uncontrolled)IUGR; low birth weight; stillbirth; neonatal hypoxia
Safe medicationsInhaled SABA (salbutamol); ICS (budesonide safe); LABA; theophylline; oral steroids if needed
AvoidUncontrolled symptoms > medications; leukotriene antagonists data limited but not contraindicated
Acute severe asthmaSame management as non-pregnant — oxygen, SABA, systemic steroids, magnesium
MonitoringPeak expiratory flow; symptom diary; consider FeNO; review every 4 weeks
Labour and deliveryContinue inhaled therapy; SABA if needed; epidural analgesia preferred
PostpartumContinue medications; breastfeeding compatible; monitor infant for steroid exposure if high-dose oral

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Asthma in pregnancy?

Asthma is the most common chronic respiratory disease in pregnancy, affecting 4-8% of women. Control may improve, worsen, or stay the same; poorly controlled asthma poses greater risk to mother and fetus than asthma medications.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Asthma in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Course, Maternal risks (uncontrolled), Fetal risks (uncontrolled), Safe medications. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Asthma in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Asthma in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Asthma in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Asthma in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.22.2 Cystic Fibrosis In Pregnancy

Definition

Cystic fibrosis (CF) in pregnancy is increasingly common as life expectancy improves (now >40 years). Pre-conception optimisation and multidisciplinary care are essential. Pregnancy is usually well tolerated in well-nourished women with good lung function (FEV1 >50-60% predicted) but carries significant risk if lung disease is advanced.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

CF carriers 1 in 25 (Caucasians); affected patients now survive to reproductive age

Preconception

Optimise lung function (FEV1 >60%); nutritional status (BMI >20); screen for CFRD; genetic counselling; partner screening; review medications

Fertility

Women with CF — normal fertility (thick cervical mucus may be issue); men — 98% azoospermia (CBAVD)

Maternal risks

Worsening lung function; cor pulmonale; malnutrition; CFRD; CFRD-related complications; infection (Pseudomonas, Staph aureus)

Fetal risks

IUGR; preterm birth; low birth weight; congenital malformations (CF if partner carrier)

Antenatal care

MDT (respiratory, obstetric, CF, dietician, physio); monthly spirometry; weight; sputum culture; CFTR modulators (limited data; case-by-case)

Treatment

Continue airway clearance; nebulisers (safe); dornase alfa; azithromycin; IV antibiotics for exacerbations; insulin for CFRD; pancreatic enzymes; vitamins (ADEK)

Drug safety

Avoid tetracyclines; fluoroquinolones (limited data); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); CFTR modulators — limited data, consider benefit

Delivery

Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage (avoid exhaustion); consider epidural; high-risk anaesthesia review

Prognosis

Pregnancy safe if FEV1 >60%; 10-20% decline in FEV1 may be permanent in advanced disease; 80%+ live birth rate

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyCF carriers 1 in 25 (Caucasians); affected patients now survive to reproductive age
PreconceptionOptimise lung function (FEV1 >60%); nutritional status (BMI >20); screen for CFRD; genetic counselling; partner screening; review medications
FertilityWomen with CF — normal fertility (thick cervical mucus may be issue); men — 98% azoospermia (CBAVD)
Maternal risksWorsening lung function; cor pulmonale; malnutrition; CFRD; CFRD-related complications; infection (Pseudomonas, Staph aureus)
Fetal risksIUGR; preterm birth; low birth weight; congenital malformations (CF if partner carrier)
Antenatal careMDT (respiratory, obstetric, CF, dietician, physio); monthly spirometry; weight; sputum culture; CFTR modulators (limited data; case-by-case)
TreatmentContinue airway clearance; nebulisers (safe); dornase alfa; azithromycin; IV antibiotics for exacerbations; insulin for CFRD; pancreatic enzymes; vitamins (ADEK)
Drug safetyAvoid tetracyclines; fluoroquinolones (limited data); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); CFTR modulators — limited data, consider benefit
DeliveryVaginal preferred; assisted second stage (avoid exhaustion); consider epidural; high-risk anaesthesia review
PrognosisPregnancy safe if FEV1 >60%; 10-20% decline in FEV1 may be permanent in advanced disease; 80%+ live birth rate

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

Cystic fibrosis (CF) in pregnancy is increasingly common as life expectancy improves (now >40 years). Pre-conception optimisation and multidisciplinary care are essential. Pregnancy is usually well tolerated in well-nourished women with good lung function (FEV1 >50-60% predicted) but carries significant risk if lung disease is advanced.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Preconception, Fertility, Maternal risks, Fetal risks. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

D) MDT (respiratory, obstetric, CF, dietician, physio); monthly spirometry; weight; sputum culture; CFTR modulators (limited data; case-by-case)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

A) Worsening lung function; cor pulmonale; malnutrition; CFRD; CFRD-related complications; infection (Pseudomonas, Staph aureus)

B) IUGR; preterm birth; low birth weight; congenital malformations (CF if partner carrier)

C) MDT (respiratory, obstetric, CF, dietician, physio); monthly spirometry; weight; sputum culture; CFTR modulators (limited data; case-by-case)

D) Continue airway clearance; nebulisers (safe); dornase alfa; azithromycin; IV antibiotics for exacerbations; insulin for CFRD; pancreatic enzymes; vitamins (ADEK)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

A) IUGR; preterm birth; low birth weight; congenital malformations (CF if partner carrier)

B) MDT (respiratory, obstetric, CF, dietician, physio); monthly spirometry; weight; sputum culture; CFTR modulators (limited data; case-by-case)

C) Continue airway clearance; nebulisers (safe); dornase alfa; azithromycin; IV antibiotics for exacerbations; insulin for CFRD; pancreatic enzymes; vitamins (ADEK)

D) Avoid tetracyclines; fluoroquinolones (limited data); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); CFTR modulators — limited data, consider benefit

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

A) MDT (respiratory, obstetric, CF, dietician, physio); monthly spirometry; weight; sputum culture; CFTR modulators (limited data; case-by-case)

B) Continue airway clearance; nebulisers (safe); dornase alfa; azithromycin; IV antibiotics for exacerbations; insulin for CFRD; pancreatic enzymes; vitamins (ADEK)

C) Avoid tetracyclines; fluoroquinolones (limited data); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); CFTR modulators — limited data, consider benefit

D) Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage (avoid exhaustion); consider epidural; high-risk anaesthesia review

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

A) Continue airway clearance; nebulisers (safe); dornase alfa; azithromycin; IV antibiotics for exacerbations; insulin for CFRD; pancreatic enzymes; vitamins (ADEK)

B) Avoid tetracyclines; fluoroquinolones (limited data); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); CFTR modulators — limited data, consider benefit

C) Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage (avoid exhaustion); consider epidural; high-risk anaesthesia review

D) Pregnancy safe if FEV1 >60%; 10-20% decline in FEV1 may be permanent in advanced disease; 80%+ live birth rate

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

A) Avoid tetracyclines; fluoroquinolones (limited data); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); CFTR modulators — limited data, consider benefit

B) Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage (avoid exhaustion); consider epidural; high-risk anaesthesia review

C) Pregnancy safe if FEV1 >60%; 10-20% decline in FEV1 may be permanent in advanced disease; 80%+ live birth rate

D) CF carriers 1 in 25 (Caucasians); affected patients now survive to reproductive age

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

A) Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage (avoid exhaustion); consider epidural; high-risk anaesthesia review

B) Pregnancy safe if FEV1 >60%; 10-20% decline in FEV1 may be permanent in advanced disease; 80%+ live birth rate

C) CF carriers 1 in 25 (Caucasians); affected patients now survive to reproductive age

D) Optimise lung function (FEV1 >60%); nutritional status (BMI >20); screen for CFRD; genetic counselling; partner screening; review medications

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Cystic fibrosis in pregnancy?

A) Pregnancy safe if FEV1 >60%; 10-20% decline in FEV1 may be permanent in advanced disease; 80%+ live birth rate

B) CF carriers 1 in 25 (Caucasians); affected patients now survive to reproductive age

C) Optimise lung function (FEV1 >60%); nutritional status (BMI >20); screen for CFRD; genetic counselling; partner screening; review medications

D) Women with CF — normal fertility (thick cervical mucus may be issue); men — 98% azoospermia (CBAVD)

Answer: A

Flashcards

Q: Epidemiology?

A: CF carriers 1 in 25 (Caucasians); affected patients now survive to reproductive age

Q: Preconception?

A: Optimise lung function (FEV1 >60%); nutritional status (BMI >20); screen for CFRD; genetic counselling; partner screening; review medications

Q: Fertility?

A: Women with CF — normal fertility (thick cervical mucus may be issue); men — 98% azoospermia (CBAVD)

Q: Maternal risks?

A: Worsening lung function; cor pulmonale; malnutrition; CFRD; CFRD-related complications; infection (Pseudomonas, Staph aureus)

Q: Fetal risks?

A: IUGR; preterm birth; low birth weight; congenital malformations (CF if partner carrier)

Q: Antenatal care?

A: MDT (respiratory, obstetric, CF, dietician, physio); monthly spirometry; weight; sputum culture; CFTR modulators (limited data; case-by-case)

Q: Treatment?

A: Continue airway clearance; nebulisers (safe); dornase alfa; azithromycin; IV antibiotics for exacerbations; insulin for CFRD; pancreatic enzymes; vitamins (ADEK)

Q: Drug safety?

A: Avoid tetracyclines; fluoroquinolones (limited data); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); CFTR modulators — limited data, consider benefit

Answer Key with Explanations

▸ 30.22.3 Pneumonia In Pregnancy

Definition

Pneumonia in pregnancy is a leading non-obstetric cause of maternal mortality. Physiological changes (raised diaphragm, reduced functional residual capacity, altered immunity) increase susceptibility and severity. Prompt empirical antibiotics, oxygen, and supportive care are essential.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

0.5-1.5% of pregnancies; leading non-obstetric infection

Pathogens

Strep pneumoniae; H. influenzae; atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia); Legionella; viral (influenza, COVID-19)

Risk factors

Asthma; anaemia; GDM; HIV; immunosuppression; smoking; substance misuse

Presentation

Fever, productive cough, dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, tachypnoea, hypoxia

Investigations

CXR (safe, lead shield); blood cultures; sputum culture; urinary antigens; viral PCR

Empirical therapy

Co-amoxiclav + clarithromycin IV; or ceftriaxone + azithromycin

Severe disease

Oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥94%; consider CPAP; ITU/HDU; early delivery if deteriorating

Influenza

Treat with oseltamivir within 48 h of symptoms

Complications

ARDS; empyema; lung abscess; preterm labour; fetal loss

Prevention

Influenza and pneumococcal vaccination preconception; smoking cessation

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence0.5-1.5% of pregnancies; leading non-obstetric infection
PathogensStrep pneumoniae; H. influenzae; atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia); Legionella; viral (influenza, COVID-19)
Risk factorsAsthma; anaemia; GDM; HIV; immunosuppression; smoking; substance misuse
PresentationFever, productive cough, dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, tachypnoea, hypoxia
InvestigationsCXR (safe, lead shield); blood cultures; sputum culture; urinary antigens; viral PCR
Empirical therapyCo-amoxiclav + clarithromycin IV; or ceftriaxone + azithromycin
Severe diseaseOxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥94%; consider CPAP; ITU/HDU; early delivery if deteriorating
InfluenzaTreat with oseltamivir within 48 h of symptoms
ComplicationsARDS; empyema; lung abscess; preterm labour; fetal loss
PreventionInfluenza and pneumococcal vaccination preconception; smoking cessation

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pneumonia in pregnancy?

Pneumonia in pregnancy is a leading non-obstetric cause of maternal mortality. Physiological changes (raised diaphragm, reduced functional residual capacity, altered immunity) increase susceptibility and severity. Prompt empirical antibiotics, oxygen, and supportive care are essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pneumonia in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Pathogens, Risk factors, Presentation, Investigations. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pneumonia in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pneumonia in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pneumonia in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pneumonia in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.22.4 Sarcoidosis In Pregnancy

Definition

Sarcoidosis is a multisystem granulomatous disease that commonly affects young women. Pregnancy often improves symptoms (immunomodulation), but advanced disease with pulmonary fibrosis, cardiac, or neurological involvement carries significant risk. Management is usually supportive; most medications are safe.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Commoner in women; peak incidence 20-40 years; often stable in pregnancy

Effect of pregnancy

Symptoms often improve (rising cortisol, Th2 shift); flares postpartum

Maternal risks

Worsening if advanced fibrosis; cor pulmonale; cardiac sarcoid (arrhythmia, heart block); neurological (cranial nerve palsies, seizures); hypercalcaemia; uveitis

Fetal risks

Minimal if mild disease; preterm and LBW if severe; neonatal hypercalcaemia if mother hypercalcaemic

Investigations

CXR (with shielding); spirometry; CT chest if needed; serum ACE (less reliable in pregnancy); serum calcium; LFTs; ECG; echo if cardiac

Biopsy

Often unnecessary if clear clinical picture; defer to postpartum; or specific tissue if needed (lymph node)

Management

Observation if asymptomatic; corticosteroids for active disease; immunosuppressants if severe; hydroxychloroquine for skin/eye

Drugs safe

Prednisolone; azathioprine; hydroxychloroquine; infliximab (limited data)

Avoid

Methotrexate (teratogenic); cyclophosphamide; avoid vitamin D supplementation in those with hypercalcaemia

Postpartum

Continue treatment; monitor for flare; contraception (avoid oestrogen if hypercoagulable state)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyCommoner in women; peak incidence 20-40 years; often stable in pregnancy
Effect of pregnancySymptoms often improve (rising cortisol, Th2 shift); flares postpartum
Maternal risksWorsening if advanced fibrosis; cor pulmonale; cardiac sarcoid (arrhythmia, heart block); neurological (cranial nerve palsies, seizures); hypercalcaemia; uveitis
Fetal risksMinimal if mild disease; preterm and LBW if severe; neonatal hypercalcaemia if mother hypercalcaemic
InvestigationsCXR (with shielding); spirometry; CT chest if needed; serum ACE (less reliable in pregnancy); serum calcium; LFTs; ECG; echo if cardiac
BiopsyOften unnecessary if clear clinical picture; defer to postpartum; or specific tissue if needed (lymph node)
ManagementObservation if asymptomatic; corticosteroids for active disease; immunosuppressants if severe; hydroxychloroquine for skin/eye
Drugs safePrednisolone; azathioprine; hydroxychloroquine; infliximab (limited data)
AvoidMethotrexate (teratogenic); cyclophosphamide; avoid vitamin D supplementation in those with hypercalcaemia
PostpartumContinue treatment; monitor for flare; contraception (avoid oestrogen if hypercoagulable state)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

Sarcoidosis is a multisystem granulomatous disease that commonly affects young women. Pregnancy often improves symptoms (immunomodulation), but advanced disease with pulmonary fibrosis, cardiac, or neurological involvement carries significant risk. Management is usually supportive; most medications are safe.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Effect of pregnancy, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, Investigations. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Sarcoidosis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Sarcoidosis in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

A) Commoner in women; peak incidence 20-40 years; often stable in pregnancy

B) Symptoms often improve (rising cortisol, Th2 shift); flares postpartum

C) Worsening if advanced fibrosis; cor pulmonale; cardiac sarcoid (arrhythmia, heart block); neurological (cranial nerve palsies, seizures); hypercalcaemia; uveitis

D) Minimal if mild disease; preterm and LBW if severe; neonatal hypercalcaemia if mother hypercalcaemic

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

A) Symptoms often improve (rising cortisol, Th2 shift); flares postpartum

B) Worsening if advanced fibrosis; cor pulmonale; cardiac sarcoid (arrhythmia, heart block); neurological (cranial nerve palsies, seizures); hypercalcaemia; uveitis

C) Minimal if mild disease; preterm and LBW if severe; neonatal hypercalcaemia if mother hypercalcaemic

D) CXR (with shielding); spirometry; CT chest if needed; serum ACE (less reliable in pregnancy); serum calcium; LFTs; ECG; echo if cardiac

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

A) Worsening if advanced fibrosis; cor pulmonale; cardiac sarcoid (arrhythmia, heart block); neurological (cranial nerve palsies, seizures); hypercalcaemia; uveitis

B) Minimal if mild disease; preterm and LBW if severe; neonatal hypercalcaemia if mother hypercalcaemic

C) CXR (with shielding); spirometry; CT chest if needed; serum ACE (less reliable in pregnancy); serum calcium; LFTs; ECG; echo if cardiac

D) Often unnecessary if clear clinical picture; defer to postpartum; or specific tissue if needed (lymph node)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

A) Minimal if mild disease; preterm and LBW if severe; neonatal hypercalcaemia if mother hypercalcaemic

B) CXR (with shielding); spirometry; CT chest if needed; serum ACE (less reliable in pregnancy); serum calcium; LFTs; ECG; echo if cardiac

C) Often unnecessary if clear clinical picture; defer to postpartum; or specific tissue if needed (lymph node)

D) Observation if asymptomatic; corticosteroids for active disease; immunosuppressants if severe; hydroxychloroquine for skin/eye

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

A) CXR (with shielding); spirometry; CT chest if needed; serum ACE (less reliable in pregnancy); serum calcium; LFTs; ECG; echo if cardiac

B) Often unnecessary if clear clinical picture; defer to postpartum; or specific tissue if needed (lymph node)

C) Observation if asymptomatic; corticosteroids for active disease; immunosuppressants if severe; hydroxychloroquine for skin/eye

D) Prednisolone; azathioprine; hydroxychloroquine; infliximab (limited data)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

A) Often unnecessary if clear clinical picture; defer to postpartum; or specific tissue if needed (lymph node)

B) Observation if asymptomatic; corticosteroids for active disease; immunosuppressants if severe; hydroxychloroquine for skin/eye

C) Prednisolone; azathioprine; hydroxychloroquine; infliximab (limited data)

D) Methotrexate (teratogenic); cyclophosphamide; avoid vitamin D supplementation in those with hypercalcaemia

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

A) Observation if asymptomatic; corticosteroids for active disease; immunosuppressants if severe; hydroxychloroquine for skin/eye

B) Prednisolone; azathioprine; hydroxychloroquine; infliximab (limited data)

C) Methotrexate (teratogenic); cyclophosphamide; avoid vitamin D supplementation in those with hypercalcaemia

D) Continue treatment; monitor for flare; contraception (avoid oestrogen if hypercoagulable state)

Flashcards

Q: Epidemiology?

A: Commoner in women; peak incidence 20-40 years; often stable in pregnancy

Q: Effect of pregnancy?

A: Symptoms often improve (rising cortisol, Th2 shift); flares postpartum

Q: Maternal risks?

A: Worsening if advanced fibrosis; cor pulmonale; cardiac sarcoid (arrhythmia, heart block); neurological (cranial nerve palsies, seizures); hypercalcaemia; uveitis

Q: Fetal risks?

A: Minimal if mild disease; preterm and LBW if severe; neonatal hypercalcaemia if mother hypercalcaemic

Q: Investigations?

A: CXR (with shielding); spirometry; CT chest if needed; serum ACE (less reliable in pregnancy); serum calcium; LFTs; ECG; echo if cardiac

Q: Biopsy?

A: Often unnecessary if clear clinical picture; defer to postpartum; or specific tissue if needed (lymph node)

Q: Management?

A: Observation if asymptomatic; corticosteroids for active disease; immunosuppressants if severe; hydroxychloroquine for skin/eye

Q: Drugs safe?

A: Prednisolone; azathioprine; hydroxychloroquine; infliximab (limited data)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.22.5 Tuberculosis In Pregnancy

Definition

Tuberculosis in pregnancy, both pulmonary and extrapulmonary, requires prompt diagnosis and treatment. Active TB poses greater risk to the pregnancy than the standard anti-TB regimen, which is largely safe.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Highest in low/middle-income countries; latent TB common

Maternal risks

Active TB: ↑preterm birth, low birth weight, pre-eclampsia, maternal mortality

Diagnosis

Sputum AFB + culture + NAAT; CXR (with shielding); TST/IGRA; if extrapulmonary, biopsy/aspiration

Treatment of active TB

Standard RIF + INH + PZA + EMB for 2 months, then RIF + INH for 7 months

Drug safety

Rifampicin, isoniazid, ethambutol — all safe; pyrazinamide safe

Avoid

Streptomycin (ototoxic to fetus); second-line drugs limited data

Latent TB

Treat postpartum usually; if high-risk recent exposure, treat in pregnancy with INH + pyridoxine 9 months

BCG

Contraindicated in pregnancy; give to neonate at birth if mother has active TB

Multidrug-resistant TB

Individualised; expert input; bedaquiline, linezolid — limited safety data

Postpartum and breastfeeding

Continue treatment; breastfeeding safe with non-infectious TB; mother-infant separation only if active pulmonary

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyHighest in low/middle-income countries; latent TB common
Maternal risksActive TB: ↑preterm birth, low birth weight, pre-eclampsia, maternal mortality
DiagnosisSputum AFB + culture + NAAT; CXR (with shielding); TST/IGRA; if extrapulmonary, biopsy/aspiration
Treatment of active TBStandard RIF + INH + PZA + EMB for 2 months, then RIF + INH for 7 months
Drug safetyRifampicin, isoniazid, ethambutol — all safe; pyrazinamide safe
AvoidStreptomycin (ototoxic to fetus); second-line drugs limited data
Latent TBTreat postpartum usually; if high-risk recent exposure, treat in pregnancy with INH + pyridoxine 9 months
BCGContraindicated in pregnancy; give to neonate at birth if mother has active TB
Multidrug-resistant TBIndividualised; expert input; bedaquiline, linezolid — limited safety data
Postpartum and breastfeedingContinue treatment; breastfeeding safe with non-infectious TB; mother-infant separation only if active pulmonary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Tuberculosis in pregnancy?

Tuberculosis in pregnancy, both pulmonary and extrapulmonary, requires prompt diagnosis and treatment. Active TB poses greater risk to the pregnancy than the standard anti-TB regimen, which is largely safe.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Tuberculosis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Maternal risks, Diagnosis, Treatment of active TB, Drug safety. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Tuberculosis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Tuberculosis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Tuberculosis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Tuberculosis in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Mind Map

├── Clinical Features

├── Investigation
├── Management

    └── Fetal

▸ 30.23 Sexual and Reproductive Health in Pregnancy 1
▸ 30.23.1 Contraception Postpartum

Definition

Postpartum contraception should be discussed antenatally and initiated before hospital discharge. Breastfeeding, VTE risk, and future fertility influence choice. Lactational amenorrhoea is effective only if exclusively breastfeeding and amenorrhoeic.

Core Clinical Content

Counselling

Discuss antenatally; document plan; offer before discharge; consider breastfeeding, VTE, future fertility

Lactational amenorrhoea

98% effective if <6 months, exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoeic; LARC fails if any of these relax

Postpartum timing

Ovulation can return at 6 weeks (earlier in non-breastfeeding); contraception needed from 21 days

Combined hormonal contraception (CHC)

Avoid <21 days; if VTE risk factors, avoid <42 days; not generally recommended in breastfeeding <6 months

Progestogen-only pill (POP)

Safe from 21 days; breastfeeding compatible; desogestrel POP preferred for efficacy

Injectable (DMPA)

Safe; breastfeeding compatible; delayed return of fertility (6-12 months)

Implant (Nexplanon)

Safe; breastfeeding compatible; quick to insert; lasts 3 years

IUD (copper)

Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum; safe; non-hormonal; lasts 10 years; increased bleeding

IUS (Mirena)

Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum (or at caesarean); safe; breastfeeding; reduced bleeding; lasts 8 years (off-licence 5)

Sterilisation

Female sterilisation at caesarean or laparoscopy; male vasectomy ideal; counsel carefully — regret 5-10%

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
CounsellingDiscuss antenatally; document plan; offer before discharge; consider breastfeeding, VTE, future fertility
Lactational amenorrhoea98% effective if <6 months, exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoeic; LARC fails if any of these relax
Postpartum timingOvulation can return at 6 weeks (earlier in non-breastfeeding); contraception needed from 21 days
Combined hormonal contraception (CHC)Avoid <21 days; if VTE risk factors, avoid <42 days; not generally recommended in breastfeeding <6 months
Progestogen-only pill (POP)Safe from 21 days; breastfeeding compatible; desogestrel POP preferred for efficacy
Injectable (DMPA)Safe; breastfeeding compatible; delayed return of fertility (6-12 months)
Implant (Nexplanon)Safe; breastfeeding compatible; quick to insert; lasts 3 years
IUD (copper)Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum; safe; non-hormonal; lasts 10 years; increased bleeding
IUS (Mirena)Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum (or at caesarean); safe; breastfeeding; reduced bleeding; lasts 8 years (off-licence 5)
SterilisationFemale sterilisation at caesarean or laparoscopy; male vasectomy ideal; counsel carefully — regret 5-10%

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Contraception postpartum?

Postpartum contraception should be discussed antenatally and initiated before hospital discharge. Breastfeeding, VTE risk, and future fertility influence choice. Lactational amenorrhoea is effective only if exclusively breastfeeding and amenorrhoeic.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Contraception postpartum?

Key features include: Counselling, Lactational amenorrhoea, Postpartum timing, Combined hormonal contraception (CHC), Progestogen-only pill (POP). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Contraception postpartum diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Contraception postpartum?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Contraception postpartum?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Contraception postpartum — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)
