MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Contraception postpartum?

A) Discuss antenatally; document plan; offer before discharge; consider breastfeeding, VTE, future fertility

B) 98% effective if <6 months, exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoeic; LARC fails if any of these relax

C) Ovulation can return at 6 weeks (earlier in non-breastfeeding); contraception needed from 21 days

D) Avoid <21 days; if VTE risk factors, avoid <42 days; not generally recommended in breastfeeding <6 months

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Contraception postpartum?

A) 98% effective if <6 months, exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoeic; LARC fails if any of these relax

B) Ovulation can return at 6 weeks (earlier in non-breastfeeding); contraception needed from 21 days

C) Avoid <21 days; if VTE risk factors, avoid <42 days; not generally recommended in breastfeeding <6 months

D) Safe from 21 days; breastfeeding compatible; desogestrel POP preferred for efficacy

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Contraception postpartum?

A) Ovulation can return at 6 weeks (earlier in non-breastfeeding); contraception needed from 21 days

B) Avoid <21 days; if VTE risk factors, avoid <42 days; not generally recommended in breastfeeding <6 months

C) Safe from 21 days; breastfeeding compatible; desogestrel POP preferred for efficacy

D) Safe; breastfeeding compatible; delayed return of fertility (6-12 months)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Contraception postpartum?

A) Avoid <21 days; if VTE risk factors, avoid <42 days; not generally recommended in breastfeeding <6 months

B) Safe from 21 days; breastfeeding compatible; desogestrel POP preferred for efficacy

C) Safe; breastfeeding compatible; delayed return of fertility (6-12 months)

D) Safe; breastfeeding compatible; quick to insert; lasts 3 years

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Contraception postpartum?

A) Safe from 21 days; breastfeeding compatible; desogestrel POP preferred for efficacy

B) Safe; breastfeeding compatible; delayed return of fertility (6-12 months)

C) Safe; breastfeeding compatible; quick to insert; lasts 3 years

D) Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum; safe; non-hormonal; lasts 10 years; increased bleeding

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Contraception postpartum?

A) Safe; breastfeeding compatible; delayed return of fertility (6-12 months)

B) Safe; breastfeeding compatible; quick to insert; lasts 3 years

C) Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum; safe; non-hormonal; lasts 10 years; increased bleeding

D) Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum (or at caesarean); safe; breastfeeding; reduced bleeding; lasts 8 years (off-licence 5)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Contraception postpartum?

A) Safe; breastfeeding compatible; quick to insert; lasts 3 years

B) Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum; safe; non-hormonal; lasts 10 years; increased bleeding

C) Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum (or at caesarean); safe; breastfeeding; reduced bleeding; lasts 8 years (off-licence 5)

D) Female sterilisation at caesarean or laparoscopy; male vasectomy ideal; counsel carefully — regret 5-10%

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Contraception postpartum?

A) Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum; safe; non-hormonal; lasts 10 years; increased bleeding

B) Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum (or at caesarean); safe; breastfeeding; reduced bleeding; lasts 8 years (off-licence 5)

C) Female sterilisation at caesarean or laparoscopy; male vasectomy ideal; counsel carefully — regret 5-10%

D) Discuss antenatally; document plan; offer before discharge; consider breastfeeding, VTE, future fertility

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Contraception postpartum?

A) Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum (or at caesarean); safe; breastfeeding; reduced bleeding; lasts 8 years (off-licence 5)

B) Female sterilisation at caesarean or laparoscopy; male vasectomy ideal; counsel carefully — regret 5-10%

C) Discuss antenatally; document plan; offer before discharge; consider breastfeeding, VTE, future fertility

D) 98% effective if <6 months, exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoeic; LARC fails if any of these relax

Answer: A

▸ 30.24 Skin Disease in Pregnancy 1
▸ 30.24.1 Pruritus And Specific Dermatoses In Pregnancy

Definition

Pruritus in pregnancy has many causes, including specific pregnancy dermatoses (polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, pemphigoid gestationis, intrahepatic cholestasis), eczema, and systemic disease. Recognition of the specific dermatosis guides management and prognosis.

Core Clinical Content

Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy (PEP/PUPPP)

Late pregnancy; abdominal striae; urticarial papules and plaques; no maternal/fetal risk; resolves postpartum

Pemphigoid gestationis (HG)

Autoimmune BP180 antibodies; blistering; periumbilical; risk of preterm, SGA; neonatal blistering

Intrahepatic cholestasis

Severe pruritus; no rash; raised bile acids; see Liver chapter

Atopic eruption of pregnancy (AEP)

Eczema in pregnancy; face, neck, flexures; topical steroids; safe

Prurigo of pregnancy

Excoriated papules; limbs; no maternal/fetal risk; topical steroids

Investigations

LFT, bile acids (rule out ICP); skin biopsy if bullae; direct immunofluorescence for HG

Treatment PEP/AEP

Topical steroids; antihistamines; usually resolves within weeks postpartum

Treatment HG

Topical/oral steroids; prednisolone; cyclosporin if severe; monitor fetal growth

Other skin changes

Melasma; linea nigra; striae; spider naevi; palmar erythema — normal

Postpartum

Most resolve; HG may flare postpartum; recurrence with COC, menses, subsequent pregnancy

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy (PEP/PUPPP)Late pregnancy; abdominal striae; urticarial papules and plaques; no maternal/fetal risk; resolves postpartum
Pemphigoid gestationis (HG)Autoimmune BP180 antibodies; blistering; periumbilical; risk of preterm, SGA; neonatal blistering
Intrahepatic cholestasisSevere pruritus; no rash; raised bile acids; see Liver chapter
Atopic eruption of pregnancy (AEP)Eczema in pregnancy; face, neck, flexures; topical steroids; safe
Prurigo of pregnancyExcoriated papules; limbs; no maternal/fetal risk; topical steroids
InvestigationsLFT, bile acids (rule out ICP); skin biopsy if bullae; direct immunofluorescence for HG
Treatment PEP/AEPTopical steroids; antihistamines; usually resolves within weeks postpartum
Treatment HGTopical/oral steroids; prednisolone; cyclosporin if severe; monitor fetal growth
Other skin changesMelasma; linea nigra; striae; spider naevi; palmar erythema — normal
PostpartumMost resolve; HG may flare postpartum; recurrence with COC, menses, subsequent pregnancy

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy?

Pruritus in pregnancy has many causes, including specific pregnancy dermatoses (polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, pemphigoid gestationis, intrahepatic cholestasis), eczema, and systemic disease. Recognition of the specific dermatosis guides management and prognosis.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy?

Key features include: Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy (PEP/PUPPP), Pemphigoid gestationis (HG), Intrahepatic cholestasis, Atopic eruption of pregnancy (AEP), Prurigo of pregnancy. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.3 Connective Tissue Disease in Pregnancy 4
▸ 30.3.1 Antiphospholipid Syndrome In Pregnancy Obstetric

Definition

Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is a leading cause of recurrent pregnancy loss and is associated with late fetal loss, pre-eclampsia, IUGR, and thrombosis. Diagnosis requires persistent aPL antibodies and clinical events; treatment with aspirin ± LMWH improves outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

Antibodies

Lupus anticoagulant; anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I — confirm 12 weeks apart

Obstetric morbidity criteria

≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

Pathophysiology

Placental thrombosis + inflammation; impaired trophoblast invasion; complement activation

Maternal risks

VTE; pre-eclampsia; HELLP; placental abruption; stroke

Fetal risks

Miscarriage (early and late); IUGR; stillbirth; preterm birth

Treatment

Low-dose aspirin 75-150 mg + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy

High-risk (prior thrombosis/lupus)

Therapeutic LMWH dose; consider warfarin postpartum for life

Monitoring

Uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

Postpartum

Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong if thrombosis; preconception counselling for future

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPersistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)
AntibodiesLupus anticoagulant; anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I — confirm 12 weeks apart
Obstetric morbidity criteria≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency
PathophysiologyPlacental thrombosis + inflammation; impaired trophoblast invasion; complement activation
Maternal risksVTE; pre-eclampsia; HELLP; placental abruption; stroke
Fetal risksMiscarriage (early and late); IUGR; stillbirth; preterm birth
TreatmentLow-dose aspirin 75-150 mg + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy
High-risk (prior thrombosis/lupus)Therapeutic LMWH dose; consider warfarin postpartum for life
MonitoringUterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance
PostpartumContinue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong if thrombosis; preconception counselling for future

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is a leading cause of recurrent pregnancy loss and is associated with late fetal loss, pre-eclampsia, IUGR, and thrombosis. Diagnosis requires persistent aPL antibodies and clinical events; treatment with aspirin ± LMWH improves outcomes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

Key features include: Definition, Antibodies, Obstetric morbidity criteria, Pathophysiology, Maternal risks. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

A) Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

B) Lupus anticoagulant; anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I — confirm 12 weeks apart

C) ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

D) Placental thrombosis + inflammation; impaired trophoblast invasion; complement activation

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

A) Lupus anticoagulant; anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I — confirm 12 weeks apart

B) ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

C) Placental thrombosis + inflammation; impaired trophoblast invasion; complement activation

D) VTE; pre-eclampsia; HELLP; placental abruption; stroke

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

A) ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

B) Placental thrombosis + inflammation; impaired trophoblast invasion; complement activation

C) VTE; pre-eclampsia; HELLP; placental abruption; stroke

D) Miscarriage (early and late); IUGR; stillbirth; preterm birth

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

A) Placental thrombosis + inflammation; impaired trophoblast invasion; complement activation

B) VTE; pre-eclampsia; HELLP; placental abruption; stroke

C) Miscarriage (early and late); IUGR; stillbirth; preterm birth

D) Low-dose aspirin 75-150 mg + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

A) VTE; pre-eclampsia; HELLP; placental abruption; stroke

B) Miscarriage (early and late); IUGR; stillbirth; preterm birth

C) Low-dose aspirin 75-150 mg + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy

D) Therapeutic LMWH dose; consider warfarin postpartum for life

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

A) Miscarriage (early and late); IUGR; stillbirth; preterm birth

B) Low-dose aspirin 75-150 mg + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy

C) Therapeutic LMWH dose; consider warfarin postpartum for life

D) Uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

A) Low-dose aspirin 75-150 mg + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy

B) Therapeutic LMWH dose; consider warfarin postpartum for life

C) Uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

D) Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong if thrombosis; preconception counselling for future

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy (obstetric)?

A) Therapeutic LMWH dose; consider warfarin postpartum for life

B) Uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

C) Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong if thrombosis; preconception counselling for future

D) Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

Answer: A

A) Uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

B) Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong if thrombosis; preconception counselling for future

C) Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

D) Lupus anticoagulant; anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I — confirm 12 weeks apart

Answer: A

A) Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong if thrombosis; preconception counselling for future

Flashcards

Q: Definition?

A: Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

Q: Antibodies?

A: Lupus anticoagulant; anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I — confirm 12 weeks apart

Q: Obstetric morbidity criteria?

A: ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

Q: Pathophysiology?

A: Placental thrombosis + inflammation; impaired trophoblast invasion; complement activation

Q: Maternal risks?

A: VTE; pre-eclampsia; HELLP; placental abruption; stroke

Q: Fetal risks?

A: Miscarriage (early and late); IUGR; stillbirth; preterm birth

Q: Treatment?

A: Low-dose aspirin 75-150 mg + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy

Q: High-risk (prior thrombosis/lupus)?

A: Therapeutic LMWH dose; consider warfarin postpartum for life

  3. ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency
  4. Placental thrombosis + inflammation; impaired trophoblast invasion; complement activation
  5. VTE; pre-eclampsia; HELLP; placental abruption; stroke
  6. Miscarriage (early and late); IUGR; stillbirth; preterm birth
  7. Low-dose aspirin 75-150 mg + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy
  8. Therapeutic LMWH dose; consider warfarin postpartum for life
  9. Uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance
  10. Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong if thrombosis; preconception counselling for future

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.3.2 Rheumatoid Arthritis In Pregnancy

Definition

Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) in pregnancy often improves due to immunomodulation (Th2 shift), but flares occur postpartum in 50-70%. Active disease at conception increases risk of preterm birth and low birth weight. Most medications (sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine, low-dose steroids) are safe; methotrexate and leflunomide are teratogenic.

Core Clinical Content

Effect of pregnancy on RA

50-75% improve (esp. 1st and 2nd trimesters); 50% flare postpartum; hormonal and immunomodulatory factors

Effect of RA on pregnancy

Active disease → preterm, LBW, pre-eclampsia; quiescent → generally good outcomes

Preconception

Achieve remission 3-6 months; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months, leflunomide cholestyramine washout); review biologics

Safe drugs

Sulfasalazine (with folate); hydroxychloroquine (continue); azathioprine; low-dose prednisolone; certolizumab (limited placental transfer — safe); other anti-TNF generally safe until 3rd trimester

Avoid

Methotrexate (teratogenic); leflunomide (teratogenic); cyclophosphamide; baricitinib; JAK inhibitors (limited data)

Biologics

Anti-TNF (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept) — generally safe; discontinue in 3rd trimester if possible to avoid neonatal immunosuppression; live vaccines contraindicated in exposed infants <6 months

Monitoring

Disease activity (DAS28); FBC; U&E; LFT; trough levels if on biologic; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

Postpartum

Flare common; resume medications; breastfeeding compatible with sulfasalazine, HCQ, azathioprine, prednisolone; avoid MTX; contraception

Anaesthesia

Difficult intubation if cricoarytenoid involvement; careful airway assessment if C-section

Long-term

Joint damage during pregnancy limited; functional outcomes good; preconception counselling important

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Effect of RA on pregnancyActive disease → preterm, LBW, pre-eclampsia; quiescent → generally good outcomes
PreconceptionAchieve remission 3-6 months; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months, leflunomide cholestyramine washout); review biologics
Safe drugsSulfasalazine (with folate); hydroxychloroquine (continue); azathioprine; low-dose prednisolone; certolizumab (limited placental transfer — safe); other anti-TNF generally safe until 3rd trimester
AvoidMethotrexate (teratogenic); leflunomide (teratogenic); cyclophosphamide; baricitinib; JAK inhibitors (limited data)
BiologicsAnti-TNF (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept) — generally safe; discontinue in 3rd trimester if possible to avoid neonatal immunosuppression; live vaccines contraindicated in exposed infants <6 months
MonitoringDisease activity (DAS28); FBC; U&E; LFT; trough levels if on biologic; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance
PostpartumFlare common; resume medications; breastfeeding compatible with sulfasalazine, HCQ, azathioprine, prednisolone; avoid MTX; contraception
AnaesthesiaDifficult intubation if cricoarytenoid involvement; careful airway assessment if C-section
Long-termJoint damage during pregnancy limited; functional outcomes good; preconception counselling important

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy?

Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) in pregnancy often improves due to immunomodulation (Th2 shift), but flares occur postpartum in 50-70%. Active disease at conception increases risk of preterm birth and low birth weight. Most medications (sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine, low-dose steroids) are safe; methotrexate and leflunomide are teratogenic.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Effect of pregnancy on RA, Effect of RA on pregnancy, Preconception, Safe drugs, Avoid. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Q: Effect of pregnancy on RA?

A: 50-75% improve (esp. 1st and 2nd trimesters); 50% flare postpartum; hormonal and immunomodulatory factors

Q: Effect of RA on pregnancy?

A: Active disease → preterm, LBW, pre-eclampsia; quiescent → generally good outcomes

Q: Preconception?

A: Achieve remission 3-6 months; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months, leflunomide cholestyramine washout); review biologics

A: Sulfasalazine (with folate); hydroxychloroquine (continue); azathioprine; low-dose prednisolone; certolizumab (limited placental transfer — safe); other anti-TNF generally safe until 3rd trimester

Q: Avoid?

A: Methotrexate (teratogenic); leflunomide (teratogenic); cyclophosphamide; baricitinib; JAK inhibitors (limited data)

Q: Biologics?

A: Anti-TNF (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept) — generally safe; discontinue in 3rd trimester if possible to avoid neonatal immunosuppression; live vaccines contraindicated in exposed infants <6 months

Q: Monitoring?

A: Disease activity (DAS28); FBC; U&E; LFT; trough levels if on biologic; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

Q: Postpartum?

  4. Sulfasalazine (with folate); hydroxychloroquine (continue); azathioprine; low-dose prednisolone; certolizumab (limited placental transfer — safe); other anti-TNF generally safe until 3rd trimester
  5. Methotrexate (teratogenic); leflunomide (teratogenic); cyclophosphamide; baricitinib; JAK inhibitors (limited data)
  6. Anti-TNF (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept) — generally safe; discontinue in 3rd trimester if possible to avoid neonatal immunosuppression; live vaccines contraindicated in exposed infants <6 months
  7. Disease activity (DAS28); FBC; U&E; LFT; trough levels if on biologic; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance
  8. Flare common; resume medications; breastfeeding compatible with sulfasalazine, HCQ, azathioprine, prednisolone; avoid MTX; contraception
  9. Difficult intubation if cricoarytenoid involvement; careful airway assessment if C-section
  10. Joint damage during pregnancy limited; functional outcomes good; preconception counselling important

▸ 30.3.3 Scleroderma In Pregnancy

Definition

Scleroderma (systemic sclerosis) in pregnancy is uncommon as disease often affects older women, but it does occur. Limited cutaneous disease has better outcomes; diffuse cutaneous disease with renal crisis risk, pulmonary hypertension, and severe organ involvement carries high maternal mortality. Multidisciplinary care is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

F:M 3-4:1; usually perimenopausal; F childbearing years have worse disease

Types

Limited cutaneous (CREST — old definition; better prognosis); diffuse cutaneous (rapid skin, internal organ); systemic sclerosis overlap

Effect of pregnancy

Disease often stable; renal crisis risk (especially diffuse + abrupt BP rise); cardiopulmonary complications dangerous

Maternal risks

Renal crisis (scleroderma renal crisis — malignant HTN, AKI, microangiopathic haemolysis — 5-10% mortality); PAH crisis; PPH (poor wound healing; uterine vessels affected)

Fetal risks

IUGR; preterm; miscarriage; stillbirth; small for gestational age; placental insufficiency

Preconception

Stable disease ≥3-5 years; echo (PAH, LV); PFTs; renal function; switch MMF to azathioprine; avoid teratogens

Monitoring

Monthly BP, U&E, urine; growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler; echo 2nd/3rd trimester; review symptoms

Treatment

Continue nifedipine; ACE inhibitors for renal crisis (despite pregnancy; life-saving); proton pump inhibitors; iloprost for severe Raynaud's; steroids

Renal crisis management

Captopril or enalapril IV (off-label but life-saving); control BP urgently; ITU; avoid NSAIDs

Delivery and postpartum

Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage; avoid ergometrine; PPH risk; breastfeeding considerations

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyF:M 3-4:1; usually perimenopausal; F childbearing years have worse disease
TypesLimited cutaneous (CREST — old definition; better prognosis); diffuse cutaneous (rapid skin, internal organ); systemic sclerosis overlap
Effect of pregnancyDisease often stable; renal crisis risk (especially diffuse + abrupt BP rise); cardiopulmonary complications dangerous
Maternal risksRenal crisis (scleroderma renal crisis — malignant HTN, AKI, microangiopathic haemolysis — 5-10% mortality); PAH crisis; PPH (poor wound healing; uterine vessels affected)
Fetal risksIUGR; preterm; miscarriage; stillbirth; small for gestational age; placental insufficiency
PreconceptionStable disease ≥3-5 years; echo (PAH, LV); PFTs; renal function; switch MMF to azathioprine; avoid teratogens
MonitoringMonthly BP, U&E, urine; growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler; echo 2nd/3rd trimester; review symptoms
TreatmentContinue nifedipine; ACE inhibitors for renal crisis (despite pregnancy; life-saving); proton pump inhibitors; iloprost for severe Raynaud's; steroids
Renal crisis managementCaptopril or enalapril IV (off-label but life-saving); control BP urgently; ITU; avoid NSAIDs
Delivery and postpartumVaginal preferred; assisted second stage; avoid ergometrine; PPH risk; breastfeeding considerations

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Scleroderma in pregnancy?

Scleroderma (systemic sclerosis) in pregnancy is uncommon as disease often affects older women, but it does occur. Limited cutaneous disease has better outcomes; diffuse cutaneous disease with renal crisis risk, pulmonary hypertension, and severe organ involvement carries high maternal mortality. Multidisciplinary care is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Scleroderma in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Types, Effect of pregnancy, Maternal risks, Fetal risks. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Scleroderma in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Scleroderma in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Scleroderma in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Scleroderma in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.3.4 Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Sle In Pregnancy

Definition

Systemic lupus erythematosus affects women in childbearing years and is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy is safe in quiescent disease but flares (especially renal) and increased rates of pre-eclampsia, miscarriage, preterm birth, and fetal loss are seen.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

F:M = 9:1; common in 20s-40s; affects 1 in 1,000 pregnancies

Preconception

Disease quiescent >6 months; renal function stable; switch MMF to azathioprine; stop ACEI

Maternal risks

Flare (esp. renal); pre-eclampsia (20-30%); thrombosis; infection; hypertension

Fetal risks

Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; neonatal lupus; congenital heart block (anti-Ro/La)

Anti-Ro/La antibodies

Risk of neonatal lupus (1-2%) and congenital heart block (1-2%); serial fetal echocardiography 16-34 weeks

Monitoring

Monthly U&E, urinalysis, anti-dsDNA, complement (C3/C4); growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler

Treatment

Hydroxychloroquine continued (reduces flares); azathioprine; prednisolone; aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH if APS

Avoid

Cyclophosphamide (1st trimester); MMF; methotrexate; ACEI/ARB

Lupus nephritis flare

Often mimics pre-eclampsia; treat with steroids/azathioprine; check anti-dsDNA/complement

Postpartum

Restart MMF; breastfeeding compatible with HCQ/azathioprine/prednisolone; contraception

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PrevalenceF:M = 9:1; common in 20s-40s; affects 1 in 1,000 pregnancies
PreconceptionDisease quiescent >6 months; renal function stable; switch MMF to azathioprine; stop ACEI
Maternal risksFlare (esp. renal); pre-eclampsia (20-30%); thrombosis; infection; hypertension
Fetal risksMiscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; neonatal lupus; congenital heart block (anti-Ro/La)
Anti-Ro/La antibodiesRisk of neonatal lupus (1-2%) and congenital heart block (1-2%); serial fetal echocardiography 16-34 weeks
MonitoringMonthly U&E, urinalysis, anti-dsDNA, complement (C3/C4); growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler
TreatmentHydroxychloroquine continued (reduces flares); azathioprine; prednisolone; aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH if APS
AvoidCyclophosphamide (1st trimester); MMF; methotrexate; ACEI/ARB
Lupus nephritis flareOften mimics pre-eclampsia; treat with steroids/azathioprine; check anti-dsDNA/complement
PostpartumRestart MMF; breastfeeding compatible with HCQ/azathioprine/prednisolone; contraception

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?

Systemic lupus erythematosus affects women in childbearing years and is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy is safe in quiescent disease but flares (especially renal) and increased rates of pre-eclampsia, miscarriage, preterm birth, and fetal loss are seen.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Preconception, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, Anti-Ro/La antibodies. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

A) Restart MMF; breastfeeding compatible with HCQ/azathioprine/prednisolone; contraception

B) F:M = 9:1; common in 20s-40s; affects 1 in 1,000 pregnancies

C) Disease quiescent >6 months; renal function stable; switch MMF to azathioprine; stop ACEI

D) Flare (esp. renal); pre-eclampsia (20-30%); thrombosis; infection; hypertension

Answer: A

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | F:M = 9:1; common in 20s-40s; affects 1 in 1,000 pregnancies
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Disease quiescent >6 months; renal function stable; switch MMF to azathioprine; stop ACEI
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Flare (esp. renal); pre-eclampsia (20-30%); thrombosis; infection; hypertension
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; neonatal lupus; congenital heart block (anti-Ro/La)
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Risk of neonatal lupus (1-2%) and congenital heart block (1-2%); serial fetal echocardiography 16-34 weeks
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Monthly U&E, urinalysis, anti-dsDNA, complement (C3/C4); growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Hydroxychloroquine continued (reduces flares); azathioprine; prednisolone; aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH if APS
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Cyclophosphamide (1st trimester); MMF; methotrexate; ACEI/ARB
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Often mimics pre-eclampsia; treat with steroids/azathioprine; check anti-dsDNA/complement
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Restart MMF; breastfeeding compatible with HCQ/azathioprine/prednisolone; contraception

Flashcards

Q: Prevalence?

A: F:M = 9:1; common in 20s-40s; affects 1 in 1,000 pregnancies

Q: Preconception?

A: Disease quiescent >6 months; renal function stable; switch MMF to azathioprine; stop ACEI

Q: Maternal risks?

A: Flare (esp. renal); pre-eclampsia (20-30%); thrombosis; infection; hypertension

Q: Fetal risks?

A: Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; neonatal lupus; congenital heart block (anti-Ro/La)

Q: Anti-Ro/La antibodies?

A: Risk of neonatal lupus (1-2%) and congenital heart block (1-2%); serial fetal echocardiography 16-34 weeks

Q: Monitoring?

A: Monthly U&E, urinalysis, anti-dsDNA, complement (C3/C4); growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler

Q: Treatment?

A: Hydroxychloroquine continued (reduces flares); azathioprine; prednisolone; aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH if APS

Q: Avoid?

A: Cyclophosphamide (1st trimester); MMF; methotrexate; ACEI/ARB

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?
  2. A — Key feature of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?
  3. A — Key feature of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?
  4. A — Key feature of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?
  5. A — Key feature of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?
  6. A — Key feature of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?
  7. A — Key feature of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?
  8. A — Key feature of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?
  9. A — Key feature of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?
  10. A — Key feature of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. F:M = 9:1; common in 20s-40s; affects 1 in 1,000 pregnancies
  2. Disease quiescent >6 months; renal function stable; switch MMF to azathioprine; stop ACEI
  3. Flare (esp. renal); pre-eclampsia (20-30%); thrombosis; infection; hypertension
  4. Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; neonatal lupus; congenital heart block (anti-Ro/La)
  5. Risk of neonatal lupus (1-2%) and congenital heart block (1-2%); serial fetal echocardiography 16-34 weeks
  6. Monthly U&E, urinalysis, anti-dsDNA, complement (C3/C4); growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler
  7. Hydroxychloroquine continued (reduces flares); azathioprine; prednisolone; aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH if APS
  8. Cyclophosphamide (1st trimester); MMF; methotrexate; ACEI/ARB
  9. Often mimics pre-eclampsia; treat with steroids/azathioprine; check anti-dsDNA/complement
  10. Restart MMF; breastfeeding compatible with HCQ/azathioprine/prednisolone; contraception

▸ 30.4 Diabetes in Pregnancy 2
▸ 30.4.1 Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Gdm

Definition

Gestational diabetes mellitus is glucose intolerance first recognised during pregnancy that is not overt pre-existing diabetes. It typically resolves after delivery but confers long-term maternal risk of type 2 diabetes and fetal risk of macrosomia and metabolic disease.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Glucose intolerance first recognised in pregnancy; excludes overt pre-pregnational diabetes

Epidemiology

Affects 5-18% of pregnancies; rising with obesity

Risk factors

BMI >30, age >25, prior GDM, family history, prior macrosomic baby, PCOS, ethnicity (South Asian)

Screening

Universal at 24-28 weeks (NICE); 2-step 50 g GCT → 100 g OGTT or 1-step 75 g OGTT

Diagnostic OGTT (IADPSG)

Fasting ≥5.1; 1-h ≥10.0; 2-h ≥8.5 mmol/L — one abnormal value = GDM

Complications

Macrosomia; shoulder dystocia; pre-eclampsia; caesarean; neonatal hypoglycaemia; stillbirth

Management

Diet and exercise first-line; metformin or insulin if glycaemic targets not met

Targets

Fasting <5.3; 1-h <7.8; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L

Fetal monitoring

Growth scans 28, 32, 36 weeks; daily fetal movement count from 28 weeks

Postpartum

75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks; 50% progress to T2DM over 5-10 years; lifestyle

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionGlucose intolerance first recognised in pregnancy; excludes overt pre-pregnational diabetes
EpidemiologyAffects 5-18% of pregnancies; rising with obesity
Risk factorsBMI >30, age >25, prior GDM, family history, prior macrosomic baby, PCOS, ethnicity (South Asian)
ScreeningUniversal at 24-28 weeks (NICE); 2-step 50 g GCT → 100 g OGTT or 1-step 75 g OGTT
Diagnostic OGTT (IADPSG)Fasting ≥5.1; 1-h ≥10.0; 2-h ≥8.5 mmol/L — one abnormal value = GDM
ComplicationsMacrosomia; shoulder dystocia; pre-eclampsia; caesarean; neonatal hypoglycaemia; stillbirth
ManagementDiet and exercise first-line; metformin or insulin if glycaemic targets not met
TargetsFasting <5.3; 1-h <7.8; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L
Fetal monitoringGrowth scans 28, 32, 36 weeks; daily fetal movement count from 28 weeks
Postpartum75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks; 50% progress to T2DM over 5-10 years; lifestyle

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)?

Gestational diabetes mellitus is glucose intolerance first recognised during pregnancy that is not overt pre-existing diabetes. It typically resolves after delivery but confers long-term maternal risk of type 2 diabetes and fetal risk of macrosomia and metabolic disease.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)?

Key features include: Definition, Epidemiology, Risk factors, Screening, Diagnostic OGTT (IADPSG). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.4.2 Pre Existing Diabetes In Pregnancy

Definition

Pre-existing (overt) diabetes in pregnancy refers to type 1 or type 2 diabetes present before conception. Pregnancy is associated with increased insulin resistance and risk of complications; preconception optimisation and tight glycaemic control are essential.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

1-2% of pregnancies; rising with obesity and T2DM

Preconception

HbA1c <6.5% (ideally <48 mmol/mol); folic acid 5 mg; switch to insulin; review drugs

Risks to mother

Miscarriage; pre-eclampsia (2x); infection; caesarean; DKA; hypoglycaemia

Risks to fetus

Congenital malformation (4-10%); macrosomia; IUGR (T1DM with vasculopathy); stillbirth; preterm; neonatal hypoglycaemia

Fetal surveillance

Anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; growth scans 28, 32, 36 weeks

Glycaemic targets

Fasting <5.3 mmol/L; 1-h postprandial <7.8 mmol/L; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L

Insulin

First-line; rapid-acting (aspart, lispro) + NPH; basal-bolus; CSII pumps

Metformin

Can continue; crosses placenta; long-term offspring data reassuring

Labour

IV insulin + dextrose sliding scale; target glucose 4-7 mmol/L

Postpartum

Insulin sensitivity rises immediately — reduce insulin by ~50% on day 1

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence1-2% of pregnancies; rising with obesity and T2DM
PreconceptionHbA1c <6.5% (ideally <48 mmol/mol); folic acid 5 mg; switch to insulin; review drugs
Risks to motherMiscarriage; pre-eclampsia (2x); infection; caesarean; DKA; hypoglycaemia
Risks to fetusCongenital malformation (4-10%); macrosomia; IUGR (T1DM with vasculopathy); stillbirth; preterm; neonatal hypoglycaemia
Fetal surveillanceAnomaly scan 18-20 weeks; growth scans 28, 32, 36 weeks
Glycaemic targetsFasting <5.3 mmol/L; 1-h postprandial <7.8 mmol/L; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L
InsulinFirst-line; rapid-acting (aspart, lispro) + NPH; basal-bolus; CSII pumps
MetforminCan continue; crosses placenta; long-term offspring data reassuring
LabourIV insulin + dextrose sliding scale; target glucose 4-7 mmol/L
PostpartumInsulin sensitivity rises immediately — reduce insulin by ~50% on day 1

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

Pre-existing (overt) diabetes in pregnancy refers to type 1 or type 2 diabetes present before conception. Pregnancy is associated with increased insulin resistance and risk of complications; preconception optimisation and tight glycaemic control are essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Preconception, Risks to mother, Risks to fetus, Fetal surveillance. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. Which of the following is true about Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

A) Anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; growth scans 28, 32, 36 weeks

B) Fasting <5.3 mmol/L; 1-h postprandial <7.8 mmol/L; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L

C) First-line; rapid-acting (aspart, lispro) + NPH; basal-bolus; CSII pumps

D) Can continue; crosses placenta; long-term offspring data reassuring

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

A) Fasting <5.3 mmol/L; 1-h postprandial <7.8 mmol/L; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L

B) First-line; rapid-acting (aspart, lispro) + NPH; basal-bolus; CSII pumps

C) Can continue; crosses placenta; long-term offspring data reassuring

D) IV insulin + dextrose sliding scale; target glucose 4-7 mmol/L

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

A) First-line; rapid-acting (aspart, lispro) + NPH; basal-bolus; CSII pumps

B) Can continue; crosses placenta; long-term offspring data reassuring

C) IV insulin + dextrose sliding scale; target glucose 4-7 mmol/L

D) Insulin sensitivity rises immediately — reduce insulin by ~50% on day 1

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

A) Can continue; crosses placenta; long-term offspring data reassuring

B) IV insulin + dextrose sliding scale; target glucose 4-7 mmol/L

C) Insulin sensitivity rises immediately — reduce insulin by ~50% on day 1

D) 1-2% of pregnancies; rising with obesity and T2DM

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

A) IV insulin + dextrose sliding scale; target glucose 4-7 mmol/L

B) Insulin sensitivity rises immediately — reduce insulin by ~50% on day 1

C) 1-2% of pregnancies; rising with obesity and T2DM

D) HbA1c <6.5% (ideally <48 mmol/mol); folic acid 5 mg; switch to insulin; review drugs

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

A) Insulin sensitivity rises immediately — reduce insulin by ~50% on day 1

B) 1-2% of pregnancies; rising with obesity and T2DM

C) HbA1c <6.5% (ideally <48 mmol/mol); folic acid 5 mg; switch to insulin; review drugs

D) Miscarriage; pre-eclampsia (2x); infection; caesarean; DKA; hypoglycaemia

Answer: A

Flashcards

Q: Prevalence?

A: 1-2% of pregnancies; rising with obesity and T2DM

Q: Preconception?

A: HbA1c <6.5% (ideally <48 mmol/mol); folic acid 5 mg; switch to insulin; review drugs

Q: Risks to mother?

A: Miscarriage; pre-eclampsia (2x); infection; caesarean; DKA; hypoglycaemia

Q: Risks to fetus?

A: Congenital malformation (4-10%); macrosomia; IUGR (T1DM with vasculopathy); stillbirth; preterm; neonatal hypoglycaemia

Q: Fetal surveillance?

A: Anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; growth scans 28, 32, 36 weeks

Q: Glycaemic targets?

A: Fasting <5.3 mmol/L; 1-h postprandial <7.8 mmol/L; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L

Q: Insulin?

A: First-line; rapid-acting (aspart, lispro) + NPH; basal-bolus; CSII pumps

Q: Metformin?

A: Can continue; crosses placenta; long-term offspring data reassuring

Answer Key with Explanations

  10. A — Key feature of Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. 1-2% of pregnancies; rising with obesity and T2DM
  2. HbA1c <6.5% (ideally <48 mmol/mol); folic acid 5 mg; switch to insulin; review drugs
  3. Miscarriage; pre-eclampsia (2x); infection; caesarean; DKA; hypoglycaemia
  4. Congenital malformation (4-10%); macrosomia; IUGR (T1DM with vasculopathy); stillbirth; preterm; neonatal hypoglycaemia
  5. Anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; growth scans 28, 32, 36 weeks
  6. Fasting <5.3 mmol/L; 1-h postprandial <7.8 mmol/L; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L
  7. First-line; rapid-acting (aspart, lispro) + NPH; basal-bolus; CSII pumps
  8. Can continue; crosses placenta; long-term offspring data reassuring
  9. IV insulin + dextrose sliding scale; target glucose 4-7 mmol/L
  10. Insulin sensitivity rises immediately — reduce insulin by ~50% on day 1

▸ 30.5 Drugs and Prescribing in Pregnancy 2
▸ 30.5.1 Drug Safety In Breastfeeding

Definition

Most drugs are compatible with breastfeeding; few are absolute contraindications. Assess risk-benefit, use lowest dose, schedule feeds around peak drug levels, and consult LactMed or UKDILAS.

Core Clinical Content

Principles

Most drugs safe; few absolute contraindications; assess risk-benefit; monitor infant

Safe analgesics

Paracetamol; ibuprofen; codeine in short courses (caution CYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolisers)

Safe antibiotics

Penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin

Avoid

Cytotoxic drugs (chemotherapy); radioisotopes (withhold breastfeeding for 5 half-lives); ergot alkaloids; chloramphenicol (neonate grey baby)

Caution

Codeine; tramadol; benzodiazepines (accumulation, sedation); lithium (monitor infant levels); lamotrigine (monitor); SSRIs (sertraline preferred)

Safe antihypertensives

Labetalol, nifedipine, enalapril (small amounts), methyldopa

Safe antiepileptics

Lamotrigine, levetiracetam, valproate (avoid — developmental delay); carbamazepine; monitor infant

Safe anticoagulants

LMWH, warfarin (small amount, monitor INR); DOACs avoid (limited data)

Drug entry into milk

Lipid-soluble, low molecular weight, low protein-bound drugs cross more readily

Resources

LactMed (NIH); UKDILAS; BNF 'Breast feeding' appendix

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PrinciplesMost drugs safe; few absolute contraindications; assess risk-benefit; monitor infant
Safe analgesicsParacetamol; ibuprofen; codeine in short courses (caution CYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolisers)
Safe antibioticsPenicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin
AvoidCytotoxic drugs (chemotherapy); radioisotopes (withhold breastfeeding for 5 half-lives); ergot alkaloids; chloramphenicol (neonate grey baby)
CautionCodeine; tramadol; benzodiazepines (accumulation, sedation); lithium (monitor infant levels); lamotrigine (monitor); SSRIs (sertraline preferred)
Safe antihypertensivesLabetalol, nifedipine, enalapril (small amounts), methyldopa
Safe antiepilepticsLamotrigine, levetiracetam, valproate (avoid — developmental delay); carbamazepine; monitor infant
Safe anticoagulantsLMWH, warfarin (small amount, monitor INR); DOACs avoid (limited data)
Drug entry into milkLipid-soluble, low molecular weight, low protein-bound drugs cross more readily
ResourcesLactMed (NIH); UKDILAS; BNF 'Breast feeding' appendix

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Drug safety in breastfeeding?

Most drugs are compatible with breastfeeding; few are absolute contraindications. Assess risk-benefit, use lowest dose, schedule feeds around peak drug levels, and consult LactMed or UKDILAS.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Drug safety in breastfeeding?

Key features include: Principles, Safe analgesics, Safe antibiotics, Avoid, Caution. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Drug safety in breastfeeding diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Drug safety in breastfeeding?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Drug safety in breastfeeding?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Drug safety in breastfeeding — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.5.2 Drug Safety In Pregnancy

Definition

Drug use in pregnancy requires balancing maternal benefit with fetal risk. Most drugs cross the placenta; the first trimester carries the highest teratogenic risk. Use the lowest effective dose, avoid new drugs where possible, and consult specific resources.

Core Clinical Content

Principles

Lowest effective dose; minimal duration; avoid new drugs; consider non-pharmacological; benefit > risk

FDA categories (legacy)

A: safe; B: animal safe; C: risk can't be ruled out; D: evidence of risk; X: contraindicated (B3 UK)

Safe drugs (common)

Paracetamol; penicillins/cephalosporins; labetalol/nifedipine/methyldopa; levothyroxine; insulin; aciclovir; ondansetron

Teratogenic

Valproate (NTD, neurodevelopment); carbimazole (aplasia cutis); ACEI/ARB (renal); warfarin (1st trimester); methotrexate; retinoids; misoprostol; thalidomide; lithium (cardiac)

First-trimester risks

Organogenesis; congenital malformations; avoid all unnecessary drugs

Late-pregnancy risks

NSAIDs (closure of ductus, oligohydramnios); ACEI/ARB; opioids (neonatal abstinence); benzodiazepines (floppy baby)

Antimicrobials safe

Penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin (avoid near term)

Antimicrobials avoid

Tetracyclines (teeth, bones); quinolones (cartilage); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); metronidazole (1st trimester caution)

Resource

UKTIS (UK Teratology Information Service); BNF; LactMed for breastfeeding; app-based decision support

Vaccination in pregnancy

Safe — inactivated influenza, COVID-19, pertussis (dTpa, 16-32 weeks). Avoid live: MMR, varicella, BCG, yellow fever (if needed) live OK

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PrinciplesLowest effective dose; minimal duration; avoid new drugs; consider non-pharmacological; benefit > risk
FDA categories (legacy)A: safe; B: animal safe; C: risk can't be ruled out; D: evidence of risk; X: contraindicated (B3 UK)
Safe drugs (common)Paracetamol; penicillins/cephalosporins; labetalol/nifedipine/methyldopa; levothyroxine; insulin; aciclovir; ondansetron
TeratogenicValproate (NTD, neurodevelopment); carbimazole (aplasia cutis); ACEI/ARB (renal); warfarin (1st trimester); methotrexate; retinoids; misoprostol; thalidomide; lithium (cardiac)
First-trimester risksOrganogenesis; congenital malformations; avoid all unnecessary drugs
Late-pregnancy risksNSAIDs (closure of ductus, oligohydramnios); ACEI/ARB; opioids (neonatal abstinence); benzodiazepines (floppy baby)
Antimicrobials safePenicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin (avoid near term)
Antimicrobials avoidTetracyclines (teeth, bones); quinolones (cartilage); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); metronidazole (1st trimester caution)
ResourceUKTIS (UK Teratology Information Service); BNF; LactMed for breastfeeding; app-based decision support
Vaccination in pregnancySafe — inactivated influenza, COVID-19, pertussis (dTpa, 16-32 weeks). Avoid live: MMR, varicella, BCG, yellow fever (if needed) live OK

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Drug safety in pregnancy?

Drug use in pregnancy requires balancing maternal benefit with fetal risk. Most drugs cross the placenta; the first trimester carries the highest teratogenic risk. Use the lowest effective dose, avoid new drugs where possible, and consult specific resources.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Drug safety in pregnancy?

Key features include: Principles, FDA categories (legacy), Safe drugs (common), Teratogenic, First-trimester risks. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Drug safety in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Drug safety in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Drug safety in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Drug safety in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Drug safety in pregnancy?

A) Lowest effective dose; minimal duration; avoid new drugs; consider non-pharmacological; benefit > risk

B) A: safe; B: animal safe; C: risk can't be ruled out; D: evidence of risk; X: contraindicated (B3 UK)

C) Paracetamol; penicillins/cephalosporins; labetalol/nifedipine/methyldopa; levothyroxine; insulin; aciclovir; ondansetron

D) Valproate (NTD, neurodevelopment); carbimazole (aplasia cutis); ACEI/ARB (renal); warfarin (1st trimester); methotrexate; retinoids; misoprostol; thalidomide; lithium (cardiac)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Drug safety in pregnancy?

A) A: safe; B: animal safe; C: risk can't be ruled out; D: evidence of risk; X: contraindicated (B3 UK)

B) Paracetamol; penicillins/cephalosporins; labetalol/nifedipine/methyldopa; levothyroxine; insulin; aciclovir; ondansetron

C) Valproate (NTD, neurodevelopment); carbimazole (aplasia cutis); ACEI/ARB (renal); warfarin (1st trimester); methotrexate; retinoids; misoprostol; thalidomide; lithium (cardiac)

D) Organogenesis; congenital malformations; avoid all unnecessary drugs

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Drug safety in pregnancy?

A) Paracetamol; penicillins/cephalosporins; labetalol/nifedipine/methyldopa; levothyroxine; insulin; aciclovir; ondansetron

B) Valproate (NTD, neurodevelopment); carbimazole (aplasia cutis); ACEI/ARB (renal); warfarin (1st trimester); methotrexate; retinoids; misoprostol; thalidomide; lithium (cardiac)

C) Organogenesis; congenital malformations; avoid all unnecessary drugs

D) NSAIDs (closure of ductus, oligohydramnios); ACEI/ARB; opioids (neonatal abstinence); benzodiazepines (floppy baby)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Drug safety in pregnancy?

A) Valproate (NTD, neurodevelopment); carbimazole (aplasia cutis); ACEI/ARB (renal); warfarin (1st trimester); methotrexate; retinoids; misoprostol; thalidomide; lithium (cardiac)

B) Organogenesis; congenital malformations; avoid all unnecessary drugs

C) NSAIDs (closure of ductus, oligohydramnios); ACEI/ARB; opioids (neonatal abstinence); benzodiazepines (floppy baby)

D) Penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin (avoid near term)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Drug safety in pregnancy?

A) Organogenesis; congenital malformations; avoid all unnecessary drugs

B) NSAIDs (closure of ductus, oligohydramnios); ACEI/ARB; opioids (neonatal abstinence); benzodiazepines (floppy baby)

C) Penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin (avoid near term)

D) Tetracyclines (teeth, bones); quinolones (cartilage); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); metronidazole (1st trimester caution)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Drug safety in pregnancy?

A) NSAIDs (closure of ductus, oligohydramnios); ACEI/ARB; opioids (neonatal abstinence); benzodiazepines (floppy baby)

B) Penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin (avoid near term)

C) Tetracyclines (teeth, bones); quinolones (cartilage); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); metronidazole (1st trimester caution)

D) UKTIS (UK Teratology Information Service); BNF; LactMed for breastfeeding; app-based decision support

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Drug safety in pregnancy?

A) Penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin (avoid near term)

B) Tetracyclines (teeth, bones); quinolones (cartilage); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); metronidazole (1st trimester caution)

C) UKTIS (UK Teratology Information Service); BNF; LactMed for breastfeeding; app-based decision support

D) Safe — inactivated influenza, COVID-19, pertussis (dTpa, 16-32 weeks). Avoid live: MMR, varicella, BCG, yellow fever (if needed) live OK

Answer: A

Flashcards

Q: Principles?

A: Lowest effective dose; minimal duration; avoid new drugs; consider non-pharmacological; benefit > risk

Q: FDA categories (legacy)?

A: A: safe; B: animal safe; C: risk can't be ruled out; D: evidence of risk; X: contraindicated (B3 UK)

Q: Safe drugs (common)?

A: Paracetamol; penicillins/cephalosporins; labetalol/nifedipine/methyldopa; levothyroxine; insulin; aciclovir; ondansetron

Q: Teratogenic?

A: Valproate (NTD, neurodevelopment); carbimazole (aplasia cutis); ACEI/ARB (renal); warfarin (1st trimester); methotrexate; retinoids; misoprostol; thalidomide; lithium (cardiac)

Q: First-trimester risks?

A: Organogenesis; congenital malformations; avoid all unnecessary drugs

Q: Late-pregnancy risks?

A: NSAIDs (closure of ductus, oligohydramnios); ACEI/ARB; opioids (neonatal abstinence); benzodiazepines (floppy baby)

Q: Antimicrobials safe?

A: Penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin (avoid near term)

Q: Antimicrobials avoid?

A: Tetracyclines (teeth, bones); quinolones (cartilage); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); metronidazole (1st trimester caution)

▸ 30.6 Early Pregnancy and Pregnancy Loss 4
▸ 30.6.1 Ectopic Pregnancy

Definition

Ectopic pregnancy is implantation of a fertilised ovum outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube (95%). It is a leading cause of first-trimester maternal mortality due to rupture and haemorrhage. Early diagnosis using β-hCG and transvaginal ultrasound is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Pregnancy implanted outside uterine cavity

Incidence

1-2% of pregnancies; rising with IVF, PID, smoking

Sites

Ampullary (70%); isthmic (12%); fimbrial (11%); interstitial (2-4%); cervical, ovarian, abdominal (rare)

Risk factors

Prior ectopic (10x); tubal damage (PID, surgery, endometriosis); IUD; smoking; IVF; tubal ligation

Presentation

Amenorrhoea 6-8 weeks; abdominal pain; vaginal bleeding; syncope; shoulder tip pain (diaphragmatic irritation); hypovolaemic shock if ruptured

Diagnosis

β-hCG (often <1000 and falling/rising abnormally); TVUS (no IUP + adnexal mass + free fluid); progesterone; diagnostic laparoscopy gold standard

Differential

Miscarriage; molar pregnancy; appendicitis; PID; ovarian torsion; corpus luteum cyst

Management unruptured

Methotrexate (50 mg/m² IM; stable; mass <3.5 cm; no fetal heart; β-hCG <5000); mifepristone + misoprostol

Surgical management

Salpingectomy (ruptured, unstable, large mass, fetal heart); salpingostomy (conservative; risk of persistent trophoblast)

Follow-up

Serial β-hCG until <5; counsel future fertility; recurrence 10%

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPregnancy implanted outside uterine cavity
Incidence1-2% of pregnancies; rising with IVF, PID, smoking
SitesAmpullary (70%); isthmic (12%); fimbrial (11%); interstitial (2-4%); cervical, ovarian, abdominal (rare)
Risk factorsPrior ectopic (10x); tubal damage (PID, surgery, endometriosis); IUD; smoking; IVF; tubal ligation
PresentationAmenorrhoea 6-8 weeks; abdominal pain; vaginal bleeding; syncope; shoulder tip pain (diaphragmatic irritation); hypovolaemic shock if ruptured
Diagnosisβ-hCG (often <1000 and falling/rising abnormally); TVUS (no IUP + adnexal mass + free fluid); progesterone; diagnostic laparoscopy gold standard
DifferentialMiscarriage; molar pregnancy; appendicitis; PID; ovarian torsion; corpus luteum cyst
Management unrupturedMethotrexate (50 mg/m² IM; stable; mass <3.5 cm; no fetal heart; β-hCG <5000); mifepristone + misoprostol
Surgical managementSalpingectomy (ruptured, unstable, large mass, fetal heart); salpingostomy (conservative; risk of persistent trophoblast)
Follow-upSerial β-hCG until <5; counsel future fertility; recurrence 10%

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Ectopic pregnancy?

Ectopic pregnancy is implantation of a fertilised ovum outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube (95%). It is a leading cause of first-trimester maternal mortality due to rupture and haemorrhage. Early diagnosis using β-hCG and transvaginal ultrasound is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Ectopic pregnancy?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Sites, Risk factors, Presentation. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Ectopic pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Ectopic pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Ectopic pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Ectopic pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Flashcards

Q: Definition?

A: Pregnancy implanted outside uterine cavity

Q: Incidence?

A: 1-2% of pregnancies; rising with IVF, PID, smoking

Q: Sites?

A: Ampullary (70%); isthmic (12%); fimbrial (11%); interstitial (2-4%); cervical, ovarian, abdominal (rare)

Q: Risk factors?

A: Prior ectopic (10x); tubal damage (PID, surgery, endometriosis); IUD; smoking; IVF; tubal ligation

Q: Presentation?

A: Amenorrhoea 6-8 weeks; abdominal pain; vaginal bleeding; syncope; shoulder tip pain (diaphragmatic irritation); hypovolaemic shock if ruptured

Q: Diagnosis?

A: β-hCG (often <1000 and falling/rising abnormally); TVUS (no IUP + adnexal mass + free fluid); progesterone; diagnostic laparoscopy gold standard

Q: Differential?

A: Miscarriage; molar pregnancy; appendicitis; PID; ovarian torsion; corpus luteum cyst

Q: Management unruptured?

A: Methotrexate (50 mg/m² IM; stable; mass <3.5 cm; no fetal heart; β-hCG <5000); mifepristone + misoprostol

▸ 30.6.2 Recurrent Miscarriage

Definition

Recurrent miscarriage is defined as ≥3 consecutive pregnancy losses before 24 weeks' gestation (or ≥2 in some guidelines). It affects 1-2% of couples and warrants investigation for underlying causes. Identifiable causes include antiphospholipid syndrome, uterine anomalies, parental chromosomal abnormalities, and endocrine disorders.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

≥3 consecutive miscarriages <24 weeks (RCOG); some use ≥2

Incidence

1-2% of couples; 5% with ≥2 losses

Causes

Antiphospholipid syndrome (15%); uterine anomalies (10%); parental karyotype (3-5%); endocrine (PCOS, thyroid, diabetes); thrombophilia; infection; idiopathic (50%)

Investigations

Antiphospholipid antibodies (LA, aCL, anti-β2GP1); parental karyotyping; pelvic USS/MRI; thrombophilia screen; TFT; OGTT; uterine cavity (HSG/SIS)

APS treatment

Low-dose aspirin + LMWH from positive pregnancy test; improves live birth rate

Uterine anomalies

Septum resection (controversial); unicornuate/bicornuate — obstetric surveillance

Parental chromosomal

Robertsonian translocations; PGD/IVF; genetic counselling

Prognosis

70-80% live birth rate with supportive care; better if no identifiable cause

Supportive care

Tender loving care (TLC); early pregnancy unit access; psychological support

Future pregnancy

Folic acid 5 mg; aspirin if indicated; close monitoring; serial scans

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Definition≥3 consecutive miscarriages <24 weeks (RCOG); some use ≥2
Incidence1-2% of couples; 5% with ≥2 losses
CausesAntiphospholipid syndrome (15%); uterine anomalies (10%); parental karyotype (3-5%); endocrine (PCOS, thyroid, diabetes); thrombophilia; infection; idiopathic (50%)
InvestigationsAntiphospholipid antibodies (LA, aCL, anti-β2GP1); parental karyotyping; pelvic USS/MRI; thrombophilia screen; TFT; OGTT; uterine cavity (HSG/SIS)
APS treatmentLow-dose aspirin + LMWH from positive pregnancy test; improves live birth rate
Uterine anomaliesSeptum resection (controversial); unicornuate/bicornuate — obstetric surveillance
Parental chromosomalRobertsonian translocations; PGD/IVF; genetic counselling
Prognosis70-80% live birth rate with supportive care; better if no identifiable cause
Supportive careTender loving care (TLC); early pregnancy unit access; psychological support
Future pregnancyFolic acid 5 mg; aspirin if indicated; close monitoring; serial scans

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Recurrent miscarriage?

Recurrent miscarriage is defined as ≥3 consecutive pregnancy losses before 24 weeks' gestation (or ≥2 in some guidelines). It affects 1-2% of couples and warrants investigation for underlying causes. Identifiable causes include antiphospholipid syndrome, uterine anomalies, parental chromosomal abnormalities, and endocrine disorders.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Recurrent miscarriage?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Causes, Investigations, APS treatment. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Recurrent miscarriage diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Recurrent miscarriage?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Recurrent miscarriage?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Recurrent miscarriage — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)
