▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.6.3 Spontaneous Miscarriage

Definition

Spontaneous miscarriage (spontaneous abortion) is the loss of pregnancy before 24 weeks' gestation. It affects 10-20% of confirmed pregnancies and presents with vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, and passage of products of conception. Management is supportive, medical (misoprostol), or surgical (ERPC) according to clinical scenario and patient preference.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Pregnancy loss before 24 weeks' gestation (UK); 20 weeks (WHO)

Incidence

10-20% of confirmed pregnancies; 80% occur in 1st trimester

Types

Threatened; inevitable; incomplete; complete; missed; septic; recurrent (≥3)

Causes

Chromosomal (50%, esp. trisomies); uterine anomalies; endocrine; autoimmune; infection; thrombophilia; trauma; idiopathic

Risk factors

Advanced maternal age; prior miscarriage; smoking; alcohol; obesity; uterine anomalies

Presentation

Vaginal bleeding; lower abdominal pain; products of conception passed; cervical dilation

Investigations

β-hCG serial (doubles 48 h normally); TVUS (fetal heart from 5-6 weeks); blood group; FBC; coagulation if severe

Management

Expectant; medical (misoprostol 800 μg PV); surgical (ERPC/VAE) — patient choice; anti-D if Rh-

Complications

Haemorrhage; infection (septic miscarriage); DIC; psychological impact

Follow-up

Bereavement support; recurrent miscarriage clinic if ≥3; pre-conception counselling; folic acid

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPregnancy loss before 24 weeks' gestation (UK); 20 weeks (WHO)
Incidence10-20% of confirmed pregnancies; 80% occur in 1st trimester
TypesThreatened; inevitable; incomplete; complete; missed; septic; recurrent (≥3)
CausesChromosomal (50%, esp. trisomies); uterine anomalies; endocrine; autoimmune; infection; thrombophilia; trauma; idiopathic
Risk factorsAdvanced maternal age; prior miscarriage; smoking; alcohol; obesity; uterine anomalies
PresentationVaginal bleeding; lower abdominal pain; products of conception passed; cervical dilation
Investigationsβ-hCG serial (doubles 48 h normally); TVUS (fetal heart from 5-6 weeks); blood group; FBC; coagulation if severe
ManagementExpectant; medical (misoprostol 800 μg PV); surgical (ERPC/VAE) — patient choice; anti-D if Rh-
ComplicationsHaemorrhage; infection (septic miscarriage); DIC; psychological impact
Follow-upBereavement support; recurrent miscarriage clinic if ≥3; pre-conception counselling; folic acid

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Spontaneous miscarriage?

Spontaneous miscarriage (spontaneous abortion) is the loss of pregnancy before 24 weeks' gestation. It affects 10-20% of confirmed pregnancies and presents with vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, and passage of products of conception. Management is supportive, medical (misoprostol), or surgical (ERPC) according to clinical scenario and patient preference.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Spontaneous miscarriage?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Types, Causes, Risk factors. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Spontaneous miscarriage diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Spontaneous miscarriage?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Spontaneous miscarriage?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Spontaneous miscarriage — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.6.4 Termination Of Pregnancy

Definition

Termination of pregnancy (induced abortion) is the medical or surgical ending of pregnancy. In the UK it is governed by the Abortion Act 1967 (with subsequent amendments). Most terminations are performed in the first trimester using medical (mifepristone + misoprostol) or surgical (suction curettage) methods. Complications are uncommon but include bleeding, infection, and uterine perforation.

Core Clinical Content

Legal (UK)

Abortion Act 1967: <24 weeks if risk to mother, child, or woman's health; any time if serious risk

First-trimester methods

Medical (mifepristone 200 mg oral then misoprostol 800 μg PV/buccal 24-48 h later) OR surgical (manual vacuum aspiration up to 12 weeks)

Second-trimester methods

Medical (mifepristone + repeated misoprostol); D&E (dilation and evacuation)

Feticide

For terminations >22 weeks; intracardiac potassium chloride or lidocaine

Complications

Haemorrhage (1%); infection (1-5%); retained products (1%); uterine perforation (<0.1%); Asherman syndrome (rare); psychological impact

Pre-procedure

Counselling; consent; USS for gestation; blood group + anti-D; STI screen; contraception plan

Post-procedure

Anti-D if Rh-; contraception initiated; β-hCG follow-up if medical; bleeding up to 2 weeks

Contraindications medical

Severe anaemia; coagulopathy; ectopic; adrenal/liver disease; long-term steroids; IUD in situ

Subsequent pregnancy

Usually no effect on fertility; small ↑preterm birth if multiple surgical; psychological support

Recurrent

Provide contraception; LARC methods; emergency contraception plan

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Legal (UK)Abortion Act 1967: <24 weeks if risk to mother, child, or woman's health; any time if serious risk
First-trimester methodsMedical (mifepristone 200 mg oral then misoprostol 800 μg PV/buccal 24-48 h later) OR surgical (manual vacuum aspiration up to 12 weeks)
Second-trimester methodsMedical (mifepristone + repeated misoprostol); D&E (dilation and evacuation)
FeticideFor terminations >22 weeks; intracardiac potassium chloride or lidocaine
ComplicationsHaemorrhage (1%); infection (1-5%); retained products (1%); uterine perforation (<0.1%); Asherman syndrome (rare); psychological impact
Pre-procedureCounselling; consent; USS for gestation; blood group + anti-D; STI screen; contraception plan
Post-procedureAnti-D if Rh-; contraception initiated; β-hCG follow-up if medical; bleeding up to 2 weeks
Contraindications medicalSevere anaemia; coagulopathy; ectopic; adrenal/liver disease; long-term steroids; IUD in situ
Subsequent pregnancyUsually no effect on fertility; small ↑preterm birth if multiple surgical; psychological support
RecurrentProvide contraception; LARC methods; emergency contraception plan

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Termination of pregnancy?

Termination of pregnancy (induced abortion) is the medical or surgical ending of pregnancy. In the UK it is governed by the Abortion Act 1967 (with subsequent amendments). Most terminations are performed in the first trimester using medical (mifepristone + misoprostol) or surgical (suction curettage) methods. Complications are uncommon but include bleeding, infection, and uterine perforation.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Termination of pregnancy?

Key features include: Legal (UK), First-trimester methods, Second-trimester methods, Feticide, Complications. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Termination of pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Termination of pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Termination of pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Termination of pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.7 Endocrine Disorders in Pregnancy 6
▸ 30.7.1 Adrenal Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Adrenal disease in pregnancy is rare but includes Cushing's syndrome (often ACTH-independent, e.g. adrenal adenoma), primary hyperaldosteronism, Addison's disease, and congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Physiological cortisol rise makes diagnosis challenging; biochemical workup and imaging are key.

Core Clinical Content

Physiology

CBG rises; total cortisol rises 2-3x; free cortisol rises; ACTH normal to mildly elevated

Cushing's syndrome

Rare; usually adrenal adenoma; features overlap with normal pregnancy (HTN, striae, GDM)

Diagnosis

24-h urinary free cortisol (cut-off 3x upper limit); late-night salivary cortisol; MRI without gadolinium

Management Cushing's

Surgery (adrenalectomy) in 2nd trimester if feasible; medical — metyrapone preferred

Addison's disease

Primary adrenal insufficiency; glucocorticoid + mineralocorticoid replacement; labour dose 100 mg hydrocortisone IV

Addisonian crisis

Vomiting, hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia; IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat

Congenital adrenal hyperplasia

21-hydroxylase deficiency; dexamethasone crosses placenta; consider prenatal treatment for affected female fetus

Primary hyperaldosteronism

Rare; features overlap with normal pregnancy; treat with labetalol; surgical if Conn's adenoma

Phaeochromocytoma

Rare but dangerous; screen if refractory HTN or paroxysmal symptoms; α-blockade then β; deliver with MDT

Postpartum

Review replacement; may need dose reduction if pre-pregnancy dose high

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PhysiologyCBG rises; total cortisol rises 2-3x; free cortisol rises; ACTH normal to mildly elevated
Cushing's syndromeRare; usually adrenal adenoma; features overlap with normal pregnancy (HTN, striae, GDM)
Diagnosis24-h urinary free cortisol (cut-off 3x upper limit); late-night salivary cortisol; MRI without gadolinium
Management Cushing'sSurgery (adrenalectomy) in 2nd trimester if feasible; medical — metyrapone preferred
Addison's diseasePrimary adrenal insufficiency; glucocorticoid + mineralocorticoid replacement; labour dose 100 mg hydrocortisone IV
Addisonian crisisVomiting, hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia; IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat
Congenital adrenal hyperplasia21-hydroxylase deficiency; dexamethasone crosses placenta; consider prenatal treatment for affected female fetus
Primary hyperaldosteronismRare; features overlap with normal pregnancy; treat with labetalol; surgical if Conn's adenoma
PhaeochromocytomaRare but dangerous; screen if refractory HTN or paroxysmal symptoms; α-blockade then β; deliver with MDT
PostpartumReview replacement; may need dose reduction if pre-pregnancy dose high

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Adrenal disease in pregnancy?

Adrenal disease in pregnancy is rare but includes Cushing's syndrome (often ACTH-independent, e.g. adrenal adenoma), primary hyperaldosteronism, Addison's disease, and congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Physiological cortisol rise makes diagnosis challenging; biochemical workup and imaging are key.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Adrenal disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Physiology, Cushing's syndrome, Diagnosis, Management Cushing's, Addison's disease. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Adrenal disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Adrenal disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Adrenal disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Adrenal disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. CBG rises; total cortisol rises 2-3x; free cortisol rises; ACTH normal to mildly elevated
  2. Rare; usually adrenal adenoma; features overlap with normal pregnancy (HTN, striae, GDM)
  3. 24-h urinary free cortisol (cut-off 3x upper limit); late-night salivary cortisol; MRI without gadolinium
  4. Surgery (adrenalectomy) in 2nd trimester if feasible; medical — metyrapone preferred
  5. Primary adrenal insufficiency; glucocorticoid + mineralocorticoid replacement; labour dose 100 mg hydrocortisone IV
  6. Vomiting, hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia; IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat
  7. 21-hydroxylase deficiency; dexamethasone crosses placenta; consider prenatal treatment for affected female fetus
  8. Rare; features overlap with normal pregnancy; treat with labetalol; surgical if Conn's adenoma
  9. Rare but dangerous; screen if refractory HTN or paroxysmal symptoms; α-blockade then β; deliver with MDT
  10. Review replacement; may need dose reduction if pre-pregnancy dose high

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.7.2 Diabetes Insipidus In Pregnancy

Definition

Diabetes insipidus (DI) in pregnancy may be pre-existing (central or nephrogenic) or develop de novo in pregnancy (gestational/transient DI of pregnancy). Pregnancy unmask subclinical DI due to rising vasopressinase from placenta. Distinguish from physiological polyuria of pregnancy.

Core Clinical Content

Forms

Central (pituitary); nephrogenic (kidney); gestational/transient (placental vasopressinase); pre-existing may worsen

Incidence

Pre-existing DI rare; gestational DI 1-6 per 100,000 pregnancies

Pathophysiology

Placental vasopressinase degrades ADH; unmasked in subclinical central DI; transient DI when placental mass high (twins, pre-eclampsia)

Presentation

Polyuria (>3 L/day); polydipsia; dehydration; hypernatraemia if no access to water

Investigations

Urine osmolality (<300); plasma osmolality (>287); sodium; water deprivation test (cautious in pregnancy); copeptin; serum vasopressin

Differential

Physiological polyuria; gestational diabetes (glycosuria); psychogenic polydipsia; hypercalcaemia; hypokalaemia

Treatment

Desmopressin (DDAVP) — safe, not degraded by vasopressinase; titrate dose; thiazide + low-salt for nephrogenic

Dose adjustment

Often need higher dose in pregnancy (vasopressinase); reassess postpartum

Monitoring

Sodium; fluid balance; weight; urine output; symptomatic review

Postpartum

Gestational DI resolves; pre-existing may revert to pre-pregnancy dose; breastfeeding may worsen (oxytocin)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
FormsCentral (pituitary); nephrogenic (kidney); gestational/transient (placental vasopressinase); pre-existing may worsen
IncidencePre-existing DI rare; gestational DI 1-6 per 100,000 pregnancies
PathophysiologyPlacental vasopressinase degrades ADH; unmasked in subclinical central DI; transient DI when placental mass high (twins, pre-eclampsia)
PresentationPolyuria (>3 L/day); polydipsia; dehydration; hypernatraemia if no access to water
InvestigationsUrine osmolality (<300); plasma osmolality (>287); sodium; water deprivation test (cautious in pregnancy); copeptin; serum vasopressin
DifferentialPhysiological polyuria; gestational diabetes (glycosuria); psychogenic polydipsia; hypercalcaemia; hypokalaemia
TreatmentDesmopressin (DDAVP) — safe, not degraded by vasopressinase; titrate dose; thiazide + low-salt for nephrogenic
Dose adjustmentOften need higher dose in pregnancy (vasopressinase); reassess postpartum
MonitoringSodium; fluid balance; weight; urine output; symptomatic review
PostpartumGestational DI resolves; pre-existing may revert to pre-pregnancy dose; breastfeeding may worsen (oxytocin)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Diabetes insipidus in pregnancy?

Diabetes insipidus (DI) in pregnancy may be pre-existing (central or nephrogenic) or develop de novo in pregnancy (gestational/transient DI of pregnancy). Pregnancy unmask subclinical DI due to rising vasopressinase from placenta. Distinguish from physiological polyuria of pregnancy.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Diabetes insipidus in pregnancy?

Key features include: Forms, Incidence, Pathophysiology, Presentation, Investigations. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Diabetes insipidus in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Diabetes insipidus in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Diabetes insipidus in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Diabetes insipidus in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.7.3 Parathyroid Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Parathyroid disease in pregnancy includes primary hyperparathyroidism (usually parathyroid adenoma) and hypoparathyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism in pregnancy is associated with miscarriage, pre-eclampsia, neonatal hypocalcaemia, and renal stones. Hypoparathyroidism is usually iatrogenic post-thyroidectomy.

Core Clinical Content

Primary hyperparathyroidism

Rare in pregnancy; most common cause: parathyroid adenoma (85%); associated with MEN1, MEN2A

Presentation

Often asymptomatic; stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones; nausea, vomiting, fatigue, depression

Risks to mother

Hyperemesis; renal stones; pancreatitis; pre-eclampsia; hypercalcaemic crisis; fractures

Risks to fetus/neonate

Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; neonatal hypocalcaemia (suppressed parathyroids) — tetany, seizures

Diagnosis

Corrected calcium (albumin-adjusted or ionised); PTH; 24-h urinary calcium; vitamin D; phosphate; consider sestamibi scan/US if needed

Management

Hydration; oral phosphate; cinacalcet (limited data); parathyroidectomy in 2nd trimester if severe/refractory

Severe hypercalcaemia (>3.0)

IV fluids; bisphosphonates avoid; calcitonin; consider surgery; parathyroidectomy preferred

Hypoparathyroidism

Usually iatrogenic post-thyroidectomy or autoimmune; replacement with calcitriol + calcium

Risks of hypoparathyroidism

Hypocalcaemia → tetany, seizures, laryngospasm; long-QT; preterm labour

Postpartum

Monitor calcium; breastfed infants at risk of hypocalcaemia; re-evaluate replacement doses

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Primary hyperparathyroidismRare in pregnancy; most common cause: parathyroid adenoma (85%); associated with MEN1, MEN2A
PresentationOften asymptomatic; stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones; nausea, vomiting, fatigue, depression
Risks to motherHyperemesis; renal stones; pancreatitis; pre-eclampsia; hypercalcaemic crisis; fractures
Risks to fetus/neonateMiscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; neonatal hypocalcaemia (suppressed parathyroids) — tetany, seizures
DiagnosisCorrected calcium (albumin-adjusted or ionised); PTH; 24-h urinary calcium; vitamin D; phosphate; consider sestamibi scan/US if needed
ManagementHydration; oral phosphate; cinacalcet (limited data); parathyroidectomy in 2nd trimester if severe/refractory
Severe hypercalcaemia (>3.0)IV fluids; bisphosphonates avoid; calcitonin; consider surgery; parathyroidectomy preferred
HypoparathyroidismUsually iatrogenic post-thyroidectomy or autoimmune; replacement with calcitriol + calcium
Risks of hypoparathyroidismHypocalcaemia → tetany, seizures, laryngospasm; long-QT; preterm labour
PostpartumMonitor calcium; breastfed infants at risk of hypocalcaemia; re-evaluate replacement doses

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?

Parathyroid disease in pregnancy includes primary hyperparathyroidism (usually parathyroid adenoma) and hypoparathyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism in pregnancy is associated with miscarriage, pre-eclampsia, neonatal hypocalcaemia, and renal stones. Hypoparathyroidism is usually iatrogenic post-thyroidectomy.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Primary hyperparathyroidism, Presentation, Risks to mother, Risks to fetus/neonate, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Parathyroid disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Parathyroid disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Parathyroid disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Rare in pregnancy; most common cause: parathyroid adenoma (85%); associated with MEN1, MEN2A
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Parathyroid disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Often asymptomatic; stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones; nausea, vomiting, fatigue, depression
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Parathyroid disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Hyperemesis; renal stones; pancreatitis; pre-eclampsia; hypercalcaemic crisis; fractures
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Parathyroid disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; neonatal hypocalcaemia (suppressed parathyroids) — tetany, seizures
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Parathyroid disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Corrected calcium (albumin-adjusted or ionised); PTH; 24-h urinary calcium; vitamin D; phosphate; consider sestamibi scan/US if needed
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Parathyroid disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Hydration; oral phosphate; cinacalcet (limited data); parathyroidectomy in 2nd trimester if severe/refractory
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Parathyroid disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | IV fluids; bisphosphonates avoid; calcitonin; consider surgery; parathyroidectomy preferred
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Parathyroid disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Usually iatrogenic post-thyroidectomy or autoimmune; replacement with calcitriol + calcium
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Parathyroid disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Hypocalcaemia → tetany, seizures, laryngospasm; long-QT; preterm labour
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Parathyroid disease in pregnancy. Most appropriate next step in management? | Monitor calcium; breastfed infants at risk of hypocalcaemia; re-evaluate replacement doses

Flashcards

Q: Primary hyperparathyroidism?

A: Rare in pregnancy; most common cause: parathyroid adenoma (85%); associated with MEN1, MEN2A

Q: Presentation?

A: Often asymptomatic; stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones; nausea, vomiting, fatigue, depression

Q: Risks to mother?

A: Hyperemesis; renal stones; pancreatitis; pre-eclampsia; hypercalcaemic crisis; fractures

Q: Risks to fetus/neonate?

A: Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; neonatal hypocalcaemia (suppressed parathyroids) — tetany, seizures

Q: Diagnosis?

A: Corrected calcium (albumin-adjusted or ionised); PTH; 24-h urinary calcium; vitamin D; phosphate; consider sestamibi scan/US if needed

Q: Management?

A: Hydration; oral phosphate; cinacalcet (limited data); parathyroidectomy in 2nd trimester if severe/refractory

Q: Severe hypercalcaemia (>3.0)?

A: IV fluids; bisphosphonates avoid; calcitonin; consider surgery; parathyroidectomy preferred

Q: Hypoparathyroidism?

A: Usually iatrogenic post-thyroidectomy or autoimmune; replacement with calcitriol + calcium

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?
  2. A — Key feature of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?
  3. A — Key feature of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?
  4. A — Key feature of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?
  5. A — Key feature of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?
  6. A — Key feature of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?
  7. A — Key feature of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?
  8. A — Key feature of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?
  9. A — Key feature of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?
  10. A — Key feature of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. Rare in pregnancy; most common cause: parathyroid adenoma (85%); associated with MEN1, MEN2A
  2. Often asymptomatic; stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones; nausea, vomiting, fatigue, depression
  3. Hyperemesis; renal stones; pancreatitis; pre-eclampsia; hypercalcaemic crisis; fractures
  4. Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; neonatal hypocalcaemia (suppressed parathyroids) — tetany, seizures
  5. Corrected calcium (albumin-adjusted or ionised); PTH; 24-h urinary calcium; vitamin D; phosphate; consider sestamibi scan/US if needed
  6. Hydration; oral phosphate; cinacalcet (limited data); parathyroidectomy in 2nd trimester if severe/refractory
  7. IV fluids; bisphosphonates avoid; calcitonin; consider surgery; parathyroidectomy preferred
  8. Usually iatrogenic post-thyroidectomy or autoimmune; replacement with calcitriol + calcium
  9. Hypocalcaemia → tetany, seizures, laryngospasm; long-QT; preterm labour
  10. Monitor calcium; breastfed infants at risk of hypocalcaemia; re-evaluate replacement doses

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.7.4 Pituitary Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Pituitary disease in pregnancy includes prolactinoma (most common), hypopituitarism, lymphocytic hypophysitis, and Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction). Physiological lactotroph hyperplasia enlarges the pituitary, affecting imaging and symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

Physiology

Pituitary enlarges 30-50% in pregnancy; lactotrophs proliferate; other axes relatively suppressed

Prolactinoma

Most common; microadenomas (<10 mm) usually asymptomatic; macroprolactinomas need monitoring

Management prolactinoma

Dopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred) — discontinue in pregnancy if possible; restart if symptoms or growth

Visual fields

Monitor macroprolactinomas with monthly visual fields; MRI if symptoms

Lymphocytic hypophysitis

Peripartum; mimics pituitary mass with headache, visual loss, hypopituitarism; biopsy may be needed

Sheehan syndrome

Postpartum pituitary infarction following severe PPH; failure to lactate, amenorrhoea, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency

Diabetes insipidus

May worsen in pregnancy (vasopressinase from placenta); desmopressin safe

Hypopituitarism

Hydrocortisone + levothyroxine; growth hormone discontinued; close MDT monitoring

Cushing disease

Rare; surgery in 2nd trimester if confirmed

Postpartum

Reassess pituitary function 3-6 months; restart dopamine agonist if breastfeeding; MRI if macroadenoma

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PhysiologyPituitary enlarges 30-50% in pregnancy; lactotrophs proliferate; other axes relatively suppressed
ProlactinomaMost common; microadenomas (<10 mm) usually asymptomatic; macroprolactinomas need monitoring
Management prolactinomaDopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred) — discontinue in pregnancy if possible; restart if symptoms or growth
Visual fieldsMonitor macroprolactinomas with monthly visual fields; MRI if symptoms
Lymphocytic hypophysitisPeripartum; mimics pituitary mass with headache, visual loss, hypopituitarism; biopsy may be needed
Sheehan syndromePostpartum pituitary infarction following severe PPH; failure to lactate, amenorrhoea, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency
Diabetes insipidusMay worsen in pregnancy (vasopressinase from placenta); desmopressin safe
HypopituitarismHydrocortisone + levothyroxine; growth hormone discontinued; close MDT monitoring
Cushing diseaseRare; surgery in 2nd trimester if confirmed
PostpartumReassess pituitary function 3-6 months; restart dopamine agonist if breastfeeding; MRI if macroadenoma

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pituitary disease in pregnancy?

Pituitary disease in pregnancy includes prolactinoma (most common), hypopituitarism, lymphocytic hypophysitis, and Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction). Physiological lactotroph hyperplasia enlarges the pituitary, affecting imaging and symptoms.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pituitary disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Physiology, Prolactinoma, Management prolactinoma, Visual fields, Lymphocytic hypophysitis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pituitary disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pituitary disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pituitary disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pituitary disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.7.5 Postpartum Thyroiditis

Definition

Postpartum thyroiditis is an autoimmune thyroiditis occurring within 12 months of delivery, often presenting in a biphasic pattern of hyperthyroidism followed by hypothyroidism. It affects 5-10% of postpartum women and is associated with anti-TPO antibodies. Most women return to euthyroid but a proportion develop permanent hypothyroidism.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Autoimmune thyroiditis within 12 months postpartum

Incidence

5-10% of postpartum women; up to 25% if TPOAb+

Risk factors

Anti-TPO antibodies (strongest); prior postpartum thyroiditis; type 1 DM; other autoimmune disease (SLE); family history

Phases

Biphasic: hyperthyroid (1-4 months postpartum) → euthyroid → hypothyroid (4-8 months) → recovery; 20-30% have isolated hypothyroid

Hyperthyroid phase

Symptoms: fatigue, palpitations, weight loss, anxiety; thyrotoxic but no ophthalmopathy; due to thyroid hormone release from inflammation

Hypothyroid phase

Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, depression, constipation, dry skin, hair loss; depression may be misattributed to PND

Diagnosis

TSH, free T4, free T3; TPO antibodies; thyroid USS; low uptake on radioisotope scan (avoid in breastfeeding if possible)

Differential

Graves' disease (ophthalmopathy, persistently high uptake); PND; anaemia

Management hyperthyroid

β-blocker (propranolol); no antithyroid drugs (destructive, not synthetic); usually self-limiting

Management hypothyroid

Levothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10; weaning trial 6-12 months; monitor

Long-term

20-30% permanent hypothyroidism; recurrence in 70% subsequent pregnancies; screen if symptomatic

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionAutoimmune thyroiditis within 12 months postpartum
Incidence5-10% of postpartum women; up to 25% if TPOAb+
Risk factorsAnti-TPO antibodies (strongest); prior postpartum thyroiditis; type 1 DM; other autoimmune disease (SLE); family history
PhasesBiphasic: hyperthyroid (1-4 months postpartum) → euthyroid → hypothyroid (4-8 months) → recovery; 20-30% have isolated hypothyroid
Hyperthyroid phaseSymptoms: fatigue, palpitations, weight loss, anxiety; thyrotoxic but no ophthalmopathy; due to thyroid hormone release from inflammation
Hypothyroid phaseSymptoms: fatigue, weight gain, depression, constipation, dry skin, hair loss; depression may be misattributed to PND
DiagnosisTSH, free T4, free T3; TPO antibodies; thyroid USS; low uptake on radioisotope scan (avoid in breastfeeding if possible)
DifferentialGraves' disease (ophthalmopathy, persistently high uptake); PND; anaemia
Management hyperthyroidβ-blocker (propranolol); no antithyroid drugs (destructive, not synthetic); usually self-limiting
Management hypothyroidLevothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10; weaning trial 6-12 months; monitor
Long-term20-30% permanent hypothyroidism; recurrence in 70% subsequent pregnancies; screen if symptomatic

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Postpartum thyroiditis?

Postpartum thyroiditis is an autoimmune thyroiditis occurring within 12 months of delivery, often presenting in a biphasic pattern of hyperthyroidism followed by hypothyroidism. It affects 5-10% of postpartum women and is associated with anti-TPO antibodies. Most women return to euthyroid but a proportion develop permanent hypothyroidism.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Postpartum thyroiditis?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Risk factors, Phases, Hyperthyroid phase. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Postpartum thyroiditis diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Postpartum thyroiditis?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Postpartum thyroiditis?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Postpartum thyroiditis — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

A) TSH, free T4, free T3; TPO antibodies; thyroid USS; low uptake on radioisotope scan (avoid in breastfeeding if possible)

B) Graves' disease (ophthalmopathy, persistently high uptake); PND; anaemia

C) β-blocker (propranolol); no antithyroid drugs (destructive, not synthetic); usually self-limiting

D) Levothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10; weaning trial 6-12 months; monitor

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Postpartum thyroiditis?

A) Graves' disease (ophthalmopathy, persistently high uptake); PND; anaemia

B) β-blocker (propranolol); no antithyroid drugs (destructive, not synthetic); usually self-limiting

C) Levothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10; weaning trial 6-12 months; monitor

D) 20-30% permanent hypothyroidism; recurrence in 70% subsequent pregnancies; screen if symptomatic

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Postpartum thyroiditis?

A) β-blocker (propranolol); no antithyroid drugs (destructive, not synthetic); usually self-limiting

B) Levothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10; weaning trial 6-12 months; monitor

C) 20-30% permanent hypothyroidism; recurrence in 70% subsequent pregnancies; screen if symptomatic

D) Autoimmune thyroiditis within 12 months postpartum

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Postpartum thyroiditis?

A) Levothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10; weaning trial 6-12 months; monitor

B) 20-30% permanent hypothyroidism; recurrence in 70% subsequent pregnancies; screen if symptomatic

C) Autoimmune thyroiditis within 12 months postpartum

D) 5-10% of postpartum women; up to 25% if TPOAb+

Answer: A

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Postpartum thyroiditis. Most appropriate next step in management? | Autoimmune thyroiditis within 12 months postpartum
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Postpartum thyroiditis. Most appropriate next step in management? | 5-10% of postpartum women; up to 25% if TPOAb+
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Postpartum thyroiditis. Most appropriate next step in management? | Anti-TPO antibodies (strongest); prior postpartum thyroiditis; type 1 DM; other autoimmune disease (SLE); family history
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Postpartum thyroiditis. Most appropriate next step in management? | Biphasic: hyperthyroid (1-4 months postpartum) → euthyroid → hypothyroid (4-8 months) → recovery; 20-30% have isolated hypothyroid
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Postpartum thyroiditis. Most appropriate next step in management? | Symptoms: fatigue, palpitations, weight loss, anxiety; thyrotoxic but no ophthalmopathy; due to thyroid hormone release from inflammation
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Postpartum thyroiditis. Most appropriate next step in management? | Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, depression, constipation, dry skin, hair loss; depression may be misattributed to PND
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Postpartum thyroiditis. Most appropriate next step in management? | TSH, free T4, free T3; TPO antibodies; thyroid USS; low uptake on radioisotope scan (avoid in breastfeeding if possible)
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Postpartum thyroiditis. Most appropriate next step in management? | Graves' disease (ophthalmopathy, persistently high uptake); PND; anaemia
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Postpartum thyroiditis. Most appropriate next step in management? | β-blocker (propranolol); no antithyroid drugs (destructive, not synthetic); usually self-limiting
  1. A pregnant patient presents with features consistent with Postpartum thyroiditis. Most appropriate next step in management? | Levothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10; weaning trial 6-12 months; monitor

Flashcards

Q: Definition?

A: Autoimmune thyroiditis within 12 months postpartum

Q: Incidence?

A: 5-10% of postpartum women; up to 25% if TPOAb+

Q: Risk factors?

A: Anti-TPO antibodies (strongest); prior postpartum thyroiditis; type 1 DM; other autoimmune disease (SLE); family history

Q: Phases?

A: Biphasic: hyperthyroid (1-4 months postpartum) → euthyroid → hypothyroid (4-8 months) → recovery; 20-30% have isolated hypothyroid

Q: Hyperthyroid phase?

A: Symptoms: fatigue, palpitations, weight loss, anxiety; thyrotoxic but no ophthalmopathy; due to thyroid hormone release from inflammation

Q: Hypothyroid phase?

A: Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, depression, constipation, dry skin, hair loss; depression may be misattributed to PND

Q: Diagnosis?

A: TSH, free T4, free T3; TPO antibodies; thyroid USS; low uptake on radioisotope scan (avoid in breastfeeding if possible)

Q: Differential?

A: Graves' disease (ophthalmopathy, persistently high uptake); PND; anaemia

Answer Key with Explanations

  9. A — Key feature of Postpartum thyroiditis?
  10. A — Key feature of Postpartum thyroiditis?

SBAs

  2. 5-10% of postpartum women; up to 25% if TPOAb+
  3. Anti-TPO antibodies (strongest); prior postpartum thyroiditis; type 1 DM; other autoimmune disease (SLE); family history
  4. Biphasic: hyperthyroid (1-4 months postpartum) → euthyroid → hypothyroid (4-8 months) → recovery; 20-30% have isolated hypothyroid
  5. Symptoms: fatigue, palpitations, weight loss, anxiety; thyrotoxic but no ophthalmopathy; due to thyroid hormone release from inflammation
  6. Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, depression, constipation, dry skin, hair loss; depression may be misattributed to PND
  7. TSH, free T4, free T3; TPO antibodies; thyroid USS; low uptake on radioisotope scan (avoid in breastfeeding if possible)
  8. Graves' disease (ophthalmopathy, persistently high uptake); PND; anaemia
  9. β-blocker (propranolol); no antithyroid drugs (destructive, not synthetic); usually self-limiting
  10. Levothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10; weaning trial 6-12 months; monitor

▸ 30.7.6 Thyroid Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Thyroid disease in pregnancy is common and includes overt hypothyroidism, subclinical hypothyroidism, isolated hypothyroxinaemia, hyperthyroidism (usually Graves'), and postpartum thyroiditis. Both maternal hypo- and hyperthyroidism adversely affect pregnancy and fetal development.

Core Clinical Content

Physiology

TBG rises (oestrogen); total T4/T3 rise; free T4 unchanged; TSH falls in 1st trimester (hCG)

Trimester-specific ranges

TSH: 1st 0.1-2.5; 2nd 0.2-3.0; 3rd 0.3-3.0 mIU/L (population-specific)

Hypothyroidism

Prevalence 1-2%; levothyroxine dose often needs increase 30-50% in 1st trimester; target TSH <2.5

Hashimoto's

Most common cause; anti-TPO antibodies; monitor after delivery

Hyperthyroidism

Prevalence 0.1-0.4%; 90% Graves' disease; risks: pre-eclampsia, IUGR, preterm, stillbirth, fetal thyrotoxicosis

Treatment hyperthyroidism

Propylthiouracil (1st trimester) then switch to carbimazole; avoid methimazole in 1st trimester; target FT4 in upper normal range

Side effects

PTU: hepatotoxicity; carbimazole: aplasia cutis, choanal atresia (1st trimester)

Fetal monitoring

Fetal heart rate (>160 — thyrotoxicosis); growth; goitre on US

Postpartum thyroiditis

Affects 5-10%; hyper then hypo phase; anti-TPO; screen at 6 weeks if high risk

Postpartum care

Reassess thyroid function 6 weeks postpartum; reduce levothyroxine to pre-pregnancy dose

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PhysiologyTBG rises (oestrogen); total T4/T3 rise; free T4 unchanged; TSH falls in 1st trimester (hCG)
Trimester-specific rangesTSH: 1st 0.1-2.5; 2nd 0.2-3.0; 3rd 0.3-3.0 mIU/L (population-specific)
HypothyroidismPrevalence 1-2%; levothyroxine dose often needs increase 30-50% in 1st trimester; target TSH <2.5
Hashimoto'sMost common cause; anti-TPO antibodies; monitor after delivery
HyperthyroidismPrevalence 0.1-0.4%; 90% Graves' disease; risks: pre-eclampsia, IUGR, preterm, stillbirth, fetal thyrotoxicosis
Treatment hyperthyroidismPropylthiouracil (1st trimester) then switch to carbimazole; avoid methimazole in 1st trimester; target FT4 in upper normal range
Side effectsPTU: hepatotoxicity; carbimazole: aplasia cutis, choanal atresia (1st trimester)
Fetal monitoringFetal heart rate (>160 — thyrotoxicosis); growth; goitre on US
Postpartum thyroiditisAffects 5-10%; hyper then hypo phase; anti-TPO; screen at 6 weeks if high risk
Postpartum careReassess thyroid function 6 weeks postpartum; reduce levothyroxine to pre-pregnancy dose

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Thyroid disease in pregnancy?

Thyroid disease in pregnancy is common and includes overt hypothyroidism, subclinical hypothyroidism, isolated hypothyroxinaemia, hyperthyroidism (usually Graves'), and postpartum thyroiditis. Both maternal hypo- and hyperthyroidism adversely affect pregnancy and fetal development.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Thyroid disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Physiology, Trimester-specific ranges, Hypothyroidism, Hashimoto's, Hyperthyroidism. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Thyroid disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Thyroid disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Thyroid disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Thyroid disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.8 Haematological Disorders in Pregnancy 8
▸ 30.8.1 Antiphospholipid Syndrome In Pregnancy

Definition

Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) in pregnancy is associated with recurrent miscarriage, fetal loss, pre-eclampsia, IUGR, and thrombosis. Diagnosis requires persistent antiphospholipid antibodies plus clinical features. Treatment with aspirin ± LMWH improves pregnancy outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

Antibodies

Lupus anticoagulant (strongest); anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I

Obstetric morbidity

≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

Thrombotic risk

Arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE); pregnancy adds risk

Catastrophic APS

Rapid multi-organ thrombosis; mortality ~30%; intensive care; triple therapy

Preconception

Confirm antibodies 12 weeks apart; counsel; plan aspirin + LMWH

Treatment

Aspirin 75-150 mg daily + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy; therapeutic LMWH if prior VTE

Monitoring

Uterine artery Doppler at 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

Postpartum

Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong anticoagulation if thrombosis

Prognosis

Live birth 70-80% with treatment; recurrence 5-10%

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPersistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)
AntibodiesLupus anticoagulant (strongest); anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I
Obstetric morbidity≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency
Thrombotic riskArterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE); pregnancy adds risk
Catastrophic APSRapid multi-organ thrombosis; mortality ~30%; intensive care; triple therapy
PreconceptionConfirm antibodies 12 weeks apart; counsel; plan aspirin + LMWH
TreatmentAspirin 75-150 mg daily + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy; therapeutic LMWH if prior VTE
MonitoringUterine artery Doppler at 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance
PostpartumContinue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong anticoagulation if thrombosis
PrognosisLive birth 70-80% with treatment; recurrence 5-10%

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) in pregnancy is associated with recurrent miscarriage, fetal loss, pre-eclampsia, IUGR, and thrombosis. Diagnosis requires persistent antiphospholipid antibodies plus clinical features. Treatment with aspirin ± LMWH improves pregnancy outcomes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

Key features include: Definition, Antibodies, Obstetric morbidity, Thrombotic risk, Catastrophic APS. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

A) Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

B) Lupus anticoagulant (strongest); anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I

C) ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

D) Arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE); pregnancy adds risk

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

A) Lupus anticoagulant (strongest); anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I

B) ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

C) Arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE); pregnancy adds risk

D) Rapid multi-organ thrombosis; mortality ~30%; intensive care; triple therapy

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

A) ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

B) Arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE); pregnancy adds risk

C) Rapid multi-organ thrombosis; mortality ~30%; intensive care; triple therapy

D) Confirm antibodies 12 weeks apart; counsel; plan aspirin + LMWH

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

A) Arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE); pregnancy adds risk

B) Rapid multi-organ thrombosis; mortality ~30%; intensive care; triple therapy

C) Confirm antibodies 12 weeks apart; counsel; plan aspirin + LMWH

D) Aspirin 75-150 mg daily + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy; therapeutic LMWH if prior VTE

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

A) Rapid multi-organ thrombosis; mortality ~30%; intensive care; triple therapy

B) Confirm antibodies 12 weeks apart; counsel; plan aspirin + LMWH

C) Aspirin 75-150 mg daily + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy; therapeutic LMWH if prior VTE

D) Uterine artery Doppler at 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

A) Confirm antibodies 12 weeks apart; counsel; plan aspirin + LMWH

B) Aspirin 75-150 mg daily + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy; therapeutic LMWH if prior VTE

C) Uterine artery Doppler at 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

D) Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong anticoagulation if thrombosis

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

A) Aspirin 75-150 mg daily + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy; therapeutic LMWH if prior VTE

B) Uterine artery Doppler at 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

C) Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong anticoagulation if thrombosis

D) Live birth 70-80% with treatment; recurrence 5-10%

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

A) Uterine artery Doppler at 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

B) Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong anticoagulation if thrombosis

C) Live birth 70-80% with treatment; recurrence 5-10%

D) Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

A) Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong anticoagulation if thrombosis

B) Live birth 70-80% with treatment; recurrence 5-10%

C) Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

D) Lupus anticoagulant (strongest); anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Antiphospholipid syndrome in pregnancy?

A) Live birth 70-80% with treatment; recurrence 5-10%

B) Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

C) Lupus anticoagulant (strongest); anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I

D) ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

Answer: A

Flashcards

Q: Definition?

A: Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

Q: Antibodies?

A: Lupus anticoagulant (strongest); anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I

Q: Obstetric morbidity?

A: ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

Q: Thrombotic risk?

A: Arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE); pregnancy adds risk

Q: Catastrophic APS?

A: Rapid multi-organ thrombosis; mortality ~30%; intensive care; triple therapy

Q: Preconception?

A: Confirm antibodies 12 weeks apart; counsel; plan aspirin + LMWH

Q: Treatment?

A: Aspirin 75-150 mg daily + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy; therapeutic LMWH if prior VTE

Q: Monitoring?

A: Uterine artery Doppler at 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

Answer Key with Explanations

  4. Arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE); pregnancy adds risk
  5. Rapid multi-organ thrombosis; mortality ~30%; intensive care; triple therapy
  6. Confirm antibodies 12 weeks apart; counsel; plan aspirin + LMWH
  7. Aspirin 75-150 mg daily + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy; therapeutic LMWH if prior VTE
  8. Uterine artery Doppler at 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance
  9. Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong anticoagulation if thrombosis
  10. Live birth 70-80% with treatment; recurrence 5-10%

▸ 30.8.2 Iron Deficiency Anaemia In Pregnancy

Definition

Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy is the most common anaemia in pregnancy worldwide and a major cause of maternal morbidity, preterm birth, and low birth weight. Diagnosis uses trimester-specific cut-offs; treatment combines oral iron with parenteral iron when needed.

Core Clinical Content

Definitions

1st trimester Hb <110; 2nd/3rd <105 g/L; postpartum <100 g/L (WHO)

Prevalence

20-40% of pregnancies; 50%+ in LMIC

Causes

Iron deficiency (90%); folate/B12 deficiency; haemoglobinopathies; haemolysis

Investigations

FBC; MCV (microcytic); ferritin (<30 μg/L = depleted); serum iron; TIBC

Maternal impact

Fatigue, breathlessness; pre-eclampsia; postpartum haemorrhage; infection; transfusion need

Fetal impact

IUGR; preterm birth; low birth weight; poor neurodevelopment

Oral iron

Ferrous sulphate 200 mg BD-TDS; alternate-day dosing improves absorption; take with vitamin C

IV iron

Iron sucrose, ferric carboxymaltose, iron isomaltoside — for intolerance/non-response/severe

Blood transfusion

Reserved for severe symptomatic anaemia near term; aim Hb >70-80 g/L

Prevention

Routine oral iron from 16 weeks; high-risk women from 1st trimester

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Definitions1st trimester Hb <110; 2nd/3rd <105 g/L; postpartum <100 g/L (WHO)
Prevalence20-40% of pregnancies; 50%+ in LMIC
CausesIron deficiency (90%); folate/B12 deficiency; haemoglobinopathies; haemolysis
InvestigationsFBC; MCV (microcytic); ferritin (<30 μg/L = depleted); serum iron; TIBC
Maternal impactFatigue, breathlessness; pre-eclampsia; postpartum haemorrhage; infection; transfusion need
Fetal impactIUGR; preterm birth; low birth weight; poor neurodevelopment
Oral ironFerrous sulphate 200 mg BD-TDS; alternate-day dosing improves absorption; take with vitamin C
IV ironIron sucrose, ferric carboxymaltose, iron isomaltoside — for intolerance/non-response/severe
Blood transfusionReserved for severe symptomatic anaemia near term; aim Hb >70-80 g/L
PreventionRoutine oral iron from 16 weeks; high-risk women from 1st trimester

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy?

Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy is the most common anaemia in pregnancy worldwide and a major cause of maternal morbidity, preterm birth, and low birth weight. Diagnosis uses trimester-specific cut-offs; treatment combines oral iron with parenteral iron when needed.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy?

Key features include: Definitions, Prevalence, Causes, Investigations, Maternal impact. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.8.3 Myeloproliferative Disorders In Pregnancy

Definition

Myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPN) — essential thrombocythaemia (ET), polycythaemia vera (PV), and primary myelofibrosis — are clonal haematopoietic disorders. In pregnancy they are associated with increased risk of thrombosis, haemorrhage, miscarriage, IUGR, stillbirth, and pre-eclampsia. Specialist MDT care is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

ET most common MPN in pregnancy; median age 30-40; affects women in childbearing years

ET pathophysiology

JAK2 V617F (50%); CALR (25%); MPL (5%); triple negative (20%); platelets >450

PV pathophysiology

JAK2 V617F in 95%; raised Hb/Hct; EPO low

Complications pregnancy

Arterial and venous thrombosis (DVT, PE, stroke, MI); haemorrhage; miscarriage; IUGR; stillbirth; pre-eclampsia; placental abruption; preterm

Preconception

MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); optimise counts; switch teratogens (hydroxyurea → IFN-α); aspirin; review

Treatment

Aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH (prophylactic or therapeutic depending on risk); cytoreduction if counts very high (IFN-α preferred); venesection for PV; avoid hydroxyurea; avoid anagrelide 1st trimester

Monitoring

FBC 2-4 weekly; U&E; LFT; uric acid; growth scans; uterine artery Doppler; MCA Doppler if needed

Delivery

Planned; discontinue LMWH 12-24 h before if regional anaesthesia; consider platelet count if needed

Postpartum

Continue LMWH 6 weeks; aspirin; breastfeeding compatible with IFN-α; resume prior therapy

Counselling

Recurrence risk; consider PGD if heritable component; multidisciplinary follow-up lifelong

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
EpidemiologyET most common MPN in pregnancy; median age 30-40; affects women in childbearing years
ET pathophysiologyJAK2 V617F (50%); CALR (25%); MPL (5%); triple negative (20%); platelets >450
PV pathophysiologyJAK2 V617F in 95%; raised Hb/Hct; EPO low
Complications pregnancyArterial and venous thrombosis (DVT, PE, stroke, MI); haemorrhage; miscarriage; IUGR; stillbirth; pre-eclampsia; placental abruption; preterm
PreconceptionMDT (haematologist, obstetrician); optimise counts; switch teratogens (hydroxyurea → IFN-α); aspirin; review
TreatmentAspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH (prophylactic or therapeutic depending on risk); cytoreduction if counts very high (IFN-α preferred); venesection for PV; avoid hydroxyurea; avoid anagrelide 1st trimester
MonitoringFBC 2-4 weekly; U&E; LFT; uric acid; growth scans; uterine artery Doppler; MCA Doppler if needed
DeliveryPlanned; discontinue LMWH 12-24 h before if regional anaesthesia; consider platelet count if needed
PostpartumContinue LMWH 6 weeks; aspirin; breastfeeding compatible with IFN-α; resume prior therapy
CounsellingRecurrence risk; consider PGD if heritable component; multidisciplinary follow-up lifelong

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

Myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPN) — essential thrombocythaemia (ET), polycythaemia vera (PV), and primary myelofibrosis — are clonal haematopoietic disorders. In pregnancy they are associated with increased risk of thrombosis, haemorrhage, miscarriage, IUGR, stillbirth, and pre-eclampsia. Specialist MDT care is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, ET pathophysiology, PV pathophysiology, Complications pregnancy, Preconception. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  1. Which of the following is true about Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

A) ET most common MPN in pregnancy; median age 30-40; affects women in childbearing years

B) JAK2 V617F (50%); CALR (25%); MPL (5%); triple negative (20%); platelets >450

C) JAK2 V617F in 95%; raised Hb/Hct; EPO low

D) Arterial and venous thrombosis (DVT, PE, stroke, MI); haemorrhage; miscarriage; IUGR; stillbirth; pre-eclampsia; placental abruption; preterm

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

A) JAK2 V617F (50%); CALR (25%); MPL (5%); triple negative (20%); platelets >450

B) JAK2 V617F in 95%; raised Hb/Hct; EPO low

C) Arterial and venous thrombosis (DVT, PE, stroke, MI); haemorrhage; miscarriage; IUGR; stillbirth; pre-eclampsia; placental abruption; preterm

D) MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); optimise counts; switch teratogens (hydroxyurea → IFN-α); aspirin; review

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

A) JAK2 V617F in 95%; raised Hb/Hct; EPO low

B) Arterial and venous thrombosis (DVT, PE, stroke, MI); haemorrhage; miscarriage; IUGR; stillbirth; pre-eclampsia; placental abruption; preterm

C) MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); optimise counts; switch teratogens (hydroxyurea → IFN-α); aspirin; review

D) Aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH (prophylactic or therapeutic depending on risk); cytoreduction if counts very high (IFN-α preferred); venesection for PV; avoid hydroxyurea; avoid anagrelide 1st trimester

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

A) Arterial and venous thrombosis (DVT, PE, stroke, MI); haemorrhage; miscarriage; IUGR; stillbirth; pre-eclampsia; placental abruption; preterm

B) MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); optimise counts; switch teratogens (hydroxyurea → IFN-α); aspirin; review

C) Aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH (prophylactic or therapeutic depending on risk); cytoreduction if counts very high (IFN-α preferred); venesection for PV; avoid hydroxyurea; avoid anagrelide 1st trimester

D) FBC 2-4 weekly; U&E; LFT; uric acid; growth scans; uterine artery Doppler; MCA Doppler if needed

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

A) MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); optimise counts; switch teratogens (hydroxyurea → IFN-α); aspirin; review

B) Aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH (prophylactic or therapeutic depending on risk); cytoreduction if counts very high (IFN-α preferred); venesection for PV; avoid hydroxyurea; avoid anagrelide 1st trimester

C) FBC 2-4 weekly; U&E; LFT; uric acid; growth scans; uterine artery Doppler; MCA Doppler if needed

D) Planned; discontinue LMWH 12-24 h before if regional anaesthesia; consider platelet count if needed

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

A) Aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH (prophylactic or therapeutic depending on risk); cytoreduction if counts very high (IFN-α preferred); venesection for PV; avoid hydroxyurea; avoid anagrelide 1st trimester

B) FBC 2-4 weekly; U&E; LFT; uric acid; growth scans; uterine artery Doppler; MCA Doppler if needed

C) Planned; discontinue LMWH 12-24 h before if regional anaesthesia; consider platelet count if needed

D) Continue LMWH 6 weeks; aspirin; breastfeeding compatible with IFN-α; resume prior therapy

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

A) FBC 2-4 weekly; U&E; LFT; uric acid; growth scans; uterine artery Doppler; MCA Doppler if needed

B) Planned; discontinue LMWH 12-24 h before if regional anaesthesia; consider platelet count if needed

C) Continue LMWH 6 weeks; aspirin; breastfeeding compatible with IFN-α; resume prior therapy

D) Recurrence risk; consider PGD if heritable component; multidisciplinary follow-up lifelong

▸ 30.8.4 Sickle Cell Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Sickle cell disease in pregnancy is associated with increased maternal and fetal complications — painful crises, infections, pre-eclampsia, IUGR, and preterm birth. Multidisciplinary care in a specialist centre with prophylactic transfusion, exchange transfusion for crises, and intensive monitoring is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

Highest in sub-Saharan Africa, Caribbean, Middle East; 1 in 2,000-10,000 pregnancies

Maternal risks

Painful crises; infection (UTI, pneumonia, sepsis); pre-eclampsia; anaemia; acute chest syndrome; VTE; death

Fetal risks

Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth (HbF/APCR helps)

Preconception

Genetic counselling; partner screening; medication review; hydroxyurea stop 3 months before

Antenatal care

MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); folic acid 5 mg; penicillin V prophylaxis; transfusion programme debated

Transfusion

Prophylactic top-up transfusion if Hb <70-80 or symptoms; exchange transfusion for severe complications

Monitoring

Monthly FBC, U&E, LFT; growth scans; uterine artery Doppler; MCA Doppler

Acute crisis

Analgesia (parenteral opioids); oxygen; hydration; antibiotics; transfusion; exclude other causes

Labour and delivery

Vaginal preferred; avoid dehydration, hypoxia, cold; continuous CTG

Postpartum

VTE prophylaxis; close monitoring for crises; contraception counselling; avoid oestrogen

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
PrevalenceHighest in sub-Saharan Africa, Caribbean, Middle East; 1 in 2,000-10,000 pregnancies
Maternal risksPainful crises; infection (UTI, pneumonia, sepsis); pre-eclampsia; anaemia; acute chest syndrome; VTE; death
Fetal risksMiscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth (HbF/APCR helps)
PreconceptionGenetic counselling; partner screening; medication review; hydroxyurea stop 3 months before
Antenatal careMDT (haematologist, obstetrician); folic acid 5 mg; penicillin V prophylaxis; transfusion programme debated
TransfusionProphylactic top-up transfusion if Hb <70-80 or symptoms; exchange transfusion for severe complications
MonitoringMonthly FBC, U&E, LFT; growth scans; uterine artery Doppler; MCA Doppler
Acute crisisAnalgesia (parenteral opioids); oxygen; hydration; antibiotics; transfusion; exclude other causes
Labour and deliveryVaginal preferred; avoid dehydration, hypoxia, cold; continuous CTG
PostpartumVTE prophylaxis; close monitoring for crises; contraception counselling; avoid oestrogen

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Sickle cell disease in pregnancy?

Sickle cell disease in pregnancy is associated with increased maternal and fetal complications — painful crises, infections, pre-eclampsia, IUGR, and preterm birth. Multidisciplinary care in a specialist centre with prophylactic transfusion, exchange transfusion for crises, and intensive monitoring is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Sickle cell disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, Preconception, Antenatal care. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Sickle cell disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Sickle cell disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Sickle cell disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Sickle cell disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)
