▸ 30.8.5 Thalassaemia In Pregnancy

Definition

Thalassaemia in pregnancy — alpha and beta — are inherited disorders of haemoglobin synthesis common in Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, South Asian, and South-East Asian populations. Maternal complications include anaemia, transfusion requirements, iron overload, and thromboembolism; severe fetal forms (α-thalassaemia major, β-thalassaemia major) cause hydrops fetalis or lifelong transfusion dependence.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Inherited disorders of globin chain synthesis; α (4 genes) and β (2 genes)

α-thalassaemia

Silent carrier (1 gene); trait (2); HbH (3) — moderate anaemia; Hb Barts (4) — hydrops fetalis, lethal

β-thalassaemia

Trait (carrier) — mild anaemia; intermedia — moderate; major — transfusion dependent; chelation

Screening

MCV <80; ferritin normal; Hb electrophoresis/HPLC; partner testing if MCV low; cascade screening in families

Preconception

Genetic counselling; partner testing; PGD if both carriers; optimise transfusion/chelation

Antenatal care

MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); serial Hb; transfuse if <80 g/L or symptomatic; folic acid; iron if deficient (cautious); monitor chelation (deferoxamine safe; deferasirox avoid)

Fetal monitoring

Anomaly scan; MCA Doppler for fetal anaemia; consideration of intrauterine transfusion if hydrops

Risks maternal

Anaemia; transfusion; thromboembolism; alloimmunisation; iron overload; cardiac complications

Risks fetal/neonatal

IUGR; preterm birth; hydrops (α-major); congenital anomalies if transfused in 1st trimester

Delivery

Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage if cardiomyopathy; avoid ergometrine; active management

Postpartum

VTE prophylaxis; contraception (avoid COC if iron overload/high thrombosis risk); transfusion plan

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionInherited disorders of globin chain synthesis; α (4 genes) and β (2 genes)
α-thalassaemiaSilent carrier (1 gene); trait (2); HbH (3) — moderate anaemia; Hb Barts (4) — hydrops fetalis, lethal
β-thalassaemiaTrait (carrier) — mild anaemia; intermedia — moderate; major — transfusion dependent; chelation
ScreeningMCV <80; ferritin normal; Hb electrophoresis/HPLC; partner testing if MCV low; cascade screening in families
PreconceptionGenetic counselling; partner testing; PGD if both carriers; optimise transfusion/chelation
Antenatal careMDT (haematologist, obstetrician); serial Hb; transfuse if <80 g/L or symptomatic; folic acid; iron if deficient (cautious); monitor chelation (deferoxamine safe; deferasirox avoid)
Fetal monitoringAnomaly scan; MCA Doppler for fetal anaemia; consideration of intrauterine transfusion if hydrops
Risks maternalAnaemia; transfusion; thromboembolism; alloimmunisation; iron overload; cardiac complications
Risks fetal/neonatalIUGR; preterm birth; hydrops (α-major); congenital anomalies if transfused in 1st trimester
DeliveryVaginal preferred; assisted second stage if cardiomyopathy; avoid ergometrine; active management
PostpartumVTE prophylaxis; contraception (avoid COC if iron overload/high thrombosis risk); transfusion plan

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

Thalassaemia in pregnancy — alpha and beta — are inherited disorders of haemoglobin synthesis common in Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, South Asian, and South-East Asian populations. Maternal complications include anaemia, transfusion requirements, iron overload, and thromboembolism; severe fetal forms (α-thalassaemia major, β-thalassaemia major) cause hydrops fetalis or lifelong transfusion dependence.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

Key features include: Definition, α-thalassaemia, β-thalassaemia, Screening, Preconception. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Thalassaemia in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Thalassaemia in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

MCQs (10)

  1. Which of the following is true about Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

A) Inherited disorders of globin chain synthesis; α (4 genes) and β (2 genes)

B) Silent carrier (1 gene); trait (2); HbH (3) — moderate anaemia; Hb Barts (4) — hydrops fetalis, lethal

C) Trait (carrier) — mild anaemia; intermedia — moderate; major — transfusion dependent; chelation

D) MCV <80; ferritin normal; Hb electrophoresis/HPLC; partner testing if MCV low; cascade screening in families

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

A) Silent carrier (1 gene); trait (2); HbH (3) — moderate anaemia; Hb Barts (4) — hydrops fetalis, lethal

B) Trait (carrier) — mild anaemia; intermedia — moderate; major — transfusion dependent; chelation

C) MCV <80; ferritin normal; Hb electrophoresis/HPLC; partner testing if MCV low; cascade screening in families

D) Genetic counselling; partner testing; PGD if both carriers; optimise transfusion/chelation

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

A) Trait (carrier) — mild anaemia; intermedia — moderate; major — transfusion dependent; chelation

B) MCV <80; ferritin normal; Hb electrophoresis/HPLC; partner testing if MCV low; cascade screening in families

C) Genetic counselling; partner testing; PGD if both carriers; optimise transfusion/chelation

D) MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); serial Hb; transfuse if <80 g/L or symptomatic; folic acid; iron if deficient (cautious); monitor chelation (deferoxamine safe; deferasirox avoid)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

A) MCV <80; ferritin normal; Hb electrophoresis/HPLC; partner testing if MCV low; cascade screening in families

B) Genetic counselling; partner testing; PGD if both carriers; optimise transfusion/chelation

C) MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); serial Hb; transfuse if <80 g/L or symptomatic; folic acid; iron if deficient (cautious); monitor chelation (deferoxamine safe; deferasirox avoid)

D) Anomaly scan; MCA Doppler for fetal anaemia; consideration of intrauterine transfusion if hydrops

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

A) Genetic counselling; partner testing; PGD if both carriers; optimise transfusion/chelation

B) MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); serial Hb; transfuse if <80 g/L or symptomatic; folic acid; iron if deficient (cautious); monitor chelation (deferoxamine safe; deferasirox avoid)

C) Anomaly scan; MCA Doppler for fetal anaemia; consideration of intrauterine transfusion if hydrops

D) Anaemia; transfusion; thromboembolism; alloimmunisation; iron overload; cardiac complications

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

A) MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); serial Hb; transfuse if <80 g/L or symptomatic; folic acid; iron if deficient (cautious); monitor chelation (deferoxamine safe; deferasirox avoid)

B) Anomaly scan; MCA Doppler for fetal anaemia; consideration of intrauterine transfusion if hydrops

C) Anaemia; transfusion; thromboembolism; alloimmunisation; iron overload; cardiac complications

D) IUGR; preterm birth; hydrops (α-major); congenital anomalies if transfused in 1st trimester

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

A) Anomaly scan; MCA Doppler for fetal anaemia; consideration of intrauterine transfusion if hydrops

B) Anaemia; transfusion; thromboembolism; alloimmunisation; iron overload; cardiac complications

C) IUGR; preterm birth; hydrops (α-major); congenital anomalies if transfused in 1st trimester

D) Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage if cardiomyopathy; avoid ergometrine; active management

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

A) Anaemia; transfusion; thromboembolism; alloimmunisation; iron overload; cardiac complications

B) IUGR; preterm birth; hydrops (α-major); congenital anomalies if transfused in 1st trimester

C) Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage if cardiomyopathy; avoid ergometrine; active management

D) VTE prophylaxis; contraception (avoid COC if iron overload/high thrombosis risk); transfusion plan

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

A) IUGR; preterm birth; hydrops (α-major); congenital anomalies if transfused in 1st trimester

B) Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage if cardiomyopathy; avoid ergometrine; active management

C) VTE prophylaxis; contraception (avoid COC if iron overload/high thrombosis risk); transfusion plan

D) Inherited disorders of globin chain synthesis; α (4 genes) and β (2 genes)

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

A) Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage if cardiomyopathy; avoid ergometrine; active management

B) VTE prophylaxis; contraception (avoid COC if iron overload/high thrombosis risk); transfusion plan

C) Inherited disorders of globin chain synthesis; α (4 genes) and β (2 genes)

D) Silent carrier (1 gene); trait (2); HbH (3) — moderate anaemia; Hb Barts (4) — hydrops fetalis, lethal

Answer: A

A: Trait (carrier) — mild anaemia; intermedia — moderate; major — transfusion dependent; chelation

Q: Fetal monitoring?

A: Anomaly scan; MCA Doppler for fetal anaemia; consideration of intrauterine transfusion if hydrops

Q: Risks maternal?

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.8.6 Thrombocytopenia In Pregnancy

Definition

Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy affects 6-10% of pregnancies. Gestational thrombocytopenia is the most common cause, but pre-eclampsia, HELLP, ITP, TTP, and HUS require urgent differentiation. Severe thrombocytopenia (<50 × 10^9/L) increases bleeding risk and affects delivery mode.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

6-10% of pregnancies; 1% have platelets <100 × 10^9/L

Causes (mnemonic)

GESTATIONAL — Gestational, Eclampsia, Sepsis, TTP, HELLP, AFLP, ITP, DIC, Neonatal alloimmune, APLS, Malignant marrow

Gestational

Mild (>70); 2nd/3rd trimester; no bleeding; resolves postpartum

ITP

Autoimmune; platelets <100; 1st/2nd trimester onset; bleeding tendency; steroids/IVIG for <30

TTP

Pentad — MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neuro, renal, fever; ADAMTS13 <10%; plasma exchange; mortality 90% untreated

Pre-eclampsia/HELLP

Severe features; LFT deranged; coagulopathy in HELLP

Neonatal alloimmune

Mother makes antibodies against fetal platelets; risk ICH; treat with IVIG

Investigation

FBC, blood film, LFT, LDH, haptoglobin, coagulation, autoantibodies, viral screen

Management

Treat cause; ITP — steroids/IVIG for <30 or bleeding; platelet transfusion only for surgery/bleeding

Delivery threshold

Vaginal safe >50; C-section >50; epidural/anaesthesia requires >70-80

Postpartum

ITP may flare; monitor; neonatal platelet count in ITP

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence6-10% of pregnancies; 1% have platelets <100 × 10^9/L
Causes (mnemonic)GESTATIONAL — Gestational, Eclampsia, Sepsis, TTP, HELLP, AFLP, ITP, DIC, Neonatal alloimmune, APLS, Malignant marrow
GestationalMild (>70); 2nd/3rd trimester; no bleeding; resolves postpartum
ITPAutoimmune; platelets <100; 1st/2nd trimester onset; bleeding tendency; steroids/IVIG for <30
TTPPentad — MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neuro, renal, fever; ADAMTS13 <10%; plasma exchange; mortality 90% untreated
Pre-eclampsia/HELLPSevere features; LFT deranged; coagulopathy in HELLP
Neonatal alloimmuneMother makes antibodies against fetal platelets; risk ICH; treat with IVIG
InvestigationFBC, blood film, LFT, LDH, haptoglobin, coagulation, autoantibodies, viral screen
ManagementTreat cause; ITP — steroids/IVIG for <30 or bleeding; platelet transfusion only for surgery/bleeding
Delivery thresholdVaginal safe >50; C-section >50; epidural/anaesthesia requires >70-80
PostpartumITP may flare; monitor; neonatal platelet count in ITP

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy?

Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy affects 6-10% of pregnancies. Gestational thrombocytopenia is the most common cause, but pre-eclampsia, HELLP, ITP, TTP, and HUS require urgent differentiation. Severe thrombocytopenia (<50 × 10^9/L) increases bleeding risk and affects delivery mode.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Causes (mnemonic), Gestational, ITP, TTP. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Local Navigation
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.8.7 Venous Thromboembolism In Pregnancy

Definition

Venous thromboembolism (VTE) — deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism — is a leading direct cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy increases VTE risk 4-5 fold due to venous stasis, hypercoagulability, and vascular damage. Prompt investigation and treatment with LMWH are essential.

Core Clinical Content

Risk

5-6x non-pregnant; 15-35x postpartum (first 6 weeks)

Sites

DVT: 80% left leg; iliofemoral > calf in pregnancy; PE: postpartum > antenatal

Risk factors

Prior VTE (strongest); obesity; age >35; thrombophilia; APS; caesarean; pre-eclampsia; multiple pregnancy; immobility; smoking

Clinical features DVT

Unilateral leg swelling/pain; tenderness along venous tract; pitting oedema; low-grade fever

Clinical features PE

Pleuritic chest pain; dyspnoea; tachycardia; haemoptysis; syncope; hypoxia

Diagnosis DVT

Compression duplex ultrasound (no radiation); iliac vein imaging if negative

Diagnosis PE

CXR; V/Q scan or CTPA (low fetal radiation); bilateral leg Doppler; D-dimer not useful

Treatment

LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BD); warfarin teratogenic; DOACs contraindicated; UFH if haemodynamic instability or delivery imminent

Duration

Minimum 3 months and until 6 weeks postpartum

Prevention

Risk assessment at booking and delivery; LMWH prophylaxis for intermediate/high risk; compression stockings; early mobilisation

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Risk5-6x non-pregnant; 15-35x postpartum (first 6 weeks)
SitesDVT: 80% left leg; iliofemoral > calf in pregnancy; PE: postpartum > antenatal
Risk factorsPrior VTE (strongest); obesity; age >35; thrombophilia; APS; caesarean; pre-eclampsia; multiple pregnancy; immobility; smoking
Clinical features DVTUnilateral leg swelling/pain; tenderness along venous tract; pitting oedema; low-grade fever
Clinical features PEPleuritic chest pain; dyspnoea; tachycardia; haemoptysis; syncope; hypoxia
Diagnosis DVTCompression duplex ultrasound (no radiation); iliac vein imaging if negative
Diagnosis PECXR; V/Q scan or CTPA (low fetal radiation); bilateral leg Doppler; D-dimer not useful
TreatmentLMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BD); warfarin teratogenic; DOACs contraindicated; UFH if haemodynamic instability or delivery imminent
DurationMinimum 3 months and until 6 weeks postpartum
PreventionRisk assessment at booking and delivery; LMWH prophylaxis for intermediate/high risk; compression stockings; early mobilisation

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy?

Venous thromboembolism (VTE) — deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism — is a leading direct cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy increases VTE risk 4-5 fold due to venous stasis, hypercoagulability, and vascular damage. Prompt investigation and treatment with LMWH are essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy?

Key features include: Risk, Sites, Risk factors, Clinical features DVT, Clinical features PE. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Flashcards

Q: Risk?

A: 5-6x non-pregnant; 15-35x postpartum (first 6 weeks)

Q: Sites?

A: DVT: 80% left leg; iliofemoral > calf in pregnancy; PE: postpartum > antenatal

Q: Risk factors?

A: Prior VTE (strongest); obesity; age >35; thrombophilia; APS; caesarean; pre-eclampsia; multiple pregnancy; immobility; smoking

Q: Clinical features DVT?

A: Unilateral leg swelling/pain; tenderness along venous tract; pitting oedema; low-grade fever

Q: Clinical features PE?

A: Pleuritic chest pain; dyspnoea; tachycardia; haemoptysis; syncope; hypoxia

Q: Diagnosis DVT?

A: Compression duplex ultrasound (no radiation); iliac vein imaging if negative

Q: Diagnosis PE?

A: CXR; V/Q scan or CTPA (low fetal radiation); bilateral leg Doppler; D-dimer not useful

Q: Treatment?

A: LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BD); warfarin teratogenic; DOACs contraindicated; UFH if haemodynamic instability or delivery imminent

▸ 30.8.8 Vitamin B12 And Folate Deficiency In Pregnancy

Definition

Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy cause megaloblastic anaemia and are associated with neural tube defects (folate), preterm birth, and neurodevelopmental delay (B12). Preconception folate supplementation reduces NTD risk. B12 deficiency is uncommon but important in vegan/vegetarian mothers, after bariatric surgery, or with pernicious anaemia.

Core Clinical Content

Folate requirements

Increased 5-10x in pregnancy; deficiency common if poor diet, malabsorption, anticonvulsants

Folate functions

DNA synthesis; cell division; NTD prevention; placental growth

B12 functions

Myelin synthesis; DNA synthesis; neurological function

Causes folate deficiency

Poor diet; multiple pregnancy; haemolysis; malabsorption; drugs (anticonvulsants, methotrexate, trimethoprim)

Causes B12 deficiency

Vegan/vegetarian diet; pernicious anaemia; post-bariatric; ileal disease (Crohn's); metformin

Folate deficiency effects

Megaloblastic anaemia; NTD (anencephaly, spina bifida); cleft lip/palate; cardiac defects; placental abruption; recurrent miscarriage

B12 deficiency effects

Megaloblastic anaemia; neurological (paresthesia, ataxia, dementia); infertility; preterm; low birth weight

Investigations

FBC (MCV >100); blood film (macroovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils); serum folate; B12; MMA; homocysteine; anti-IF antibody

Supplementation

Folic acid 400 μg daily preconception to 12 weeks; 5 mg if high risk (prior NTD, diabetes, antiepileptics, BMI >30, sickle); B12 IM for severe deficiency; oral if dietary

Postpartum

Continue supplementation if breastfeeding; assess mother's B12/folate if breastfed infant symptomatic

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Folate requirementsIncreased 5-10x in pregnancy; deficiency common if poor diet, malabsorption, anticonvulsants
Folate functionsDNA synthesis; cell division; NTD prevention; placental growth
B12 functionsMyelin synthesis; DNA synthesis; neurological function
Causes folate deficiencyPoor diet; multiple pregnancy; haemolysis; malabsorption; drugs (anticonvulsants, methotrexate, trimethoprim)
Causes B12 deficiencyVegan/vegetarian diet; pernicious anaemia; post-bariatric; ileal disease (Crohn's); metformin
Folate deficiency effectsMegaloblastic anaemia; NTD (anencephaly, spina bifida); cleft lip/palate; cardiac defects; placental abruption; recurrent miscarriage
B12 deficiency effectsMegaloblastic anaemia; neurological (paresthesia, ataxia, dementia); infertility; preterm; low birth weight
InvestigationsFBC (MCV >100); blood film (macroovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils); serum folate; B12; MMA; homocysteine; anti-IF antibody
SupplementationFolic acid 400 μg daily preconception to 12 weeks; 5 mg if high risk (prior NTD, diabetes, antiepileptics, BMI >30, sickle); B12 IM for severe deficiency; oral if dietary
PostpartumContinue supplementation if breastfeeding; assess mother's B12/folate if breastfed infant symptomatic

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?

Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy cause megaloblastic anaemia and are associated with neural tube defects (folate), preterm birth, and neurodevelopmental delay (B12). Preconception folate supplementation reduces NTD risk. B12 deficiency is uncommon but important in vegan/vegetarian mothers, after bariatric surgery, or with pernicious anaemia.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?

Key features include: Folate requirements, Folate functions, B12 functions, Causes folate deficiency, Causes B12 deficiency. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Flashcards

Q: Folate requirements?

A: Increased 5-10x in pregnancy; deficiency common if poor diet, malabsorption, anticonvulsants

Q: Folate functions?

A: DNA synthesis; cell division; NTD prevention; placental growth

Q: B12 functions?

A: Myelin synthesis; DNA synthesis; neurological function

Q: Causes folate deficiency?

A: Poor diet; multiple pregnancy; haemolysis; malabsorption; drugs (anticonvulsants, methotrexate, trimethoprim)

Q: Causes B12 deficiency?

A: Vegan/vegetarian diet; pernicious anaemia; post-bariatric; ileal disease (Crohn's); metformin

Q: Folate deficiency effects?

A: Megaloblastic anaemia; NTD (anencephaly, spina bifida); cleft lip/palate; cardiac defects; placental abruption; recurrent miscarriage

Q: B12 deficiency effects?

A: Megaloblastic anaemia; neurological (paresthesia, ataxia, dementia); infertility; preterm; low birth weight

Q: Investigations?

A: FBC (MCV >100); blood film (macroovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils); serum folate; B12; MMA; homocysteine; anti-IF antibody

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Key feature of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?
  2. A — Key feature of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?
  3. A — Key feature of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?
  4. A — Key feature of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?
  5. A — Key feature of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?
  6. A — Key feature of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?
  7. A — Key feature of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?
  8. A — Key feature of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?
  9. A — Key feature of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?
  10. A — Key feature of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?

SBAs

  1. Increased 5-10x in pregnancy; deficiency common if poor diet, malabsorption, anticonvulsants
  2. DNA synthesis; cell division; NTD prevention; placental growth
  3. Myelin synthesis; DNA synthesis; neurological function
  4. Poor diet; multiple pregnancy; haemolysis; malabsorption; drugs (anticonvulsants, methotrexate, trimethoprim)
  5. Vegan/vegetarian diet; pernicious anaemia; post-bariatric; ileal disease (Crohn's); metformin
  6. Megaloblastic anaemia; NTD (anencephaly, spina bifida); cleft lip/palate; cardiac defects; placental abruption; recurrent miscarriage
  7. Megaloblastic anaemia; neurological (paresthesia, ataxia, dementia); infertility; preterm; low birth weight
  8. FBC (MCV >100); blood film (macroovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils); serum folate; B12; MMA; homocysteine; anti-IF antibody
  9. Folic acid 400 μg daily preconception to 12 weeks; 5 mg if high risk (prior NTD, diabetes, antiepileptics, BMI >30, sickle); B12 IM for severe deficiency; oral if dietary
  10. Continue supplementation if breastfeeding; assess mother's B12/folate if breastfed infant symptomatic

▸ 30.9 Heart Disease in Pregnancy 7
▸ 30.9.1 Aortic Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Aortic disease in pregnancy, including aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection, and conditions predisposing to dissection (Marfan, Loeys-Dietz, vascular Ehlers-Danlos, bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation), carries significant mortality. Pregnancy increases aortic wall stress due to hyperdynamic circulation and hormonal effects on connective tissue.

Core Clinical Content

Aortic dissection

1 per 10,000 pregnancies; 50% occur in 3rd trimester or postpartum; type A 2/3

Risk factors

Marfan syndrome (5-10% risk); bicuspid aortic valve; coarctation; HTN; cocaine; trauma; vascular Ehlers-Danlos

Marfan in pregnancy

Aortic root >45 mm — pregnancy contraindicated; 40-45 mm — individualised; <40 mm — generally safe with β-blocker

Presentation dissection

Severe tearing chest/back pain; pulse deficits; BP differential; aortic regurgitation murmur; shock; neurological signs

Diagnosis

CT aortogram (gold standard); TTE/TEE; MRI; ECG (LVH, ischaemia); D-dimer may be raised

Management dissection

Type A: emergency surgery (Caesarean if viable fetus first); Type B: medical (β-blocker, analgesia) ± TEVAR

Medical management

β-blocker (labetalol, metoprolol) to reduce dP/dt; target HR <70; systolic BP 100-120 mmHg; analgesia

Surgical

Aortic root replacement; valve-sparing; Caesarean at same time if needed

Preconception

Aortic root imaging; replace if >45 mm; genetic counselling; switch teratogens; β-blocker preconception

Delivery

MDT; epidural (reduces stress response); assisted second stage; avoid ergometrine; consider C-section in Marfan >40 mm

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Aortic dissection1 per 10,000 pregnancies; 50% occur in 3rd trimester or postpartum; type A 2/3
Risk factorsMarfan syndrome (5-10% risk); bicuspid aortic valve; coarctation; HTN; cocaine; trauma; vascular Ehlers-Danlos
Marfan in pregnancyAortic root >45 mm — pregnancy contraindicated; 40-45 mm — individualised; <40 mm — generally safe with β-blocker
Presentation dissectionSevere tearing chest/back pain; pulse deficits; BP differential; aortic regurgitation murmur; shock; neurological signs
DiagnosisCT aortogram (gold standard); TTE/TEE; MRI; ECG (LVH, ischaemia); D-dimer may be raised
Management dissectionType A: emergency surgery (Caesarean if viable fetus first); Type B: medical (β-blocker, analgesia) ± TEVAR
Medical managementβ-blocker (labetalol, metoprolol) to reduce dP/dt; target HR <70; systolic BP 100-120 mmHg; analgesia
SurgicalAortic root replacement; valve-sparing; Caesarean at same time if needed
PreconceptionAortic root imaging; replace if >45 mm; genetic counselling; switch teratogens; β-blocker preconception
DeliveryMDT; epidural (reduces stress response); assisted second stage; avoid ergometrine; consider C-section in Marfan >40 mm

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Aortic disease in pregnancy?

Aortic disease in pregnancy, including aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection, and conditions predisposing to dissection (Marfan, Loeys-Dietz, vascular Ehlers-Danlos, bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation), carries significant mortality. Pregnancy increases aortic wall stress due to hyperdynamic circulation and hormonal effects on connective tissue.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Aortic disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Aortic dissection, Risk factors, Marfan in pregnancy, Presentation dissection, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Aortic disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Aortic disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Aortic disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Aortic disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.9.2 Arrhythmias In Pregnancy

Definition

Arrhythmias in pregnancy include atrial and ventricular ectopics, supraventricular tachycardia (SVT), atrial fibrillation/flutter, and ventricular tachycardia. They are usually benign but may indicate underlying heart disease, thyroid dysfunction, or electrolyte disturbance. Treatment is similar to non-pregnant with attention to fetal safety of drugs.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

Palpitations common (50%); AF rare (2/1000) — usually with structural heart disease or hyperthyroidism

Causes

Structural heart disease; hyperthyroidism; electrolyte disturbance; stimulants; caffeine; alcohol; drug use; pre-excitation (WPW); congenital arrhythmia syndromes

SVT

Most common sustained arrhythmia in pregnancy; vagal manoeuvres first; adenosine if fails (safe in pregnancy); β-blocker; verapamil; DC cardioversion if unstable

Atrial fibrillation/flutter

Often with structural heart disease (MS, congenital); anticoagulate (LMWH); rate control (β-blocker, digoxin); cardioversion if unstable

Ventricular tachycardia

Rare; assess for structural disease and channelopathy (Long QT, Brugada, CPVT); DC cardioversion if unstable; ICD if recurrent; avoid QT-prolonging drugs

Bradyarrhythmias

Rare; consider heart block (congenital or SLE); pacing if symptomatic; complete heart block often needs permanent pacing

Drug safety

Adenosine, β-blockers (labetalol, metoprolol), verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, lidocaine — generally safe; amiodarone avoid (thyroid, fetal); flecainide safe; sotalol safe

Investigation

ECG; 24-h Holter; echo; TFT; electrolytes; consider EP study postpartum

Anticoagulation

Score and consider LMWH if AF >48 h or high risk; cardioversion with TOE if duration unclear

Delivery planning

Avoid Valsalva; consider assisted second stage; consider epidural; continuous CTG; postpartum monitoring

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
IncidencePalpitations common (50%); AF rare (2/1000) — usually with structural heart disease or hyperthyroidism
CausesStructural heart disease; hyperthyroidism; electrolyte disturbance; stimulants; caffeine; alcohol; drug use; pre-excitation (WPW); congenital arrhythmia syndromes
SVTMost common sustained arrhythmia in pregnancy; vagal manoeuvres first; adenosine if fails (safe in pregnancy); β-blocker; verapamil; DC cardioversion if unstable
Atrial fibrillation/flutterOften with structural heart disease (MS, congenital); anticoagulate (LMWH); rate control (β-blocker, digoxin); cardioversion if unstable
Ventricular tachycardiaRare; assess for structural disease and channelopathy (Long QT, Brugada, CPVT); DC cardioversion if unstable; ICD if recurrent; avoid QT-prolonging drugs
BradyarrhythmiasRare; consider heart block (congenital or SLE); pacing if symptomatic; complete heart block often needs permanent pacing
Drug safetyAdenosine, β-blockers (labetalol, metoprolol), verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, lidocaine — generally safe; amiodarone avoid (thyroid, fetal); flecainide safe; sotalol safe
InvestigationECG; 24-h Holter; echo; TFT; electrolytes; consider EP study postpartum
AnticoagulationScore and consider LMWH if AF >48 h or high risk; cardioversion with TOE if duration unclear
Delivery planningAvoid Valsalva; consider assisted second stage; consider epidural; continuous CTG; postpartum monitoring

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Arrhythmias in pregnancy?

Arrhythmias in pregnancy include atrial and ventricular ectopics, supraventricular tachycardia (SVT), atrial fibrillation/flutter, and ventricular tachycardia. They are usually benign but may indicate underlying heart disease, thyroid dysfunction, or electrolyte disturbance. Treatment is similar to non-pregnant with attention to fetal safety of drugs.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Arrhythmias in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Causes, SVT, Atrial fibrillation/flutter, Ventricular tachycardia. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Arrhythmias in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Arrhythmias in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Arrhythmias in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Arrhythmias in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

B) Rare; assess for structural disease and channelopathy (Long QT, Brugada, CPVT); DC cardioversion if unstable; ICD if recurrent; avoid QT-prolonging drugs

C) Rare; consider heart block (congenital or SLE); pacing if symptomatic; complete heart block often needs permanent pacing

D) Adenosine, β-blockers (labetalol, metoprolol), verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, lidocaine — generally safe; amiodarone avoid (thyroid, fetal); flecainide safe; sotalol safe

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Arrhythmias in pregnancy?

A) Rare; assess for structural disease and channelopathy (Long QT, Brugada, CPVT); DC cardioversion if unstable; ICD if recurrent; avoid QT-prolonging drugs

B) Rare; consider heart block (congenital or SLE); pacing if symptomatic; complete heart block often needs permanent pacing

C) Adenosine, β-blockers (labetalol, metoprolol), verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, lidocaine — generally safe; amiodarone avoid (thyroid, fetal); flecainide safe; sotalol safe

D) ECG; 24-h Holter; echo; TFT; electrolytes; consider EP study postpartum

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Arrhythmias in pregnancy?

A) Rare; consider heart block (congenital or SLE); pacing if symptomatic; complete heart block often needs permanent pacing

B) Adenosine, β-blockers (labetalol, metoprolol), verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, lidocaine — generally safe; amiodarone avoid (thyroid, fetal); flecainide safe; sotalol safe

C) ECG; 24-h Holter; echo; TFT; electrolytes; consider EP study postpartum

D) Score and consider LMWH if AF >48 h or high risk; cardioversion with TOE if duration unclear

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Arrhythmias in pregnancy?

A) Adenosine, β-blockers (labetalol, metoprolol), verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, lidocaine — generally safe; amiodarone avoid (thyroid, fetal); flecainide safe; sotalol safe

B) ECG; 24-h Holter; echo; TFT; electrolytes; consider EP study postpartum

C) Score and consider LMWH if AF >48 h or high risk; cardioversion with TOE if duration unclear

D) Avoid Valsalva; consider assisted second stage; consider epidural; continuous CTG; postpartum monitoring

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Arrhythmias in pregnancy?

A) ECG; 24-h Holter; echo; TFT; electrolytes; consider EP study postpartum

B) Score and consider LMWH if AF >48 h or high risk; cardioversion with TOE if duration unclear

C) Avoid Valsalva; consider assisted second stage; consider epidural; continuous CTG; postpartum monitoring

D) Palpitations common (50%); AF rare (2/1000) — usually with structural heart disease or hyperthyroidism

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Arrhythmias in pregnancy?

A) Score and consider LMWH if AF >48 h or high risk; cardioversion with TOE if duration unclear

B) Avoid Valsalva; consider assisted second stage; consider epidural; continuous CTG; postpartum monitoring

C) Palpitations common (50%); AF rare (2/1000) — usually with structural heart disease or hyperthyroidism

D) Structural heart disease; hyperthyroidism; electrolyte disturbance; stimulants; caffeine; alcohol; drug use; pre-excitation (WPW); congenital arrhythmia syndromes

Answer: A

  1. Which of the following is true about Arrhythmias in pregnancy?

A) Avoid Valsalva; consider assisted second stage; consider epidural; continuous CTG; postpartum monitoring

B) Palpitations common (50%); AF rare (2/1000) — usually with structural heart disease or hyperthyroidism

C) Structural heart disease; hyperthyroidism; electrolyte disturbance; stimulants; caffeine; alcohol; drug use; pre-excitation (WPW); congenital arrhythmia syndromes

D) Most common sustained arrhythmia in pregnancy; vagal manoeuvres first; adenosine if fails (safe in pregnancy); β-blocker; verapamil; DC cardioversion if unstable

Answer: A

Flashcards

Q: Incidence?

A: Palpitations common (50%); AF rare (2/1000) — usually with structural heart disease or hyperthyroidism

Q: Causes?

A: Structural heart disease; hyperthyroidism; electrolyte disturbance; stimulants; caffeine; alcohol; drug use; pre-excitation (WPW); congenital arrhythmia syndromes

Q: SVT?

A: Most common sustained arrhythmia in pregnancy; vagal manoeuvres first; adenosine if fails (safe in pregnancy); β-blocker; verapamil; DC cardioversion if unstable

Q: Atrial fibrillation/flutter?

A: Often with structural heart disease (MS, congenital); anticoagulate (LMWH); rate control (β-blocker, digoxin); cardioversion if unstable

Q: Ventricular tachycardia?

A: Rare; assess for structural disease and channelopathy (Long QT, Brugada, CPVT); DC cardioversion if unstable; ICD if recurrent; avoid QT-prolonging drugs

Q: Bradyarrhythmias?

A: Rare; consider heart block (congenital or SLE); pacing if symptomatic; complete heart block often needs permanent pacing

Q: Drug safety?

A: Adenosine, β-blockers (labetalol, metoprolol), verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, lidocaine — generally safe; amiodarone avoid (thyroid, fetal); flecainide safe; sotalol safe

Q: Investigation?

A: ECG; 24-h Holter; echo; TFT; electrolytes; consider EP study postpartum

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.9.3 Ischaemic Heart Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy — including stable angina, acute coronary syndrome (ACS), and myocardial infarction — is rare but increasing due to advanced maternal age, obesity, and pre-existing risk factors. Pregnancy is hypercoagulable and hyperdynamic, increasing cardiovascular risk. Presentation may be atypical and is often missed.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

MI 1-3 per 100,000 deliveries; rising; leading non-obstetric cause of maternal death in some series

Risk factors

Advanced maternal age; obesity; smoking; hypertension; diabetes; hyperlipidaemia; family history; APS; previous MI

Mechanism

Atherosclerosis (50%); coronary dissection (P-SCAD; 30% — especially postpartum); thrombosis (coronary spasm); embolism

Presentation

Chest pain (atypical — epigastric, jaw, back); dyspnoea; diaphoresis; nausea; fatigue; collapse

Diagnosis

ECG (ST changes; may be hard to interpret in pregnancy); troponin (valid); echo (regional wall motion abnormalities); CT coronary angiography (low dose)

Management ACS

Aspirin 300 mg; clopidogrel; LMWH; β-blocker; nitrates; oxygen; PCI if STEMI (preferred over thrombolysis); radial approach preferred

Drugs to avoid

Statins (teratogenic — stop at pregnancy diagnosis); ACEI/ARB; warfarin in 1st trimester; glycoprotein IIb/IIIa — limited data

PCI

Bare metal stent preferred (avoid drug-eluting for 1st trimester); dual antiplatelet; LMWH cover for delivery

Delivery planning

MDT; vaginal preferred (avoid tachycardia, hypotension); epidural; assisted second stage; avoid ergometrine (causes vasospasm)

Prognosis

Maternal mortality 5-10% (higher if peripartum or anterior MI); recurrence 15-30% in subsequent pregnancies

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
IncidenceMI 1-3 per 100,000 deliveries; rising; leading non-obstetric cause of maternal death in some series
Risk factorsAdvanced maternal age; obesity; smoking; hypertension; diabetes; hyperlipidaemia; family history; APS; previous MI
MechanismAtherosclerosis (50%); coronary dissection (P-SCAD; 30% — especially postpartum); thrombosis (coronary spasm); embolism
PresentationChest pain (atypical — epigastric, jaw, back); dyspnoea; diaphoresis; nausea; fatigue; collapse
DiagnosisECG (ST changes; may be hard to interpret in pregnancy); troponin (valid); echo (regional wall motion abnormalities); CT coronary angiography (low dose)
Management ACSAspirin 300 mg; clopidogrel; LMWH; β-blocker; nitrates; oxygen; PCI if STEMI (preferred over thrombolysis); radial approach preferred
Drugs to avoidStatins (teratogenic — stop at pregnancy diagnosis); ACEI/ARB; warfarin in 1st trimester; glycoprotein IIb/IIIa — limited data
PCIBare metal stent preferred (avoid drug-eluting for 1st trimester); dual antiplatelet; LMWH cover for delivery
Delivery planningMDT; vaginal preferred (avoid tachycardia, hypotension); epidural; assisted second stage; avoid ergometrine (causes vasospasm)
PrognosisMaternal mortality 5-10% (higher if peripartum or anterior MI); recurrence 15-30% in subsequent pregnancies

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy?

Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy — including stable angina, acute coronary syndrome (ACS), and myocardial infarction — is rare but increasing due to advanced maternal age, obesity, and pre-existing risk factors. Pregnancy is hypercoagulable and hyperdynamic, increasing cardiovascular risk. Presentation may be atypical and is often missed.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Risk factors, Mechanism, Presentation, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.9.4 Peripartum Cardiomyopathy

Definition

Peripartum cardiomyopathy is an idiopathic cardiomyopathy presenting with heart failure in the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum, in the absence of other identifiable causes. Diagnosis requires LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF <45%).

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Idiopathic cardiomyopathy with LVEF <45% presenting last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum

Epidemiology

1 in 1,000-4,000 births; higher in Black women, age >30, multiparity, pre-eclampsia

Pathophysiology

Likely cleaved prolactin, sFlt-1, vasculopathy, viral, autoimmune

Presentation

Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, fatigue, oedema, palpitations, chest pain — often misdiagnosed as 'normal pregnancy'

Diagnosis

Echo (LVEF <45%); ECG; BNP/NT-proBNP; exclude other causes (TTE, CMR)

Differential

Pre-existing dilated cardiomyopathy; pre-eclampsia/HELLP with cardiac involvement; PE; sepsis

Acute management

Oxygen, diuretics (furosemide), vasodilators; ACEI/ARB/ARNI postpartum; beta-blocker; bromocriptine 2.5 mg BD 2 weeks (reduces mortality)

Anticoagulation

LMWH if LVEF <35% or AF

Subsequent pregnancy

Contraindicated if LVEF <50%; if LVEF recovers to >50% — close monitoring

Prognosis

~50-70% recover LV function by 6 months; 10-25% deteriorate; 5-year mortality 5-25%

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionIdiopathic cardiomyopathy with LVEF <45% presenting last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum
Epidemiology1 in 1,000-4,000 births; higher in Black women, age >30, multiparity, pre-eclampsia
PathophysiologyLikely cleaved prolactin, sFlt-1, vasculopathy, viral, autoimmune
PresentationDyspnoea, orthopnoea, fatigue, oedema, palpitations, chest pain — often misdiagnosed as 'normal pregnancy'
DiagnosisEcho (LVEF <45%); ECG; BNP/NT-proBNP; exclude other causes (TTE, CMR)
DifferentialPre-existing dilated cardiomyopathy; pre-eclampsia/HELLP with cardiac involvement; PE; sepsis
Acute managementOxygen, diuretics (furosemide), vasodilators; ACEI/ARB/ARNI postpartum; beta-blocker; bromocriptine 2.5 mg BD 2 weeks (reduces mortality)
AnticoagulationLMWH if LVEF <35% or AF
Subsequent pregnancyContraindicated if LVEF <50%; if LVEF recovers to >50% — close monitoring
Prognosis~50-70% recover LV function by 6 months; 10-25% deteriorate; 5-year mortality 5-25%

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Peripartum cardiomyopathy?

Peripartum cardiomyopathy is an idiopathic cardiomyopathy presenting with heart failure in the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum, in the absence of other identifiable causes. Diagnosis requires LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF <45%).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Peripartum cardiomyopathy?

Key features include: Definition, Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, Presentation, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Peripartum cardiomyopathy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Peripartum cardiomyopathy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Peripartum cardiomyopathy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Peripartum cardiomyopathy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Peripartum cardiomyopathy

Q: Definition?

A: Idiopathic cardiomyopathy with LVEF <45% presenting last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum

Q: Epidemiology?

Q: Pathophysiology?

A: Likely cleaved prolactin, sFlt-1, vasculopathy, viral, autoimmune

Q: Presentation?

A: Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, fatigue, oedema, palpitations, chest pain — often misdiagnosed as 'normal pregnancy'

Q: Diagnosis?

A: Echo (LVEF <45%); ECG; BNP/NT-proBNP; exclude other causes (TTE, CMR)

Q: Differential?

A: Pre-existing dilated cardiomyopathy; pre-eclampsia/HELLP with cardiac involvement; PE; sepsis

Q: Acute management?

A: Oxygen, diuretics (furosemide), vasodilators; ACEI/ARB/ARNI postpartum; beta-blocker; bromocriptine 2.5 mg BD 2 weeks (reduces mortality)

Q: Anticoagulation?

A: LMWH if LVEF <35% or AF

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 30.9.5 Pre Existing Heart Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Pre-existing heart disease affects 1-4% of pregnancies and is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Risk assessment (modified WHO classification) and multidisciplinary care in a specialist centre are essential. Pregnancy is generally contraindicated in WHO class IV conditions.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

1-4% of pregnancies; cardiac disease is leading non-obstetric cause of maternal death

Modified WHO classification

I: small risk; II: small-moderate; III: significant (specialist care); IV: pregnancy contraindicated

WHO IV (contraindicated)

Severe systemic ventricular dysfunction (EF <30%); severe MS/AS; previous PPCM with residual EF <50%; cyanotic CHD; Marfan with aortic root >45 mm

Common conditions

Congenital (ASD, VSD, ToF, Marfan, bicuspid aortic valve); acquired (rheumatic, MVP, ischaemic); cardiomyopathy (PPCMP)

Preconception

Echo, ECG, medication review; switch ACEI/warfarin; genetic counselling if inherited

Antenatal care

Multidisciplinary team; review every 4-6 weeks; serial BNP/echo; anaemia prevention

Labour and delivery

Vaginal usually preferred; assisted second stage; avoid supine position; early epidural

Antibiotic prophylaxis

For delivery in women with prosthetic valves or unrepaired cyanotic CHD

VTE prophylaxis

LMWH if class III/IV; immobility; mechanical valves

Postpartum

Haemodynamic monitoring 24-48 h; warfarin can be restarted; breastfeeding compatible with most cardiac drugs

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence1-4% of pregnancies; cardiac disease is leading non-obstetric cause of maternal death
Modified WHO classificationI: small risk; II: small-moderate; III: significant (specialist care); IV: pregnancy contraindicated
WHO IV (contraindicated)Severe systemic ventricular dysfunction (EF <30%); severe MS/AS; previous PPCM with residual EF <50%; cyanotic CHD; Marfan with aortic root >45 mm
Common conditionsCongenital (ASD, VSD, ToF, Marfan, bicuspid aortic valve); acquired (rheumatic, MVP, ischaemic); cardiomyopathy (PPCMP)
PreconceptionEcho, ECG, medication review; switch ACEI/warfarin; genetic counselling if inherited
Antenatal careMultidisciplinary team; review every 4-6 weeks; serial BNP/echo; anaemia prevention
Labour and deliveryVaginal usually preferred; assisted second stage; avoid supine position; early epidural
Antibiotic prophylaxisFor delivery in women with prosthetic valves or unrepaired cyanotic CHD
VTE prophylaxisLMWH if class III/IV; immobility; mechanical valves
PostpartumHaemodynamic monitoring 24-48 h; warfarin can be restarted; breastfeeding compatible with most cardiac drugs

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy?

Pre-existing heart disease affects 1-4% of pregnancies and is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Risk assessment (modified WHO classification) and multidisciplinary care in a specialist centre are essential. Pregnancy is generally contraindicated in WHO class IV conditions.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Modified WHO classification, WHO IV (contraindicated), Common conditions, Preconception. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

Flashcards

Q: Prevalence?

A: 1-4% of pregnancies; cardiac disease is leading non-obstetric cause of maternal death

Q: Modified WHO classification?

A: I: small risk; II: small-moderate; III: significant (specialist care); IV: pregnancy contraindicated

Q: WHO IV (contraindicated)?

A: Severe systemic ventricular dysfunction (EF <30%); severe MS/AS; previous PPCM with residual EF <50%; cyanotic CHD; Marfan with aortic root >45 mm

Q: Common conditions?

A: Congenital (ASD, VSD, ToF, Marfan, bicuspid aortic valve); acquired (rheumatic, MVP, ischaemic); cardiomyopathy (PPCMP)

Q: Preconception?

A: Echo, ECG, medication review; switch ACEI/warfarin; genetic counselling if inherited

Q: Antenatal care?

A: Multidisciplinary team; review every 4-6 weeks; serial BNP/echo; anaemia prevention

Q: Labour and delivery?

A: Vaginal usually preferred; assisted second stage; avoid supine position; early epidural

Q: Antibiotic prophylaxis?

A: For delivery in women with prosthetic valves or unrepaired cyanotic CHD

▸ 30.9.6 Pulmonary Hypertension In Pregnancy

Definition

Pulmonary hypertension (PH) in pregnancy is a high-risk condition associated with up to 30-50% maternal mortality. Pregnancy is generally contraindicated in severe PH (WHO class IV). Multidisciplinary care in a specialist PH centre is essential if pregnancy continues.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Mean pulmonary artery pressure >20 mmHg at rest (revised); previously ≥25 mmHg

Classification

Group 1 pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH); Group 2 (left heart); Group 3 (lung); Group 4 (CTEPH); Group 5 (misc)

Maternal mortality

30-50% without therapy; reduces to ~10-30% with modern PH care

Pathophysiology

Fixed PVR + pregnancy-related ↑CO + ↓SVR → RV failure; peripartum particularly dangerous

Symptoms

Progressive dyspnoea, fatigue, syncope, chest pain, RV failure signs

Investigations

Echo (PASP, RV function); RHC for definitive diagnosis; V/Q scan if CTEPH suspected

Preconception

Counsel about mortality; offer termination; switch endothelin receptor antagonists (teratogenic)

Therapy

Prostacyclins (epoprostenol, iloprost); sildenafil; LMWH; planned delivery under MDT

Labour and delivery

Avoid hypotension; assisted second stage; avoid general anaesthesia; central line; pulmonary vasodilators continued

Postpartum

24-48 h ITU monitoring; PH team input; VTE prophylaxis; avoid pregnancy for 1 year minimum

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionMean pulmonary artery pressure >20 mmHg at rest (revised); previously ≥25 mmHg
ClassificationGroup 1 pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH); Group 2 (left heart); Group 3 (lung); Group 4 (CTEPH); Group 5 (misc)
Maternal mortality30-50% without therapy; reduces to ~10-30% with modern PH care
PathophysiologyFixed PVR + pregnancy-related ↑CO + ↓SVR → RV failure; peripartum particularly dangerous
SymptomsProgressive dyspnoea, fatigue, syncope, chest pain, RV failure signs
InvestigationsEcho (PASP, RV function); RHC for definitive diagnosis; V/Q scan if CTEPH suspected
PreconceptionCounsel about mortality; offer termination; switch endothelin receptor antagonists (teratogenic)
TherapyProstacyclins (epoprostenol, iloprost); sildenafil; LMWH; planned delivery under MDT
Labour and deliveryAvoid hypotension; assisted second stage; avoid general anaesthesia; central line; pulmonary vasodilators continued
Postpartum24-48 h ITU monitoring; PH team input; VTE prophylaxis; avoid pregnancy for 1 year minimum

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pulmonary hypertension in pregnancy?

Pulmonary hypertension (PH) in pregnancy is a high-risk condition associated with up to 30-50% maternal mortality. Pregnancy is generally contraindicated in severe PH (WHO class IV). Multidisciplinary care in a specialist PH centre is essential if pregnancy continues.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pulmonary hypertension in pregnancy?

Key features include: Definition, Classification, Maternal mortality, Pathophysiology, Symptoms. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pulmonary hypertension in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pulmonary hypertension in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pulmonary hypertension in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pulmonary hypertension in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

▸ 30.9.7 Valvular Heart Disease In Pregnancy

Definition

Valvular heart disease — most commonly rheumatic mitral stenosis in developing countries and congenital bicuspid aortic valve/stenosis in developed countries — is a leading cause of maternal morbidity. Stenotic lesions are higher risk than regurgitant lesions because the fixed output cannot accommodate pregnancy demands.

Core Clinical Content

Mitral stenosis

Most common rheumatic lesion; pre-conception balloon valvuloplasty if severe (MVA <1.5 cm²)

Symptoms MS

Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, PND, haemoptysis, AF — often worsen in 2nd/3rd trimesters

Aortic stenosis

Bicuspid valve; symptoms (angina, syncope) may develop as CO fails to rise

Mitral/aortic regurgitation

Lower risk — reduced SVR is protective; treat medically

Anticoagulation

AF, mechanical valve, prior thrombus — LMWH throughout pregnancy; switch warfarin at 6-12 weeks

Mechanical valve

High risk: 1-2% maternal mortality; valve thrombosis; LMWH requires anti-Xa monitoring; warfarin 5 mg max preferred for mechanical AV after 12 weeks

Antibiotic prophylaxis

Not routinely for delivery; per current guidelines

Labour and delivery

Vaginal preferred; haemodynamic monitoring; assisted second stage; avoid tachycardia/hypotension

Percutaneous valvotomy

Option for severe MS failing medical therapy; experienced centre

Postpartum

VTE prophylaxis; close follow-up; valve surgery deferred if possible until after delivery

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Mitral stenosisMost common rheumatic lesion; pre-conception balloon valvuloplasty if severe (MVA <1.5 cm²)
Symptoms MSDyspnoea, orthopnoea, PND, haemoptysis, AF — often worsen in 2nd/3rd trimesters
Aortic stenosisBicuspid valve; symptoms (angina, syncope) may develop as CO fails to rise
Mitral/aortic regurgitationLower risk — reduced SVR is protective; treat medically
AnticoagulationAF, mechanical valve, prior thrombus — LMWH throughout pregnancy; switch warfarin at 6-12 weeks
Mechanical valveHigh risk: 1-2% maternal mortality; valve thrombosis; LMWH requires anti-Xa monitoring; warfarin 5 mg max preferred for mechanical AV after 12 weeks
Antibiotic prophylaxisNot routinely for delivery; per current guidelines
Labour and deliveryVaginal preferred; haemodynamic monitoring; assisted second stage; avoid tachycardia/hypotension
Percutaneous valvotomyOption for severe MS failing medical therapy; experienced centre
PostpartumVTE prophylaxis; close follow-up; valve surgery deferred if possible until after delivery

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Valvular heart disease in pregnancy?

Valvular heart disease — most commonly rheumatic mitral stenosis in developing countries and congenital bicuspid aortic valve/stenosis in developed countries — is a leading cause of maternal morbidity. Stenotic lesions are higher risk than regurgitant lesions because the fixed output cannot accommodate pregnancy demands.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Valvular heart disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Mitral stenosis, Symptoms MS, Aortic stenosis, Mitral/aortic regurgitation, Anticoagulation. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Valvular heart disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Valvular heart disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Valvular heart disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Valvular heart disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

  4. Lower risk — reduced SVR is protective; treat medically
  5. AF, mechanical valve, prior thrombus — LMWH throughout pregnancy; switch warfarin at 6-12 weeks
  6. High risk: 1-2% maternal mortality; valve thrombosis; LMWH requires anti-Xa monitoring; warfarin 5 mg max preferred for mechanical AV after 12 weeks
  7. Not routinely for delivery; per current guidelines
  8. Vaginal preferred; haemodynamic monitoring; assisted second stage; avoid tachycardia/hypotension
  9. Option for severe MS failing medical therapy; experienced centre
  10. VTE prophylaxis; close follow-up; valve surgery deferred if possible until after delivery
